```mermaid
    resultCheck -- Assigned with or No --> bindOrInvis[Stage 4b: Bind to name OR mark invisible OR side effect only]
    autoPrint --> loop[Loop back to await next command]
    bindOrInvis --> loop
    synErr --> loop
```

## 4.2 R's Three Execution Modes — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IM[Interactive Mode]
        i1[User at gt prompt] --> i2[Single command per line]
        i2 --> i3[Immediate evaluation and auto print]
    end

    subgraph SM[Script Mode]
        s1[Edit file with extension dot R] --> s2[Call source filename R in console]
        s2 --> s3[OR run Rscript filename R from terminal]
        s3 --> s4[Sequential line by line execution]
    end

    subgraph BM[Batch Mode]
        b1[Submit job to server or scheduler] --> b2[R CMD BATCH or Rscript]
        b2 --> b3[Output written to dot Rout file]
        b3 --> b4[No human in the loop]
    end

    REPL[Common REPL Engine Parse Evaluate Return] -.serves.-> IM
    REPL -.serves.-> SM
    REPL -.serves.-> BM
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — What Happens to a Command's Result?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cmd[Command Entered] --> assigned{Is it an assignment using lt dash?}
    assigned -- Yes --> silent[Silent Bind value to name in global env Nothing printed]
    assigned -- No --> wrapped{Is it wrapped in invisible parens?}
    wrapped -- Yes --> silent2[Result exists but is NOT auto printed]
    wrapped -- No --> topLevel{Is it at the top level of the prompt?}
    topLevel -- Yes --> printed[Auto printed with 1 prefix for vectors]
    topLevel -- No inside function --> returned[Returned to the calling code Visible or invisible per caller]
    silent --> done[Command complete]
    silent2 --> done
    printed --> done
    returned --> done
```

## 4.4 Workspace Persistence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    session1[Session 1 Start R] --> work[Create objects x y z in global env]
    work --> saveCmd[Run save image command writes dot RData]
    saveCmd --> quit[Run q command]
    quit --> file[File dot RData saved to working directory]

    file --> session2[Session 2 Restart R in same directory]
    session2 --> autoLoad[R auto loads dot RData on startup]
    autoLoad --> restored[Objects x y z restored silently]
    restored --> work2[Continue working]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Best Practice:** Use **Projects in RStudio** (`.Rproj` files) to avoid ambiguity in working directories and ensure `.RData`, `.Rhistory`, and scripts travel together as a self-contained unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`**
Explain the concept of the **REPL cycle** in R. How does the `>` prompt differ from the `+` continuation prompt?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **REPL (Read–Eval–Print Loop)** is the fundamental execution cycle of the R interactive console. It continuously performs four steps:

1. **Read** — R reads a user-typed expression from standard input.
2. **Evaluate** — R parses the expression into an Abstract Syntax Tree and evaluates it.
3. **Print** — If the result is a top-level auto-printable expression, R displays it (with the `[1]` index prefix for vectors).
4. **Loop** — R returns to step 1 and awaits the next command.

The **`>` prompt** is the standard ready prompt, indicating that R is waiting for a new, complete command. The **`+` continuation prompt** appears when the user has entered a command that is **syntactically incomplete** — for example, an unmatched parenthesis `(` or bracket `{` — and R expects the rest of the input on subsequent lines. Pressing **Esc** (RStudio) or **Ctrl+C** (terminal) cancels the in-progress command and returns to `>`. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`**
Distinguish between **interactive mode**, **script mode**, and **batch mode** of executing commands in R. State one use-case for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Invocation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Interactive** | Typing at `>` prompt | Quick data exploration and debugging |
| **Script** | `source("file.R")` or `Rscript file.R` | Reproducible analysis, lab submissions |
| **Batch** | `R CMD BATCH file.R` (output to `.Rout`) | Server-side, unattended, long jobs |

In **interactive mode**, the user enters one command at a time and sees immediate output — ideal for learning. In **script mode**, a sequence of commands saved in a `.R` file is executed in order via `source()` — ideal for reproducible research. In **batch mode**, R runs a script non-interactively and writes output to a file (`.Rout`) — ideal for production pipelines where no human is present. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't** confuse the **continuation prompt `+`** with the **addition operator `+`**. They look identical but occur in completely different contexts. Always explain that the continuation prompt is a UI signal, not a syntactic token.
> - **Don't** say R "compiles" code. R is an **interpreted language** — it parses and evaluates at runtime, with no separate compile-to-binary step in the standard workflow.
> - **Don't** forget to mention the **`[1]` prefix** in printed vectors — it is often a giveaway that a student has actually run R, and missing it loses a mark.
> - **Don't** mix up `=` (argument-binding inside function calls) with `<-` (general assignment). The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests this distinction.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Predicted Pattern]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **REPL cycle** in R. Explain, with a worked example, what happens internally when the user types the command `x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5); mean(x)` at the R prompt. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the roles of the following in command execution: (i) the `source()` function, (ii) the `sink()` function, (iii) the `library()` function. Illustrate each with a one-line example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) The REPL Cycle and Worked Example (7 marks):**

**Block diagram of REPL:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Read expression from stdin] --> B[Parse into AST]
    B --> C[Evaluate the AST]
    C --> D[Auto print OR bind OR return invisibly]
    D --> A
```

**Worked example — `x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5); mean(x)`:**

The semicolon `;` is a command separator in R, so the input is split into **two separate commands** that execute sequentially.

- **Command 1: `x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)`**
  1. **Read** the string `"x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)"`.
  2. **Tokenize** into `NAME(x)`, `ASSIGN(<-)`, `NAME(c)`, `LPAREN`, `NUM(1)`, `COMMA`, ..., `RPAREN`.
  3. **Parse** into an AST showing an assignment whose right-hand side is a call to `c()` with five numeric arguments.
  4. **Evaluate** the call to `c()`: the function concatenates its arguments into the numeric vector `(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)`.
  5. **Bind** the resulting vector to the name `x` in the global environment.
  6. **No auto-print** — assignment suppresses printing.

- **Command 2: `mean(x)`**
  1. **Read**, **tokenize**, **parse** into a function call.
  2. **Evaluate**: R looks up `x`, retrieves the vector, passes it to `mean()`. The internal sum is

   $$\text{sum} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15$$

   and the length is

   $$n = 5,$$

   so the result is

   $$\text{mean}(x) = \frac{15}{5} = 3.$$

  3. **Auto-print**: R displays

   ```r
   [1] 3
   ```

**(b) Roles of `source()`, `sink()`, and `library()` (7 marks):**

| Function | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `source()` | Reads, parses, and evaluates a `.R` script file in the current environment. Used to load reusable code or run analyses from a file. | `source("analysis.R")` |
| `sink()` | Redirects `stdout()` output from the console to a specified text file. Useful for logging analysis results. Pair with `sink()` (no argument) to restore. | `sink("log.txt"); print(x); sink()` |
| `library()` | Loads a previously installed package's namespace into the current R session, making its functions and datasets available by name. | `library(ggplot2)` |

The distinction: `source()` executes a **user-written script**; `sink()` controls **where output goes**; `library()` activates a **packaged extension**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Predicted Pattern]`**

**(a)** List and explain any **four categories of R commands** with one example each. Describe the difference between `<-` and `=` for assignment in R. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A junior programmer complains: *"I typed a long command and now the prompt is stuck on `+`."* Explain what is happening, why it occurs, and how to recover. Also explain the role of the `Esc` and `Ctrl+C` keys in this situation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Categories of R Commands (7 marks):**

1. **Arithmetic/Logical Expressions** — direct computations.
   Example: `(3 + 5) * 2` → `[1] 16`.
2. **Assignment Statements** — bind values to names.
   Example: `score <- 95.5` binds the numeric value to `score`.
3. **Function Definitions** — create reusable functions.
   Example:

   ```r
   cube <- function(n) n^3
   ```

4. **Workspace/Help Commands** — manage session state.
   Example: `ls()` lists all objects in the global environment; `?sd` opens the help page for standard deviation.

**Difference between `<-` and `=`:**

- The `<-` operator is the **canonical, idiomatic** assignment operator in R and is recommended in the R style guides. It always means assignment, everywhere.
- The `=` operator is **syntactically ambiguous**: at the **top level**, it performs assignment just like `<-`, but **inside a function call's argument list**, `=` means *argument binding*, not assignment.
- For example, in `mean(x = c(1, 2, 3))`, the `x = ` part is **not** creating a global object named `x`; it is naming the first argument of `mean`. Using `<-` there would attempt to assign the vector to `x` *before* `mean()` is even called — a common source of bugs.

**(b) The "Stuck on `+`" Problem (7 marks):**

**What is happening:** The R interpreter has received a command that is **syntactically incomplete** — typically an unmatched opening parenthesis `(`, bracket `[`, or brace `{`, or an unclosed string literal `"`. Because R cannot yet build a valid AST, it switches its prompt from `>` to the **continuation prompt `+`** and waits for the rest of the input.

**Why it occurs:** Common causes include:

- Forgetting a closing `)` after a function call.
- Writing a multi-line `function() { ... }` block and not closing the brace.
- Forgetting the closing quote of a string.

**How to recover:**

- In **RStudio**, press the **`Esc`** key — this immediately aborts the half-typed command and returns the console to the `>` prompt.
- In a **terminal R session**, press **`Ctrl+C`** — this sends an interrupt signal that cancels the current input or running computation and restores the `>` prompt.
- **Best practice:** Keep commands short and well-parenthesized; use an R-aware editor (RStudio, VS Code) that auto-completes brackets and quotes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **R is interpreted**, not compiled — every command goes through the REPL at runtime.
- ✅ The **`>`** prompt is the **ready** prompt; the **`+`** prompt is the **continuation** prompt for incomplete input.
- ✅ Use **`source("file.R")`** to execute external scripts; **`Rscript file.R`** to run from the terminal.
- ✅ Use **`R CMD BATCH file.R`** for true batch execution — output goes to `file.Rout`.
- ✅ Assignment is done with **`<-`** (preferred) or **`->`**, not `=` (which is for argument matching inside function calls).
- ✅ Top-level expressions are **auto-printed**; assignments are **silent**; wrap an assignment in `(...)` to make it print.
- ✅ Comments start with **`#`**; R is **case-sensitive**.
- ✅ **Three output streams:** `stdout()` (normal), `stderr()` (warnings/errors), `stdin()` (input).
- ✅ Press **Esc** (RStudio) or **Ctrl+C** (terminal) to break out of a stuck `+` prompt or running computation.
- ✅ `library()` loads a package; `install.packages()` first downloads it from CRAN.
- ✅ `ls()` lists objects; `rm(list = ls())` clears the workspace; `save.image()` persists the session to `.RData`.
- ✅ The **`[1]`** prefix in printed output is the index of the **first element** of the displayed vector — a hallmark of R's print method.
- ✅ Use **RStudio Projects** to keep your working directory, scripts, and data files portable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Packages in R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importing Packages in R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **R programming language**, a *package* is a formally structured, standardized collection of reusable code units — comprising **R functions**, **compiled code** (C/C++/Fortran), **sample datasets**, **documentation**, and **vignettes** — distributed in a well-defined, install-ready archive format (`.tar.gz` for source / `.zip` for Windows binaries).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A *package* in R is a directory of `R` code, data, documentation, and tests, organized according to the *R Packaging System* standard, which allows modular distribution, version control, and reproducible computational workflows.

The act of **importing (loading) a package** makes its exported objects (functions, datasets, S3/S4 methods) available in the current R session's *search path* without polluting the global namespace unnecessarily.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smartphone App Model"

Think of R itself as a brand-new **smartphone**. Out of the box, it can make calls, send messages, and take photos — these are the **Base R** functions. But if you want Google Maps, WhatsApp, or Spotify, you must:

| Real-World Analogy | R Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Smartphone | R Interpreter / Base R |
| App Store (one-time install) | `install.packages()` |
| Tapping an app icon (each session) | `library()` or `require()` |
| App running in background | Loaded namespace in search path |
| Auto-update notifications | `update.packages()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** *Installing* a package is a **one-time** operation (persists on disk). *Loading* a package is a **per-session** operation (must be repeated every time R is restarted).

## 1.3 Core Terminology Highlights

- **CRAN** *(Comprehensive R Archive Network)*: The official global repository of R packages — **> 20,000** packages as of 2024.
- **Bioconductor**: A specialized repository for bioinformatics packages, primarily used in genomics and life sciences.
- **Search Path**: The ordered list of environments R consults when resolving a variable name.
- **Namespace**: The hidden environment where a package's internal objects live, preventing naming collisions.
- **Vignette**: A long-form, human-readable tutorial document bundled with many packages.
- **Tidyverse**: A popular *meta-package* that installs and loads a coherent ecosystem of data-science packages (e.g., `ggplot2`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Package Lifecycle — Operational Logic

The complete lifecycle of using any R package follows a strict three-stage pipeline:

### Stage 1 — Discovery / Resolution
R must be told *where* to find the package:
- Default: `CRAN` mirror
- Alternative: `Bioconductor`, `GitHub`, local `.zip` / `.tar.gz` file, GitLab, Bitbucket.

### Stage 2 — Installation (Persistent, One-Time)
Command: `install.packages("package_name")`

This downloads the binary (or compiles from source) and stores it permanently in the **library directory** — on Windows typically `C:\Program Files\R\R-x.y.z\library`.

### Stage 3 — Loading / Importing (Per-Session)
Commands: `library(package_name)` **or** `require(package_name)`

This attaches the package to the **search path**, making its exported objects resolvable by their bare names.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Feature | `library()` | `require()` |
|---|---|---|
| Throws **error** if package missing? | **Yes** (stops execution) | **No** (returns `FALSE` and warns) |
| Preferred in **scripts**? | **Yes** — fail-fast philosophy | No |
| Preferred in **packages / Shiny**? | No | **Yes** — graceful fallback |
| Returns a value? | Returns the package name invisibly | Returns `TRUE` or `FALSE` |
| Quotes required around name? | No (with backticks works) | No |
| Suppresses startup messages? | Use `quietly = TRUE` | Use `quietly = TRUE` |

| Installation Source | Command Pattern |
|---|---|
| CRAN | `install.packages("dplyr")` |
| Bioconductor | `BiocManager::install("DESeq2")` |
| GitHub (via devtools) | `devtools::install_github("tidyverse/ggplot2")` |
| Local file | `install.packages("path/to/file.tar.gz", repos = NULL, type = "source")` |
| Specific version | `remotes::install_version("dplyr", version = "1.0.10")` |

## 2.3 The Search Path Mechanism — *Why* This Matters

When you type `mean(x)` in R, the interpreter walks the search path **from left to right** to find the first object named `mean`. You can inspect this path at any time:

```r
search()
```

The output is a character vector — the **first** entry is always `"package:base"`, the last is `"workspace"` (the global environment). Every call to `library()` *prepends* a new entry to this list, and the order matters for **name shadowing** (a function in a later-loaded package can mask one from an earlier package).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Importing packages is foundational for:
- **Reproducible Data Science** — Locked dependency versions via `renv` or `groundhog`.
- **Bioinformatics pipelines** — `Bioconductor` packages such as `DESeq2`, `edgeR`, `limma`.
- **Machine Learning workflows** — `caret`, `mlr3`, `tidymodels`, `keras`.
- **Statistical Modeling** — `lme4`, `survival`, `MASS`.
- **Production-grade Shiny dashboards** — Modular `library()` calls in `global.R`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Practical Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Context for KTU Examiners:** Because this is a programming course (PEEBT756), the "derivations" below are *executable code traces*. Each block is followed by the *expected console output* and a *logic explanation row*, exactly as required by the KTU analytical-answer rubric.

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Canonical Import Pattern

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate the three-stage lifecycle — install, load, use — using the `dplyr` package.

### Step 1 — Installation
```r
# Run ONLY ONCE on this machine
install.packages("dplyr")
```

**Logic Explanation:** This connects to the nearest CRAN mirror, downloads the binary `.zip` (Windows) or `.tgz` (macOS/Linux), places it under the R library directory, and indexes it in the local package database. *No code from dplyr is yet in memory.*

### Step 2 — Loading
```r
library(dplyr)
```

**Console Output (truncated):**
```text
Attaching package: 'dplyr'

The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag

The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
```

**Logic Explanation:** The `Attaching package` message confirms `dplyr` has been prepended to the search path. The `masked` warnings tell us dplyr's versions of `filter()`, `lag()`, `intersect()`, etc. now *shadow* the base-R equivalents. This is intentional — dplyr uses different argument semantics.

### Step 3 — Usage Verification
```r
search()
```

**Console Output (truncated):**
```text
[1] ".GlobalEnv"        "package:dplyr"     "package:stats"    
[4] "package:graphics"  "package:utils"     "package:datasets" 
[7] "package:methods"   "Autoloads"         "package:base"
```

**Logic Explanation:** `package:dplyr` now appears *before* `package:stats`, which is why dplyr's `filter()` takes precedence. This is a **search-path proof** of the import.

## 3.2 Derivation II — `library()` vs `require()` Behavioral Comparison

**Problem Statement:** Contrast the two loading functions under a *failure scenario*.

```r
# --- Scenario A: Missing package handled by library() ---
library(nonexistent_package_xyz)
```

**Console Output:**
```text
Error in library(nonexistent_package_xyz) : 
  there is no package called 'nonexistent_package_xyz'
Execution halted
```

**Logic Explanation:** Execution **stops immediately**. Suitable for production scripts where the workflow must abort if a dependency is missing.

```r
# --- Scenario B: Missing package handled by require() ---
result <- require(nonexistent_package_xyz)
print(result)
```

**Console Output:**
```text
Loading required package: nonexistent_package_xyz
Warning message:
In library(package, lib.loc, logical.return = TRUE, quietly = FALSE) :
  there is no package called 'nonexistent_package_xyz'
[1] FALSE
```

**Logic Explanation:** Execution **continues**; `result` is now `FALSE` and can be tested with an `if` block. This is the recommended pattern inside *other packages* and *Shiny apps* where the function may be called in environments where you don't want a hard crash.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Conflict Resolution via Explicit Namespace

**Problem Statement:** Resolve a name conflict between `dplyr::filter()` and `stats::filter()`.

```r
# Force the stats version using the :: operator
x <- 1:10
result <- stats::filter(x, filter = rep(1, 3), sides = 2)
print(result)
```

**Console Output:**
```text
Time Series: 
Start = 1
End = 10
Frequency = 1
 [1] NA  2  3  4  5  6  7  8  9 NA
```

**Logic Explanation:** Using `package::function` syntax *bypasses* the search path entirely and calls the function directly from the specified package's namespace. This is **best practice** in production code because it eliminates ambiguity.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Installing from a Non-CRAN Source

**Problem Statement:** Install a package directly from GitHub.

```r
# One-time prerequisite
install.packages("devtools")

# Install a specific GitHub repo
devtools::install_github("rstudio/gt")
```

**Logic Explanation:** `install_github()` clones the Git repository, builds the package source, and installs it into the local library. The path follows the convention `username/repository`. This is the standard workflow for installing *development* or *pre-release* versions of packages that have not yet been pushed to CRAN.

## 3.5 Derivation V — Meta-Package Installation (Tidyverse)

**Problem Statement:** Install and load the entire Tidyverse ecosystem.

```r
install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)
```

**Console Output (truncated):**
```text
── Attaching packages ───────────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ ggplot2 3.4.3     ✔ purrr   1.0.2
✔ tibble  3.2.1     ✔ dplyr   1.1.3
✔ tidyr   1.3.0     ✔ stringr 1.5.0
✔ readr   2.1.4     ✔ forcats 1.0.0
── Conflicts ───────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
```

**Logic Explanation:** The `tidyverse` package is a *wrapper* that lists 8 core packages as dependencies. CRAN automatically installs all of them in one operation. Calling `library(tidyverse)` attaches all 8 simultaneously. The `Conflicts` section is a built-in diagnostic for shadowing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The R Package Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: R Session Initiated]) --> B{Is Package Already Installed?}

    B -- No --> C[install.packages from CRAN]
    B -- No --> D[BiocManager::install from Bioconductor]
    B -- No --> E[devtools::install_github from GitHub]

    C --> F[Package Stored in Local Library Directory]
    D --> F
    E --> F

    B -- Yes --> F
    F --> G[Choose Loading Function]

    G --> H[library package_name]
    G --> I[require package_name]

    H --> J{Function Exists in Package?}
    I --> J

    J -- Yes --> K[Call Function: package_name::func args]
    J -- No --> L[Check Namespace: Use :: Operator]

    K --> M([Function Executes Successfully])
    L --> M

    H --> N{Error if Missing?}
    I --> O{Warning if Missing?}

    N -- Yes --> P([Execution Halted: Hard Error])
    O -- Yes --> Q[Return FALSE: Continue Execution]

    P --> R([End])
    Q --> R
    M --> R
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Search Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[R Session Search Path - Top to Bottom Priority]
        S1[1. Global Environment]
        S2[2. package:dplyr]
        S3[3. package:ggplot2]
        S4[4. package:stats]
        S5[5. package:graphics]
        S6[6. package:utils]
        S7[7. package:datasets]
        S8[8. package:methods]
        S9[9. package:base]
    end

    USER([User Types: filter df x greater than 5]) --> RESOLVE{Variable Resolution Engine}
    RESOLVE --> S1
    S1 -- Found --> USE1([Use Local Variable])
    S1 -- Not Found --> S2
    S2 -- Found --> USE2([Use dplyr Filter])
    S2 -- Not Found --> S3
    S3 -- Not Found --> S4
    S4 -- Found --> USE4([Use stats Filter])
    S4 -- Not Found --> S5
    S5 -- Not Found --> S9
    S9 -- Found --> USE9([Use Base R Function])
    S9 -- Not Found --> ERR([Error: Object Not Found])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Installation Source Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Need to Install R Package]) --> Q1{Is it on CRAN?}

    Q1 -- Yes --> PATH1[Use install.packages]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it a Bioinformatics Tool?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> PATH2[Use BiocManager::install]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Source Code on GitHub?}

    Q3 -- Yes --> PATH3[Use devtools::install_github]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Do You Have Local .tar.gz File?}

    Q4 -- Yes --> PATH4[Use install.packages with repos = NULL]
    Q4 -- No --> ERR([Package Unavailable: Check Name and Internet])

    PATH1 --> DONE([Package Installed Successfully])
    PATH2 --> DONE
    PATH3 --> DONE
    PATH4 --> DONE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define an R package. Differentiate between `library()` and `require()` functions in R.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **R package** is a standardized collection of R functions, compiled code, sample data, and documentation bundled into a single distributable unit, conforming to the R packaging system specification (1 mark).

| Aspect | `library()` | `require()` |
|---|---|---|
| Error Behavior | Throws hard error if package not found | Gives warning and returns `FALSE` |
| Use Case | Production scripts, fail-fast | Packages, Shiny apps, conditional loading |
| Return Value | Invisible package name | `TRUE` or `FALSE` |

(2 marks for the comparison)

### Question 2
**Q: What is CRAN? List any two methods to install R packages from sources other than CRAN.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**CRAN** (Comprehensive R Archive Network) is the official global network of FTP and web servers that stores up-to-date versions of base R, contributed packages, and their documentation. It serves as the primary verified source for R package distribution (1.5 marks).

Two alternative installation methods:
1. `BiocManager::install("package_name")` — for **Bioconductor** bioinformatics packages (0.75 marks).
2. `devtools::install_github("username/repo")` — for development versions hosted on **GitHub** (0.75 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Compliance:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer *either* the full Question A *or* the full Question B.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Explain the three-stage lifecycle of importing an R package. Write R code to install and load the `ggplot2` package, and demonstrate one plotting function from it.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Installation (One-time):**
```r
install.packages("ggplot2")
```
This connects to the CRAN mirror, downloads the binary, and stores it permanently in the R library directory (typically `C:\Program Files\R\R-4.x.x\library\ggplot2` on Windows). (2 marks)

**Stage 2 — Loading (Per-session):**
```r
library(ggplot2)
```
This attaches the package to the search path. A `package:ggplot2` entry is prepended to the result of `search()`. Any conflicts (e.g., with `stats::filter`) are reported. (2 marks)

**Stage 3 — Usage Demonstration:**
```r
library(ggplot2)
data(mtcars)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Weight vs Fuel Efficiency",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon")
```
Output: A scatter plot with weight on the x-axis and MPG on the y-axis. The plot window renders 32 points corresponding to the `mtcars` dataset. (3 marks for code + correct execution explanation)

---

**`(b)` Discuss the concept of name masking in R with a suitable example. How can it be resolved using the `::` operator?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** When two or more packages on the search path export functions with the same name, the one loaded *later* (i.e., higher in the search path) takes precedence. This is called **name masking**. (2 marks)

**Example:**
```r
library(dplyr)        # loaded after stats
filter <- stats::filter  # user-defined or explicit

# Now calling filter() will use dplyr's version because
# package:dplyr is above package:stats in search()
```
The startup message after `library(dplyr)` explicitly warns:
> *"The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': filter, lag"* (2 marks)

**Resolution using `::` operator:**
The `package::function` syntax bypasses the search path entirely. Examples:
```r
stats::filter(x, filter = rep(1, 3), sides = 2)   # forces stats version
dplyr::filter(mtcars, cyl == 4)                   # forces dplyr version
```
This is the **best practice** in production R code and R packages to eliminate ambiguity and improve readability. (3 marks)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**`(a)` What are the different sources from which R packages can be installed? Write the syntax for installing a package from CRAN, Bioconductor, and GitHub.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

R packages can be installed from the following sources (1 mark each, 3 marks for listing + 4 marks for syntax):

| Source | Use Case | Syntax |
|---|---|---|
| **CRAN** | Stable, production packages | `install.packages("dplyr")` |
| **Bioconductor** | Bioinformatics, genomics | `BiocManager::install("DESeq2")` |
| **GitHub** | Development, pre-release versions | `devtools::install_github("rstudio/gt")` |
| **Local file** | Offline or custom builds | `install.packages("C:/pkg.tar.gz", repos = NULL, type = "source")` |

```r
# CRAN
install.packages("ggplot2")

# Bioconductor (requires BiocManager first)
if (!require("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install("limma")

# GitHub
install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("tidyverse/dplyr")
```

---

**`(b)` Explain the difference between installing and loading a package. Why is loading required in every R session even after successful installation? Illustrate with a suitable example.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Installation** is a *persistent*, one-time operation that writes package files to the **library directory** on disk. The package is *registered* with R's package database but is *not* loaded into memory. (2 marks)

**Loading** is a *transient*, per-session operation that *attaches* the package to the search path, making its exported objects (functions, datasets) callable by their bare names. (2 marks)

**Why per-session loading is required:**
R is designed as an **interactive, memory-efficient** statistical environment. Each new R session starts with a *clean* workspace and a *minimal* search path containing only the base packages. To avoid unnecessary memory consumption and to ensure reproducibility (the same script should produce the same result regardless of which packages happen to be installed), R does *not* auto-load installed packages. The user must explicitly declare dependencies via `library()` calls at the top of every script. (2 marks)

**Illustration:**
```r
# Session 1
install.packages("jsonlite")  # disk write, one-time
library(jsonlite)              # attaches to search path
fromJSON('{"name":"R"}')       # works: returns "R"

# Session 2 (next day, fresh R)
fromJSON('{"name":"R"}')
# Error: could not find function "fromJSON"
# Fix: re-run library(jsonlite) at the top
```
(1 mark for the example)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common 2-mark loss:** Writing only the *command* without explaining the *mechanism* (e.g., "library() loads the package" without mentioning the *search path*).
> 2. **Most common 3-mark loss in Part B:** Confusing *installation* with *loading*. Examiners explicitly look for the distinction — installation is *disk-level*, loading is *memory-level*.
> 3. **Name-masking questions:** Students often forget to mention that `::` operator **bypasses the search path entirely**. Mentioning only "use a different function" will cost a mark.
> 4. **GitHub installation:** Forgetting the prerequisite `install.packages("devtools")` step will be penalized.
> 5. **`require()` use case:** Many students wrongly recommend `require()` for scripts. The correct answer is `library()` for scripts and `require()` for packages/Shiny.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- ☐ A **package** = functions + code + data + docs, bundled into a distributable unit.
- ☐ **CRAN** is the primary verified source; **Bioconductor** for bioinformatics; **GitHub** for dev versions.
- ☐ `install.packages()` = **one-time, persistent, disk-level**.
- ☐ `library()` / `require()` = **per-session, transient, memory-level**.
- ☐ `library()` **throws an error** if package missing → use in **scripts**.
- ☐ `require()` **warns and returns FALSE** → use in **packages and Shiny apps**.
- ☐ `package::function` syntax **bypasses search path** → best practice in production code.
- ☐ `search()` returns the current package search path; library calls **prepend** entries.
- ☐ **Name masking** occurs when two packages export the same function name; later-loaded wins.
- ☐ `BiocManager::install("name")` for Bioconductor (not `install.packages`).
- ☐ `devtools::install_github("user/repo")` for GitHub.
- ☐ `install.packages("file.tar.gz", repos = NULL, type = "source")` for local files.
- ☐ Tidyverse is a **meta-package** that installs 8 core packages in one call.
- ☐ Loading must be repeated **every R session** — it is *not* persistent like installation.
- ☐ Use `conflicts()` to list all masked functions across loaded packages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Getting Data into R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Getting Data into R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT756 / Module 1)**
> *Data importation in R* refers to the systematic process of ingesting, parsing, and binding external data sources—spanning flat files, delimited text, binary spreadsheets, relational databases, web services (REST/JSON), and Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)—into R's in-memory workspace as R objects (vectors, data frames, tibbles, or lists) suitable for statistical computation, transformation, and visualization.

In the R ecosystem, "getting data in" is governed by a family of functions bundled in the base R distribution (e.g., `read.csv`, `read.table`, `scan`) and extended by **Tidyverse** packages such as **`readr`** (delimited files), **`readxl`** (Excel), **`haven`** (SPSS/SAS/Stata), **`jsonlite`** (JSON), **`httr2`** (HTTP/URLs), and **`DBI`** (databases).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy**
> Think of R as a **modern kitchen**, and your data as **raw ingredients** stored in a distant warehouse (your hard drive, the internet, or a database). *Getting data into R* is the act of:
> 1. **Locating the warehouse** (specifying the file path or URL).
> 2. **Choosing the right vehicle** (a `.csv` reader, an Excel reader, an API caller).
> 3. **Unpacking and inspecting the goods** (loading into a `data.frame` and running `str()`, `head()`, `summary()`).
> 
> Just as you would not use a spoon to carry a sack of rice, you would not use `read.csv()` to load a 2 GB JSON stream—you would use specialized, memory-efficient tools like `data.table::fread()` or `jsonlite::stream_in()`.

## 1.3 Core Data Sources — A KTU High-Yield Matrix

| Source Type | Common Extensions | Primary R Function / Package |
|-------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Built-in** | — | `data()` |
| **CSV / Delimited** | `.csv`, `.tsv`, `.txt` | `read.csv()`, `readr::read_csv()` |
| **Excel** | `.xls`, `.xlsx` | `readxl::read_excel()` |
| **R Native** | `.RData`, `.rda`, `.RDS` | `load()`, `readRDS()` |
| **SPSS / SAS / Stata** | `.sav`, `.sas7bdat`, `.dta` | `haven::read_sav()`, `haven::read_dta()` |
| **JSON / Web APIs** | `.json` | `jsonlite::fromJSON()`, `httr2::request()` |
| **Databases (SQL)** | — | `DBI` + `odbc` / `RSQLite` |
| **Web Scraping** | `.html` | `rvest::read_html()` |
| **Clipboard / Keyboard** | — | `read.table("clipboard")`, `scan()` |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data-Import Pipeline (Operational Steps)

Every successful data import in R follows a deterministic, five-stage pipeline:

1. **Path Resolution** — Identify the *absolute* or *relative* path to the data source. R uses forward slashes (`/`) or double backslashes (`\\`) on Windows.
2. **Function Selection** — Match the file's format with the correct reader function (delimiter, encoding, sheet name).
3. **Argument Configuration** — Tune `header`, `sep`, `dec`, `na.strings`, `colClasses`, `nrows`, `skip`, etc.
4. **Binding to Symbol** — Assign the returned object to a variable in the global environment.
5. **Validation** — Inspect structure via `str()`, dimensions via `dim()`, and integrity via `head()` / `tail()`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Board questions frequently test *which argument fixes which issue* (e.g., `na.strings = c("", "NA", "?")` for missing-value tokens, `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` to suppress auto-conversion of character vectors to factors).

## 2.2 Detailed Function-by-Function Analysis

### 2.2.1 Built-in Datasets
R ships with curated datasets (e.g., `mtcars`, `iris`, `airquality`).

```r
data(mtcars)        # Loads the mtcars data frame
data(iris)
?mtcars             # Documentation
```

### 2.2.2 CSV / Delimited Files (Base R)

```r
# Comma-separated values
df <- read.csv("data/sales_2024.csv", 
               header = TRUE, 
               sep = ",", 
               dec = ".",
               na.strings = c("NA", "", "?"),
               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Tab-separated values
df2 <- read.delim("data/measurements.tsv")

# General-purpose
df3 <- read.table("data/mixed.txt", header = TRUE, sep = "\t")
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`stringsAsFactors = FALSE`** is a KTU-favorite exam point. In R versions `< 4.0.0`, characters defaulted to factors; post 4.0.0 the default is `FALSE`, but explicit declaration is best practice.

### 2.2.3 Tidyverse `readr` Package (Modern, Fast)

```r
install.packages("readr")
library(readr)

df <- read_csv("data/sales_2024.csv", 
               col_types = cols(price = col_double(),
                                date  = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d")),
               na = c("", "NA", "NULL", "?"))

# Tab-separated
df_tsv <- read_tsv("data/measurements.tsv")

# Whitespace-separated
df_ws  <- read_table("data/space_delim.txt")
```

> **Key Advantage:** `readr` returns a **`tibble`** (modern data frame with better printing behavior) and reports parsing problems via `problems(df)`.

### 2.2.4 Excel Files (`readxl` / `openxlsx`)

```r
install.packages("readxl")
library(readxl)

# Read first sheet by default
df_xl <- read_excel("data/finance_2024.xlsx", sheet = 1)

# Read a specific sheet by name
df_q3 <- read_excel("data/finance_2024.xlsx", 
                    sheet = "Q3_Report",
                    range = "B2:G50",
                    na = "N/A")
```

> [!WARNING]
> `readxl` supports both `.xls` (legacy) and `.xlsx` (modern). It is **read-only**. For writing back, use `openxlsx` or `writexl`.

### 2.2.5 R Native Binary Formats

```r
# Save (binary, multiple objects)
save(df, model, file = "workspace.RData")

# Load
load("workspace.RData")

# Single object (RDS)
saveRDS(df, "data.rds")
restored_df <- readRDS("data.rds")
```

**`RDS` vs `RData`:** RDS stores *one* object and the loaded variable name is independent; `RData` stores *multiple* objects with their original names.

### 2.2.6 Importing from SPSS / SAS / Stata (`haven`)

```r
library(haven)
spss_df <- read_sav("survey.sav")
sas_df  <- read_sas("clinical.sas7bdat")
stata_df <- read_dta("panel.dta")
```

These functions preserve **labelled** variables (value labels common in social science data).

### 2.2.7 JSON & Web APIs

```r
library(jsonlite)

# From a local JSON file
data <- fromJSON("data/weather.json", flatten = TRUE)

# From a REST API
url  <- "https://api.github.com/users/hadley/repos"
repos <- fromJSON(url)
```

For authenticated APIs use **`httr2`**:

```r
library(httr2)
req <- request("https://api.example.com/data") |>
       req_auth_bearer_token("YOUR_TOKEN") |>
       req_user_agent("my-r-app/1.0")
resp <- req |> req_perform()
df   <- resp |> resp_body_json() |> as.data.frame()
```

### 2.2.8 Databases via `DBI`

```r
library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "my_database.sqlite")
df  <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM customers WHERE region = 'Kerala'")
dbDisconnect(con)
```

### 2.2.9 Web Scraping (`rvest`)

```r
library(rvest)
page <- read_html("https://example.com/table")
tables <- html_table(page)        # Returns a list of all tables
df    <- tables[[1]]
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Argument | Purpose | Typical Value(s) |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| `file` | Path / URL / connection | `"data/file.csv"`, `"clipboard"`, `url()` |
| `header` | First row is column names? | `TRUE` / `FALSE` |
| `sep` | Field delimiter | `","`, `"\t"`, `";"` |
| `dec` | Decimal separator | `"."` (default) or `","` |
| `quote` | Quoting character | `"\""` (default) |
| `na.strings` | Tokens to interpret as `NA` | `c("NA", "", "?")` |
| `stringsAsFactors` | Convert char → factor? | `FALSE` (KTU best practice) |
| `colClasses` | Force column types | `c("integer","character","numeric")` |
| `nrows` | Max rows to read | `-1` (all) or `1000` |
| `skip` | Lines to skip at top | `0` |
| `encoding` | File character encoding | `"UTF-8"`, `"latin1"` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In production data-engineering pipelines (ETL), R is used as the *transformation* tier—data is extracted from SQL/JSON via the above methods, transformed with `dplyr`, and loaded into analytical warehouses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CSV Import with Full Validation (K-Style)

**Problem:** A `sales.csv` file contains 10,000 rows of sales transactions. The file uses `;` as a separator, `,` as the decimal mark, and encodes missing data as both empty strings and the literal token `"N/A"`. The `order_date` column should be parsed as a `Date`. The `region` column must remain a character, not a factor.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

```r
# Step 1 — Set working directory
setwd("C:/KTU/data")           # Windows path
# Or use here::here() for project-relative paths
# Or use the absolute path: "C:/KTU/data/sales.csv"

# Step 2 — Read the CSV with explicit arguments
sales <- read.csv("sales.csv",
                  header     = TRUE,
                  sep        = ";",
                  dec        = ",",
                  na.strings = c("N/A", ""),
                  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Step 3 — Convert the date column
sales$order_date <- as.Date(sales$order_date, format = "%d/%m/%Y")

# Step 4 — Verify
str(sales)
# 'data.frame':  10000 obs. of  6 variables:
#  $ order_id   : int  1001 1002 1003 1004 1005 ...
#  $ order_date : Date, format: "2024-01-15" "2024-01-15" ...
#  $ region     : chr  "Kerala" "Tamil Nadu" "Kerala" "Karnataka" ...
#  $ product    : chr  "WidgetA" "WidgetB" "WidgetA" "WidgetC" ...
#  $ quantity   : int  5 3 8 2 12 ...
#  $ unit_price : num  49.9 79.5 49.9 129 49.9 ...

# Step 5 — Confirm NA handling
sum(is.na(sales$unit_price))    # Returns count of NAs from "N/A" and ""
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice the *cumulative effect* of arguments. `sep=";"` and `dec=","` are mutually dependent—European-style CSVs typically use one only if they use the other.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Excel Multi-Sheet Import with Selection

**Problem:** An `annual_report.xlsx` file has three sheets: `Summary`, `Q1_Data`, `Q2_Data`. The user must import only the `Q1_Data` sheet, skip the first 2 header rows, and treat the cell value `"–"` (en-dash) as `NA`.

```r
# Step 1 — Load library
library(readxl)

# Step 2 — List sheet names first
excel_sheets("annual_report.xlsx")
# [1] "Summary"   "Q1_Data"   "Q2_Data"

# Step 3 — Targeted import
q1 <- read_excel("annual_report.xlsx",
                 sheet   = "Q1_Data",
                 skip    = 2,
                 na      = "–",
                 col_types = c("text", "numeric", "numeric", "date"))

# Step 4 — Dimensions check
dim(q1)        # e.g., 90 4   (90 days × 4 columns)
```

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — REST API Data Ingestion

**Problem:** Fetch live COVID-19 statistics for India from a public API and load into a tibble.

```r
library(httr2)
library(jsonlite)

# Step 1 — Construct request
req <- request("https://disease.sh/v3/covid-19/countries/India") |>
       req_timeout(10) |>
       req_user_agent("ktu-r-app/1.0")

# Step 2 — Perform
resp <- req |> req_perform()

# Step 3 — Parse JSON to data frame
india_data <- resp |> resp_body_json() |> as.data.frame()

# Step 4 — Inspect
india_data[, c("country", "cases", "deaths", "recovered", "active")]
#      country   cases  deaths recovered  active
# 1     India 45000000 533000 44000000  467000
```

> [!WARNING]
> Always check the response status with `resp_status(resp)` before parsing—`200` means OK, anything in the `4xx`/`5xx` range indicates an error.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Reader

| Scenario | Recommended Function | Justification |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| 10 MB CSV, simple structure | `read.csv()` | Base R, no dependencies |
| 2 GB CSV, need speed | `data.table::fread()` | C-level parser, ~20× faster |
| Excel with formulas/macros | `readxl::read_excel()` | Pure R, no Java dependency |
| SPSS labelled data | `haven::read_sav()` | Preserves `haven_labelled` class |
| Nested JSON from API | `jsonlite::fromJSON()` | Auto-flattens to data frame |
| Paginated REST API | `httr2` + `purrr::map_dfr()` | Iterative request handling |
| SQL query result | `DBI::dbGetQuery()` | Streams from server |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 R Data-Import Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Data Source] --> B{Source Type?}

    B -->|Flat File| C[read.csv / readr::read_csv]
    B -->|Excel| D[readxl::read_excel]
    B -->|R Binary| E[load / readRDS]
    B -->|SPSS/SAS/Stata| F[haven::read_sav etc]
    B -->|JSON File| G[jsonlite::fromJSON]
    B -->|REST API| H[httr2::request]
    B -->|Database| I[DBI::dbGetQuery]
    B -->|Web Page| J[rvest::read_html]
    B -->|Built-in| K[data function]

    C --> L[R Workspace]
    D --> L
    E --> L
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Validation Layer]
    M --> N[str function]
    M --> O[head function]
    M --> P[summary function]

    N --> Q[Ready for Analysis]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Parsing-Pipeline Detail (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw File on Disk or URL] --> B[File Connection]
    B --> C[Read Line by Line]
    C --> D{Delimiter Detected?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Tokenize Fields]
    D -->|No| F[Use Whitespace Logic]
    E --> G[Type Coercion]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply na.strings Mapping]
    H --> I[Construct data.frame / tibble]
    I --> J[Bind to Symbol in Global Environment]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which `readr` Function?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Have a delimited file] --> Q1{What delimiter?}
    Q1 -->|Comma| A1[read_csv]
    Q1 -->|Tab| A2[read_tsv]
    Q1 -->|Semicolon| A3[read_csv2]
    Q1 -->|Whitespace| A4[read_table]
    Q1 -->|Any fixed-width| A5[read_fwf]
    Q1 -->|Log-style rows| A6[read_log]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the difference between `read.csv()` and `readr::read_csv()` in R. Mention any two key advantages of the latter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. `read.csv()` is a base R function that returns a base `data.frame`; `read_csv()` (from the `readr` package) returns a **`tibble`** (modern data frame) with better printing and subsetting behavior.
2. `read_csv()` is **faster** (C++-backed via `Rcpp`), provides more informative progress bars, and uses **consistent type guessing** (e.g., empty strings are not silently converted to `NA` unless specified).
3. `read_csv()` reports parsing problems via the `problems()` function, making debugging easier.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the purpose of the `na.strings` argument in `read.csv()`? Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The `na.strings` argument tells R which character tokens in the input file should be interpreted as missing values (`NA`).
- **Example:** In a survey dataset, missing responses may appear as `"NA"`, an empty string `""`, or `"?"`. We handle this with:
  ```r
  df <- read.csv("survey.csv", na.strings = c("NA", "", "?"))
  ```
- This ensures all three forms are uniformly converted to `NA`, simplifying subsequent analysis with `is.na()`, `na.omit()`, and `tidyr::drop_na()`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the various methods available in R to import data from (i) CSV files, (ii) Excel files, and (iii) relational databases. For each, provide a sample code snippet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CSV file `employee_data.csv` uses `|` as a delimiter, contains European-style decimal commas (e.g., `49,90`), and encodes missing salaries as `"--"`. The `joining_date` is in `dd-mm-yyyy` format. Write the complete R code to import this file, properly handle the missing values, parse the date column, and validate the resulting data structure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Method 1 — CSV (Base R and Tidyverse):**

```r
# Base R
df1 <- read.csv("employee_data.csv", sep = ",", header = TRUE)

# Tidyverse (preferred for large files)
library(readr)
df2 <- read_csv("employee_data.csv", 
                col_types = cols(joining_date = col_date("%Y-%m-%d")))
```

**Method 2 — Excel:**

```r
library(readxl)
df3 <- read_excel("employee_data.xlsx", 
                  sheet = "Sheet1",
                  range = "A1:D100",
                  na = "")
```

**Method 3 — Relational Databases:**

```r
library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "company.db")
df4 <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept = 'R&D'")
dbDisconnect(con)
```

For production databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server) replace `SQLite()` with `odbc::odbc()` and provide a DSN.

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

```r
# Step 1 — Read the CSV with custom delimiter and decimal mark
emp <- read.csv("employee_data.csv",
                header        = TRUE,
                sep           = "|",
                dec           = ",",
                na.strings    = c("--", "", "NA"),
                stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Step 2 — Parse the date column (format: dd-mm-yyyy)
emp$joining_date <- as.Date(emp$joining_date, format = "%d-%m-%Y")

# Step 3 — Validate structure
str(emp)
# 'data.frame':  500 obs. of  5 variables:
#  $ emp_id      : int  1 2 3 4 5 ...
#  $ name        : chr  "Anil" "Beena" "Chitra" "Deepak" ...
#  $ department  : chr  "R&D" "Finance" "R&D" "HR" ...
#  $ salary      : num  49900 72500 55300 NA 81200 ...
#  $ joining_date: Date, format: "2020-03-15" "2019-07-22" ...

# Step 4 — Count missing salaries (originally "--")
sum(is.na(emp$salary))
# e.g., 12

# Step 5 — Quick numerical summary
summary(emp$salary)
#   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's
#  30000   45000   60000   62000   75000  120000      12
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that when `dec = ","`, the `sep` *cannot also be* `","` (they will collide). Common errors:
> 1. Using `dec = ","` with `sep = ","` → produces a single-column mess.
> 2. Forgetting `stringsAsFactors = FALSE` → text columns become factors, breaking `as.Date()`.
> 3. Using `format = "%d-%m-%Y"` when the file actually uses `/` → all dates become `NA`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With suitable code examples, explain how R can import data from (i) JSON files and (ii) REST APIs. Discuss any two challenges you might face during API-based data ingestion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the complete process of: (i) saving a data frame to an RDS file, (ii) reading it back into a different variable name, and (iii) comparing it to the original to confirm integrity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**JSON File Import:**

```r
library(jsonlite)
weather <- fromJSON("data/weather.json", flatten = TRUE)
# 'flatten = TRUE' unpacks nested arrays into separate columns
class(weather)   # "data.frame"
```

**REST API Import:**

```r
library(httr2)
url <- "https://api.coindesk.com/v1/bpi/currentprice.json"
req <- request(url) |> req_user_agent("ktu-r-app")
resp <- req |> req_perform()
btc <- resp |> resp_body_json() |> as.data.frame()
```

**Two Challenges:**

1. **Rate Limiting & Authentication:** Many APIs enforce request quotas (e.g., GitHub: 60/hr unauthenticated) and require API keys or OAuth2 tokens. Failing to handle these returns `403`/`429` errors. Mitigation: cache responses and use `httr2::req_retry()` with exponential backoff.
2. **Nested JSON Structures:** APIs often return deeply nested arrays requiring recursive flattening via `jsonlite::flatten()` or `tidyr::unnest()`. Mishandling leads to list-columns that break downstream `dplyr` operations.

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

```r
# Step 1 — Create a data frame
original <- data.frame(
  id    = 1:5,
  name  = c("Anu", "Binu", "Cinu", "Dinu", "Einu"),
  score = c(85, 92, 78, 88, 95)
)

# Step 2 — Save as RDS (single object, binary)
saveRDS(original, "scores.rds")

# Step 3 — Read back with a *different* variable name
restored <- readRDS("scores.rds")

# Step 4 — Integrity check
identical(original, restored)        # Returns TRUE
all.equal(original, restored)        # Returns TRUE
dim(original); dim(restored)         # 5 3  for both
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike `load()` (which restores objects with their *original* names), `readRDS()` lets you assign to any variable—making it the preferred method for **reproducible analytical pipelines** and package-building workflows.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Getting Data into R"**

- ✅ **Five-stage import pipeline:** Path → Function → Arguments → Bind → Validate.
- ✅ **Base R readers:** `read.csv()`, `read.table()`, `read.delim()` — return `data.frame`.
- ✅ **Tidyverse readers (`readr`):** `read_csv()`, `read_tsv()`, `read_csv2()` — return `tibble`.
- ✅ **Excel import:** `readxl::read_excel()` is read-only; `na`, `sheet`, `range`, `col_types` are key arguments.
- ✅ **R native formats:** `save()/load()` for `.RData`; `saveRDS()/readRDS()` for `.rds` (single object, free naming).
- ✅ **Statistical software:** `haven` package supports SPSS (`.sav`), SAS (`.sas7bdat`), Stata (`.dta`) with labelled-class preservation.
- ✅ **JSON & APIs:** `jsonlite::fromJSON()` for local files; `httr2::request() |> req_perform() |> resp_body_json()` for live APIs.
- ✅ **Databases:** `DBI` + driver (`RSQLite`, `odbc`) → `dbConnect()` → `dbGetQuery()` → `dbDisconnect()`.
- ✅ **Web scraping:** `rvest::read_html()` → `html_table()` for tabular HTML.
- ✅ **Critical arguments to memorize:** `header`, `sep`, `dec`, `na.strings`, `stringsAsFactors`, `colClasses`, `encoding`, `nrows`, `skip`.
- ✅ **Validation triad:** Always run `str()`, `head()`, and `summary()` after import.
- ✅ **Memory tip:** For files > 1 GB, prefer `data.table::fread()` (C-speed) over base R.
- ✅ **Encoding pitfall:** If non-English characters render as `â€™` etc., add `encoding = "UTF-8"` or `"latin1"`.
- ✅ **Reproducibility tip:** Use **`here::here()`** for project-relative paths instead of `setwd()`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Warnings:**
> 1. Never name your loaded data `data` — it shadows the `data()` function.
> 2. Always close database connections with `dbDisconnect(con)` and file connections with `close(con)`.
> 3. In exam code, **always include `stringsAsFactors = FALSE`** even though R 4.x defaults are safer—examiners reward explicit good practice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saving Output in R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Saving Output in R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

In the R programming environment, **saving output** refers to the systematic redirection, capture, and persistent storage of data, textual results, tabular structures, and graphical visualizations generated during an R session into external files, secondary memory devices, or reproducible formats. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEBT756 - Advanced Computer Programming Techniques)**, saving output is treated as a critical I/O (Input/Output) operation that bridges ephemeral console activity with long-term data archival.

The R ecosystem provides multiple inbuilt mechanisms — categorized as **text-based sinks**, **binary serializers**, **tabular writers**, and **graphical device drivers** — each engineered for a specific output modality (console text capture, serialized R objects, CSV/TSV files, and plots/images respectively).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The 2024 scheme explicitly identifies output redirection (`sink()`, `writeLines()`), file connections (`file()`, `writeLines()`), tabular export (`write.csv()`, `write.table()`), binary persistence (`save()`, `saveRDS()`), and graphical export (`pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`) as core learning outcomes mapped to **CO1: Apply advanced programming constructs for data handling in R.**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Notebook" Metaphor

Imagine you are solving math problems on a **chalkboard**:

1. The **chalkboard** is your R console — every calculation is visible, but vanishes the moment the board is wiped.
2. **Saving output** is like asking a smart assistant to **simultaneously transcribe** everything you write into a **permanent notebook** (a text file), **photograph** your diagrams (a PNG plot), and **archive** your entire working desk (an `.RData` file).
3. Some assistants photograph the chalkboard (`pdf()`), some copy only the words (`sink()`), while others make a full photocopy of the desk including erasers and pens (`save.image()`).

The choice of "assistant" depends on **what** you want preserved and **how** you intend to reuse it later.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** R never saves output automatically. The programmer must **explicitly invoke** an output function before computation, and **explicitly close** the output stream after computation — failure to close is one of the top sources of file corruption in R scripts.

---

### Classification of Output Types in R

The output ecosystem in R can be classified into four major families:

| # | Output Family | Primary Use-Case | Representative Function(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Text Stream Sink** | Capture console text output | `sink()`, `writeLines()`, `cat()` |
| 2 | **Tabular / Delimited Export** | Export data frames to CSV/TSV | `write.csv()`, `write.table()` |
| 3 | **Binary Serialization** | Persist R objects for later reload | `save()`, `saveRDS()`, `save.image()` |
| 4 | **Graphical Device Drivers** | Save plots to image/PDF files | `pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`, `tiff()`, `bmp()` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Text Stream Sinks — `sink()`

The `sink()` function diverts all textual output that would normally appear on the R console into an external file. It is conceptually equivalent to Unix's `>` redirection operator.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. `sink(file = "output.txt", append = FALSE, split = FALSE)` opens a connection to the file.
2. If `append = FALSE`, the file is **overwritten**; if `TRUE`, output is **appended** to existing content.
3. If `split = TRUE`, output is **simultaneously** mirrored to the console *and* the file (useful for debugging).
4. All subsequent `print()`, `cat()`, and implicit display calls are routed to the file.
5. `sink()` (with no arguments) **closes** the active sink and restores console output.
6. Always invoke `sink()` twice — once to open, once to close. A common idiom is:

```r
sink("log.txt"); ... computations ...; sink()
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** `sink()` does **not** capture **error messages** by default. To capture errors, use `sink("log.txt", type = "message")`.

### 2.2 Tabular Export — `write.table()` Family

The `write.table()` family exports rectangular data (typically a `data.frame` or `matrix`) into delimited text files.

**Parameters of High Importance:**

- `file` — destination path or connection.
- `sep` — field separator (default is a single space `" "`).
- `dec` — decimal separator (default `"."`).
- `row.names` — boolean; whether to write row labels.
- `col.names` — boolean; whether to write column headers.
- `quote` — whether to wrap character fields in quotes.
- `append` — append vs overwrite.

**Specialized Wrappers:**

- `write.csv(x, file)` — wrapper for `write.table()` with `sep = ","` and `dec = "."`.
- `write.csv2(x, file)` — wrapper using `sep = ";"` and `dec = ","` (European convention).
- `writeLines(text, con)` — writes a character vector line-by-line.

### 2.3 Binary Serialization — `save()`, `saveRDS()`, `save.image()`

Binary serialization converts R objects into a compact, platform-independent binary representation.

| Function | Saves | File Format | Reload Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| `save(...)` | One or more named objects | `.RData` / `.rda` | `load("file.rda")` |
| `saveRDS(object, file)` | A single object | `.rds` | `x <- readRDS("file.rds")` |
| `save.image()` | **All** objects in workspace | `.RData` | `load(".RData")` (auto on startup) |

**Why `saveRDS()` is preferred for single objects:**

1. The restored object can be assigned to **any variable name** by the user.
2. It supports **compression** via the `compress` argument (`gzip`, `bzip2`, `xz`).
3. It is **version-stable** — works across R upgrades when combined with compression.

### 2.4 Graphical Devices — `pdf()`, `png()`, `jpeg()`, `tiff()`, `bmp()`

Before a plotting function (`plot()`, `hist()`, etc.) is called, R must be told **where** to draw. By default, it draws on a screen device. To save a plot:

1. Open a graphical device with a target file path.
2. Call the plotting function(s).
3. Close the device with `dev.off()`.

Failure to call `dev.off()` results in a **corrupted or empty file**.

### KTU Reference Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Function Signature | Output File Type | Default Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capture console text | `sink(file, append, split)` | `.txt` | Overwrites; ignores errors |
| Append text lines | `writeLines(text, con)` | `.txt` | One element per line |
| Write tabular CSV | `write.csv(x, file)` | `.csv` | Comma-separated, with quotes |
| Write general table | `write.table(x, file)` | `.txt` / `.dat` | Space-separated |
| Save single object | `saveRDS(object, file)` | `.rds` | Binary, compressed |
| Save multiple objects | `save(..., file)` | `.rda` | Binary, uncompressed |
| Save entire workspace | `save.image(file)` | `.RData` | All current objects |
| Open PDF device | `pdf(file, width, height)` | `.pdf` | Width 7, Height 7 inches |
| Open PNG device | `png(file, width, height, res)` | `.png` | Pixels, 72 ppi |
| Open JPEG device | `jpeg(file, quality)` | `.jpg` | Quality = 0.75 |
| Close current device | `dev.off()` | — | Returns next device number |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All file paths in R follow **POSIX-style forward slashes** (`/`) on Unix/Mac, or **double backslashes** (`\\`) on Windows. Using a single backslash in a Windows path string causes a syntax error because `\` is the R escape character.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Science Pipelines:** `write.csv()` is used to publish cleaned datasets to a shared repository.
- **Reproducible Research:** `saveRDS()` archives trained machine-learning models for deployment.
- **Automated Reporting:** `sink()` captures log output from overnight R batch jobs running on servers.
- **Publication Figures:** `pdf()` and `png()` produce camera-ready plots for journal/conference papers.
- **Embedded Systems & IoT:** `writeLines()` is used to generate configuration files consumed by microcontroller firmware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations

### Worked Example 1 — Capturing Console Output with `sink()`

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate the redirection of console output to a text file, including append mode and simultaneous console echo.

**Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

```r
# Step 1: Define a numeric computation
x <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
mean_x <- mean(x)
sd_x   <- sd(x)
```

```r
# Step 2: Open a sink WITHOUT split (console is silenced)
sink("report.txt", append = FALSE)
cat("Mean:", mean_x, "\n")
cat("Standard Deviation:", sd_x, "\n")
sink()    # Closes the sink — console output is restored
```

```r
# Step 3: Append additional results to the SAME file
sink("report.txt", append = TRUE)
cat("Variance:", var(x), "\n")
sink()
```

```r
# Step 4: Echo-mode sink — output appears on BOTH console and file
sink("debug.txt", split = TRUE)
print(summary(x))
sink()
```

**Verification (file contents of `report.txt`):**

```
Mean: 30 
Standard Deviation: 15.81139 
Variance: 250 
```

### Worked Example 2 — Exporting a Data Frame to CSV

**Problem Statement:** Export the built-in `mtcars` dataset (after filtering) to a CSV file with custom separators.

**Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

```r
# Step 1: Prepare the data
filtered <- mtcars[mtcars$mpg > 20, c("mpg", "cyl", "hp")]
```

```r
# Step 2: Default CSV export (comma-separated, row.names = TRUE)
write.csv(filtered, file = "filtered_mtcars.csv")
```

```r
# Step 3: European-style CSV (semicolon-separated, comma decimals)
write.csv2(filtered, file = "filtered_mtcars_eu.csv")
```

```r
# Step 4: Fully customized write.table call
write.table(filtered,
            file        = "filtered_mtcars.tsv",
            sep         = "\t",
            row.names   = FALSE,
            col.names   = TRUE,
            quote       = FALSE,
            dec         = ".")
```

**Result:** Three files are generated — `filtered_mtcars.csv`, `filtered_mtcars_eu.csv`, and `filtered_mtcars.tsv` — each adhering to a distinct delimiter convention.

### Worked Example 3 — Binary Serialization of an R Object

**Problem Statement:** Save a trained linear regression model and reload it for prediction.

**Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

```r
# Step 1: Build and "train" a model
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl, data = mtcars)
```

```r
# Step 2: Persist the model using saveRDS (preferred single-object method)
saveRDS(model, file = "mpg_model.rds", compress = "xz")
```

```r
# Step 3: At a later session, restore the model under a different name
restored_model <- readRDS("mpg_model.rds")
```

```r
# Step 4: Verify the restored object is functionally identical
summary(restored_model)$r.squared
```

The resulting `mpg_model.rds` is a **single, compressed, binary file** containing the complete model object (coefficients, residuals, formula, call) — typically a few kilobytes regardless of dataset size because only the *model summary* is serialized.

### Worked Example 4 — Saving a Plot to PDF and PNG

**Problem Statement:** Export a histogram to both PDF (vector) and PNG (raster) formats.

**Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

```r
# Step 1: Open a PDF device (vector graphics — infinitely scalable)
pdf("histogram.pdf", width = 8, height = 6)
hist(mtcars$mpg,
     main = "Distribution of MPG",
     xlab = "Miles per Gallon",
     col  = "steelblue",
     border = "white")
dev.off()    # CRITICAL — closes the device and flushes to disk
```

```r
# Step 2: Open a PNG device (raster graphics — pixel-based)
png("histogram.png", width = 1600, height = 1200, res = 200)
hist(mtcars$mpg,
     main = "Distribution of MPG",
     xlab = "Miles per Gallon",
     col  = "steelblue",
     border = "white")
dev.off()
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `res = 200` argument sets **200 pixels per inch** — a publication-quality resolution. Without it, the default is 72 ppi, which is screen-grade.

### Worked Example 5 — Capturing Expression Results with `capture.output()`

**Problem Statement:** Capture the printed representation of a complex object (e.g., a regression summary) as a character vector, then save it to disk.

**Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

```r
# Step 1: Generate an object with multi-line printed output
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
summary_text <- capture.output(print(summary(fit)))
```

```r
# Step 2: Inspect the captured character vector
length(summary_text)
class(summary_text)
```

```r
# Step 3: Persist to disk
writeLines(summary_text, con = "model_summary.txt")
```

The `capture.output()` function is **non-destructive** — it does not silence the console; it merely **clones** the text into a character vector for further programmatic manipulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Output Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: What do you want to save?]:::startNode
    A --> B{Output Type?}
    B --> C[Console Text]:::branchText
    B --> D[Tabular Data]:::branchTab
    B --> E[R Objects]:::branchBin
    B --> F[Plot or Chart]:::branchPlot

    C --> C1{Display on console too?}
    C1 -->|No| C2[sink file append FALSE]
    C1 -->|Yes| C3[sink file split TRUE]
    C2 --> C4[Call sink to close]
    C3 --> C4

    D --> D1{Format required?}
    D1 -->|CSV| D2[write.csv]
    D1 -->|TSV| D3[write.table sep t]
    D1 -->|European| D4[write.csv2]
    D2 --> D5[Done]
    D3 --> D5
    D4 --> D5

    E --> E1{Number of objects?}
    E1 -->|Single| E2[saveRDS compress xz]
    E1 -->|Multiple| E3[save file.rda]
    E1 -->|Entire workspace| E4[save.image RData]
    E2 --> E5[Done]
    E3 --> E5
    E4 --> E5

    F --> F1{Vector or raster?}
    F1 -->|Vector PDF| F2[pdf width height]
    F1 -->|Raster image| F3{png jpeg tiff bmp}
    F2 --> F4[Plot then dev off]
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> F5[Done]

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branchText fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef branchTab fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef branchBin fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef branchPlot fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Lifecycle Block Diagram of a File Connection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseA[OPEN PHASE]
        A1[User invokes output function] --> A2[File handle created]
        A2 --> A3[Connection registered with R]
    end

    subgraph PhaseB[WRITE PHASE]
        B1[Computation runs] --> B2[Output buffered in memory]
        B2 --> B3[Buffer flushed to file]
    end

    subgraph PhaseC[CLOSE PHASE]
        C1[User invokes sink or dev off] --> C2[File handle released]
        C2 --> C3[Connection deregistered]
    end

    PhaseA --> PhaseB --> PhaseC
    C3 --> D[FILE PERSISTED ON DISK]
```

### 4.3 Memory-to-Disk Architecture Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM[RAM SESSION]
        M1[Variables]
        M2[Functions]
        M3[Plots on screen device]
        M4[Console text buffer]
    end

    subgraph DISK[PERSISTENT STORAGE]
        D1[CSV or TSV files]
        D2[RDS or RDA binary files]
        D3[PNG or PDF graphics]
        D4[TXT log files]
    end

    M1 -->|write.csv or writeRDS| D1
    M1 -->|saveRDS or save| D2
    M3 -->|png or pdf and dev off| D3
    M4 -->|sink or writeLines| D4

    MEM -.ephemeral.-> X[LOST ON SESSION EXIT]
    DISK -.persistent.-> Y[AVAILABLE AFTER RESTART]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`**
*Explain the purpose of the `sink()` function in R. How do you open and close a sink connection? What is the role of the `append` argument?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `sink()` function in R is used to **divert textual console output** from the screen to an external file. It is commonly employed for logging computational results from long-running scripts.

**Opening a sink:**

```r
sink("output.txt", append = FALSE)
```

**Closing a sink:**

```r
sink()
```

The `append` argument controls whether new output **overwrites** the file (`append = FALSE`, the default) or is **appended** to existing content (`append = TRUE`). After opening, all subsequent `print()` and `cat()` calls are written to the file instead of the console, until `sink()` is invoked without arguments to restore normal console behavior.

*(Examiner's Note: Award full marks only if both opening and closing syntaxes are explicitly shown.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`**
*Differentiate between `save()` and `saveRDS()` in R. State one advantage of `saveRDS()` over `save()`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `save()` | `saveRDS()` |
|---|---|---|
| Number of objects | Multiple named objects | Exactly **one** object |
| File extension | `.rda` or `.RData` | `.rds` |
| Reload syntax | `load("file.rda")` restores **original names** | `x <- readRDS("file.rds")` assigns to a **new name** |
| Compression | Not by default | Supports `compress = "gzip"`, `"bzip2"`, `"xz"` |

**Advantage of `saveRDS()`:** The restored object can be assigned to any variable name of the user's choosing, providing flexibility in code refactoring. Additionally, compression reduces file size significantly.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how `sink()` can be used to capture and save console output in R. Discuss the significance of the `split` parameter. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the `write.table()` function in R. List any **four** important arguments and explain their use with a working example that exports a data frame to a tab-separated file. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Capturing Console Output with `sink()` (7 marks):**

The `sink()` function redirects R's textual output stream from the console to an external file.

**Example Program:**

```r
# Define a vector and compute statistics
data <- c(12, 15, 18, 22, 30, 35)
cat("Vector:", data, "\n")
cat("Sum:", sum(data), "\n")
cat("Mean:", mean(data), "\n")

# Open sink WITHOUT split — console is silenced
sink("analysis_log.txt")
cat("Sum:", sum(data), "\n")
cat("Mean:", mean(data), "\n")
cat("Median:", median(data), "\n")
sink()    # Close the sink

# Open sink WITH split=TRUE — output appears on BOTH console and file
sink("debug_log.txt", split = TRUE)
print(summary(data))
sink()
```

**Significance of the `split` parameter:**

When `split = FALSE` (default), output is **silently** written to the file and does **not** appear on the console — useful for clean batch logging. When `split = TRUE`, output is **mirrored** to both the console and the file — invaluable during **debugging**, where the programmer needs to see the output *and* preserve it.

**(b) The `write.table()` Function (7 marks):**

`write.table()` exports a matrix or data frame to a delimited text file, providing fine-grained control over formatting.

**Four Important Arguments:**

1. **`x`** — the data object (data frame or matrix) to be written.
2. **`file`** — destination file path or open connection.
3. **`sep`** — field separator string (e.g., `","`, `"\t"`, `";"`).
4. **`row.names`** — logical; if `TRUE`, row labels are written as the first column.
5. **`col.names`** — logical; if `TRUE`, column names are written as the first row.
6. **`dec`** — the decimal separator character.

**Working Example — Export to TSV:**

```r
# Prepare the data
sales <- data.frame(
  Region  = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),
  Q1      = c(120.5, 98.3, 145.7, 110.2),
  Q2      = c(135.2, 110.4, 160.1, 125.6)
)

# Export to a tab-separated file (TSV)
write.table(sales,
            file       = "sales_report.tsv",
            sep        = "\t",
            row.names  = FALSE,
            col.names  = TRUE,
            quote      = FALSE,
            dec        = ".")
```

**Resulting `sales_report.tsv`:**

```
Region  Q1  Q2
North  120.5  135.2
South  98.3  110.4
East  145.7  160.1
West  110.2  125.6
```

The tab separator (`\t`) makes the file easy to import into spreadsheet software, and `quote = FALSE` strips surrounding quotes from character fields for cleaner output.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between `save()`, `saveRDS()`, and `save.image()`. Demonstrate with code how a single linear model object is saved and later reloaded. *(7 marks)*
