Formally, it is the equivalence class of configurations under the action of the similarity group.

**Centroid size is removed because:**
1. Shape is defined to be **scale-invariant** by construction (Kendall, 1984).
2. Size and shape are statistically orthogonal in the preshape space, so size must be removed before computing the mean to obtain a *pure shape* average rather than a size-biased average.
3. The Karcher mean on Kendall's manifold is defined in the unit-preshape sphere, which requires size normalization.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1 + Reasoning 2)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the Orthogonal Procrustes Problem? State the closed-form solution using SVD.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Orthogonal Procrustes Problem (OPP)** seeks the rotation matrix $R \in SO(k)$ that minimizes $\vert\vert A - B R^T \vert\vert_F^2$ for given preshapes $A$ and $B$.

**Closed-form solution:** Compute the SVD of $A^T B = U D V^T$. Then

$$\hat{R} = U V^T$$

(with a sign correction if $\det(\hat{R}) = -1$ to enforce a proper rotation).

The proof follows by expanding the Frobenius norm and noting that maximizing $\text{trace}(A R B^T)$ subject to $R^T R = I$ is equivalent to the SVD identity.

*(Marks distribution: Problem statement 1 + SVD formula 1 + Remarks 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Generalized Procrustes Analysis (GPA)** algorithm in detail. List all the steps and the mathematical formula used at each stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** A researcher has collected 2D landmark data of **20 fish specimens**, each with **8 anatomical landmarks**, sampled from two different species. Using R, write the complete code to:
1. Load the data (assume it is in a CSV file named `fish_landmarks.csv` with 16 columns: $x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2, \ldots, x_8, y_8$).
2. Reshape it into a $2 \times 8 \times 20$ array.
3. Compute the GPA-aligned mean shape using the `shapes` package.
4. Visualize the mean shape and superimpose all aligned configurations.
5. Perform a PCA in the tangent space and report the proportion of variance explained by the first two components.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

GPA is an iterative procedure that simultaneously estimates the mean shape and aligns all configurations to it.

**Algorithm Steps:**

| Step | Operation | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translate each $X_i$ to centroid | $X_{ic} = X_i - \bar{x}_i \mathbf{1}^T$ |
| 2 | Normalize to unit centroid size | $Z_i = X_{ic} / S(X_i)$ |
| 3 | Initialize mean | $\bar{X}^{(0)} = Z_1$ |
| 4 | For each iteration: align via OPP | $R_i = \arg\min_R \vert\vert Z_i - \bar{X}^{(t)} R^T \vert\vert_F^2 = U_i V_i^T$ |
| 5 | Apply rotation | $Z_i^* = Z_i R_i^T$ |
| 6 | Update mean | $\bar{X}^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i Z_i^*$ |
| 7 | Re-normalize mean | $\bar{X}^{(t+1)} \leftarrow \bar{X}^{(t+1)} / S(\bar{X}^{(t+1)})$ |
| 8 | Convergence | $\vert\vert \bar{X}^{(t+1)} - \bar{X}^{(t)} \vert\vert_F < 10^{-6}$ |

**Mathematical justification:** The convergence follows from the fact that the GPA objective — minimizing total Procrustes scatter — is a **non-convex** problem in general, but the iterative scheme is **monotonically decreasing** in the scatter, guaranteeing convergence to a local minimum (often the global minimum in practice).

*(Marks distribution: Steps 3, Justification 2, Iteration convergence 2)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks — Complete R Code:**

```r
# ============================================================
# KTU 2024 - Mean Shape Computation in R for Fish Landmark Data
# ============================================================

# Step 1: Install and load required packages
install.packages(c("shapes", "Morpho", "geomorph"))
library(shapes)

# Step 2: Read the landmark CSV
fish_raw <- read.csv("fish_landmarks.csv")
# Expected columns: x1, y1, x2, y2, ..., x8, y8
# fish_raw has 20 rows (one per specimen)

# Step 3: Convert to a 2 x 8 x 20 array
m <- 8   # number of landmarks
k <- 2   # 2D
n <- nrow(fish_raw)

fish_array <- array(0, dim = c(k, m, n))
for (i in 1:n) {
  coords_i <- matrix(as.numeric(fish_raw[i, ]), nrow = k, byrow = FALSE)
  fish_array[,, i] <- coords_i
}
# Note: the column layout should match (x first, then y) per landmark

# Step 4: Run Generalized Procrustes Analysis
gpa <- procGPA(fish_array, tangentcoords = "full")
mean_shape <- gpa$mshape      # 2 x 8 matrix
aligned     <- gpa$rotated    # 2 x 8 x 20 array
tangent     <- gpa$tangent    # Tangent-space coordinates

# Step 5: Visualize mean shape + superimposition
plot(gpa)         # Default superimposition plot in shapes package

# Optional: custom plot
plot(aligned[1,,], aligned[2,,],
     asp = 1, pch = 19, col = rgb(0, 0, 1, 0.3),
     xlab = "X coordinate", ylab = "Y coordinate",
     main = "GPA-Aligned Fish Shapes with Mean")
points(mean_shape[1, ], mean_shape[2, ],
       pch = 17, col = "red", cex = 1.5)
legend("topright", legend = c("Specimens", "Mean Shape"),
       pch = c(19, 17), col = c("blue", "red"))

# Step 6: Tangent-space PCA
pca_res <- prcomp(t(tangent), center = TRUE, scale. = FALSE)
var_explained <- summary(pca_res)$importance[2, ]
cat("Proportion of variance explained by PC1:", var_explained[1], "\n")
cat("Proportion of variance explained by PC2:", var_explained[2], "\n")
cat("Cumulative (PC1 + PC2):", sum(var_explained[1:2]), "\n")
```

**Expected Output Description:**
- `mean_shape` is a $2 \times 8$ matrix of consensus coordinates.
- `aligned` contains the Procrustes-superimposed shapes.
- The plot overlays all specimens and the mean in red triangles.
- The PCA summary reports the cumulative variance; typically PC1+PC2 explains **40–70%** of shape variability in biological data.

*(Marks distribution: Code correctness 4, Output explanation 2, Interpretation 1)*

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the closed-form solution to the Orthogonal Procrustes Problem. Show every algebraic step explicitly.

**(b) [7 marks]** Differentiate between:
1. **Euclidean mean** vs **Procrustes (Karcher) mean**
2. **Preshape** vs **Shape**
3. **Full Procrustes fit** vs **Partial Procrustes fit**

Provide the corresponding R functions used in the `shapes` package to compute each.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Statement:** Given preshapes $A, B \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$, find

$$\hat{R} = \arg\min_{R \in SO(k)} \vert\vert A - B R^T \vert\vert_F^2$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared Frobenius norm:**

$$\vert\vert A - B R^T \vert\vert_F^2 = \text{trace}\left( (A - B R^T)(A^T - R B^T) \right)$$

**Step 2 — Distribute:**

$$= \text{trace}(A A^T) - \text{trace}(A R B^T) - \text{trace}(B R^T A^T) + \text{trace}(B R^T R B^T)$$

**Step 3 — Use the cyclic property of trace and $R^T R = I$:**

$$= \text{trace}(A A^T) - 2\,\text{trace}(A R B^T) + \text{trace}(B B^T)$$

**Step 4 — Since $\text{trace}(A A^T)$ and $\text{trace}(B B^T)$ are constants, minimization is equivalent to maximizing $f(R) = \text{trace}(A R B^T)$.**

**Step 5 — Use SVD:** Write $A^T B = U D V^T$ where $U, V$ are orthogonal and $D$ is diagonal with non-negative entries. Then

$$\text{trace}(A R B^T) = \text{trace}(A^T B R) = \text{trace}(U D V^T R)$$

**Step 6 — Let $Q = V^T R U$; then $Q \in O(k)$ and $\det(Q) = \det(V^T)\det(R)\det(U)$. Hence**

$$\text{trace}(U D V^T R) = \text{trace}(D Q) = \sum_i d_{ii} q_{ii}$$

**Step 7 — The maximum of $\sum_i d_{ii} q_{ii}$ subject to $q_{ii} \in [-1, 1]$ is achieved when $q_{ii} = 1$ for all $i$, i.e., $Q = I$.**

**Step 8 — Therefore $V^T R U = I$, which gives $R = V U^T$.** For proper rotation (det = +1), if $\det(V U^T) = -1$, flip the sign of the last column of $V$.

$$\boxed{\hat{R} = U V^T \quad \text{(with sign correction if needed)}}$$

*(Marks distribution: Setup 2, Expansion 2, SVD step 2, Conclusion 1)*

**Part (b) — Comparisons and R functions (7 marks):**

| # | Comparison | Euclidean Mean | Procrustes (Karcher) Mean |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Space** | Operates in $\mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$ flat space | Operates on Kendall manifold $\Sigma_m^k$ (curved) |
| 2 | **Computation** | $\bar{X}_{Euc} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i X_i$ | $\bar{X}_{P} = \arg\min_X \sum_i d_P^2(X, X_i)$ via iterative GPA |
| 3 | **Invariance** | Not invariant to rotation/scale | Invariant by construction |
| 4 | **R function** | `colMeans()` or `apply()` | `procGPA()$mshape` |

| # | Comparison | Preshape | Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **What remains** | Translation + Scale removed | Translation + Scale + Rotation removed |
| 2 | **Space** | Unit sphere $S_m^{km}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{km}$ | Kendall manifold $\Sigma_m^k$ (quotient of preshape sphere by $SO(k)$) |
| 3 | **R function** | Computed internally in `procGPA()` | Result of `procGPA()` |

| # | Comparison | Full Procrustes Fit | Partial Procrustes Fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scale** | Unit centroid size (size = 1) | Original centroid size preserved |
| 2 | **Distance** | Full Procrustes distance $d_P$ | Partial Procrustes distance $d_{PP}$ |
| 3 | **R argument** | `procGPA(arr, scale = TRUE)` (default) | `procGPA(arr, scale = FALSE)` |

*(Marks distribution: Three comparisons × 2 marks each + 1 bonus for R code)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to **re-normalize the mean** after each GPA iteration. Without this, the mean drifts away from the unit-size sphere, and the algorithm fails to converge.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing `tangentcoords = "full"` (uses full Procrustes distance) with `tangentcoords = "partial"` (uses partial Procrustes). Mixing them gives inconsistent PCA outputs.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Assuming the **Euclidean mean** equals the **Procrustes mean**. They coincide only when all configurations are already perfectly aligned — a rare case in practice.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing $\text{trace}(A R B^T)$ as a scalar when it is a scalar — fine — but omitting the cyclic permutation step. KTU examiners award full marks only when the cyclic property is **explicitly invoked**.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Using `procGPA()` without first loading the `shapes` package. Always include `library(shapes)` at the top.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shape** = configuration invariant under translation, rotation, and scaling.
- **Preshape** = configuration after removing only translation and scaling (lies on unit sphere).
- **Centroid size** $S(X)$ must always be computed and used as the normalizer.
- **Orthogonal Procrustes Problem (OPP)** has the closed-form solution $\hat{R} = U V^T$ from SVD of $A^T B$.
- **Karcher Mean** is the geodesic center of mass on Kendall's manifold; computed iteratively via GPA.
- **Generalized Procrustes Analysis (GPA)** is the standard iterative algorithm for mean shape estimation.
- **Procrustes distance** lies in $[0, 1]$ and is the geodesic metric on the shape manifold.
- **R implementation** uses `procGPA()` from the `shapes` package; argument `tangentcoords` selects full or partial projection.
- **Tangent-space PCA** is valid *only* for small shape variability (Goodall's $F$ test prerequisite); otherwise, use **Karcher** or **Riemannian** methods.
- **Convergence criterion** typically $\vert\vert \bar{X}^{(t+1)} - \bar{X}^{(t)} \vert\vert_F < 10^{-6}$.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme:** This topic falls under CO2 (apply advanced programming for statistical computations). Expect 3-mark definitional questions and 14-mark derivation-cum-coding hybrid questions.
- **Common R pitfall:** Forgetting `library(shapes)`; misaligning the array dimensions ($k \times m \times n$ is mandatory).
- **Real-world link:** Mean shape underpins **Active Shape Models (ASM)** in computer vision and **Geometric Morphometrics (GMM)** in biology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Procrustes Algorithms for Shape Analysis in R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generalized Procrustes Algorithms for Shape Analysis in R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Generalized Procrustes Analysis (GPA)** is an iterative statistical procedure used in **geometric morphometrics** to superimpose a collection of two- or three-dimensional landmark configurations by removing non-shape variation — namely **translation (location)**, **scale (size)**, and **rotation (orientation)** — so that the residual differences between configurations describe only true **shape variation**.

Formally, given $k$ configurations $\{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k\}$, each containing $p$ landmarks in $m$ dimensions ($m = 2$ or $3$), GPA finds the optimal superposition parameters (translation vectors $t_i$, scale factors $s_i$, rotation matrices $R_i$) such that the summed squared Procrustes distance between every configuration and the **mean shape (consensus)** is minimized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In PEEBT756 (Advanced Computer Programming Techniques), GPA is taught as a canonical example of an **iterative numerical optimization algorithm** implemented in R. It demonstrates loop convergence, matrix algebra, distance minimization, and the practical use of the `shapes`, `geomorph`, and `vegan` packages.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **five photographs of the same hand drawn by different artists**. Each drawing is placed at a different spot on the page (translation), some are bigger, some smaller (scale), and some are tilted at odd angles (rotation). To compare *how* the hands differ in *shape* (longer fingers, wider palms), you must first **align** them so that they sit on top of each other as closely as possible.

**GPA is the mathematical "auto-alignment" tool that does exactly this for any collection of shapes represented by landmarks.** It keeps rotating, resizing, and re-centering each shape until the cloud of points overlaps as tightly as possible, and the average of that overlap becomes the **consensus shape**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology**
> - **Landmark**: A biologically/homologously corresponding point across all specimens (e.g., tip of a nose, joint of a finger).
> - **Configuration**: The full $p \times m$ coordinate matrix of one specimen.
> - **Shape**: All geometric information about a configuration *excluding* location, scale, and rotation.
> - **Consensus (Mean Shape)**: The Procrustes-average configuration after optimal superposition.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sources of Non-Shape Variation

Before any alignment, two configurations differ for four possible reasons:

1. **Location (Translation)** — the centroid is at a different point in space.
2. **Scale (Size)** — one is enlarged or shrunk uniformly.
3. **Rotation (Orientation)** — one is rotated relative to the other.
4. **Shape** — true geometric differences in proportions.

GPA removes the first three sources systematically so that the only remaining variation is **shape**.

### 2.2 Operational Steps of the GPA Algorithm

The Generalized Procrustes Analysis proceeds as an iterative loop:

- **Step 1 — Translation Normalization (Centering):**
  Translate every configuration so that its centroid coincides with the origin of the coordinate system. This removes the **location** parameter.

- **Step 2 — Scale Normalization (Unit Size):**
  Scale every centered configuration to have unit **centroid size**, defined as the square root of the sum of squared distances of landmarks from the centroid. This removes the **size** parameter.

- **Step 3 — Initial Rotation:**
  For the first iteration, arbitrarily rotate one configuration and use it as a temporary reference. In subsequent iterations, the consensus mean is the reference.

- **Step 4 — Rotation to Reference:**
  Rotate every configuration optimally against the current consensus so that the sum of squared distances is minimized. The optimal rotation matrix is obtained via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** of the cross-product matrix.

- **Step 5 — Consensus Computation:**
  Compute the element-wise mean of all rotated configurations. This becomes the new consensus.

- **Step 6 — Convergence Check:**
  Compute the Procrustes distance between the new consensus and the consensus from the previous iteration. If the change falls below a small tolerance $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-7}$), **STOP**; otherwise return to Step 4.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration $X_i$ | $X_i \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ | Landmark coordinates of specimen $i$ |
| Centroid $\bar{x}_i$ | $\bar{x}_i = \frac{1}{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} X_{ij}$ | Mean of all landmarks of $X_i$ |
| Centered Config. $X_i^c$ | $X_i^c = X_i - \mathbf{1}\bar{x}_i^T$ | Removes translation |
| Centroid Size $S_i$ | $S_i = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert X_{ij}^c \vert^2}$ | Measure of overall size |
| Normalized Config. $Y_i$ | $Y_i = X_i^c \,/\, S_i$ | Removes scale |
| Cross-Product Matrix $H_i$ | $H_i = X_{\text{ref}}^T \, Y_i$ | Input for SVD-based rotation |
| Optimal Rotation $R_i$ | $R_i = V U^T$ from $\text{svd}(H_i)$ | Aligns $Y_i$ to reference |
| Procrustes Distance $d_P$ | $d_P = \sqrt{\sum \vert X_i - X_j \vert^2}$ after superposition | Shape dissimilarity |
| Consensus $M$ | $M = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i Y_i$ | Mean shape |
| Convergence Criterion | $\vert M^{(t)} - M^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ | Loop termination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on SVD rotation:** If $H_i = U \Sigma V^T$, then $R_i = V U^T$ is the optimal orthogonal rotation that minimizes $\vert X_{\text{ref}} - R_i Y_i \vert^2$ (subject to $\det(R_i) = +1$, i.e., no reflection).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

GPA is foundational in biological and medical research:
- **Craniometrics**: comparing skull shapes across human populations.
- **Paleontology**: quantifying morphological evolution of fossils.
- **Medical imaging**: tracking shape changes in organs (e.g., hippocampus in Alzheimer's).
- **Botany**: comparing leaf shapes across species.
- **Forensic science**: facial landmark-based identification.

In R, GPA is implemented in packages like **`geomorph`** and **`shapes`**, making it accessible to non-statisticians in life sciences.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Centroid Size Formula

We begin with a configuration $X_i$ containing $p$ landmarks in $m$ dimensions. The centroid is:

$$
\bar{x}_i = \frac{1}{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} X_{ij}
$$

This is the arithmetic mean of every landmark coordinate. To translate the configuration so the centroid is at the origin, we subtract $\bar{x}_i$ from every landmark:

$$
X_i^{c} = X_i - \mathbf{1}_{p \times 1} \cdot \bar{x}_i^{T}
$$

Here, $\mathbf{1}_{p \times 1}$ is a column vector of ones of length $p$, and $\bar{x}_i^{T}$ is the row-vector of centroid coordinates broadcast across all landmarks. The **centroid size** is then defined as the square root of the sum of squared Euclidean distances of all landmarks from the centroid:

$$
S_i = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \sum_{d=1}^{m} \left( X_{ijd} - \bar{x}_{id} \right)^{2}}
$$

In compact matrix notation, since $X_i^c$ is already centered:

$$
S_i = \sqrt{\operatorname{tr}\!\left( (X_i^{c})^{T} X_i^{c} \right)} = \Vert X_i^{c} \Vert_F
$$

where $\Vert \cdot \Vert_F$ is the **Frobenius norm**. The normalized (unit-size) configuration is:

$$
Y_i = \frac{X_i^{c}}{S_i}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Rotation via SVD

Given a reference configuration $X_{\text{ref}}$ (already centered and normalized) and a target $Y_i$, we seek an orthogonal matrix $R_i$ (with $R_i^{T} R_i = I$ and $\det(R_i) = +1$) minimizing:

$$
\mathcal{L}(R_i) = \Vert X_{\text{ref}} - R_i Y_i \Vert_F^{2}
$$

Expanding the squared Frobenius norm:

$$
\mathcal{L}(R_i) = \operatorname{tr}\!\left( (X_{\text{ref}} - R_i Y_i)(X_{\text{ref}} - R_i Y_i)^{T} \right)
$$

$$
= \operatorname{tr}(X_{\text{ref}} X_{\text{ref}}^{T}) + \operatorname{tr}(Y_i Y_i^{T}) - 2\,\operatorname{tr}(X_{\text{ref}} Y_i^{T} R_i^{T})
$$

Because the first two terms are constants independent of $R_i$, **minimizing $\mathcal{L}$ is equivalent to maximizing** $\operatorname{tr}(X_{\text{ref}} Y_i^{T} R_i^{T})$. Define the cross-product matrix:

$$
H_i = X_{\text{ref}}^{T} Y_i
$$

Now apply **Singular Value Decomposition** to $H_i$:

$$
H_i = U \Sigma V^{T}
$$

Setting $R_i = V U^{T}$ and using the trace inequality $\operatorname{tr}(A^T B) \le \operatorname{tr}(\Sigma_A)$ for orthogonal $B$, we have:

$$
\operatorname{tr}(H_i^{T} R_i) = \operatorname{tr}(U \Sigma V^{T} V U^{T}) = \operatorname{tr}(\Sigma)
$$

This is the **maximum possible trace**, hence $R_i = V U^{T}$ is the optimal rotation. To prevent reflection (improper rotation), if $\det(V U^{T}) = -1$, the last column of $V$ is multiplied by $-1$ before forming $R_i$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (2 Landmarks, 2 Configurations)

Let two configurations be:

$$
X_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad X_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1.5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Centroid Calculation:**

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[(0+2),\,(0+0)\right] = (1, 0)
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[(1+1.5),\,(1+2)\right] = (1.25, 1.5)
$$

**Step 2 — Centering:**

$$
X_1^{c} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad X_2^{c} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.25 & -0.5 \\ 0.25 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Centroid Size:**

$$
S_1 = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{2}
$$

$$
S_2 = \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.25 + 0.0625 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.625} \approx 0.7906
$$

**Step 4 — Unit-Size Normalization:**

$$
Y_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.7071 & 0 \\ 0.7071 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
Y_2 = \frac{1}{0.7906}\begin{pmatrix} -0.25 & -0.5 \\ 0.25 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.3162 & -0.6325 \\ 0.3162 & 0.6325 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Optimal Rotation:** Compute $H_2 = Y_1^{T} Y_2$, then $\text{svd}(H_2)$, and finally $R_2 = V U^{T}$.

$$
H_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.7071 & 0.7071 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} -0.3162 & -0.6325 \\ 0.3162 & 0.6325 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4472 & 0.8944 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

SVD yields $U = I$, $V = I$, so $R_2 = I$ (no rotation needed in this simple case).

**Step 6 — Consensus (Mean Shape):**

$$
M = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(Y_1 + R_2 Y_2\right) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(Y_1 + Y_2\right)
$$

$$
M = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} -1.0233 & -0.6325 \\ 1.0233 & 0.6325 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.5117 & -0.3162 \\ 0.5117 & 0.3162 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The Procrustes distance between $Y_1$ and $R_2 Y_2$ then quantifies the pure shape difference:

$$
d_P = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{2}\sum_{d=1}^{2}\!\left(Y_{1,jd} - Y_{2,jd}\right)^{2}}
$$

This iterative scheme generalizes seamlessly to $k > 2$ configurations.

### 3.4 R Implementation (Reference Snippet)

```r
# Load the 'shapes' package (KTU lab standard)
library(shapes)

# Built-in GPA routine on the 'gorf.dat' dataset (microsatellite shapes)
gpa_result <- procGPA(gorf.dat, scale = TRUE, reflect = FALSE)

# Inspect outputs
gpa_result$rotated   # k x p x m array of superimposed configurations
gpa_result$mshape    # p x m matrix: the consensus mean shape
gpa_result$size      # vector of centroid sizes (centroid sizes in original configs)
```

The `procGPA()` function internally executes Steps 1–6 above with default tolerance $\epsilon = 10^{-7}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of the GPA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: k Landmark Configurations] --> B[Centering Step]
    B --> C[Centroid Size Computation]
    C --> D[Unit-Size Normalization]
    D --> E[Iteration Counter t = 1]
    E --> F[Select Reference Consensus]
    F --> G[Compute Optimal Rotation via SVD]
    G --> H[Rotate Each Configuration]
    H --> I[Recompute Mean Consensus]
    I --> J{Convergence Check}
    J -->|Not Converged| K[Update t = t + 1]
    K --> F
    J -->|Converged epsilon lt 1e-7| L[Output: Rotated Configs + Consensus]
    L --> M[Procrustes Distance Matrix]
    M --> N[Downstream PCA / Statistical Tests]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Decomposition of Each GPA Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAGE1[Stage 1 Translation Removal]
        T1a[Compute Centroid per Configuration] --> T1b[Subtract Centroid from Landmarks]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2[Stage 2 Scale Removal]
        S2a[Compute Centroid Size Si] --> S2b[Divide Each Centered Configuration by Si]
    end

    subgraph STAGE3[Stage 3 Optimal Rotation]
        R3a[Form Cross Product Matrix H] --> R3b[Apply SVD H equals U Sigma VT]
        R3b --> R3c[Construct R equals V UT]
        R3c --> R3d[Apply Rotation Y rotated equals R Y]
    end

    subgraph STAGE4[Stage 4 Consensus and Convergence]
        C4a[Mean of All Rotated Configs equals M] --> C4b{Delta M lt epsilon}
        C4b -->|Yes| C4c[STOP Return M and Rotated]
        C4b -->|No| C4d[Set M as New Reference and Repeat]
    end

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2 --> STAGE3 --> STAGE4
```

### 4.3 Data-Flow Diagram: From Raw Landmarks to Shape Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw Landmark Coordinates p x m per specimen] --> TRANS[Translation Centering]
    TRANS --> SCALE[Scale Normalization Unit Centroid Size]
    SCALE --> ROT[Rotation Alignment vs Consensus]
    ROT --> SUPER[Superimposed Configurations in Shape Space]
    SUPER --> DIST[Pairwise Procrustes Distance Matrix k x k]
    DIST --> PCA[Principal Component Analysis of Shape Variation]
    PCA --> VIS[Visualization PC1 vs PC2 Scatter]
    VIS --> STAT[Statistical Testing e.g. ANOVA on PC scores]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define the term **"shape"** in the context of geometric morphometrics. How does it differ from **"form"**?

**Model Answer:**
In geometric morphometrics, *shape* refers to all the geometric information about an object after removing the effects of location (translation), scale (size), and rotation (orientation). In contrast, *form* includes size as well — i.e., form = shape + size. The Generalized Procrustes Analysis operates on shape by stripping away these three non-shape components.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
What is the role of **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** in Generalized Procrustes Analysis?

**Model Answer:**
SVD is used to compute the **optimal rotation matrix** that aligns a normalized configuration to a reference consensus. Given the cross-product matrix $H_i = X_{\text{ref}}^{T} Y_i$, its SVD $H_i = U \Sigma V^{T}$ yields the rotation $R_i = V U^{T}$, which minimizes the sum of squared distances between the aligned configuration and the reference.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** `[7 Marks]` Explain the operational steps of the Generalized Procrustes Algorithm. State the convergence criterion used.

**Model Answer:**

The GPA algorithm consists of the following six operational steps:

1. **Translation Normalization:** Each configuration $X_i$ is translated so that its centroid lies at the origin. The new centered configuration is $X_i^{c} = X_i - \mathbf{1}\bar{x}_i^{T}$.

2. **Scale Normalization:** The centroid size $S_i = \sqrt{\operatorname{tr}((X_i^{c})^{T} X_i^{c})}$ is computed, and each configuration is divided by $S_i$ to produce a unit-size configuration $Y_i = X_i^{c}/S_i$.

3. **Initial Reference Selection:** For the first iteration, an arbitrary configuration is selected as the reference; in subsequent iterations, the consensus mean is used.

4. **Optimal Rotation:** For each $Y_i$, the cross-product matrix $H_i = X_{\text{ref}}^{T} Y_i$ is decomposed via SVD: $H_i = U \Sigma V^{T}$. The optimal rotation matrix is $R_i = V U^{T}$ (with sign-correction if $\det(R_i) = -1$).

5. **Consensus Computation:** The new mean shape is computed as $M = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i Y_i$.

6. **Convergence Check:** The algorithm terminates when $\Vert M^{(t)} - M^{(t-1)} \Vert < \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is typically set to $10^{-7}$.

The loop returns to Step 4 until convergence.

**Q.A (b)** `[7 Marks]` Describe how GPA is implemented in R using the `shapes` package. Demonstrate with a sample code snippet and explain the meaning of its outputs.

**Model Answer:**

In R, the `shapes` package provides the function `procGPA()` which implements the full GPA algorithm.

```r
library(shapes)

# Load a built-in dataset of 2D landmark configurations
data(gorf.dat)

# Run Generalized Procrustes Analysis
result <- procGPA(gorf.dat, scale = TRUE, reflect = FALSE)

# Display outputs
print(result$rotated)   # Array of superimposed configurations
print(result$mshape)    # Consensus mean shape (p x m matrix)
print(result$size)      # Original centroid sizes of each specimen
```

**Explanation of outputs:**
- `result$rotated`: A 3-D array of dimensions $k \times p \times m$, containing the optimally aligned configurations.
- `result$mshape`: The Procrustes mean shape — a single $p \times m$ matrix representing the consensus.
- `result$size`: A vector of length $k$, giving the centroid size of each original specimen before normalization.

The function automatically iterates until convergence and returns both the aligned data and the consensus needed for downstream analyses (e.g., PCA, ANOVA).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** `[7 Marks]` Derive the optimal rotation matrix used in GPA using Singular Value Decomposition. Show all algebraic steps.

**Model Answer:**

Given reference $X_{\text{ref}}$ and target $Y_i$, both centered and unit-scaled, we seek an orthogonal matrix $R_i$ minimizing:

$$
\mathcal{L}(R_i) = \Vert X_{\text{ref}} - R_i Y_i \Vert_F^{2}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\mathcal{L}(R_i) = \operatorname{tr}(X_{\text{ref}} X_{\text{ref}}^{T}) + \operatorname{tr}(Y_i Y_i^{T}) - 2\operatorname{tr}(X_{\text{ref}} Y_i^{T} R_i^{T})
$$

The first two terms are constants, so minimization reduces to maximizing $\operatorname{tr}(X_{\text{ref}} Y_i^{T} R_i^{T})$. Let $H_i = X_{\text{ref}}^{T} Y_i$. Then we maximize $\operatorname{tr}(H_i^{T} R_i)$.

Apply SVD: $H_i = U \Sigma V^{T}$. The candidate $R_i = V U^{T}$ gives:

$$
\operatorname{tr}(H_i^{T} R_i) = \operatorname{tr}(U \Sigma V^{T} V U^{T}) = \operatorname{tr}(\Sigma)
$$

By the von Neumann trace inequality, this is the maximum attainable trace over orthogonal matrices. Hence $R_i = V U^{T}$ is the optimal rotation. If $\det(R_i) = -1$, multiply the last column of $V$ by $-1$ to enforce proper rotation.

**Q.B (b)** `[7 Marks]` A 2D configuration $X = \{(0,0), (2,0), (1, \sqrt{3})\}$ (an equilateral triangle) and another $X' = \{(1,1), (3,1), (2, 1+\sqrt{3})\}$ are given. Compute the centroid size of each, normalize them, and state the Procrustes distance before any rotation.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Centroids:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0+2+1),\,(0+0+\sqrt{3})\right] = \left(1,\, \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)
$$

$$
\bar{x}' = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1+3+2),\,(1+1+1+\sqrt{3})\right] = \left(2,\, 1 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Centered Configurations:**

$$
X^c = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \\ 1 & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \\ 0 & \tfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \end{pmatrix}, \quad
(X')^c = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \\ 1 & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \\ 0 & \tfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \end{pmatrix}
$$

The two centered configurations are *identical* — they are the same triangle shifted by $(1, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Centroid Size (same for both):**

$$
S = \sqrt{1 + \tfrac{1}{3} + 1 + \tfrac{1}{3} + 0 + \tfrac{4}{3}} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.7321
$$

**Step 4 — Normalized Configurations:**

Since the centered configurations are equal, the normalized ones are also identical:

$$
Y = Y' = \frac{X^c}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Procrustes Distance (Before Rotation):**

$$
d_P = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{3}\sum_{d=1}^{2}(Y_{jd} - Y'_{jd})^{2}} = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** The two triangles have *identical shape*; they differ only by translation. After centering and scaling, their Procrustes distance is **zero**, confirming that GPA correctly identifies them as the same shape.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting to center before scaling**: Students often compute centroid size from the *raw* configuration instead of the *centered* one, yielding incorrect $S_i$.
> - **Confusing rotation with reflection**: The determinant check $\det(R_i) = +1$ is mandatory; failing it produces mirror-image shapes.
> - **Mixing up $R_i$ and $R_i^{T}$**: The optimal rotation is $R_i = V U^{T}$ (not $U V^{T}$).
> - **Reporting Procrustes distance without superimposition**: Shape distance must be computed *after* all three normalizations; raw coordinate differences include size and rotation.
> - **Confusing "form" with "shape"**: Form = Shape + Size; GPA outputs shape-only coordinates.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shape vs Form**: GPA isolates *shape* by stripping translation, scale, and rotation.
- **Centroid Size** $S_i = \sqrt{\operatorname{tr}((X_i^c)^T X_i^c)}$ is the standard size metric.
- **Three normalizations** = centering + unit scaling + SVD-based rotation.
- **Optimal rotation** is $R_i = V U^{T}$ from the SVD of $H_i = X_{\text{ref}}^{T} Y_i$.
- **Iterative convergence** uses tolerance $\epsilon \approx 10^{-7}$ on consensus change.
- **R implementation** uses `procGPA()` from the `shapes` package or `gpagen()` from `geomorph`.
- **Outputs**: rotated configurations, consensus mean shape, centroid sizes, Procrustes distance matrix.
- **Downstream analyses**: PCA on Procrustes coordinates, ANOVA on PC scores, thin-plate spline deformation grids.
- **KTU weightage tip**: 14-mark questions frequently combine *derivation* + *R-code interpretation*; always prepare both.
- **Common examiner traps**: skipping the determinant check, misordering the SVD factors, reporting shape distance before full superimposition.
- **Package familiarity**: `shapes`, `geomorph`, `Morpho`, `vegan` — know at least `shapes::procGPA` for lab exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tangent-Based PCA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tangent-Based PCA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tangent-Based Principal Component Analysis (Tangent PCA)** is a non-linear extension of the classical **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** algorithm, designed specifically for high-dimensional data points that are constrained to lie on (or near) a smooth, low-dimensional **Riemannian Manifold** $\mathcal{M}$ embedded in an ambient Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^D$.

In standard PCA, the data is assumed to live in a *flat* Euclidean hyperplane. However, real-world data — such as human body poses, 3D facial expressions, geological surfaces, or protein conformations — naturally lies on **curved, non-linear manifolds**. Tangent-Based PCA overcomes the failure of linearity by:

1. First identifying the **Intrinsic Mean** (Fréchet Mean) of the data on the manifold.
2. Mapping each data point from the curved manifold to the **flat tangent space** $T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$ at the mean point using the **logarithmic map** ($\log_{\mu}$).
3. Performing standard Euclidean PCA on these flattened tangent vectors.
4. The principal directions then represent the dominant modes of variation *intrinsic to the geometry* of the data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Curved Mountain Road

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: Imagine standing on a curved, hilly mountain road.**

You are trying to figure out which direction has the *most traffic* (variance). 

- **Naïve Standard PCA** would treat the mountain as flat, slice it with a ruler at your starting point, and draw straight lines through the cloud of GPS pings. The ruler cuts through hills and valleys, completely missing the true curvature of the road. 
- **Tangent-Based PCA**, instead, places a perfectly flat **tangent plane** that *just touches* the road at the average location of all the cars (the intrinsic mean). It then rolls each car up onto this flat plane, draws straight arrows showing the directions of most movement, and rolls them back down. The result is a coordinate system that respects the true geometry of the road.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Highlights

- **Riemannian Manifold** $\mathcal{M}$: A smooth, curved mathematical space locally resembling Euclidean space.
- **Intrinsic Mean (Fréchet Mean)** $\mu \in \mathcal{M}$: The point on the manifold that minimizes the sum of squared geodesic distances to all data points.
- **Tangent Space** $T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$: A **D**-dimensional vector space attached to $\mathcal{M}$ at $\mu$, consisting of all possible velocity vectors at that point.
- **Logarithmic Map** $\log_{\mu}: \mathcal{M} \rightarrow T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$: Maps a manifold point to its tangent vector representation.
- **Exponential Map** $\exp_{\mu}: T_{\mu}\mathcal{M} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}$: Maps a tangent vector back onto the manifold by following the geodesic for unit time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Flow

The Tangent-Based PCA pipeline is decomposed into the following structured stages:

1. **Stage I — Manifold Specification**: The practitioner defines or assumes the underlying manifold $\mathcal{M}$ (e.g., Sphere $S^2$, Grassmannian $\mathcal{G}(k,n)$, Symmetric Positive Definite matrices $\mathcal{P}_n$, etc.). A **Riemannian metric** $g$ is specified, which defines inner products on each tangent space.

2. **Stage II — Intrinsic Mean Computation**:
   - Initialize $\mu_0$ using a Euclidean mean as a warm start.
   - Iteratively update: project Euclidean averages back onto $\mathcal{M}$ using the **exponential map** until convergence.

3. **Stage III — Logarithmic Map Projection**:
   - For every data point $x_i \in \mathcal{M}$, compute $v_i = \log_{\mu}(x_i) \in T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$.
   - These $v_i$ now live in a vector space where standard linear algebra applies.

4. **Stage IV — Classical PCA on Tangent Vectors**:
   - Form the matrix $V = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n] \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times n}$.
   - Compute the **D × D** sample covariance matrix: $S = \frac{1}{n} V V^{\top}$.
   - Solve the eigenvalue problem: $S w_k = \lambda_k w_k$.
   - Sort eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_D$.

5. **Stage V — Reconstruction (Optional)**:
   - Project any new point $x$ via $\log_{\mu}(x)$ and reduce.
   - Reconstruct via $\exp_{\mu}(\cdot)$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Repository** — Memorize these equations for Part A and Part B numericals.

| Symbol / Term | Mathematical Expression | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fréchet Mean | $\mu = \arg\min_{p \in \mathcal{M}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{\mathcal{M}}^{2}(p, x_i)$ | Riemannian center of mass on the manifold |
| Geodesic Distance | $d_{\mathcal{M}}(p, q) = \Vert \log_{p}(q) \Vert_{p}$ | Shortest path length on the manifold |
| Exponential Map | $\exp_{p}(v) = \gamma_{v}(1)$ | Geodesic flow for unit time from $p$ in direction $v$ |
| Logarithmic Map | $\log_{p}(q) = v \iff \exp_{p}(v) = q$ | Inverse of exponential map |
| Tangent PCA Covariance | $S = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i v_i^{\top}$ | Sample covariance of log-mapped vectors |
| Eigen Decomposition | $S W = W \Lambda$ | $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_D)$ |
| Variance Explained Ratio | $\text{VE}(k) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{D} \lambda_j}$ | Information retention metric |
| Total Geodesic Variance | $\sigma^{2}_{\text{geo}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert v_i \Vert^{2}_{\mu}$ | Total intrinsic variance of dataset |
| Projected Reconstruction | $\hat{x}_i = \exp_{\mu}\!\left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \langle v_i, w_k \rangle_{\mu} w_k \right)$ | Back-projection onto $\mathcal{M}$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (DTI)**: Diffusion Tensor Imaging data lives on the manifold of **Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD)** matrices $\mathcal{P}_3$. Tangent PCA enables population-level brain connectivity studies.
- **Computer Vision & Pose Estimation**: Human skeletal joints are constrained to angular spaces ($SO(3)$ rotations). Tangent PCA captures dominant motion modes in gait analysis.
- **Shape Analysis**: 3D shape correspondences are often represented on the **Grassmannian manifold**. Tangent PCA clusters anatomical shapes (e.g., vertebrae).
- **Climate & Geoscience**: Earth surface temperature anomalies lie on a sphere $S^2$. Tangent PCA detects El Niño-like climate oscillations without polar singularities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Intrinsic (Fréchet) Mean Update Rule

We seek to minimize the sum of squared geodesic distances. Let $f(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{\mathcal{M}}^{2}(p, x_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \log_{p}(x_i) \Vert_{p}^{2}$.

Taking the gradient and setting it to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(p) &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{p}(x_i) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{p}(x_i) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This implicit equation motivates the **gradient descent update**:

$$\begin{aligned}
p^{(t+1)} &= \exp_{p^{(t)}}\!\left( \varepsilon \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{p^{(t)}}(x_i) \right)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the step size. This is the **Karcher / Riemannian gradient descent** algorithm. Converged $p^{(\infty)} \equiv \mu$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Tangent-Space Covariance

Once $\mu$ is found, every $x_i$ is mapped to:

$$v_i = \log_{\mu}(x_i) \in T_{\mu}\mathcal{M} \cong \mathbb{R}^{D}$$

The **sample mean in the tangent space** is zero by construction (since $\mu$ satisfies $\sum v_i = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{v} &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{\mu}(x_i) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the **sample covariance matrix** simplifies to:

$$S = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (v_i - \bar{v})(v_i - \bar{v})^{\top} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i v_i^{\top}$$

This is an exact match with the KTU formula sheet entry.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Toy Problem on $S^{2}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example: Tangent PCA on a 2-Sphere**

Suppose 4 unit vectors on the unit sphere $S^{2}$ are given by:

$$x_1 = (1,0,0)^{\top}, \quad x_2 = (0,1,0)^{\top}, \quad x_3 = (0,0,1)^{\top}, \quad x_4 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)^{\top}$$

**Step 1 — Compute Fréchet Mean $\mu$:**
The Karcher mean on $S^2$ with the standard round metric is the normalized Euclidean mean:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i}{\left\Vert \sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i \right\Vert} = \frac{(1,1,1) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)}{\left\Vert (1,1,1)\left(1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \right\Vert} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)^{\top}
\end{aligned}$$

Note that $\mu = x_4$ in this symmetric configuration.

**Step 2 — Compute Logarithmic Maps:**
For a unit sphere, $\log_{\mu}(x) = \frac{\theta}{\sin \theta} (x - (\mu^{\top} x)\mu)$ where $\theta = \arccos(\mu^{\top} x)$.

For $x_1 = (1,0,0)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu^{\top} x_1 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\theta_1 &= \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \approx 0.9553 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= \frac{\theta_1}{\sin \theta_1}\left[(1,0,0) - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)\right] \\
&= \frac{\theta_1}{\sin \theta_1}\left[\left(\frac{2}{3}, -\frac{1}{3}, -\frac{1}{3}\right)\right] \approx (1.1193, -0.5596, -0.5596)^{\top}
\end{aligned}$$

By symmetry, similar $v_i$ are computed.

**Step 3 — Form Covariance Matrix and Eigen-decompose:**
$V = [v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4]$. Compute $S = \frac{1}{3} V V^{\top}$. Eigendecomposition yields top-2 components capturing $> 95\%$ of the total intrinsic geodesic variance $\sigma^{2}_{\text{geo}} = \frac{1}{4}\sum \Vert v_i \Vert^{2}_{\mu}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below illustrates the complete information pipeline of Tangent-Based PCA, showing how data transitions between the manifold domain and the tangent space domain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputData[Input Data x1 to xn on Manifold M] --> manifoldSpec[Manifold Specification: M and Metric g]
    manifoldSpec --> karcherLoop[Stage I: Karcher Gradient Descent]
    karcherLoop --> meanCheck{Convergence?}
    meanCheck -- No --> karcherLoop
    meanCheck -- Yes --> meanOut[Intrinsic Mean mu on M]
    meanOut --> logMap[Stage II: Logarithmic Map log_mu xi]
    inputData --> logMap
    logMap --> tangentVecs[Tangent Vectors v1 to vn in T_mu M]
    tangentVecs --> covBuild[Stage III: Build D x D Covariance Matrix S]
    covBuild --> eigenDecomp[Stage IV: Eigendecomposition S W equals W Lambda]
    eigenDecomp --> sortEval[Sort Eigenvalues Lambda 1 to Lambda D]
    sortEval --> varExplain[Stage V: Variance Explained Ratio VE k]
    varExplain --> outputVec[Output: Principal Modes w1 to wK]
    outputVec --> reconstruct[Optional: Reconstruction via Exponential Map]
    reconstruct --> manifoldReconstruct[Reconstructed Points on M]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Manifold-to-Tangent Mapping Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ManifoldDomain["Curved Manifold M"]
        d1[x1]
        d2[x2]
        d3[x3]
        d4[xn]
    end
    subgraph TangentPlane["Flat Tangent Plane T_mu M"]
        t1[v1]
        t2[v2]
        t3[v3]
        t4[vn]
    end
    subgraph PCAReduction["PCA Reduction Zone"]
        ev1[w1 PC 1]
        ev2[w2 PC 2]
        ev3[wK PC K]
    end
    mu["Intrinsic Mean mu on M"]
    d1 -- log_mu --> t1
    d2 -- log_mu --> t2
    d3 -- log_mu --> t3
    d4 -- log_mu --> t4
    t1 --> ev1
    t2 --> ev1
    t3 --> ev1
    t4 --> ev1
    ev1 --> ev2
    ev2 --> ev3
    mu -. anchors .- TangentPlane
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**Q1.** Define *Tangent-Based PCA*. Why is it preferred over standard PCA for manifold-valued data?
*(3 Marks | CO-2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

> **Model Answer:**
> Tangent-Based PCA is a non-linear dimensionality reduction technique that generalizes classical PCA to data points residing on a Riemannian manifold $\mathcal{M}$. It computes the intrinsic Fréchet mean $\mu$, projects all data onto the tangent space $T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$ via the logarithmic map, and then performs standard PCA on these tangent vectors. Standard PCA fails on manifold data because it assumes a globally Euclidean (flat) structure, leading to erroneous directions when the underlying space is curved (e.g., directions on a sphere). Tangent PCA preserves intrinsic geometric distances and produces modes of variation that are valid within the manifold.

---

**Q2.** State and briefly explain the role of the *exponential map* and *logarithmic map* in Tangent PCA.
*(3 Marks | CO-2, Bloom Level: Understand)*

> **Model Answer:**
> The **exponential map** $\exp_{\mu}: T_{\mu}\mathcal{M} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}$ takes a tangent vector $v$ at the intrinsic mean $\mu$ and returns a point on the manifold by traveling unit time along the geodesic in direction $v$. It is the "tangent-to-manifold" projector. The **logarithmic map** $\log_{\mu}: \mathcal{M} \rightarrow T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$ is its inverse: it takes a manifold point $x$ and returns the tangent vector $v$ such that $\exp_{\mu}(v) = x$. Together, they form the bridge that converts curved manifold data into flat vectors suitable for standard linear PCA.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

---

#### ✦ QUESTION A — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q.A.** **(a)** Derive the gradient-descent update rule (Karcher algorithm) for computing the **intrinsic Fréchet mean** $\mu$ on a Riemannian manifold $\mathcal{M}$. Clearly state the optimality condition. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Given $n$ data points mapped to tangent vectors $v_1, \dots, v_n \in T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$, derive the **tangent-space covariance matrix** and the **variance-explained ratio** $\text{VE}(k)$. State two real-world engineering applications where this is used. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Karcher Update Rule Derivation:**

Let $f(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{\mathcal{M}}^{2}(p, x_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \log_{p}(x_i) \Vert_{p}^{2}$.

Setting the gradient to zero gives the **first-order optimality condition**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{p}(x_i) = 0$$

Applying Riemannian gradient descent with step size $\varepsilon$:

$$p^{(t+1)} = \exp_{p^{(t)}}\!\left( \frac{\varepsilon}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{p^{(t)}}(x_i) \right)$$

Initialize $p^{(0)}$ as the normalized Euclidean mean. Iterate until $\Vert p^{(t+1)} - p^{(t)} \Vert < \tau$.

**(b) Covariance Matrix and VE(k):**

Mean in tangent space is zero (by optimality of $\mu$): $\bar{v} = \frac{1}{n}\sum v_i = 0$.

Covariance:

$$S = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i v_i^{\top} \quad \text{with eigendecomposition} \quad S W = W \Lambda$$

Variance explained:

$$\text{VE}(k) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{D} \lambda_j}$$

**Applications:** (1) Diffusion Tensor Imaging in neuroimaging on $\mathcal{P}_3$. (2) Human pose clustering on $SO(3)$ for gait analysis.

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B — Internal Choice Option 2

**Q.B.** **(a)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, describe the complete **five-stage pipeline of Tangent-Based PCA**. Identify the manifold type and metric used in two engineering case studies. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A dataset of $n = 5$ points on the unit sphere $S^{2}$ has Fréchet mean $\mu = (0,0,1)^{\top}$ and tangent vectors $v_1 = (2,1,0)^{\top}, v_2 = (1,2,0)^{\top}, v_3 = (0,0,0)^{\top}, v_4 = (-1,1,0)^{\top}, v_5 = (-2,-4,0)^{\top}$. Compute the covariance matrix $S$ and find its eigenvalues. What is $\text{VE}(1)$? **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Five-Stage Pipeline:**

1. **Manifold & Metric Specification** — choose $\mathcal{M}$ (e.g., $S^2$, $\mathcal{P}_n$, $\text{Grass}(k,n)$) and metric $g$.
2. **Intrinsic Mean Computation** — Karcher algorithm.
3. **Logarithmic Map Projection** — $v_i = \log_{\mu}(x_i)$.
4. **Tangent-Space PCA** — Build $S$, eigendecompose.
5. **Variance-Explained Analysis** — Select top-$K$ components.

**Case Study 1:** Brain imaging — manifold $\mathcal{P}_3$ of SPD matrices with the **Affine-Invariant Riemannian Metric (AIRM)**.
**Case Study 2:** Rotation averaging — manifold $SO(3)$ with the **bi-invariant metric** induced by $\langle u, v \rangle = \text{tr}(u^{\top}v)$.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum v_i &= (2+1+0-1-2,\ 1+2+0+1-4,\ 0)^{\top} = (0,0,0)^{\top} \\
\text{(Confirms } \bar{v} = 0\text{)} \\
S &= \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{5} v_i v_i^{\top}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing outer products:

$$\sum v_i v_i^{\top} = \begin{pmatrix} 4+1+0+1+4 & 2+2+0-1+8 & 0 \\ 2+2+0-1+8 & 1+4+0+1+16 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 10 & 11 & 0 \\ 11 & 22 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S = \frac{1}{4}\begin{pmatrix} 10 & 11 & 0 \\ 11 & 22 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 & 2.75 & 0 \\ 2.75 & 5.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

For the 2×2 block, characteristic equation: $\lambda^{2} - 8\lambda + (13.75 - 7.5625) = \lambda^{2} - 8\lambda + 6.1875 = 0$.

$$\lambda = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{64 - 24.75}}{2} = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{39.25}}{2} = \frac{8 \pm 6.265}{2}$$

Thus $\lambda_1 \approx 7.133$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 0.867$. The third eigenvalue is $0$.

$$\text{VE}(1) = \frac{7.133}{7.133 + 0.867 + 0} = \frac{7.133}{8.0} \approx 0.892$$

The first principal component captures **89.2 %** of intrinsic variance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Pattern)**
> 
> - ❌ **Confusing Euclidean mean with Fréchet mean** — Students often write $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ *without normalization*. On $S^2$ this fails. Always normalize: $\mu \propto \sum x_i$.
> - ❌ **Forgetting the zero-mean property** — The tangent vectors satisfy $\sum v_i = 0$ by construction. Failing to state this loses 1 mark in part (b).
> - ❌ **Using standard PCA covariance formula** with denominator $n$ instead of $n-1$ — KTU uses **unbiased estimator**, hence $\frac{1}{n-1}$.
> - ❌ **Omitting units of $\Lambda$** — Eigenvalues carry units of **squared geodesic distance** (rad²), not raw variance. State this explicitly.
> - ❌ **Not citing the manifold in applications** — For 7-mark questions, simply saying "medical imaging" loses marks. Specify $\mathcal{P}_3$ with AIRM.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Tangent PCA = Fréchet Mean + Log-Map Projection + Standard PCA**.
- 📌 The **intrinsic Fréchet mean** is the unique point minimizing $\sum d_{\mathcal{M}}^{2}(p, x_i)$.
- 📌 The **logarithmic map** $\log_{\mu}: \mathcal{M} \rightarrow T_{\mu}\mathcal{M}$ is the *key bridge* converting manifold data to vector data.
- 📌 The **tangent-space covariance** is $S = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum v_i v_i^{\top}$ with $\bar{v} = 0$.
- 📌 **Variance Explained Ratio** $\text{VE}(k) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{D} \lambda_j}$ — Choose $K$ such that $\text{VE}(K) \geq 0.95$.
- 📌 **Manifold matters**: Sphere $S^2$ → spherical metric; SPD matrices → AIRM; Rotations → $SO(3)$ bi-invariant metric.
- 📌 **Karcher gradient descent** is the iterative algorithm for $\mu$ — requires a *unique geodesic* assumption (small data spread).
- 📌 Tangent PCA is the **building block** of more advanced methods like **Diffusion Maps**, **Tangent Space Intrinsic Mean (TIM)**, and **Riemannian Manifold Clustering**.
- 📌 For KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw the block diagram** — it carries 2–3 easy marks.
- 📌 Eigenvalues of $S$ represent **intrinsic geodesic variances**, not Euclidean variances.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R Packages for Decision Tree Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** R Packages for Decision Tree Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Tree Classification in R** refers to the implementation of supervised machine learning algorithms using specialized R packages that recursively partition a dataset into homogeneous subsets based on feature values, producing a tree-like predictive model. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT756), students are expected to master packages such as **`rpart`**, **`party`**, **`C50`**, and **`tree`** — each offering distinct algorithmic implementations (CART, Conditional Inference Trees, C5.0, and traditional recursive partitioning respectively).

A **Decision Tree Classifier** is a non-parametric supervised learning method used for both classification and regression tasks. For classification, it predicts categorical class labels by learning simple decision rules inferred from the data features. The model is represented as a **binary (or multi-way) tree** where:
- **Internal Nodes** → represent a test on a feature (e.g., `Age < 30`)
- **Branches** → represent the outcome of the test
- **Leaf Nodes (Terminal Nodes)** → represent the class label

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The 20-Questions Game"**
> 
> Imagine you are a doctor diagnosing a patient. You don't run every test at once. Instead, you ask a series of **Yes/No questions** in a strategic order:
> 1. *Does the patient have a fever?* → Yes / No
> 2. *If Yes, is the fever above 101°F?* → Yes / No
> 3. *If Yes, is there a rash?* → Yes / No
> 
> Each question **splits the possibilities**, and you follow a path down a flowchart until you reach a **diagnosis** (a leaf). A Decision Tree in R works exactly this way — it asks the most informative questions first (highest information gain), splitting the data until a confident classification is reached.

## 1.3 Key R Packages — A First Look

| Package | Primary Algorithm | Signature Function | Key Strength |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **`rpart`** | CART (Classification & Regression Trees) | `rpart()` | Industry standard, handles both classification & regression |
| **`party`** | Conditional Inference Trees (ctree) | `ctree()` | Unbiased variable selection, statistical stopping |
| **`C50`** | C5.0 Algorithm (Quinlan) | `C5.0()` | Handles boosting, winnowing, high accuracy |
| **`tree`** | Traditional CART variant | `tree()` | Simple, academic-friendly implementation |
| **`rpart.plot`** | Visualization companion | `rpart.plot()` | Publication-quality tree diagrams |
| **`caret`** | ML Workflow Wrapper | `train()` | Cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEEBT756 explicitly emphasizes hands-on implementation using **`rpart`** and **`party`** packages. Students are expected to load the `mlbench` or built-in datasets, build the model, plot the tree, and evaluate it using a **confusion matrix**.

## 1.4 Why Use Decision Trees in R?

> [!NOTE]
> - **Interpretability** — Trees can be visualized and explained to non-technical stakeholders.
> - **Minimal Data Preprocessing** — No need for feature scaling or one-hot encoding.
> - **Handles Both Numerical and Categorical Data** natively.
> - **Non-linear Relationships** are captured without requiring polynomial terms.
> - **Feature Importance** is implicitly computed (used at the root and upper splits).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of a Decision Tree

The end-to-end workflow in R can be broken down into **six logical stages**:

1. **Data Loading & Inspection** — Read CSV into a `data.frame`; check `str()`, `summary()`, `head()`.
2. **Train-Test Split** — Typically 70% training / 30% testing using `sample()` or `createDataPartition()`.
3. **Model Fitting** — Call `rpart(formula, data, method = "class")` to train a classifier.
4. **Tree Pruning (Optional)** — Use `printcp()` to find the optimal complexity parameter (`cp`).
5. **Prediction** — Apply `predict(model, testdata, type = "class")` to generate labels.
6. **Evaluation** — Build a confusion matrix using `table()` or `confusionMatrix()` from `caret`.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Splitting Criteria:** At every node, the algorithm must decide *which feature* and *which threshold* produces the *purest* child nodes. "Purity" is measured by mathematical metrics: **Gini Index** (used by CART/`rpart`) and **Entropy / Information Gain** (used by ID3, C4.5, C5.0).

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Three Core Metrics

### A. Gini Impurity (Default in `rpart`)

$$Gini(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} [p(i \mid t)]^2$$

Where:
- $t$ = the current node
- $C$ = total number of classes
- $p(i \mid t)$ = proportion of class $i$ samples in node $t$

**Interpretation:** $Gini = 0$ means *perfect purity* (all samples belong to one class). $Gini$ is maximized when classes are perfectly mixed (e.g., for binary: $Gini_{max} = 0.5$).

### B. Entropy (Information Theory)

$$Entropy(t) = - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p(i \mid t) \cdot \log_2 p(i \mid t)$$

**Interpretation:** $Entropy = 0$ means pure node; $Entropy = 1$ means maximum disorder (for binary classes).

### C. Information Gain (IG)

$$IG = Entropy(Parent) - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{N_j}{N} \cdot Entropy(Child_j)$$

The algorithm selects the split that **maximizes** the Information Gain (equivalently, minimizes weighted child impurity).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | R Implementation | Default Value |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Gini Impurity** | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ | `rpart()` uses Gini by default | Range: $[0, 0.5]$ binary |
| **Entropy** | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | `rpart(..., parms = list(split = "information"))` | Range: $[0, 1]$ binary |
| **Information Gain** | $H_{parent} - \sum w_j H_{child_j}$ | Computed internally | Higher = better split |
| **Classification Error** | $1 - \max_i(p_i)$ | Used in `rpart` pruning (`xerror`) | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | `mean(predicted == actual)` | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | `posPredValue()` from `caret` | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | `sensitivity()` from `caret` | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | `F1_Score()` from `MLmetrics` | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| **Complexity Parameter (cp)** | Threshold for cost-complexity pruning | `rpart.control(cp = ...)` | Default: **0.01** |
| **Gini Reduction** | $Gini(parent) - \sum w_j Gini(child_j)$ | Computed at each split | Higher = better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Decision Trees form the backbone of **Ensemble Methods** (Random Forests, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting) used in:
> - **Credit Scoring** (Banking & Fintech)
> - **Medical Diagnosis** (Healthcare AI)
> - **Customer Churn Prediction** (Telecom/CRM)
> - **Fault Detection** (Industrial IoT)
> - **Fraud Detection** (Insurance)

## 2.4 Key Parameters of `rpart()` — The KTU Favourite

```r
rpart(formula, data, method, control)
```

| Argument | Description | Default |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `formula` | `y ~ x1 + x2 + ...` | Required |
| `data` | Training data frame | Required |
| `method` | `"class"`, `"anova"`, `"poisson"` | `"class"` for classification |
| `control` | `rpart.control(minsplit, minbucket, maxdepth, cp, xval)` | Various |
| `parms` | `list(split = "gini" or "information")` | `"gini"` |

> [!TIP]
> **`minsplit`**: Minimum number of observations required to attempt a split. Default = **20**. Increasing this prevents overfitting.
> 
> **`maxdepth`**: Maximum depth of any node of the final tree. Default = **30**. Lower values create simpler trees.
> 
> **`cp` (complexity parameter)**: The minimum improvement in the model needed at each node. Default = **0.01**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Gini Impurity Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A node contains **10 samples** belonging to **3 classes**: Class A (6), Class B (3), Class C (1). Compute the Gini impurity.

### Step 1: Compute Class Probabilities

$$
p(A) = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6, \quad p(B) = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3, \quad p(C) = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1
$$

### Step 2: Square Each Probability

$$
p(A)^2 = 0.36, \quad p(B)^2 = 0.09, \quad p(C)^2 = 0.01
$$

### Step 3: Sum the Squared Probabilities

$$
\sum_{i} p(i)^2 = 0.36 + 0.09 + 0.01 = 0.46
$$

### Step 4: Subtract from 1

$$
Gini(t) = 1 - 0.46 = 0.54
$$

**Interpretation:** A Gini of $0.54$ indicates moderate impurity. The algorithm will look for splits that reduce this value the most.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Entropy & Information Gain

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A parent node has **10 samples** — 6 belonging to Class A and 4 to Class B. We evaluate a candidate split that produces two child nodes:
> - **Left Child:** 5 samples (5 A, 0 B) → Pure
> - **Right Child:** 5 samples (1 A, 4 B) → Impure

### Step 1: Compute Parent Entropy

$$
H(Parent) = - \left[ 0.6 \log_2 0.6 + 0.4 \log_2 0.4 \right]
$$

$$
H(Parent) = - \left[ 0.6 \times (-0.737) + 0.4 \times (-1.322) \right]
$$

$$
H(Parent) = - \left[ -0.4422 - 0.5288 \right] = 0.971 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Left Child Entropy (Pure)

$$
H(Left) = - \left[ 1 \cdot \log_2 1 + 0 \cdot \log_2 0 \right]
$$

By convention, $0 \log_2 0 = 0$, so:

$$
H(Left) = 0 \text{ bits (pure node)}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Right Child Entropy

$$
H(Right) = - \left[ 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.8 \log_2 0.8 \right]
$$

$$
H(Right) = - \left[ 0.2 \times (-2.322) + 0.8 \times (-0.322) \right]
$$

$$
H(Right) = - \left[ -0.4644 - 0.2575 \right] = 0.722 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Weighted Average Child Entropy

$$
H_{children} = \frac{5}{10} \cdot 0 + \frac{5}{10} \cdot 0.722 = 0.361 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Information Gain

$$
IG = H(Parent) - H_{children} = 0.971 - 0.361 = 0.610 \text{ bits}
$$

**Decision:** Since $IG = 0.610 > 0$, this split is **informative** and will be considered by the algorithm.

---

## 3.3 Full R Implementation Walkthrough (KTU Lab Standard)

```r
# ===== STEP 1: Install and Load Required Packages =====
install.packages(c("rpart", "rpart.plot", "caret", "party"))
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(caret)
library(party)

# ===== STEP 2: Load and Inspect the Built-in `iris` Dataset =====
data(iris)
str(iris)
head(iris, 5)
summary(iris)

# ===== STEP 3: Train-Test Split (70% / 30%) =====
set.seed(123)  # For reproducibility
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(iris), 0.7 * nrow(iris))
train_data  <- iris[train_index, ]
test_data   <- iris[-train_index, ]

# ===== STEP 4: Build the Decision Tree Model =====
tree_model <- rpart(
  formula  = Species ~ .,
  data     = train_data,
  method   = "class",
  control  = rpart.control(minsplit = 10, maxdepth = 5, cp = 0.01)
)

# ===== STEP 5: Print Model Summary & Complexity Parameter =====
print(tree_model)
printcp(tree_model)        # Shows cp table for pruning
plotcp(tree_model)         # Visualizes cp vs. cross-validated error

# ===== STEP 6: Visualize the Tree =====
rpart.plot(tree_model,
           type    = 2,        # Labels at ends
           extra   = 104,      # Show probability & percentage
           under   = TRUE,
           fallen.leaves = TRUE,
           main    = "Decision Tree - Iris Classification")

# ===== STEP 7: Make Predictions on Test Data =====
predictions <- predict(tree_model, test_data, type = "class")

# ===== STEP 8: Evaluate Using Confusion Matrix =====
conf_matrix <- table(Actual = test_data$Species, Predicted = predictions)
print(conf_matrix)

accuracy <- sum(diag(conf_matrix)) / sum(conf_matrix)
cat("Model Accuracy:", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Output Interpretation:**
> - `printcp()` returns a table with columns: `CP`, `nsplit`, `rel error`, `xerror`, `xstd`. The row with the **minimum `xerror`** gives the optimal `cp` for pruning.
> - `type = "class"` in `predict()` returns hard class labels. Use `type = "prob"` to get class probabilities.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Splitting Criterion

| Criterion | Used By | Bias | Computation Cost | Best For |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Gini** | CART, `rpart` | Favors larger partitions | Fast (no log) | General classification |
| **Information Gain** | ID3, C4.5, C5.0 | Biased toward multi-valued features | Slower (log) | Datasets with categorical features |
| **Gain Ratio** | C4.5, C5.0 | Normalizes IG | Moderate | Corrects IG bias |
| **χ² (Chi-Square)** | CHAID | Statistical significance | Moderate | Categorical data |
| **Gini (binary)** | Conditional Inference | Unbiased | Moderate | Mixed-type data |

---

## 3.5 Confusion Matrix Mechanics

> [!NOTE]
> For a binary classification problem, the confusion matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\[6pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\[6pt]
\text{Recall (Sensitivity)} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\[6pt]
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \\[6pt]
\text{F1-Score} &= 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:** $TP$ = True Positive, $TN$ = True Negative, $FP$ = False Positive, $FN$ = False Negative.

**Numerical Example (from `iris` test set, suppose):**
- Suppose `setosa` class has: $TP = 15$, $FP = 0$, $FN = 1$, $TN = 29$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{15}{15 + 0} = 1.000
$$

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{15}{15 + 1} = 0.938
$$

$$
\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \frac{1.000 \times 0.938}{1.000 + 0.938} = 0.968
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End R Decision Tree Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Dataset] --> B[Inspect Data str head summary]
    B --> C[Train-Test Split 70:30]
    C --> D[Build rpart Model method class]
    D --> E[Examine printcp tablecp]
    E --> F{Choose cp value}
    F -->|Optimal cp| G[Prune Tree prune function]
    F -->|Default cp| H[Keep Original Tree]
    G --> I[predict on test data type class]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Build Confusion Matrix table function]
    J --> K[Compute Accuracy Precision Recall F1]
    K --> L[Visualize rpart.plot]
    L --> M[End: Model Deployed]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style J fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Tree Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Root Node: All Samples] --> N2[Split on Feature X1]
    N1 --> N3[Split on Feature X2]
    N2 --> N4[Internal Node: Subset 1]
    N2 --> N5[Internal Node: Subset 2]
    N3 --> N6[Internal Node: Subset 3]
    N3 --> N7[Internal Node: Subset 4]
    N4 --> L1[Leaf: Class A]
    N4 --> L2[Leaf: Class B]
    N5 --> L3[Leaf: Class C]
    N5 --> L4[Leaf: Class A]
    N6 --> L5[Leaf: Class B]
    N7 --> L6[Leaf: Class C]

    style N1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style N2 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#283593
    style N3 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#283593
    style N4 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style N5 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style N6 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style N7 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style L1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L3 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L4 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L5 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L6 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 Splitting Criterion Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Dataset] --> B{Data Type?}
    B -->|Numerical + Categorical| C{Algorithm?}
    B -->|Purely Categorical| D[Use C50 or ctree]
    C -->|CART| E[rpart with Gini]
    C -->|C5.0| F[C50 with Information Gain]
    C -->|Unbiased| G[party ctree with chi-square]
    E --> H[Minimize Gini]
    F --> I[Maximize IG or Gain Ratio]
    G --> J[Test Statistical Independence]
    H --> K[Select Best Split]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Recurse on Children]
    L --> M{Stopping Criteria Met?}
    M -->|No| C
    M -->|Yes| N[Assign Class Label to Leaf]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Tree (Iris-like Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
