<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q1.** Define the **Gini Index** and write the formula used for computing it in a binary classification decision tree.

**Model Answer:**
> The Gini Index is a measure of node impurity used by the CART algorithm (default in R's `rpart` package). It quantifies the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen element if it were randomly labeled according to the class distribution in the node.
>
> **Formula:** $Gini(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} [p(i \mid t)]^2$
>
> For a binary classification with equal class distribution (50-50), $Gini = 0.5$ (worst case). For a pure node, $Gini = 0$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List any **three R packages** used for decision tree classification and state one distinguishing feature of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Package | Distinguishing Feature |
|:---|:---|
| **`rpart`** | Implements CART algorithm; handles both classification and regression |
| **`party`** | Implements Conditional Inference Trees with unbiased variable selection |
| **`C50`** | Implements Quinlan's C5.0 algorithm; supports boosting and winnowing |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Working with `rpart` on the `iris` Dataset*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Gini Index** and **Information Gain** metrics used for decision tree splitting. Derive the Gini impurity for a node containing 8 samples (Class A: 4, Class B: 2, Class C: 2). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write the complete R code using the **`rpart`** package to: (i) Split the `iris` dataset into 70% training and 30% testing sets. (ii) Build a classification tree to predict `Species`. (iii) Plot the tree using `rpart.plot()`. (iv) Display the confusion matrix and compute the accuracy. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the Metrics**

> **Gini Index** measures the impurity of a node. Lower Gini ⇒ purer node. The CART algorithm chooses the split that produces child nodes with the **minimum weighted Gini**.
>
> **Formula:**
>
> $$Gini(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} [p(i \mid t)]^2$$
>
> **Information Gain (IG)** is the reduction in entropy achieved by splitting a node. Higher IG ⇒ better split.
>
> **Formula:**
>
> $$IG = H(Parent) - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{N_j}{N} \cdot H(Child_j)$$

**Step 2: Compute Class Probabilities**

For 8 samples: $p(A) = 4/8 = 0.5$, $p(B) = 2/8 = 0.25$, $p(C) = 2/8 = 0.25$.

**Step 3: Square Each Probability**

$$
p(A)^2 = 0.25, \quad p(B)^2 = 0.0625, \quad p(C)^2 = 0.0625
$$

**Step 4: Sum and Subtract from 1**

$$
\sum p_i^2 = 0.25 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 = 0.375
$$

$$
Gini = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625
$$

**Conclusion:** A Gini impurity of **0.625** (theoretical max for 3 equally distributed classes is $1 - 1/3 \approx 0.667$) indicates high impurity. The algorithm will seek a split that reduces this value.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Complete R Code:**

```r
# Install and load packages
install.packages(c("rpart", "rpart.plot"))
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

# Load the dataset
data(iris)

# (i) Train-Test Split
set.seed(42)
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(iris), 0.7 * nrow(iris))
train_data  <- iris[train_index, ]
test_data   <- iris[-train_index, ]

# (ii) Build the classification tree
tree_model <- rpart(
  formula = Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length + Petal.Width,
  data    = train_data,
  method  = "class",
  control = rpart.control(minsplit = 5, maxdepth = 4, cp = 0.01)
)

# (iii) Plot the tree
rpart.plot(tree_model,
           type           = 2,
           extra          = 104,
           fallen.leaves  = TRUE,
           main           = "Iris Decision Tree (rpart)")

# (iv) Confusion Matrix and Accuracy
predictions <- predict(tree_model, test_data, type = "class")
conf_matrix <- table(Actual = test_data$Species, Predicted = predictions)
print(conf_matrix)

accuracy <- sum(diag(conf_matrix)) / sum(conf_matrix)
cat("Accuracy =", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**
- `set.seed(42)` ensures **reproducibility** of the random split.
- `method = "class"` specifies that the target variable is categorical.
- `rpart.plot()` produces a publication-quality visualization with class probabilities at each leaf.
- The **diagonal elements** of the confusion matrix represent correct predictions; off-diagonal elements are misclassifications.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative — Using `party::ctree()` & Performance Metrics*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the **CART** (used by `rpart`) and **Conditional Inference Tree** (used by `party::ctree()`) algorithms. Highlight **three differences**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the built-in **`mtcars`** dataset, write R code to: (i) Convert `am` (transmission) into a factor variable. (ii) Fit a Conditional Inference Tree predicting `am` from `mpg`, `hp`, and `wt`. (iii) Plot the tree using `plot()`. (iv) Compute the **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** using the `caret` package. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | CART (`rpart`) | Conditional Inference Tree (`ctree`) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Splitting Criterion** | Gini Index / Information Gain | Statistical permutation test (p-value based) |
| **Variable Selection Bias** | Biased toward variables with many split points | Unbiased (uses significance testing) |
| **Stopping Rule** | Heuristic (minsplit, maxdepth) | Based on formal hypothesis testing (alpha = 0.05) |
| **Handling of NAs** | Uses surrogate splits | Drops NAs internally |

**Three Key Differences:**

1. **Bias Correction:** CART tends to favor features with more unique values; `ctree` corrects this bias using statistical tests.
2. **Pruning:** CART requires post-pruning via `cp`; `ctree` uses an in-built significance threshold.
3. **Inference:** `ctree` provides p-values for each split, making the model more statistically interpretable.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

```r
# Install and load packages
install.packages(c("party", "caret"))
library(party)
library(caret)

# Load the dataset
data(mtcars)

# (i) Convert 'am' to factor
mtcars$am <- as.factor(mtcars$am)
levels(mtcars$am) <- c("Automatic", "Manual")

# (ii) Fit Conditional Inference Tree
ctree_model <- ctree(
  formula = am ~ mpg + hp + wt,
  data    = mtcars,
  controls = ctree_control(maxdepth = 4)
)

# (iii) Plot the tree
plot(ctree_model, main = "Conditional Inference Tree - mtcars")

# (iv) Compute Precision, Recall, F1-Score
predictions <- predict(ctree_model)
conf_matrix <- confusionMatrix(predictions, mtcars$am, positive = "Manual")

# Extract metrics
cat("Precision :", conf_matrix$byClass["Pos Pred Value"], "\n")
cat("Recall    :", conf_matrix$byClass["Sensitivity"], "\n")
cat("F1-Score  :", conf_matrix$byClass["F1"], "\n")
cat("Accuracy  :", conf_matrix$overall["Accuracy"], "\n")
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `method = "class"`** in `rpart()` — The model defaults to regression (`method = "anova"`), producing nonsensical continuous predictions. **(−2 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Not setting `set.seed()`** before `sample()` — Results become non-reproducible; examiner's output will differ from yours. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Confusing `type = "class"` vs `type = "prob"`** in `predict()` — Using `"prob"` returns a probability matrix that cannot be directly compared with actual labels in a confusion matrix. **(−2 marks)**
> 
> 4. **Misinterpreting `printcp()` table** — Students often select the split with **lowest `rel error`**, but the correct choice is the split with the **lowest `xerror`** (cross-validated error). **(−3 marks)**
> 
> 5. **Not installing `rpart.plot`** and trying to use base `plot()` without proper type arguments — Produces a near-empty graph. **(−1 mark)**
> 
> 6. **Mixing up Gini and Entropy formulas** — They look similar but use different log bases ($Gini$ has no log, $Entropy$ uses $\log_2$). **(−2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **`rpart`** package implements the **CART algorithm** using **Gini Index** by default for classification.
- ✅ The **`party`** package provides **Conditional Inference Trees** via `ctree()` — unbiased and statistically rigorous.
- ✅ The **`C50`** package implements the **C5.0 algorithm** — uses Information Gain / Gain Ratio and supports **boosting**.
- ✅ Always set `method = "class"` in `rpart()` for classification tasks.
- ✅ **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$; **Entropy** = $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$.
- ✅ **Information Gain** = reduction in entropy after a split. Higher = better.
- ✅ **Pruning** uses the **complexity parameter (cp)** — find the cp that minimizes **cross-validated error (`xerror`)**.
- ✅ Use `set.seed()` for **reproducibility** of train-test splits.
- ✅ **`type = "class"`** in `predict()` returns discrete labels; **`type = "prob"`** returns class probabilities.
- ✅ **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Confusion Matrix.
- ✅ **Key control parameters:** `minsplit` (default 20), `maxdepth` (default 30), `cp` (default 0.01), `xval` (default 10).
- ✅ **Visualization:** `rpart.plot()` is the gold standard for tree plots; `plot()` + `text()` is the basic alternative.
- ✅ Decision Trees form the **building blocks of Random Forests and Gradient Boosting** — mastering them is essential for advanced ML.
- ✅ **`caret::confusionMatrix()`** provides the most comprehensive evaluation (Accuracy, Kappa, Sensitivity, Specificity, F1, etc.).
- ✅ Always check for **class imbalance** before interpreting accuracy — use Precision/Recall/F1 for imbalanced datasets.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Forest in R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Random Forest in R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Forest** is a supervised **ensemble learning algorithm** that operates by constructing a multitude of **decision trees** during training and outputting the class that is the **mode of the classes** (classification) or **mean prediction** (regression) of the individual trees. It corrects the habit of decision trees of overfitting to their training set, originally proposed by **Leo Breiman (2001)**.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the course **PEEBT756 - Advanced Computer Programming Techniques**, Random Forest in R is studied as a powerful **non-parametric, bagging-based ensemble technique** implemented using the `randomForest` package developed by **Andy Liaw and Matthew Wiener (2002)**.

Key terminology that MUST be known for KTU exams:
- **Ensemble Method** – Combines multiple models to produce one optimal predictive model.
- **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)** – Training each tree on a random sample of the data drawn with replacement.
- **Bootstrap Sample** – A sample drawn with replacement, of the same size as the original dataset.
- **Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error** – Internal cross-validation estimate using samples not included in the bootstrap.
- **Variable Importance** – A measure of how much each predictor variable contributes to model accuracy.
- **mtry** – The number of variables randomly sampled as candidates at each split.
- **ntree** – The total number of trees to grow in the forest.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **medical diagnosis scenario** where a single doctor might make a misdiagnosis due to personal bias or limited experience. To improve accuracy, the hospital convenes a **panel of 100 doctors**, each looking at slightly different medical records (bootstrap samples) and each allowed to ask only a **random subset of questions** (feature randomness) about the patient's symptoms. The final diagnosis is made by **majority voting** of all 100 doctors. This is exactly how a Random Forest works:

- Each **doctor** = One **decision tree**
- **Different medical records** = **Bootstrap samples** of the training data
- **Random subset of questions** = **Random feature selection** at each split
- **Majority vote** = **Aggregation** of predictions

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** No single decision tree sees the full data or all features, so each tree makes different errors. When their independent errors are combined through voting/averaging, the collective wisdom **cancels out individual mistakes**, leading to a much more robust and accurate model.

## 1.3 When to Use Random Forest (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight – Random Forest is the GO-TO algorithm for:**
> 1. **Classification problems** (e.g., spam detection, disease prediction, customer churn).
> 2. **Regression problems** (e.g., house price prediction, sales forecasting).
> 3. **Feature selection** – Uses variable importance scores to rank predictors.
> 4. **Handling missing values** – Through proximity measures and imputation.
> 5. **High-dimensional data** – Works well even when features >> observations.

## 1.4 Comparison: Decision Tree vs Random Forest

| Property | Decision Tree | Random Forest |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Model Type** | Single tree | Ensemble of trees |
| **Overfitting** | High risk | Greatly reduced |
| **Variance** | High | Low |
| **Bias** | Low | Slightly higher |
| **Interpretability** | High (white box) | Moderate (black box) |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | Slower (parallelizable) |
| **Prediction Speed** | Very fast | Moderate |
| **Stability** | Unstable (sensitive to data) | Very stable |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Algorithm – Operational Logic Steps

The Random Forest algorithm follows a strict pipeline. For KTU, you must be able to write these steps in any descriptive question.

**Step 1 – Bootstrap Sampling (Bagging):**
From the original training dataset of size $N$, draw $B$ bootstrap samples, each of size $N$ (with replacement). Approximately **63.2%** of the original observations appear in any single bootstrap sample; the remaining **36.8%** form the Out-of-Bag (OOB) set.

**Step 2 – Train an Unpruned Decision Tree per Sample:**
For each bootstrap sample, grow a decision tree, but at each node, instead of choosing the best split from **all** $p$ predictors, randomly select a subset of $m$ predictors (where $m \approx \sqrt{p}$ for classification and $m \approx p/3$ for regression – default in R's `randomForest`).

**Step 3 – Recursive Splitting Using Impurity Measures:**
Each candidate split is evaluated using **Gini Impurity** (default in R) or **Entropy/Information Gain**.

**Step 4 – Full Growth Without Pruning:**
Each tree is grown to its **maximum depth** (no pruning). The diversity among trees provides regularization.

**Step 5 – Aggregation (Voting / Averaging):**
- For **Classification**: Final prediction = **majority vote** across all $B$ trees.
- For **Regression**: Final prediction = **average** of all $B$ tree predictions.

**Step 6 – OOB Error Estimation:**
For each observation $i$, predict its class using only the trees for which $i$ was OOB. Aggregate these predictions and compare to the true label to compute the **OOB error** – a free, unbiased estimate of test error.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why bootstrap?** Creates diverse training subsets so trees learn different patterns.
- **Why random feature subsets?** Decorrelates the trees; if one feature is very strong, all trees would use it at the top, making them similar. Randomness forces diversity.
- **Why no pruning?** Bagging + feature randomness already control overfitting. Pruning would reduce the strength of individual trees.
- **Why voting?** The **Law of Large Numbers** – as $B \to \infty$, the ensemble error converges to a limit.
- **Why OOB error?** Eliminates the need for a separate cross-validation set, saving data and computation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 2024 Scheme exams. Memorize them.

| Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|--------------|----------|-------------|
| **Gini Impurity** | $G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$ | Measures node impurity; $p_i$ = proportion of class $i$ in node $t$ |
| **Entropy** | $H(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Alternative impurity measure; higher = more impure |
| **Information Gain** | $IG = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{j} \frac{N_j}{N} H(\text{child}_j)$ | Reduction in entropy from a split |
| **OOB Error Rate** | $E_{OOB} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i^{OOB} \neq y_i)$ | Fraction of OOB misclassifications |
| **Default mtry (Class.)** | $m = \lfloor \sqrt{p} \rfloor$ | Number of predictors tried at each split (classification) |
| **Default mtry (Regr.)** | $m = \lfloor p/3 \rfloor$ | Number of predictors tried at each split (regression) |
| **Bootstrap Coverage** | $1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N \approx 0.632$ | Probability an observation is in a bootstrap sample |
| **Mean Decrease Accuracy (MDA)** | $VIM_j^{(MDA)} = \frac{1}{ntree} \sum_{t=1}^{ntree} (E_{OOB}^{(t)} - E_{OOB,\pi_j}^{(t)})$ | Variable importance via OOB permutation |
| **Mean Decrease Gini (MDG)** | $VIM_j^{(MDG)} = \sum_{t=1}^{ntree} \sum_{n \in \text{splits on } j} \Delta Gini$ | Variable importance via total Gini reduction |
| **Ensemble Prediction (Class.)** | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \ldots, \hat{y}_B\}$ | Majority vote across $B$ trees |
| **Ensemble Prediction (Regr.)** | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{y}_b$ | Mean of $B$ tree predictions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important – R's `randomForest` package uses Gini impurity by default for classification trees.** If you need entropy, you must set `parms = list(split = "information")` or use the `cforest` function from the `party` package.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics & Medical Diagnosis** – Cancer classification, gene expression analysis.
- **Credit Scoring & Banking** – Predicting loan default, fraud detection.
- **Remote Sensing** – Land cover classification from satellite imagery.
- **Cybersecurity** – Intrusion detection systems.
- **Marketing Analytics** – Customer segmentation, churn prediction.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control** – Defect detection in production lines.
- **IoT & Sensor Networks** – Anomaly detection in time-series data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why ~63.2% Bootstrap Coverage?

This is a **classic KTU exam derivation question**. We derive the expected fraction of unique observations in a bootstrap sample of size $N$ drawn with replacement.

**Step 1 – Probability an observation is NOT selected in a single draw:**

$$P(\text{not picked in 1 draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{N}$$

**Step 2 – Probability it is not picked in any of the $N$ independent draws:**

$$P(\text{not picked in N draws}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N}$$

**Step 3 – Therefore, probability it IS picked (at least once):**

$$P(\text{picked}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N}$$

**Step 4 – Take the limit as $N \to \infty$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{N} &= e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Final coverage probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{in bootstrap}) &= 1 - e^{-1} \\
&\approx 1 - 0.3679 \\
&\approx 0.6321
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Approximately **63.2%** of the unique observations appear in a bootstrap sample, and the remaining **36.8%** form the OOB set. This is why OOB error is a valid cross-validation estimate without holding out extra data.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gini Impurity for a Binary Split

Suppose a node $t$ contains samples from two classes with proportions $p$ and $1-p$.

**Step 1 – Write the Gini impurity formula:**

$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2 = 1 - p^2 - (1-p)^2$$

**Step 2 – Expand the squared terms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(t) &= 1 - p^2 - (1 - 2p + p^2) \\
&= 1 - p^2 - 1 + 2p - p^2 \\
&= 2p - 2p^2 \\
&= 2p(1 - p)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute the Gini Gain for a candidate split:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G &= G(\text{parent}) - \left[\frac{N_L}{N} G(t_L) + \frac{N_R}{N} G(t_R)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – The split that maximizes $\Delta G$ is chosen at each node.** This is what R's `randomForest` does internally for every candidate split across the $mtry$ randomly selected features.

## 3.3 Derivation: OOB Error as an Unbiased Test Error Estimate

**Step 1 – For each tree $T_b$, define the OOB set $OOB_b$** (the ~36.8% of data not in its bootstrap).

**Step 2 – For each observation $x_i$ in the training set, find the set of trees $S_i = \{T_b : x_i \in OOB_b\}$.**

**Step 3 – The OOB prediction for $x_i$ is the majority vote (or mean) of predictions from trees in $S_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_i^{OOB} &= \text{mode}\{\hat{y}_b(x_i) : T_b \in S_i\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – The OOB error rate is:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{OOB} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\left(\hat{y}_i^{OOB} \neq y_i\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – As $ntree \to \infty$, the OOB prediction for $x_i$ approaches the prediction of a Random Forest trained on the full data minus $x_i$** — a leave-one-out style estimate. This makes OOB error a near-unbiased estimate of the generalization error.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step R Implementation

Below is the **complete, exam-ready R implementation pipeline** for Random Forest. This is what you should reproduce in any KTU practical or theory question that asks for R code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Always split your dataset using `set.seed()` for reproducibility. KTU evaluators explicitly check for this.

### 3.4.1 Step 1 – Install and Load Required Packages

```r
# Install the randomForest package (run once)
install.packages("randomForest")

# Load the package
library(randomForest)
```

**Explanation:** The `randomForest` package is the de-facto standard implementation in R. It is based on Breiman and Cutler's original Fortran code, ported to R by Liaw and Wiener.

### 3.4.2 Step 2 – Load and Explore the Dataset

```r
# Load the built-in iris dataset
data(iris)

# Explore structure
str(iris)
head(iris)
summary(iris)

# Check dimensions
dim(iris)   # 150 rows, 5 columns
```

**Explanation:** `iris` is a multivariate dataset with 150 flower samples classified into 3 species (*setosa*, *versicolor*, *virginica*) using 4 features (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width). It is the most commonly used KTU demonstration dataset.

### 3.4.3 Step 3 – Split Data into Training and Test Sets

```r
# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

# Create random index for 80/20 split
train_index <- sample(1:nrow(iris), size = 0.8 * nrow(iris))

# Subset the data
train_data <- iris[train_index, ]
test_data  <- iris[-train_index, ]

# Verify split
cat("Training samples:", nrow(train_data), "\n")
cat("Test samples:",    nrow(test_data),    "\n")
```

**Explanation:** A typical 80:20 train-test split reserves 120 samples for training and 30 for testing. `set.seed(123)` ensures the same random split on every run — **mandatory for KTU practicals**.

### 3.4.4 Step 4 – Train the Random Forest Model

```r
# Train Random Forest classifier
rf_model <- randomForest(
  formula   = Species ~ .,
  data      = train_data,
  ntree     = 500,        # number of trees
  mtry      = 2,          # variables tried at each split
  importance= TRUE,       # compute variable importance
  na.action = na.omit     # handle missing values
)

# Print model summary
print(rf_model)
```

**Explanation of parameters:**

| Parameter | Meaning | KTU Default |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `formula` | Model formula: `Species ~ .` means predict Species using all other columns | — |
| `data` | Training data frame | — |
| `ntree` | Number of trees in the forest | **500** |
| `mtry` | Number of predictors sampled at each split | $\lfloor \sqrt{p} \rfloor$ = **2** for iris |
| `importance` | Whether to compute variable importance | `FALSE` |
| `na.action` | How to handle NAs | `na.omit` |

### 3.4.5 Step 5 – View Model Output and OOB Error

```r
# Inspect OOB confusion matrix and error
rf_model$confusion
rf_model$err.rate

# Plot OOB error vs number of trees
plot(rf_model, main = "OOB Error vs Number of Trees")
legend("topright", legend = colnames(rf_model$err.rate),
       col = 1:ncol(rf_model$err.rate), lty = 1)
```

**Explanation:** `err.rate` is a matrix with columns `OOB`, and one column per class. The OOB column converges to the true test error as `ntree` grows. The plot shows stabilization — if the curve has flattened, you have enough trees.

### 3.4.6 Step 6 – Predict on the Test Set

```r
# Generate predictions on the test set
predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data)

# Compare predictions with actual values
comparison <- data.frame(
  Actual    = test_data$Species,
  Predicted = predictions
)
print(comparison)

# Compute test accuracy
accuracy <- mean(predictions == test_data$Species)
cat("Test Accuracy:", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
```

**Explanation:** `predict()` for classification returns the predicted **class label** (after majority voting). For regression, it returns the **numeric mean prediction**. To get class probabilities, use `predict(rf_model, newdata, type = "prob")`.

### 3.4.7 Step 7 – Variable Importance

```r
# Extract importance matrix
importance(rf_model)

# Plot variable importance
varImpPlot(rf_model,
           main = "Variable Importance in Random Forest")
```

**Explanation:** Two metrics are printed:
- **MeanDecreaseAccuracy** – How much accuracy drops if the variable is permuted.
- **MeanDecreaseGini** – How much the Gini impurity decreases due to splits on that variable.

> [!NOTE]
> Higher values = more important variable. For iris, `Petal.Length` and `Petal.Width` are almost always the top two.

### 3.4.8 Step 8 – Hyperparameter Tuning with `tuneRF()`

```r
# Tune the mtry parameter
best_mtry <- tuneRF(
  x         = train_data[, -5],   # predictors only
  y         = train_data$Species, # target
  ntreeTry  = 300,
  stepFactor= 1.5,
  improve   = 0.01,
  trace     = TRUE,
  plot      = TRUE
)

print(best_mtry)
```

**Explanation:** `tuneRF()` performs a guided search over `mtry` values. `stepFactor` controls the multiplicative step size, and `improve` is the OOB improvement threshold to stop searching.

## 3.5 Complete Worked-Out Example: Confusion Matrix Computation

Suppose on the 30-sample test set, the predictions yield:

| | Pred. setosa | Pred. versicolor | Pred. virginica |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual setosa** | 10 | 0 | 0 |
| **Actual versicolor** | 0 | 9 | 1 |
| **Actual virginica** | 0 | 1 | 9 |

**Step 1 – Correct predictions (diagonal):**

$$10 + 9 + 9 = 28$$

**Step 2 – Total test samples:**

$$N = 30$$

**Step 3 – Compute test accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{28}{30} \\
&= 0.9333 \\
&= 93.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute misclassification (error) rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error Rate} &= 1 - 0.9333 = 0.0667 = 6.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Per-class accuracy (recall):**

- Setosa: $10/10 = 100\%$
- Versicolor: $9/10 = 90\%$
- Virginica: $9/10 = 90\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always show the confusion matrix and the formula for accuracy in your exam answers. The evaluator looks for both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Random Forest Architecture Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete internal architecture of a Random Forest, from input data to final ensemble prediction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Training Dataset N samples, p features] --> B[Step 1: Generate B Bootstrap Samples]
    B --> C1[Bootstrap 1]
    B --> C2[Bootstrap 2]
    B --> C3[Bootstrap B]
    C1 --> D1[OOB Set 1 approx 36.8 percent]
    C1 --> E1[Tree 1 grown on mtry features per split]
    C2 --> D2[OOB Set 2]
    C2 --> E2[Tree 2]
    C3 --> D3[OOB Set B]
    C3 --> E3[Tree B]
    E1 --> F[Step 2: Aggregate Predictions]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    D1 --> G[Step 3: OOB Error Estimation]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    F --> H{Classification or Regression}
    H --> I[Majority Vote for Classification]
    H --> J[Mean Average for Regression]
    I --> K[Final Predicted Class]
    J --> L[Final Predicted Numeric Value]
    G --> M[OOB Error Rate]
    E1 --> N[Variable Importance via MDA and MDG]
    E2 --> N
    E3 --> N
    N --> O[Ranked Feature Importance Plot]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (R Implementation Pipeline)

This block diagram maps the R-level workflow, showing how data flows from raw input through the `randomForest` function to evaluation outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw CSV or Built in Dataset] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values via na omit]
    B --> B2[Encode Categorical Variables as Factors]
    B --> B3[set seed for Reproducibility]
    B1 --> C[Train Test Split 80 by 20]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Training Subset]
    C --> E[Test Subset]
    D --> F[randomForest Function Call]
    F --> F1[Parameter ntree equals 500]
    F --> F2[Parameter mtry equals sqrt of p]
    F --> F3[Parameter importance equals TRUE]
    F1 --> G[Trained RF Model Object]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Model Outputs]
    H --> H1[OOB Confusion Matrix]
    H --> H2[err rate matrix]
    H --> H3[Variable Importance Matrix]
    E --> I[predict Function]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Predicted Labels or Probabilities]
    J --> K[Evaluation]
    K --> K1[Confusion Matrix via table]
    K --> K2[Accuracy Score via mean]
    K --> K3[varImpPlot Visualization]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree vs Random Forest Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DT["Single Decision Tree"]
        DT1[Root Node: All Data] --> DT2[Split on Best Feature]
        DT2 --> DT3[Left Child Node]
        DT2 --> DT4[Right Child Node]
        DT3 --> DT5[Leaf: Prediction A]
        DT4 --> DT6[Leaf: Prediction B]
    end
    subgraph RF["Random Forest Ensemble"]
        RF1[Bootstrap 1] --> RT1[Tree 1]
        RF2[Bootstrap 2] --> RT2[Tree 2]
        RF3[Bootstrap 3] --> RT3[Tree 3]
        RT1 --> AGG[Aggregator: Majority Vote or Mean]
        RT2 --> AGG
        RT3 --> AGG
        AGG --> FINAL[Final Ensemble Prediction]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The key visual takeaway: A **single decision tree** uses **all features** and the **full dataset**, while a **Random Forest** uses **bootstrap samples** and **random feature subsets** for **each tree**, then combines outputs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define Random Forest. List any two advantages of Random Forest over a single Decision Tree.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Random Forest is an **ensemble learning technique** that constructs a multitude of decision trees during training and outputs the class that is the **mode of the classes** (for classification) or **mean prediction** (for regression) of the individual trees. It uses **bagging** and **random feature selection** to decorrelate the trees.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Reduced Overfitting:** While a single decision tree tends to overfit the training data, Random Forest averages multiple trees trained on different bootstrap samples with random feature subsets, significantly lowering variance without substantially increasing bias.
2. **Built-in Cross-Validation (OOB Error):** Random Forest provides an **Out-of-Bag error estimate** using the ~36.8% of data not included in each bootstrap sample, eliminating the need for a separate validation set.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "Random Forest uses multiple decision trees" without mentioning **bagging** and **feature randomness**. You will lose 1 mark for omitting these keywords.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: What is the Out-of-Bag (OOB) error in a Random Forest? Why is it considered a reliable estimate of test error?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** OOB error is the misclassification rate computed on the **~36.8% of training samples** that were **not included** in the bootstrap sample used to train a particular tree. For each observation, predictions are made using only the trees for which it was OOB, and these predictions are aggregated to compute the overall OOB error.

**Why it is reliable:**
1. Each OOB prediction uses only trees that have **never seen** that observation during training — analogous to a **leave-one-out cross-validation** scenario.
2. As the number of trees (`ntree`) approaches infinity, the OOB estimate becomes an **unbiased estimator** of the true generalization error.
3. It saves computation by **eliminating the need for an explicit cross-validation loop**.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of the Random Forest algorithm. Discuss the role of **bagging** and **feature randomness** in decorrelating the trees. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete R program to build a Random Forest classifier on the `iris` dataset. Include model training, prediction, and computation of test accuracy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) – Architecture and Working

**1. Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
A Random Forest is a collection of $B$ decision trees $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_B\}$, each trained on a different bootstrap sample of the original training data. Each tree is grown using a random subset of $mtry$ predictors at every split, ensuring diversity. The final prediction is obtained by **majority voting** (classification) or **averaging** (regression).

**2. Bagging – Bootstrap Aggregating (2.5 marks):**
- Given training data $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$, generate $B$ bootstrap samples $\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{D}_B$, each of size $N$ drawn **with replacement** from $\mathcal{D}$.
- Each bootstrap sample contains approximately **63.2%** unique observations; the remaining **36.8%** form the OOB set.
- Each tree $T_b$ is trained on its corresponding $\mathcal{D}_b$.
- **Why it helps:** Bagging reduces **variance** of the predictor without increasing bias. The variance of the average of $B$ independent random variables is $\sigma^2 / B$, so averaging many decorrelated trees significantly reduces prediction variance.

**3. Feature Randomness (2.5 marks):**
- At each node of every tree, instead of evaluating all $p$ predictors, only $mtry$ predictors are randomly sampled as candidates for the best split.
- **Default in R:** $mtry = \lfloor \sqrt{p} \rfloor$ for classification, $\lfloor p/3 \rfloor$ for regression.
- **Why it helps:** If one predictor is very strong, all bagged trees would use it at the top split, making them correlated. Random feature selection **forces** consideration of other predictors, producing **decorrelated trees** whose errors cancel out upon aggregation.

#### Model Solution for (b) – R Program

```r
# Step 1: Load library and data
library(randomForest)
data(iris)

# Step 2: Data partitioning
set.seed(42)
idx    <- sample(1:nrow(iris), 0.8 * nrow(iris))
train  <- iris[idx, ]
test   <- iris[-idx, ]

# Step 3: Train Random Forest
set.seed(42)
rf <- randomForest(
  Species ~ .,
  data       = train,
  ntree      = 500,
  mtry       = 2,
  importance = TRUE
)
print(rf)

# Step 4: Predict on test data
pred <- predict(rf, newdata = test)

# Step 5: Evaluate accuracy
accuracy <- mean(pred == test$Species)
cat("Test Accuracy =", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")

# Step 6: Confusion matrix
print(table(Actual = test$Species, Predicted = pred))
```

**Expected Output (sample):**
```
Test Accuracy = 96.67 %
              Predicted
Actual         setosa versicolor virginica
  setosa          10          0         0
  versicolor       0          9         1
  virginica        0          0        10
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `set.seed()`** – Results will not be reproducible; you lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing `randomForest(data = train)` without the formula** – The function will fail; you must specify `Species ~ .`.
> 3. **Not showing the confusion matrix** – Always include `table(Actual, Predicted)` for full marks.
> 4. **Confusing classification vs regression syntax** – `randomForest()` automatically detects the type from the response variable; for regression, the response must be numeric.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper, Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **variable importance** in Random Forest. Differentiate between **Mean Decrease Accuracy (MDA)** and **Mean Decrease Gini (MDG)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the `mtry` and `ntree` hyperparameters. How would you use the `tuneRF()` function in R to find the optimal `mtry`? Provide the R code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) – Variable Importance

**1. Why Variable Importance Matters (1.5 marks):**
In high-dimensional datasets (e.g., genomics with 20,000+ genes), not all predictors are useful. Random Forest provides two built-in metrics to rank predictors: **Mean Decrease Accuracy (MDA)** and **Mean Decrease Gini (MDG)**. These allow automated **feature selection** and **model interpretation**.

**2. Mean Decrease Accuracy (MDA) – Permutation Importance (3 marks):**
- For each tree $T_b$, record the OOB prediction accuracy $E_b^{OOB}$.
- **Permute** the values of variable $X_j$ in the OOB samples and recompute accuracy $E_{b,j}^{permuted}$.
- The decrease in accuracy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{b,j} &= E_b^{OOB} - E_{b,j}^{permuted}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: The closing brace in the Mermaid block was a stray from the diagram generation. In the LaTeX derivation, the correct form is shown below. Let me re-state it cleanly:

$$
\Delta E_{b,j} = E_b^{OOB} - E_{b,j}^{permuted}
$$

- The final MDA importance is the **average** of $\Delta E_{b,j}$ across all trees:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VIM_j^{(MDA)} = \frac{1}{ntree} \sum_{b=1}^{ntree} \Delta E_{b,j}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Interpretation:** Higher MDA = the variable contributes more to predictive accuracy. A variable with MDA ≈ 0 is useless; a negative MDA suggests the variable adds noise.

**3. Mean Decrease Gini (MDG) – Impurity-Based Importance (2 marks):**
- Whenever a split is made on variable $X_j$ in any tree, compute the **Gini impurity decrease** $\Delta Gini_{j,b}$.
- MDG is the **sum** of all $\Delta Gini_{j,b}$ across all trees and all splits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VIM_j^{(MDG)} = \sum_{b=1}^{ntree} \sum_{\text{splits on } j} \Delta Gini_{j,b}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Interpretation:** Higher MDG = the variable is used more often and reduces impurity more. It is **faster to compute** than MDA (no permutation needed) but can be **biased** toward variables with many categories.

**4. Key Difference (0.5 marks):**

| Aspect | MDA | MDG |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Based on** | Prediction accuracy (permutation) | Impurity reduction (Gini) |
| **Computation** | Slower (needs OOB permutation) | Faster (already tracked) |
| **Bias** | Unbiased | Biased toward high-cardinality vars |
| **Robustness** | More robust | Less robust |
| **KTU Default** | Yes (if `importance = TRUE`) | Yes (always computed) |

#### Model Solution for (b) – `mtry`, `ntree`, and `tuneRF()`

**1. The `ntree` Hyperparameter (2 marks):**
- `ntree` = number of trees in the forest.
- **Trade-off:** More trees = better stability and lower variance, but higher computation and memory.
- **Rule of thumb:** Start with `ntree = 500`. Plot the OOB error vs. ntree; if the curve has flattened, you have enough trees. Typical values range from **100 to 1000**.
- **Important:** Random Forest does **not overfit** as `ntree` increases — the OOB error converges monotonically.

**2. The `mtry` Hyperparameter (2 marks):**
- `mtry` = number of predictors randomly sampled as candidates at each split.
- **Small mtry** = more random, more decorrelated trees, but each tree is weaker (higher bias).
- **Large mtry** = less random, trees become more correlated, reducing the variance-reduction benefit of bagging.
- **Defaults in R:** Classification: $\lfloor \sqrt{p} \rfloor$, Regression: $\lfloor p/3 \rfloor$.
- **Tuning is essential** for optimal performance.

**3. `tuneRF()` Function and R Code (3 marks):**

```r
library(randomForest)
data(iris)
set.seed(42)

# Prepare predictors and response separately
x <- iris[, -5]
y <- iris[,  5]

# Run tuneRF
best_mtry <- tuneRF(
  x          = x,
  y          = y,
  ntreeTry   = 300,       # trees per mtry trial
  stepFactor = 1.5,       # multiplication factor for next mtry
  improve    = 0.01,      # OOB improvement threshold
  trace      = TRUE,      # print progress
  plot       = TRUE       # plot OOB error vs mtry
)

print(best_mtry)

# Use the best mtry to train final model
optimal_mtry <- best_mtry[which.min(best_mtry[, "OOBError"]), "mtry"]
final_rf <- randomForest(
  Species ~ ., data = iris,
  ntree = 500, mtry = optimal_mtry
)
```

**Explanation of `tuneRF` parameters:**

| Parameter | Meaning | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| `x` | Predictor data frame | — |
| `y` | Response vector | — |
| `ntreeTry` | Number of trees grown per mtry trial | 300–500 |
| `stepFactor` | Multiplier to get the next mtry value | 1.5 or 2 |
| `improve` | Minimum OOB improvement to continue search | 0.01–0.05 |
| `trace` | Whether to print progress | `TRUE` |
| `plot` | Whether to plot OOB error | `TRUE` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Confusing MDA and MDG** – Many students mix up the two. Remember: **MDA = permutation-based, MDG = impurity-based**.
> 2. **Writing `tuneRF(data = iris)`** – The function requires `x` (predictors) and `y` (response) **separately**, not a single data frame.
> 3. **Claiming Random Forest overfits as `ntree` increases** – This is **FALSE**. OOB error is monotonically non-increasing in `ntree`. You will lose marks for this misconception.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme – Random Forest in R**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Random Forest is an **ensemble of decision trees** using **bagging + random feature subsets**.
- [ ] For **classification**: final output = **majority vote**; for **regression**: final output = **mean**.
- [ ] **Default `mtry`**: $\lfloor \sqrt{p} \rfloor$ (classification), $\lfloor p/3 \rfloor$ (regression).
- [ ] **Bootstrap coverage** ≈ **63.2%**; OOB set ≈ **36.8%**.
- [ ] **OOB error** is an unbiased estimate of test error — no separate validation set needed.
- [ ] R's `randomForest` package uses **Gini impurity** by default.

### Key R Functions (Must Memorize)
- [ ] `randomForest(formula, data, ntree, mtry, importance)` — Train the model.
- [ ] `predict(rf_model, newdata)` — Predict class labels.
- [ ] `predict(rf_model, newdata, type = "prob")` — Predict class probabilities.
- [ ] `importance(rf_model)` — Extract variable importance matrix.
- [ ] `varImpPlot(rf_model)` — Plot variable importance.
- [ ] `tuneRF(x, y, ntreeTry, stepFactor, improve)` — Tune `mtry`.
- [ ] `plot(rf_model)` — Plot OOB error vs. ntree.
- [ ] `print(rf_model)` — View OOB confusion matrix and error.

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- [ ] Always use `set.seed()` before sampling and training.
- [ ] Use `Species ~ .` syntax (formula interface) or `x = , y = ` syntax.
- [ ] Don't confuse `mtry` with the total number of features.
- [ ] `importance = TRUE` is required to compute variable importance.
- [ ] For regression, the response variable must be **numeric**; for classification, it must be a **factor**.

### Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] $G(t) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ (Gini)
- [ ] $H(t) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ (Entropy)
- [ ] $1 - (1 - 1/N)^N \to 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ (Bootstrap coverage)
- [ ] $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{T_1(x), \ldots, T_B(x)\}$ (Classification aggregation)
- [ ] $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B}\sum T_b(x)$ (Regression aggregation)

### One-Line Definitions (for 2-mark sub-questions)
- [ ] **Bagging:** Training each tree on a bootstrap sample (sampling with replacement).
- [ ] **OOB Error:** Misclassification rate on samples not included in a tree's bootstrap.
- [ ] **Variable Importance:** Ranked score of each predictor's contribution to model accuracy.
- [ ] **mtry:** Number of predictors randomly tried at each node split.
- [ ] **ntree:** Total number of trees in the Random Forest.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R in Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** R in Healthcare ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **R in Healthcare** refers to the application of the **R programming language** (a free, open-source statistical computing and graphics environment) to solve problems in **clinical research, public health, epidemiology, biostatistics, genomics, and hospital data analytics**. R is governed by the GNU General Public License and is the de-facto standard for reproducible statistical reporting in the medical and life-sciences community. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the topic covers the use of R for: 1. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** on patient records. 2. **Inferential statistics** for clinical trials. 3. **Predictive modeling** (Logistic Regression, Cox Proportional-Hazards, Random Forests) for disease prognosis. 4. **Visualization** of epidemiological and biomedical data using packages like `ggplot2`. 5. **Reproducible research** through R Markdown and Shiny dashboards. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "R as a Hospital's Central Diagnostic Lab"** Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. The hospital has many departments (Cardiology, Oncology, Radiology). Each department generates **raw data** — ECG strips, biopsy slides, blood test numbers. Alone, these numbers are meaningless piles of paper. The **Diagnostic Laboratory** of the hospital takes these raw inputs and converts them into **actionable medical reports**. R plays the **exact same role** in healthcare analytics: - The **raw inputs** = clinical datasets (CSV, EHR exports, FASTA genomic sequences). - **R packages** (`survival`, `epiR`, `caret`, `ggplot2`) = the diagnostic instruments. - **R scripts** = the laboratory protocols (standardized, reproducible). - **Output plots & p-values** = the diagnostic report a doctor reads to make a decision. > Just as a good lab protocol is **reproducible** (any trained technician following the SOP gets the same result), a well-written R script gives the same result on any analyst's machine, anywhere in the world. This is why leading medical journals (*The Lancet*, *NEJM*, *JAMA*) mandate R-based reproducible analysis. ### 1.3 Why R and not Python / SPSS / SAS? | Feature | R | SPSS / SAS | Python | |---|---|---|---| | **License Cost** | Free (Open Source) | Expensive commercial | Free | | **CRAN Biomedical Packages** | **9,000+** (e.g., `survival`, `rms`) | Limited | Smaller stats library | | **Publication-quality graphics** | **Native (`ggplot2`)** | Decent | Needs `matplotlib` tweaking | | **Reproducible Reports** | R Markdown (single click) | Bulky syntax | Jupyter (less formal) | | **Regulatory Acceptance** | **FDA, EMA, WHO** recognize R | Long history | Growing | ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used While R is software (not a physics equation), the **statistical thresholds** universally used in healthcare analytics are: - **Significance level**: $\alpha = 0.05$ (95% confidence is the medical standard). - **Odds Ratio (OR)** clinical benchmark: $OR = 1.0$ means "no effect"; $OR > 1$ = risk factor; $OR < 1$ = protective factor. - **Hazard Ratio (HR)**: $HR = 1$ means equal hazard between groups. - **Area Under Curve (AUC)**: $0.5$ = useless model; $\geq 0.8$ = clinically useful; $\geq 0.9$ = excellent. - **Likelihood Ratio**: $LR^{+} > 10$ or $LR^{-} < 0.1$ = strong diagnostic evidence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorize the threshold values $\alpha = 0.05$, $AUC \geq 0.8$, and $OR = 1$ — these appear repeatedly in Part-A and Part-B questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Layers of an R-based Healthcare Analytics Project An R healthcare pipeline is organized into **five logical layers**. Understanding this layered architecture is a **high-yield KTU question** (frequently asked for 7 marks). - **Layer 1 — Data Ingestion Layer** - Reads CSVs from the Hospital Information System (HIS), lab machines, or APIs. - Key R functions: `read.csv()`, `read_excel()` from the `readxl` package, `fromJSON()` from `jsonlite`. - *Why:* Healthcare data is siloed in HL7 / FHIR / CSV formats — R must ingest them all. - **Layer 2 — Data Cleaning & Transformation Layer** - Handles **missing values** (a real hospital dataset has 5–25% missing labs). - Functions: `na.omit()`, `tidyr::replace_na()`, `dplyr::mutate()`. - *Why:* Garbage in → Garbage out. A 1 mg/dL data-entry typo for creatinine can flip a diagnosis. - **Layer 3 — Statistical Modeling Layer** - The brain of the pipeline. - For **diagnosis** → `glm(family = "binomial")` (Logistic Regression). - For **survival** (time-to-death / time-to-relapse) → `coxph(Surv(time, event) ~ ...)` from `survival` package. - For **multi-class disease classification** → `randomForest()`, `caret::train()`. - **Layer 4 — Visualization Layer** - `ggplot2` + `geom_smooth()` for trend lines. - `survminer::ggsurvplot()` for Kaplan–Meier survival curves. - `pROC::ggroc()` for ROC curves. - *Why:* A clinician cannot read 10,000 regression coefficients — they need a **picture**. - **Layer 5 — Reporting & Deployment Layer** - `rmarkdown::render()` → produces **PDF / HTML reports** with embedded code, plots, and narrative. - `shiny` → turns the R script into an **interactive web dashboard** for hospital administrators. - *Why:* Regulatory bodies (FDA, DCGI) require **audit trails** — R Markdown provides them automatically. ### 2.2 Core Statistical Formulas Used in R for Healthcare > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (memorize the LaTeX forms for the ESE exam)** | # | Concept | Formula | R Function | Clinical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Logistic Regression Logit | $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ | `glm(y ~ x, family=binomial)` | Probability of disease | | 2 | Odds Ratio (OR) | $OR = \dfrac{a/c}{b/d} = \dfrac{ad}{bc}$ | `epiR::epi.2by2()` | Strength of risk factor | | 3 | Relative Risk (RR) | $RR = \dfrac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}$ | `epiR::epi.2by2()` | Probability comparison | | 4 | Sensitivity | $\text{Sens} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | `caret::sensitivity()` | True-positive rate of a test | | 5 | Specificity | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | `caret::specificity()` | True-negative rate of a test | | 6 | Kaplan–Meier Survival | $S(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$ | `survival::survfit()` | Probability of surviving past time $t$ | | 7 | Cox Hazard | $h(t \vert X) = h_0(t)\cdot e^{\beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_p X_p}$ | `survival::coxph()` | Time-to-event risk | | 8 | Hazard Ratio (HR) | $HR = e^{\beta}$ | `summary(cox_model)` | Relative instantaneous risk | | 9 | ROC AUC | $AUC = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(x))\,dx$ | `pROC::auc()` | Discriminative power | | 10 | Youden's J Statistic | $J = \text{Sens} + \text{Spec} - 1$ | Manual computation | Optimal diagnostic cut-off | | 11 | Positive Predictive Value | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | `caret::posPredValue()` | Probability of disease given +ve test | | 12 | Negative Predictive Value | $NPV = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FN}$ | `caret::negPredValue()` | Probability of being healthy given -ve test | > **Notation for the 2×2 table:** $a = TP$, $b = FP$, $c = FN$, $d = TN$. ### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice - **Hospital Procurement Decisions:** When a hospital buys a ₹50-lakh MRI machine, the procurement committee uses **Sensitivity / Specificity / PPV / NPV** to compare vendor claims. - **Drug Trial Approval:** A Phase-III trial's primary endpoint is usually a **Cox HR** with 95% CI not crossing 1.0. - **Outbreak Forecasting:** Public-health engineers (epidemiologists) use **RR** to decide if a new COVID variant warrants a lockdown. - **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS):** Modern AI-CDSS systems in ICUs are **R models wrapped in Shiny**, validated by **AUC** before deployment. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** A biomedical engineer designing a wearable ECG monitor will prototype their arrhythmia-detection algorithm in R (using `signal` + `caret` packages) before porting it to embedded C on the device. R is the **engineering sketchbook** of healthcare data science. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section walks through **two end-to-end analytical derivations** that a KTU examiner loves to set as a 14-mark Part-B question. --- ### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough #1 — Deriving & Interpreting an Odds Ratio in R **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A hospital records the following data for a case-control study on **lung cancer vs. smoking**: | Group | Smoker (Exposed) | Non-Smoker (Unexposed) | Total | |---|---|---|---| | Lung Cancer Cases | $a = 50$ | $b = 10$ | 60 | | Controls (No Cancer) | $c = 30$ | $d = 90$ | 120 | **Derive the Odds Ratio (OR) and interpret it clinically. Write the equivalent R code.** #### Step 1 — Define the Odds in Each Group For a case-control study, the **odds of exposure** in the diseased group is: $$ \text{Odds}_{\text{cases}} = \frac{a}{c} = \frac{50}{30} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667 $$ The **odds of exposure** in the control group is: $$ \text{Odds}_{\text{controls}} = \frac{b}{d} = \frac{10}{90} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111 $$ *Why this step:* Odds (not probabilities) are used in case-control studies because the ratio of cases to controls is fixed by the investigator, not by nature. Odds neutralise this sampling bias. #### Step 2 — Compute the Odds Ratio $$ OR = \frac{\text{Odds}_{\text{cases}}}{\text{Odds}_{\text{controls}}} = \frac{a/c}{b/d} = \frac{ad}{bc} $$ Substituting the values: $$ OR = \frac{50 \times 90}{10 \times 30} = \frac{4500}{300} = 15.0 $$ #### Step 3 — Compute the 95% Confidence Interval (Wald Method) The standard error of $\ln(OR)$ is: $$ SE\bigl[\ln(OR)\bigr] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{d}} $$ $$ SE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{90}} = \sqrt{0.02 + 0.1 + 0.0333 + 0.0111} $$ $$ SE = \sqrt{0.1644} \approx 0.4055 $$ The 95% CI uses the critical value $z_{0.025} = 1.96$: $$ CI_{95\%} = \exp\!\left(\ln(OR) \pm 1.96 \times SE\right) $$ $$ CI_{95\%} = \exp\!\left(\ln(15) \pm 1.96 \times 0.4055\right) = \exp\!\left(2.7081 \pm 0.7948\right) $$ $$ CI_{95\%} = \bigl[\,e^{1.9133},\; e^{3.5029}\,\bigr] = [6.78,\; 33.20] $$ #### Step 4 — Clinical Interpretation > Since $OR = 15.0$ and the 95% CI $[6.78,\ 33.20]$ **does not include 1**, the association is **statistically significant** at $\alpha = 0.05$. > > **Interpretation:** Smokers in this study have **15 times the odds** of having lung cancer compared to non-smokers. The true population OR lies between 6.78 and 33.20 with 95% confidence. #### Step 5 — Equivalent R Code (Complete & Runnable) ```r # Install & load the epiR package install.packages("epiR") # run once library(epiR) # Build the 2x2 table (rows = outcome, cols = exposure) table_data <- matrix(c(50, 10, # Cases: smoker=50, non-smoker=10 30, 90), # Controls: smoker=30, non-smoker=90 nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) colnames(table_data) <- c("Smoker", "NonSmoker") rownames(table_data) <- c("Cancer", "Control") # Compute Odds Ratio with 95% CI result <- epi.2by2(dat = table_data, method = "wald", conf.level = 0.95) print(result) ``` **Expected R Output:** ``` Outcome + Outcome - Total Exposed + 50 30 80 Exposed - 10 90 100 Total 60 120 180 Point estimates : OR = 15.00 (95% CI: 6.78, 33.20) ``` --- ### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough #2 — Kaplan–Meier Survival Curve Derivation **Problem Statement:** A clinical trial follows 8 cancer patients for 12 months. Recurrence times (months) and censoring flags are: $$ \text{times} = (2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12), \quad \text{status} = (1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0) $$ Status $1$ = recurrence (event); Status $0$ = lost to follow-up (censored). **Compute the Kaplan–Meier survival estimate $S(t)$ and plot it in R.** #### Step 1 — Sort the Data by Time | Patient | Time $t_i$ | Status $\delta_i$ | At Risk $n_i$ | Events $d_i$ | Censored | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | 2 | 1 | 8 | 1 | 0 | | P2 | 4 | 1 | 7 | 1 | 0 | | P3 | 5 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 1 | | P4 | 6 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 0 | | P5 | 7 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 0 | | P6 | 8 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 1 | | P7 | 10 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0 | | P8 | 12 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | *Why this step:* KM is a step-function that updates only at event times. We must identify the *number at risk* just before each event. #### Step 2 — Apply the Product-Limit Formula The KM estimator is: $$ S(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right) $$ Compute step-by-step: $$ S(0) = 1 $$ $$ S(2) = 1 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) = \frac{7}{8} = 0.875 $$ $$ S(4) = 0.875 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{7}\right) = 0.875 \times 0.8571 = 0.750 $$ At $t = 5$, the patient is censored, so $S$ does **not** change. $S(5) = 0.750$. $$ S(6) = 0.750 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{5}\right) = 0.750 \times 0.8 = 0.600 $$ $$ S(7) = 0.600 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = 0.600 \times 0.75 = 0.450 $$ At $t = 8$, censored, $S(8) = 0.450$. $$ S(10) = 0.450 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = 0.450 \times 0.5 = 0.225 $$ At $t = 12$, censored, $S(12) = 0.225$. #### Step 3 — Tabulate the Survival Function | Time $t$ | $S(t)$ | |---|---| | 0 | 1.000 | | 2 | 0.875 | | 4 | 0.750 | | 6 | 0.600 | | 7 | 0.450 | | 10 | 0.225 | *Interpretation:* The probability of remaining recurrence-free up to 10 months is **22.5%**. The median survival time (where $S(t) = 0.5$) lies between 7 and 10 months. #### Step 4 — Complete R Code (survival + survminer) ```r install.packages(c("survival", "survminer")) # run once library(survival) library(survminer) # 1. Create the dataframe df <- data.frame( patient = paste0("P", 1:8), time = c(2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12), status = c(1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0) # 1 = event, 0 = censored ) # 2. Build the survival object and fit KM km_fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = df) # 3. Display the life table summary(km_fit) # 4. Plot the Kaplan-Meier curve ggsurvplot(km_fit, data = df, conf.int = TRUE, # 95% confidence band risk.table = TRUE, # show "number at risk" pval = FALSE, xlab = "Months", ylab = "Survival Probability S(t)", title = "Kaplan-Meier Recurrence-Free Curve", ggtheme = theme_bw()) ``` **Expected R Output (text portion):** ``` time n.risk n.event survival std.err 95%CI 2 8 1 0.875 0.117 [0.671, 1.000] 4 7 1 0.750 0.153 [0.502, 1.000] 6 5 1 0.600 0.187 [0.328, 1.000] 7 4 1 0.450 0.197 [0.197, 1.000] 10 2 1 0.225 0.193 [0.049, 1.000] ``` The values match our hand-derivation exactly: $0.875, 0.750, 0.600, 0.450, 0.225$ — a perfect cross-check. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — R Packages ↔ Healthcare Problem Mapping | Healthcare Problem | Primary R Package | Key Function | Output | |---|---|---|---| | Survival analysis (Cancer) | `survival` | `survfit()`, `coxph()` | KM curve, HR | | Publication plots | `ggplot2` | `ggplot()`, `geom_*()` | Box/Violin/Scatter | | ROC analysis | `pROC` | `roc()`, `auc()` | AUC value, curve | | Sample size for trial | `pwr` | `pwr.t.test()` | $n$ per arm | | Epidemic modeling | `epitools`, `Epi` | `epi.2by2()` | OR, RR, Attrib Risk | | Missing data imputation | `mice` | `mice()` | Imputed dataframe | | Machine-learning classifier | `caret` | `train()` | Confusion matrix | | Random Forest (genomics) | `randomForest` | `randomForest()` | Variable importance | | Reproducible report | `rmarkdown` | `render("file.Rmd")` | PDF / HTML report | | Interactive dashboard | `shiny` | `shinyApp()` | Web app | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End R Healthcare Analytics Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hospital Information System HIS] --> B[HL7 FHIR CSV Export] B --> C[Layer 1: Data Ingestion
read.csv read_excel fromJSON] C --> D[Layer 2: Cleaning
tidyr dplyr na.omit] D --> E[Layer 3: Statistical Modeling
glm coxph survfit randomForest] E --> F[Layer 4: Visualization
ggplot2 ggsurvplot pROC] F --> G[Layer 5: Reporting
rmarkdown Shiny] G --> H[Clinician Dashboard PDF FDA Audit Trail] D --> I[EDA Boxplot Histogram] I --> F E --> J[Model Metrics AUC Sensitivity Specificity] J --> H style A fill:#f9d5e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#d5f9e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#fff4b8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right R Test (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD K[Start: What is the outcome variable?] --> L{Is it time-to-event?} L -- Yes --> M[Use survival package
KM curve plus coxph] L -- No --> N{Is it binary Yes or No?} N -- Yes --> O{Adjusting for confounders?} O -- Yes --> P[Logistic Regression
glm family equals binomial] O -- No --> Q[Chi-square or Fisher exact
epiR epi.2by2] N -- No --> R{More than 2 groups?} R -- Yes --> S[ANOVA or Kruskal Wallis] R -- No --> T{Paired or Unpaired?} T -- Paired --> U[Paired t-test or Wilcoxon] T -- Unpaired --> V[Two-sample t-test or Mann-Whitney] style M fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#d5f9e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.3 R-Package Ecosystem Map (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CoreR[Core R Engine] R1[base] R2[stats] R3[graphics] end subgraph TidyVerse[Tidyverse Data Wrangling] T1[dplyr] T2[tidyr] T3[readr] end subgraph BioMed[Biomedical Statistical Suite] B1[survival] B2[survminer] B3[epiR] B4[pROC] end subgraph ML[Machine Learning Stack] M1[caret] M2[randomForest] M3[e1071] end subgraph Report[Reporting and Deployment] Rep1[rmarkdown] Rep2[Shiny] Rep3[knitr] end CoreR --> TidyVerse TidyVerse --> BioMed BioMed --> ML ML --> Report style CoreR fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style BioMed fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Report fill:#d5f9e5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q1. Define R programming. List any four advantages of using R in healthcare analytics.** *[3 marks]* **Model Answer:** R is an open-source programming language and software environment for statistical computing and graphics, developed by Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman at the University of Auckland. The Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN) hosts thousands of community packages. **Four advantages in healthcare:** 1. **Free & open-source** — affordable for low-resource hospitals. 2. **Reproducibility** — R Markdown produces audit-ready reports accepted by FDA / EMA. 3. **Rich biomedical packages** — `survival`, `epiR`, `pROC`, `caret`. 4. **Publication-quality graphics** — `ggplot2` produces journal-ready Kaplan–Meier and ROC plots. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **Q2. What is an Odds Ratio (OR)? State its formula and the clinical interpretation of $OR = 1$, $OR > 1$, and $OR < 1$.** *[3 marks]* **Model Answer:** The **Odds Ratio** quantifies the strength of association between an exposure and a disease in a case-control study. $$ OR = \frac{ad}{bc} = \frac{\text{Odds of exposure in cases}}{\text{Odds of exposure in controls}} $$ | OR value | Clinical meaning | |---|---| | $OR = 1$ | Exposure is **not associated** with the disease (null effect) | | $OR > 1$ | Exposure is a **risk factor** (e.g., smoking and lung cancer) | | $OR < 1$ | Exposure is **protective** (e.g., vaccination against influenza) | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students **lose 2 marks** for forgetting the `library(package_name)` call before using `survival` / `epiR` / `pROC` functions. > - Students **lose 2 marks** for not writing the **clinical interpretation** of the numeric output (AUC, OR, HR) — KTU examiners allocate a dedicated "Interpretation" mark band. > - Students **lose 1 mark** for hard-coding data inside `survfit()`/`glm()` instead of using a dataframe; the latter is best practice. > - Always mention the **95% CI** alongside any point estimate. A HR of 1.2 with CI [0.9, 1.6] is **not significant** — students forget this. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Pattern, 2024 Scheme]* > **(a) Explain the five-layer architecture of an R-based healthcare analytics pipeline. For each layer, name one R package and one function used.** *(7 marks)* > > **(b) A diagnostic test is evaluated on 200 patients. The 2×2 table is: TP=80, FP=20, FN=10, TN=90. Compute Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, and the Youden's J Index. Write and explain the equivalent R code.** *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** The five-layer architecture is: 1. **Layer 1 – Data Ingestion:** Reads hospital data from CSVs, Excel, JSON, FHIR. *Package:* `readxl` *Function:* `read_excel("labs.xlsx")`. **1.5 marks** 2. **Layer 2 – Cleaning / Transformation:** Removes duplicates, handles `NA`, normalises units. *Package:* `dplyr` *Function:* `mutate()`, `na.omit()`. **1.5 marks** 3. **Layer 3 – Statistical Modeling:** Applies regression / survival / ML. *Package:* `survival` *Function:* `coxph()`. **1.5 marks** 4. **Layer 4 – Visualization:** Renders Kaplan-Meier, ROC, box-plots. *Package:* `ggplot2` *Function:* `ggplot()`. **1 mark** 5. **Layer 5 – Reporting / Deployment:** R Markdown, Shiny. *Package:* `rmarkdown` *Function:* `render()`. **1.5 marks** --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** Given: $TP=80$, $FP=20$, $FN=10$, $TN=90$. **Step 1 – Sensitivity** (1 mark): $$ \text{Sens} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{80}{80+10} = \frac{80}{90} \approx 0.8889 \;(88.89\%) $$ **Step 2 – Specificity** (1 mark): $$ \text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{90}{90+20} = \frac{90}{110} \approx 0.8182 \;(81.82\%) $$ **Step 3 – PPV** (1 mark): $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{80}{80+20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 \;(80\%) $$ **Step 4 – NPV** (1 mark): $$ NPV = \frac{TN}{TN+FN} = \frac{90}{90+10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \;(90\%) $$ **Step 5 – Youden's J Index** (1 mark): $$ J = \text{Sens} + \text{Spec} - 1 = 0.8889 + 0.8182 - 1 = 0.7071 $$ **Step 6 – Equivalent R Code** (2 marks): ```r # Create the confusion matrix actual <- c(rep("Disease", 90), rep("Healthy", 110)) predicted <- c(rep("Pos", 80), rep("Neg", 10), rep("Pos", 20), rep("Neg", 90)) df <- data.frame(actual, predicted) # Load caret and compute metrics library(caret) cm <- confusionMatrix(data = factor(predicted, levels = c("Pos","Neg")), reference = factor(actual, levels = c("Disease","Healthy")), positive = "Pos") print(cm$byClass["Sensitivity"]) # 0.8889 print(cm$byClass["Specificity"]) # 0.8182 print(cm$byClass["Pos Pred Value"])# 0.8000 print(cm$byClass["Neg Pred Value"])# 0.9000 # Youden's J J <- cm$byClass["Sensitivity"] + cm$byClass["Specificity"] - 1 cat("Youden J =", J, "\n") # 0.7071 ``` **Clinical Interpretation:** $J = 0.7071$ is close to the maximum of 1.0, indicating the test has **strong combined sensitivity and specificity** and is suitable for clinical use. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Pattern, 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]* > **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the `survival`, `survminer`, and `pROC` packages in healthcare analytics. Differentiate between Kaplan–Meier estimator and Cox Proportional-Hazards model.** *(7 marks)* > > **(b) A cohort of 100 patients is followed for 5 years. Twenty patients develop the disease. Among smokers, 15/50 develop disease; among non-smokers, 5/50 develop disease. Compute the Relative Risk (RR) and interpret it. Write the R code using `epiR`.** *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Role of packages** (3 marks): - **`survival`** – The foundational package. Implements `Surv()`, `survfit()` for non-parametric KM curves, and `coxph()` for semi-parametric Cox regression. The 2024 *survival* package (Therneau) is the gold-standard for time-to-event analysis. - **`survminer`** – A wrapper around `survival` providing publication-quality `ggsurvplot()` with risk tables, censoring tick marks, and confidence bands. - **`pROC`** – Computes and plots **ROC curves**, calculates **AUC**, and performs **DeLong's test** for comparing two AUCs. **KM vs Cox** (4 marks): | Aspect | Kaplan-Meier | Cox PH | |---|---|---| | **Type** | Non-parametric | Semi-parametric | | **Purpose** | Estimate $S(t)$ for one or more groups | Model effect of covariates on hazard | | **Formula** | $S(t)=\prod(1-d_i/n_i)$ | $h(t)=h_0(t)\,e^{\beta X}$ | | **Output** | Step curve, median survival | Hazard Ratio (HR) with 95% CI | | **Limitation** | Cannot adjust for confounders | Assumes proportional hazards | | **R function** | `survfit()` | `coxph()` | --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 – Build the 2×2 cohort table** (1 mark): | Group | Disease + | Disease – | Total | |---|---|---|---| | Smoker | 15 | 35 | 50 | | Non-smoker | 5 | 45 | 50 | **Step 2 – Compute risk in each group** (1 mark): $$ \text{Risk}_{\text{smoker}} = \frac{15}{50} = 0.30 $$ $$ \text{Risk}_{\text{non-smoker}} = \frac{5}{50} = 0.10 $$ **Step 3 – Compute Relative Risk** (1 mark): $$ RR = \frac{\text{Risk}_{\text{smoker}}}{\text{Risk}_{\text{non-smoker}}} = \frac{0.30}{0.10} = 3.0 $$ **Step 4 – Interpretation** (1 mark): Smokers are **3 times more likely** to develop the disease than non-smokers in this 5-year cohort. **Step 5 – 95% CI for $\ln(RR)$** (1 mark): $$ SE\bigl[\ln(RR)\bigr] = \sqrt{\frac{1}{15} - \frac{1}{50} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{50}} = \sqrt{0.0467 + 0.18} \approx 0.476 $$ $$ CI_{95\%} = \exp\!\bigl(\ln(3) \pm 1.96 \times 0.476\bigr) = \exp\!\bigl(1.0986 \pm 0.9330\bigr) = [1.21,\ 7.42] $$ Since the CI excludes 1, the association is statistically significant. **Step 6 – Equivalent R code using `epiR`** (2 marks): ```r library(epiR) cohort <- matrix(c(15, 35, 5, 45), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE) colnames(cohort) <- c("Disease", "NoDisease") rownames(cohort) <- c("Smoker", "NonSmoker") result <- epi.2by2(dat = cohort, method = "cohort.count", conf.level = 0.95) print(result) # RR = 3.00, 95% CI [1.21, 7.42] ``` --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorize Points for the KTU Exam (2024 Scheme)** - **R** = free, open-source statistical language; **CRAN** hosts packages; governed by **GPL**. - The **five-layer pipeline** is the most expected 7-mark theory question. - **Odds Ratio** is for **case-control** studies; **Relative Risk** is for **cohort** studies. - **Kaplan–Meier** is **non-parametric**; **Cox PH** is **semi-parametric** and gives the **Hazard Ratio**. - **Sensitivity** catches true positives; **Specificity** catches true negatives; **Youden's J** combines both. - **AUC** $\geq 0.8$ = clinically useful model; $\geq 0.9$ = excellent; $0.5$ = useless. - Always report **95% CI** alongside any point estimate (OR, HR, RR). - Essential R packages: `survival`, `survminer`, `pROC`, `epiR`, `caret`, `ggplot2`, `rmarkdown`, `shiny`, `mice`, `randomForest`. - **R Markdown** is the reproducibility weapon — produces PDF/HTML with embedded code, output, and narrative. - **Censoring** in survival data: the patient is *lost to follow-up* but we know they were disease-free up to that time; never treat censored observations as events. - **Threshold values** to memorize: $\alpha = 0.05$, $OR = 1$, $HR = 1$, $AUC = 0.5$ (chance), $J_{\max} = 1$. - For every R script in the exam, always include `install.packages(...)` (commented out) and `library(...)` calls — KTU expects a **runnable** answer.

topics.title: Data Analysis with Pandas: The Data Structure of Pandas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Analysis with Pandas: The Data Structure of Pandas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Pandas?

**Pandas** (Python Data Analysis Library) is an open-source, high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis library built on top of the **NumPy** programming language, developed originally by **Wes McKinney in 2008**. It provides fast, flexible, and expressive data structures designed to make working with "relational" or "labeled" data both easy and intuitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Pandas is a Python library that provides high-performance, easy-to-use data structures such as Series (1-Dimensional) and DataFrame (2-Dimensional) for data analysis, manipulation, and cleaning tasks. It is the de-facto standard tool for data wrangling in Python.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Core Data Structures of Pandas

Pandas provides **three primary data structures** that form the foundation of all data manipulation tasks:

| # | Data Structure | Dimensionality | Description |
|---|---------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Series** | 1-Dimensional | A labeled homogeneous array capable of holding any data type (int, str, float, objects, etc.) |
| 2 | **DataFrame** | 2-Dimensional | A labeled, two-dimensional, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous tabular data structure with axes (rows and columns) |
| 3 | **Panel** | 3-Dimensional | A labeled, three-dimensional data structure (deprecated since v0.25.0 in favor of MultiIndex DataFrames) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:**
> The two **most important** data structures you must master are **Series** and **DataFrame**. Panel is mostly historical/legacy knowledge and may appear as a 3-mark question only.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spreadsheet Workbook**
>
> Imagine you are working in **Microsoft Excel**:
>
> - A **Series** is like a **single column** in Excel (with an index/row label on the left). It is one-dimensional — just a list of values with labels.
>
> - A **DataFrame** is like an **entire Excel worksheet** — a complete table with multiple columns (each column being a Series) sharing a common row index. It is two-dimensional.
>
> - A **Panel** is like having **multiple worksheets (sheets)** stacked together — a 3D container. Think of it as a workbook of spreadsheets indexed by sheet name.
>
> Just as Excel gives you powerful tools to sort, filter, pivot, and compute over spreadsheets, **Pandas gives Python the same power, but programmatically and at scale**.

---

## 1.4 Why Pandas for Data Analysis?

- **Speed**: Built on top of NumPy (C-optimized), Pandas operations are extremely fast — often **10x to 100x** faster than pure Python loops.
- **Flexibility**: Handles missing data (`NaN`), time-series data, heterogeneous data types.
- **Rich Functionality**: Built-in methods for groupby, pivot, melt, merge, join, reshape.
- **Integration**: Works seamlessly with NumPy, Matplotlib, Scikit-learn, SQL databases, CSV, Excel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard import convention in industry and KTU exams:**
> ```python
> import pandas as pd
> import numpy as np
> ```
> Always use `pd` as the alias — this is universally accepted.
