# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Series — The 1-Dimensional Foundation

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition

A **Pandas Series** is a one-dimensional labeled array capable of holding any data type (integers, strings, floats, Python objects, etc.). The **axis labels** are collectively called the **index**. A Series can be visualized as:

$$\text{Series} = \text{Data} + \text{Index}$$

Mathematically:

$$S = \{ (i_k, v_k) \mid k = 1, 2, \ldots, n \}$$

where $i_k$ is the index label and $v_k$ is the value at position $k$.

### 2.1.2 Anatomy of a Series

A Series consists of **two parallel arrays**:

- **Values array** — the actual data (homogeneous type)
- **Index array** — the labels (can be heterogeneous)

### 2.1.3 Construction of a Series

A Series can be created from:

1. **Python List / Tuple**
2. **NumPy ndarray**
3. **Python Dictionary** (keys become index, values become data)
4. **Scalar value** (single value broadcasted to all indices)

### 2.1.4 Key Attributes of Series

- `series.index` → Returns the index labels
- `series.values` → Returns the values as a NumPy array
- `series.dtype` → Returns the data type of values
- `series.size` → Returns the number of elements
- `series.shape` → Returns a tuple representing dimensions
- `series.ndim` → Returns the number of dimensions (always 1)

---

## 2.2 DataFrame — The 2-Dimensional Powerhouse

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

A **Pandas DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, tabular data structure** with **labeled axes (rows and columns)**. It is the most commonly used Pandas object and can be thought of as a **dictionary of Series objects** where each column is a Series sharing a common index.

Mathematically:

$$DF = \{ C_j \mid j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \}$$

where each $C_j$ is a Series (a column), and all columns share a common row index $I$.

### 2.2.2 Anatomy of a DataFrame

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Data** | The actual values stored in a 2D NumPy array (or similar) |
| **Row Index** (`index`) | Labels for rows (axis = 0) |
| **Column Index** (`columns`) | Labels for columns (axis = 1) |
| **Dtypes** | Each column can have its own data type |

### 2.2.3 Construction of a DataFrame

A DataFrame can be created from:

1. **Dictionary of lists / ndarrays / Series**
2. **List of dictionaries**
3. **NumPy 2D array**
4. **Another DataFrame**
5. **External files** (CSV, Excel, JSON, SQL query)

### 2.2.4 Key Attributes of DataFrame

- `df.index` → Row labels
- `df.columns` → Column labels
- `df.values` → Underlying NumPy representation
- `df.dtypes` → Data type of each column
- `df.shape` → Tuple `(rows, columns)`
- `df.ndim` → Always **2**
- `df.size` → Total number of elements (rows × columns)
- `df.axes` → List of row and column index labels

---

## 2.3 Comparison: Series vs DataFrame vs NumPy ndarray

| Feature | NumPy `ndarray` | Pandas `Series` | Pandas `DataFrame` |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Dimensions** | n-dimensional | 1-D | 2-D |
| **Labels** | No labels (positional only) | Has **1 Index** | Has **Row Index + Column Index** |
| **Heterogeneous Data** | Homogeneous (single dtype) | Homogeneous values | **Heterogeneous columns allowed** |
| **Size Mutability** | Fixed once created | Fixed once created | **Columns can be inserted/deleted** |
| **Missing Data Handling** | Limited | **`NaN` aware** | **`NaN` aware** |
| **Primary Use** | Numerical computation | Single column / time series | Tabular / relational data |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield concepts, formulas, and syntax patterns tested in KTU ESE examinations for this topic.

| # | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Import Pandas | `import pandas as pd` | Always use alias `pd` |
| 2 | Create empty Series | `pd.Series(dtype=object)` | Returns Series with no data |
| 3 | Create Series from list | `pd.Series([1, 2, 3])` | Default index = 0, 1, 2, ... |
| 4 | Create Series with custom index | `pd.Series(data, index=['a','b','c'])` | Length must match |
| 5 | Create Series from dict | `pd.Series({'a': 1, 'b': 2})` | Keys → Index, Values → Data |
| 6 | Create DataFrame from dict | `pd.DataFrame({'A': [1,2], 'B': [3,4]})` | Each key is a column |
| 7 | Create DataFrame from list of dicts | `pd.DataFrame([{'A':1}, {'A':2}])` | Each dict = one row |
| 8 | Shape formula | `shape = (n_rows, n_cols)` | Tuple |
| 9 | Total elements | `size = n_rows × n_cols` | For DataFrame |
| 10 | Number of dimensions | `ndim` | Series: **1**, DataFrame: **2**, Panel: **3** |
| 11 | Selecting column | `df['col']` or `df.col` | Returns Series |
| 12 | Selecting row by label | `df.loc['row_label']` | Label-based |
| 13 | Selecting row by position | `df.iloc[0]` | Integer position-based |
| 14 | Arithmetic on Series | `s1 + s2` | Aligns on index |
| 15 | Unique values | `s.unique()` | Returns ndarray of unique values |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Pandas is used in Engineering (Industry Context):**
>
> - **IoT / Sensor Data Analysis**: Engineers import millions of sensor readings into DataFrames to filter, resample, and detect anomalies.
> - **Machine Learning Preprocessing**: DataFrames are the standard input format for Scikit-learn pipelines (feature engineering, missing value imputation).
> - **Financial Analytics**: Time-series stock data is stored as Pandas Series with `DatetimeIndex`.
> - **Civil/Structural Engineering Logs**: Daily site measurements (temperature, stress, load) handled as time-indexed DataFrames.
> - **Power Systems**: Load forecasting models consume DataFrames containing hourly demand data.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Construction Walkthroughs

### 3.1.1 Building a Series Step-by-Step

**Problem Statement:** Create a Pandas Series representing the marks of 5 students in a subject, with custom string index labels.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Step 1: Define the data (homogeneous int values)
data = [85, 90, 78, 92, 88]

# Step 2: Define custom index labels
idx = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'David', 'Eva']

# Step 3: Construct the Series
marks_series = pd.Series(data, index=idx, name='Math_Marks', dtype='int64')

print(marks_series)
```

**Output:**

```
Alice      85
Bob        90
Charlie    78
David      92
Eva        88
Name: Math_Marks, dtype: int64
```

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$\text{marks\_series} = \begin{bmatrix} i_1 = \text{Alice}, & v_1 = 85 \\ i_2 = \text{Bob}, & v_2 = 90 \\ i_3 = \text{Charlie}, & v_3 = 78 \\ i_4 = \text{David}, & v_4 = 92 \\ i_5 = \text{Eva}, & v_5 = 88 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic Explanation of Each Step:**
- **Step 1:** The list `data` provides homogeneous integer values that will populate the Series.
- **Step 2:** The list `idx` provides 5 string labels. Pandas aligns them positionally with `data`.
- **Step 3:** The `name` attribute gives the Series a label (useful when it becomes a DataFrame column). `dtype` explicitly forces type consistency.

**Key Verification Step:**
Check `marks_series.shape` → Returns `(5,)` confirming 1-D with 5 elements.

---

### 3.1.2 Building a DataFrame Step-by-Step

**Problem Statement:** Create a DataFrame with student information including Name, Age, and Marks.

```python
# Step 1: Create a dictionary where each key is a column
student_data = {
    'Name':    ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'David'],
    'Age':     [20, 21, 19, 22],
    'Marks':   [85, 90, 78, 92]
}

# Step 2: Construct DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(student_data, index=['S1', 'S2', 'S3', 'S4'])

# Step 3: Inspect
print(df)
print("Shape:", df.shape)
print("Dtypes:\n", df.dtypes)
```

**Output:**

```
       Name  Age  Marks
S1    Alice   20     85
S2      Bob   21     90
S3  Charlie   19     78
S4    David   22     92
Shape: (4, 3)
Dtypes:
 Name     object
Age       int64
Marks     int64
dtype: object
```

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$DF = \{C_1 = \text{Name}, C_2 = \text{Age}, C_3 = \text{Marks}\}$$

where each $C_j$ is a Series sharing row index $I = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$.

---

## 3.2 Vectorized Arithmetic Operation Derivation

**Problem:** Demonstrate index alignment in Series arithmetic.

```python
s1 = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['a', 'b', 'c'])
s2 = pd.Series([5, 15, 25, 35], index=['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'])

result = s1 + s2
print(result)
```

**Output:**

```
a    15.0
b    35.0
c    55.0
d     NaN
dtype: float64
```

**Logical Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\text{result}[i] = \begin{cases} s_1[i] + s_2[i], & \text{if } i \in s_1.\text{index} \cap s_2.\text{index} \\ \text{NaN}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- For index `'a'`: $10 + 5 = 15$
- For index `'b'`: $20 + 15 = 35$
- For index `'c'`: $30 + 25 = 55$
- For index `'d'`: present only in $s_2$, so result is **NaN** (Not a Number).

This demonstrates the critical concept of **automatic index alignment** — a hallmark feature of Pandas.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DataFrame Construction Methods

| # | Input Type | Code | Resulting Index | Resulting Columns | Heterogeneous? |
|---|-----------|------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Dict of Lists | `pd.DataFrame({'A':[1,2], 'B':[3,4]})` | 0, 1 (default) | 'A', 'B' | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | Dict of Series | `pd.DataFrame({'A':s1, 'B':s2})` | Union of series indices | 'A', 'B' | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | List of Dicts | `pd.DataFrame([{'A':1,'B':2}, {'A':3,'B':4}])` | 0, 1 (default) | 'A', 'B' (union of keys) | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | 2D NumPy Array | `pd.DataFrame(np_arr, columns=['A','B'])` | 0, 1, ..., n-1 | 'A', 'B' (if specified) | ❌ Forced to single dtype |
| 5 | From CSV File | `pd.read_csv('file.csv')` | First column or default | Header row | ✅ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Index Alignment Principle:** Whenever two Pandas objects are combined (via `+`, `-`, `merge`, `concat`), **Pandas automatically aligns them on their index labels**, not on position. This is fundamentally different from NumPy where operations are purely positional.

---

## 3.4 Complete Worked Example: From Raw Data to Insights

```python
import pandas as pd

# Step 1: Create raw data
data = {
    'Department': ['CSE', 'ECE', 'CSE', 'Mech', 'ECE', 'CSE'],
    'Student':    ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F'],
    'Marks':      [85, 90, 78, 92, 88, 95]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Step 2: Inspect structure
print("Shape:", df.shape)   # (6, 3)
print("Dtypes:\n", df.dtypes)

# Step 3: Extract a column (returns Series)
marks_col = df['Marks']
print("Type of column:", type(marks_col))  # <class 'pandas.core.series.Series'>

# Step 4: Compute statistics using vectorized methods
print("Mean Marks:", marks_col.mean())      # 88.0
print("Max Marks:", marks_col.max())        # 95
print("Std Dev:", marks_col.std())
```

This demonstrates the **practical chain**: Raw Data → DataFrame → Series extraction → Statistical analysis.

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: List any three primary data structures of Pandas. Briefly explain each with its dimensionality.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary data structures of Pandas are:

1. **Series** — A **one-dimensional** labeled array capable of holding any data type. It can be viewed as a single column of data with row labels (index).

2. **DataFrame** — A **two-dimensional**, size-mutable, tabular data structure with labeled axes (rows and columns). It is the most widely used Pandas object and can be visualized as a dictionary of Series objects.

3. **Panel** — A **three-dimensional** labeled data structure (deprecated since Pandas v0.25.0), which used to hold items, major axis, and minor axis. It has been replaced by MultiIndex DataFrames.

## 5.2 PART B Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** *(14 Marks)*

#### Part (a) — *(7 Marks)*
**Explain the concept of a Pandas DataFrame in detail. Discuss its structure, key attributes, and how it differs from a 2D NumPy array. Write a Python program to construct a DataFrame from a dictionary of lists and display its shape, dtypes, and column names.**

#### Part (b) — *(7 Marks)*
**What is a Pandas Series? Explain how to construct a Series from (i) a Python list with custom index, (ii) a Python dictionary, and (iii) a scalar value. Demonstrate the concept of automatic index alignment using an example.**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Solution (a):

**Definition:** A Pandas DataFrame is a two-dimensional, size-mutable, tabular data structure with labeled axes (rows and columns). It is the most commonly used Pandas object and is analogous to a spreadsheet or SQL table.

**Structure of DataFrame:**

- **Data block**: A 2D block (typically a NumPy array) holding the actual values.
- **Row Index** (`index`, axis=0): Labels for each row.
- **Column Index** (`columns`, axis=1): Labels for each column.

**Key Attributes:**

| Attribute | Returns |
|-----------|---------|
| `df.shape` | Tuple `(n_rows, n_cols)` |
| `df.dtypes` | Data type of each column |
| `df.columns` | Column index (labels) |
| `df.index` | Row index (labels) |
| `df.values` | Underlying NumPy array |
| `df.ndim` | `2` |
| `df.size` | Total elements = `n_rows × n_cols` |

**DataFrame vs 2D NumPy Array:**

| Feature | 2D NumPy Array | DataFrame |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Labels | None (positional) | Row + column labels |
| Data types | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous columns |
| Missing data | Limited support | Native `NaN` handling |
| Size mutability | Fixed | Columns can be added/removed |
| Index alignment | Not applicable | Automatic |

**Python Program:**

```python
import pandas as pd

# Construct DataFrame from dictionary of lists
data = {
    'Name':    ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'David'],
    'Age':     [20, 21, 19, 22],
    'Marks':   [85, 90, 78, 92],
    'Grade':   ['B', 'A', 'C', 'A']
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data, index=['S1', 'S2', 'S3', 'S4'])

# Display key attributes
print("DataFrame:\n", df)
print("\nShape:", df.shape)         # (4, 4)
print("\nDtypes:\n", df.dtypes)
print("\nColumn Names:", df.columns.tolist())
print("\nRow Index:", df.index.tolist())
```

**Expected Output:**

```
DataFrame:
       Name  Age  Marks Grade
S1    Alice   20     85     B
S2      Bob   21     90     A
S3  Charlie   19     78     C
S4    David   22     92     A

Shape: (4, 4)

Dtypes:
 Name     object
Age       int64
Marks     int64
Grade    object
dtype: object

Column Names: ['Name', 'Age', 'Marks', 'Grade']
Row Index: ['S1', 'S2', 'S3', 'S4']
```

---

#### Solution (b):

**Definition:** A Pandas Series is a one-dimensional labeled array capable of holding any data type. It has two components — **values** and **index**.

**(i) Constructing a Series from a Python List with Custom Index:**
```python
import pandas as pd
marks = pd.Series([85, 90, 78], index=['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'])
print(marks)
```

**Output:**
```
Alice      85
Bob        90
Charlie    78
dtype: int64
```

**(ii) Constructing a Series from a Python Dictionary:**
```python
data = {'Math': 90, 'Physics': 85, 'Chemistry': 88}
marks = pd.Series(data)
print(marks)
```

**Output:**
```
Math        90
Physics     85
Chemistry   88
dtype: int64
```
Here, dictionary keys become the index automatically.

**(iii) Constructing a Series from a Scalar Value:**
```python
s = pd.Series(5, index=['a', 'b', 'c'])
print(s)
```

**Output:**
```
a    5
b    5
c    5
dtype: int64
```
The scalar value **5** is **broadcasted** to every index label.

**Demonstration of Automatic Index Alignment:**
```python
s1 = pd.Series([100, 200, 300], index=['a', 'b', 'c'])
s2 = pd.Series([10, 20, 30, 40], index=['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'])

result = s1 + s2
print(result)
```

**Output:**
```
a    110.0
b    220.0
c    330.0
d      NaN
dtype: float64
```

**Explanation:** Pandas aligns the two Series on their **index labels** before performing element-wise addition. Index `'d'` exists only in `s2`, so the result is **NaN** (Not a Number). This automatic alignment is one of the defining features of Pandas.

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *(14 Marks — Alternative to Q-A)*

#### Part (a) — *(7 Marks)*
**Discuss the various ways of creating a Pandas DataFrame. Write Python programs to demonstrate DataFrame creation from (i) a dictionary of lists, (ii) a list of dictionaries, and (iii) a 2D NumPy array.**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Solution (a):

There are multiple ways to construct a Pandas DataFrame:

**(i) From a Dictionary of Lists:**
```python
import pandas as pd
data = {'Name': ['Ram', 'Sham', 'Geeta'], 'Age': [25, 30, 22]}
df1 = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df1)
```

**Output:**
```
    Name  Age
0    Ram   25
1   Sham   30
2  Geeta   22
```

Each **key** in the dictionary becomes a **column name**, and each **list** becomes the column data.

**(ii) From a List of Dictionaries:**
```python
data = [{'Name': 'Ram', 'Age': 25}, {'Name': 'Sham', 'Age': 30}, {'Name': 'Geeta', 'Age': 22}]
df2 = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df2)
```

**Output:**
```
    Name  Age
0    Ram   25
1   Sham   30
2  Geeta   22
```

Each **dictionary** in the list represents one **row**. Missing keys result in `NaN`.

**(iii) From a 2D NumPy Array:**
```python
import numpy as np
arr = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]])
df3 = pd.DataFrame(arr, columns=['X', 'Y'], index=['r1', 'r2', 'r3'])
print(df3)
```

**Output:**
```
    X  Y
r1  1  2
r2  3  4
r3  5  6
```

The NumPy 2D array provides the **values**, while `columns` and `index` arguments provide the labels.

---

#### Solution (b):

**Key Attributes of a Pandas Series:**

| Attribute | Description | Example Output |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| `series.index` | Returns the index (labels) | `Index(['a','b','c'], dtype='object')` |
| `series.values` | Returns values as NumPy array | `array([10, 20, 30])` |
| `series.dtype` | Returns data type | `dtype('int64')` |
| `series.size` | Number of elements | `3` |
| `series.shape` | Tuple of dimensions | `(3,)` |
| `series.ndim` | Number of dimensions | `1` |
| `series.name` | Name of the Series | `'Marks'` |

**Demonstration of Vectorized Arithmetic and Index Alignment:**
```python
import pandas as pd

s1 = pd.Series([10, 20, 30, 40], index=['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'], name='First')
s2 = pd.Series([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], index=['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e'], name='Second')

print("Series 1:\n", s1)
print("\nSeries 2:\n", s2)

print("\nAddition (s1 + s2):")
print(s1 + s2)

print("\nMultiplication (s1 * s2):")
print(s1 * s2)
```

**Output:**
```
Series 1:
 a    10
 b    20
 c    30
 d    40
Name: First, dtype: int64

Series 2:
 a    1
 b    2
 c    3
 d    4
 e    5
Name: Second, dtype: int64

Addition (s1 + s2):
a    11.0
b    22.0
c    33.0
d    44.0
e     NaN
dtype: float64

Multiplication (s1 * s2):
a    10.0
b    40.0
c    90.0
d    160.0
e      NaN
dtype: float64
```

**Explanation:**
- For indices `a, b, c, d` (present in both Series), element-wise arithmetic is performed.
- For index `e` (present only in `s2`), the result is **`NaN`** because `s1` has no corresponding value.
- This automatic alignment based on labels — not positions — is the most powerful feature distinguishing Pandas from NumPy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU ESE Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Series with DataFrame attributes:** Writing `df.ndim = 1` for DataFrame is a common error. Correct: **`DataFrame.ndim = 2`**, **`Series.ndim = 1`**. Examiner will deduct **1 mark** for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting `pd.` alias:** Writing `Series([1,2,3])` instead of `pd.Series([1,2,3])` shows the student has not imported Pandas. Examiner may deduct **0.5–1 mark** depending on strictness.
>
> 3. **Ignoring index alignment:** When asked about Series arithmetic, students often write "element-wise addition" but forget to mention **automatic label-based alignment** and the resulting `NaN` values for unmatched indices. This is a **2-mark** concept and must be explicitly stated.
>
> 4. **Mixing up `loc` and `iloc`:** `df.loc[0]` is **label-based** (works if 0 is a row label), while `df.iloc[0]` is **position-based** (always first row). Examiner expects precise terminology.
>
> 5. **Heterogeneous vs Homogeneous confusion:** A DataFrame can hold **heterogeneous** columns (different dtypes per column). A Series is **homogeneous** (single dtype for all values). This is a frequently tested 2-mark point.
>
> 6. **Panel data structure:** If asked about Panel, students often confuse it with NumPy 3D arrays. Panel is **deprecated** since Pandas v0.25.0 — mention this fact for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Pandas = **Python Data Analysis Library**, built on top of NumPy, created by **Wes McKinney (2008)**.
- ✅ Standard import: `import pandas as pd`.
- ✅ **Three data structures**: **Series (1D)**, **DataFrame (2D)**, **Panel (3D, deprecated)**.
- ✅ **Series** = 1-D labeled homogeneous array → has `index` and `values`.
- ✅ **DataFrame** = 2-D labeled tabular structure → dictionary of Series, has row `index` and `columns`.
- ✅ **DataFrame can hold heterogeneous dtypes**; Series is **homogeneous**.
- ✅ **Key attributes**: `index`, `columns`, `values`, `dtypes`, `shape`, `ndim`, `size`, `axes`.
- ✅ `df.shape` returns `(rows, columns)`; `df.size = rows × columns`.
- ✅ `Series.ndim = 1`; `DataFrame.ndim = 2`; `Panel.ndim = 3`.
- ✅ **Construction methods**:
    - Series: from list, dict, ndarray, scalar
    - DataFrame: from dict of lists, list of dicts, 2D ndarray, CSV file
- ✅ **Automatic index alignment**: Series operations align on labels, not positions → unmatched indices produce `NaN`.
- ✅ **Selection**: `df['col']` → column; `df.loc[label]` → row by label; `df.iloc[pos]` → row by position.
- ✅ **Panel is deprecated** since Pandas v0.25.0; use MultiIndex DataFrames instead.
- ✅ **NaN** (Not a Number) is used to represent missing data in Pandas.
- ✅ Vectorized operations on Series/DataFrame are **element-wise** by default.
- ✅ Each column of a DataFrame is internally a **Series**.
- ✅ Real-world analogy: **Series = Single Excel Column**, **DataFrame = Entire Worksheet**, **Panel = Multiple Worksheets**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Frame Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Frame Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous (mixed data type), tabular data structure** with **labeled axes (rows and columns)**. It is the primary data structure of the **Pandas** library in Python and is conceptually analogous to a dictionary of **Series** objects, a relational database table, or an Excel spreadsheet.

Mathematically, a DataFrame can be defined as a mapping from a set of row labels $L_r = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}$ and column labels $L_c = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_m\}$ to a set of values $V$:

$$D : L_r \times L_c \rightarrow V$$

where each cell value $v_{ij} = D(r_i, c_j)$ represents the intersection of row $r_i$ and column $c_j$. The structure has dimensions $n \times m$, where **n** is the number of rows (observations/records) and **m** is the number of columns (features/variables).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A DataFrame is the **most important** object in Pandas. Every student must master its creation, indexing (`.loc`, `.iloc`), and basic manipulation. It carries **highest weightage** in Module 3 questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spreadsheet View

Imagine an **Excel spreadsheet**:

| | Name | Age | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Anu | 20 | 88 |
| **1** | Raj | 21 | 76 |
| **2** | Meena | 22 | 92 |

That is exactly a DataFrame:
- The **columns** (`Name`, `Age`, `Marks`) are the *features* or *attributes* — each column is essentially a **Series** (a 1-D labeled array).
- The **rows** (`0`, `1`, `2`) are the *records* or *observations*.
- The whole grid is the **DataFrame** — a 2-D labeled table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A **Series** is 1-Dimensional (one column), while a **DataFrame** is 2-Dimensional (rows × columns). A DataFrame is essentially a **collection of Series** that share the same row index.

## 1.3 Why DataFrames Matter in Engineering

DataFrames are the backbone of:
- **Data Science & Machine Learning** — feeding data to scikit-learn, TensorFlow models.
- **Signal Processing** — handling time-series sensor data.
- **IoT Analytics** — processing CSV logs from edge devices.
- **Financial Engineering** — manipulating stock-market tick data.
- **Database Operations** — performing SQL-like joins, merges, and group-bys in memory.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard:** Pandas DataFrames are used in **97% of Python-based data analytics** workflows (per PyData ecosystem surveys). Mastering them is a non-negotiable skill for any KTU 2024 CS/AI engineer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a DataFrame

A DataFrame consists of three principal components, often called its **three-axis anatomy**:

1. **`index`** (row labels) — a Pandas `Index` object of length **n**.
2. **`columns`** (column labels) — a Pandas `Index` object of length **m**.
3. **`values`** (data) — a NumPy 2-D ndarray of shape **n × m**, or a mixture of compatible dtypes when columns have heterogeneous types.

Let the underlying data matrix be $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$. Then:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1m} \\ x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2m} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{nm} \end{bmatrix}$$

Each column $X_{:,j}$ is treated as a **homogeneous Series** sharing the row index $\mathcal{I}$.

## 2.2 Construction Logic (How a DataFrame is Built)

A DataFrame can be created from **any** of the following structures:

| Source | Constructor Call | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Python `dict` of lists/arrays | `pd.DataFrame(dict)` | Most common in KTU exams |
| List of dictionaries | `pd.DataFrame(list_of_dicts)` | JSON-like data |
| 2-D NumPy array | `pd.DataFrame(np_array, columns=[...])` | Pure numerical work |
| CSV file | `pd.read_csv("file.csv")` | Real-world data ingestion |
| Excel file | `pd.read_excel("file.xlsx")` | Office data |
| List of Series | `pd.DataFrame([s1, s2, ...])` | Combining series |
| Another DataFrame | `pd.DataFrame(existing_df)` | Copying/cloning |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — DataFrame Attributes & Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this cheat-sheet.** Every KTU question on DataFrames tests at least one row from this table.

| Attribute / Method | Syntax | Returns | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shape | `df.shape` | Tuple `(n, m)` | Number of rows & columns |
| Size | `df.size` | Integer $n \times m$ | Total number of cells |
| Dimensions | `df.ndim` | Integer `2` | Confirms 2-D structure |
| Column list | `df.columns` | Index of labels | View column names |
| Row index | `df.index` | Index of labels | View row labels |
| Data types | `df.dtypes` | Series of dtypes | Check schema |
| First n rows | `df.head(n)` | DataFrame | Preview top records |
| Last n rows | `df.tail(n)` | DataFrame | Preview bottom records |
| Summary stats | `df.describe()` | DataFrame | Mean, std, min, max |
| Unique values | `df['col'].unique()` | ndarray | Distinct entries |
| Value counts | `df['col'].value_counts()` | Series | Frequency table |
| Transpose | `df.T` | DataFrame | Swap rows & columns |
| Information | `df.info()` | Print to stdout | Schema + memory |

## 2.4 Indexing Algebra (The Heart of DataFrame Access)

KTU exams **love** testing indexing. The two master selectors are:

### 2.4.1 Label-based: `.loc[]`
Accesses by **row label** and **column label**:

$$df.loc[r, c] = X_{i(r), j(c)}$$

where $i(r)$ is the position of row label $r$ and $j(c)$ is the position of column label $c$.

### 2.4.2 Position-based: `.iloc[]`
Accesses by **integer position** (0-indexed):

$$df.iloc[i, j] = X_{i, j}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing `.loc[]` (label) with `.iloc[]` (integer position). If the index is `['a', 'b', 'c']`, then `df.loc['b']` works but `df.loc[1]` is ambiguous and often fails.

## 2.5 Mathematical Operations on DataFrames

When operating with scalars (broadcasting):

$$Y = X + k \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{ij} = x_{ij} + k \quad \forall i,j$$

When operating between two DataFrames (element-wise, requires aligned labels):

$$Z = X + Y \quad \Rightarrow \quad z_{ij} = x_{ij} + y_{ij} \text{ for matched } (i, j)$$

Unmatched indices produce **`NaN`** (Not a Number).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Creation Walkthroughs

### 3.1.1 From a Dictionary of Lists

```python
import pandas as pd

# Step 1: Define the data as a Python dictionary
data = {
    'Name':   ['Anu', 'Raj', 'Meena'],
    'Age':    [20, 21, 22],
    'Marks':  [88, 76, 92]
}

# Step 2: Convert to DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Step 3: Inspect the resulting DataFrame
print(df)
print("Shape:", df.shape)
print("Columns:", df.columns.tolist())
```

**Step-by-step logical derivation of what happens internally:**

1. Pandas receives the dict, identifies **3 keys** → these become column labels.
2. Pandas determines each value is a **list of length 3** → this becomes the number of rows.
3. Pandas builds the index as `RangeIndex(start=0, stop=3, step=1)` automatically.
4. Internally, NumPy arrays are allocated: one per column (for homogeneous dtype storage).
5. The final 2-D wrapper (the `DataFrame` object) is returned with `shape = (3, 3)`.

**Output:**
```
    Name  Age  Marks
0    Anu   20     88
1    Raj   21     76
2  Meena   22     92
Shape: (3, 3)
Columns: ['Name', 'Age', 'Marks']
```

### 3.1.2 From a 2-D NumPy Array

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

arr = np.array([[10, 20, 30],
                [40, 50, 60],
                [70, 80, 90]])

# Explicitly provide row and column labels
df2 = pd.DataFrame(arr,
                   index   = ['R1', 'R2', 'R3'],
                   columns = ['C1', 'C2', 'C3'])

print(df2)
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

1. NumPy creates the raw $3 \times 3$ matrix:

$$arr = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 50 & 60 \\ 70 & 80 & 90 \end{bmatrix}$$

2. Pandas wraps this matrix and attaches:
   - `index = ['R1', 'R2', 'R3']` (length 3, must match `arr.shape[0]`).
   - `columns = ['C1', 'C2', 'C3']` (length 3, must match `arr.shape[1]`).
3. If a mismatch occurs, Pandas raises a `ValueError`.

### 3.1.3 From a List of Dictionaries

```python
records = [
    {'Name': 'Anu',   'Age': 20, 'Marks': 88},
    {'Name': 'Raj',   'Age': 21, 'Marks': 76},
    {'Name': 'Meena', 'Age': 22, 'Marks': 92}
]

df3 = pd.DataFrame(records)
```

**Derivation:** Each dict in the list becomes one row. Pandas performs a **union** of all keys to form the column set; missing keys become `NaN`.

## 3.2 Analytical Indexing Matrix — All Access Patterns

Let us define a sample DataFrame for exhaustive demonstration:

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'A': [1, 2, 3, 4],
    'B': [5, 6, 7, 8],
    'C': [9, 10, 11, 12]
}, index=['w', 'x', 'y', 'z'])
```

The internal representation is:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 9 \\ 2 & 6 & 10 \\ 3 & 7 & 11 \\ 4 & 8 & 12 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{I} = \{w, x, y, z\}, \quad \mathcal{C} = \{A, B, C\}$$

### 3.2.1 Column Selection (Series Extraction)

| Code | Returns | Type | Mathematical Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df['A']` | Column A values | Series | $X_{:, A} = [1, 2, 3, 4]^T$ |
| `df[['A','B']]` | Columns A & B | DataFrame | $X[:, \{A, B\}]$ |
| `df.A` | Column A values | Series | Same as `df['A']` |

**Derivation of `df['A']` step-by-step:**
1. Pandas reads the label `'A'`.
2. It locates `'A'` in `df.columns` at position $j=0$.
3. It slices every row $i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ at column index $0$.
4. Result: 1-D array $[1, 2, 3, 4]$, wrapped as a Series with index $\mathcal{I}$.

### 3.2.2 Row Selection — `.loc[]` (Label-Based)

| Code | Returns | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| `df.loc['w']` | Row `w` as Series | $X_{w,:} = [1, 5, 9]$ |
| `df.loc[['w','y']]` | Rows `w`,`y` | $X_{\{w,y\},:}$ |
| `df.loc['w':'y']` | Rows `w` to `y` (inclusive!) | $X_{\{w,x,y\},:}$ |
| `df.loc['w', 'B']` | Scalar 5 | $X_{w, B}$ |
| `df.loc[['w','y'], ['A','C']]` | Sub-DataFrame | $X_{\{w,y\}, \{A,C\}}$ |

**Step-by-step derivation of `df.loc['x', 'C']`:**
1. Locate label `'x'` in index → position $i=1$.
2. Locate label `'C'` in columns → position $j=2$.
3. Return value $x_{1,2} = 10$.

### 3.2.3 Row/Column Selection — `.iloc[]` (Integer-Based)

| Code | Returns | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| `df.iloc[0]` | First row | $X_{0,:} = [1, 5, 9]$ |
| `df.iloc[0, 2]` | Scalar 9 | $X_{0,2}$ |
| `df.iloc[0:2, 1:3]` | $2 \times 2$ sub-DF | $X_{0:2, 1:3}$ |
| `df.iloc[[0,2], [0,2]]` | Specific positions | $X_{\{0,2\}, \{0,2\}}$ |

**Critical derivation: Why `.loc` includes the endpoint but `.iloc` does not:**

- `.loc['w':'y']` → inclusive on **both** ends because labels are discrete categorical entries.
- `.iloc[0:2]` → Python slice convention: stops at index 2, returns indices $\{0, 1\}$.

## 3.3 Adding and Removing Columns — Algebra

### 3.3.1 Adding a New Column (Feature Engineering)

```python
df['D'] = df['A'] + df['B']
```

**Derivation:**
1. `df['A']` returns Series $\mathcal{S}_A = [1, 2, 3, 4]$.
2. `df['B']` returns Series $\mathcal{S}_B = [5, 6, 7, 8]$.
3. Element-wise addition:

$$\mathcal{S}_A + \mathcal{S}_B = [1+5, 2+6, 3+7, 4+8] = [6, 8, 10, 12]$$

4. New column `'D'` is appended; new shape becomes $(4, 4)$.

### 3.3.2 Deleting a Column

```python
df_new = df.drop('D', axis=1)
# OR
df_new = df.drop(columns=['D'])
```

**Derivation:** `axis=1` refers to columns; Pandas drops the column and re-indexes the remaining columns. Original `df` is **unchanged** unless `inplace=True` is passed.

### 3.3.3 Deleting a Row

```python
df_new = df.drop('x', axis=0)
```

**Derivation:** `axis=0` refers to rows; row labeled `'x'` (which had values $[2, 6, 10]$) is removed. New shape becomes $(3, 4)$.

## 3.4 Boolean Indexing — Conditional Filtering

Boolean indexing is a **filtering operation**:

$$\mathcal{F} = \{ X_i \mid \text{condition}(X_i) = \text{True} \}$$

**Example:**

```python
filtered = df[df['Marks'] > 80]
```

**Step-by-step:**
1. `df['Marks'] > 80` evaluates column-wise:

$$\text{mask} = [88 > 80, \; 76 > 80, \; 92 > 80] = [\text{True}, \text{False}, \text{True}]$$

2. Apply mask to retain only rows where `mask[i] == True`:

$$\text{filtered} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Anu} & 20 & 88 \\ \text{Meena} & 22 & 92 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.5 Summary Statistics — Mathematical Formulas

For a column $X_{:,j}$ with values $x_{1j}, x_{2j}, \dots, x_{nj}$:

$$\text{Mean: } \mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$$

$$\text{Standard Deviation: } \sigma_j = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \mu_j)^2}$$

$$\text{Min: } \min_j = \min_i x_{ij}, \quad \text{Max: } \max_j = \max_i x_{ij}$$

Pandas computes all of these via `df.describe()`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DataFrame High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sources["Data Sources"]
        srcA["Python Dictionary"]
        srcB["NumPy 2D Array"]
        srcC["List of Dicts"]
        srcD["CSV / Excel File"]
        srcE["List of Series"]
    end

    subgraph Constructor["pd.DataFrame Engine"]
        idx["Index Builder"]
        col["Column Builder"]
        dtyp["dtype Inference"]
        mem["Memory Allocator"]
    end

    subgraph Output["DataFrame Object"]
        dfIdx["Index (Row Labels)"]
        dfCol["Columns (Col Labels)"]
        dfVals["Values (2D NumPy Block)"]
        dfMeta["Metadata: dtypes, shape"]
    end

    subgraph Access["Access APIs"]
        a1[".loc[] label-based"]
        a2[".iloc[] position-based"]
        a3["df['col'] column"]
        a4["Boolean mask filter"]
    end

    Sources --> Constructor
    Constructor --> Output
    Output --> Access
    idx --> dfIdx
    col --> dfCol
    dtyp --> dfMeta
    mem --> dfVals
```

## 4.2 DataFrame Memory Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RowIndex["Row Index"]
        ri0["0"]
        ri1["1"]
        ri2["2"]
    end

    subgraph ColA["Column A (Series)"]
        a0["10"]
        a1["20"]
        a2["30"]
    end

    subgraph ColB["Column B (Series)"]
        b0["40"]
        b1["50"]
        b2["60"]
    end

    subgraph ColC["Column C (Series)"]
        c0["70"]
        c1["80"]
        c2["90"]
    end

    RowIndex --- ColA
    ColA --- ColB
    ColB --- ColC
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each column is an **independent Series** sharing the same Row Index. The DataFrame is the **horizontal stacking** of these Series.

## 4.3 Indexing Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need to access data?"] --> q1{"Know the LABEL?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Need multiple rows/cols?"}
    q1 -- No  --> q3{"Know the POSITION?"}
    q2 -- Yes  --> useLoc["Use .loc[row_labels, col_labels]"]
    q2 -- No   --> useLocSingle["Use .loc[label] for one cell"]
    q3 -- Yes  --> useIloc["Use .iloc[i, j]"]
    q3 -- No   --> useMask["Use Boolean Mask df[condition]"]
    useLoc --> result["Return Series or DataFrame"]
    useLocSingle --> result
    useIloc --> result
    useMask --> result
```

## 4.4 DataFrame Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Step A: Create\n(pd.DataFrame)"] --> stepB["Step B: Explore\n(head, info, describe)"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step C: Select\n(.loc, .iloc, mask)"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step D: Modify\n(add/drop columns)"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step E: Aggregate\n(mean, sum, groupby)"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step F: Export\n(to_csv, to_excel)"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Wisdom:** The lifecycle `A → B → C → D → E → F` mirrors the **ETL (Extract-Transform-Load)** pipeline used in every production data system. KTU examiners test this full pipeline in lab exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Pandas DataFrame. How is it different from a Pandas Series?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, labeled tabular data structure** in Pandas, capable of holding columns of **heterogeneous data types** (e.g., int, float, string). It can be visualized as a dictionary of Series objects where each column is a Series sharing a common row index.

**Difference from Series:**

| Feature | Series | DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensions | 1-D | 2-D |
| Structure | Single column | Rows × Columns |
| Number of data types | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous (per column) |
| Shape output | `(n,)` | `(n, m)` |

**(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for tabular comparison.)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between `df.loc[]` and `df.iloc[]` with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `df.loc[]` is a **label-based** indexer: it accepts row and column *names* (labels). Example: `df.loc['row1', 'Age']` returns the value at the row labeled `'row1'` and column `'Age'`.
- `df.iloc[]` is a **position-based** indexer: it accepts integer indices (0-indexed). Example: `df.iloc[0, 1]` returns the value at the first row and second column.

Slicing behavior also differs:
- `df.loc['a':'c']` is **inclusive** of both endpoints.
- `df.iloc[0:3]` is **exclusive** of the endpoint (Python slice convention).

**(3 marks: 1.5 for distinction + 1 for example + 0.5 for slice behavior.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Explain the different ways to create a Pandas DataFrame in Python. Write a program to create a DataFrame from a dictionary of lists and display its shape, columns, and data types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the use of `.loc[]`, `.iloc[]`, and boolean indexing on a DataFrame containing student records. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-A

#### Part (a) — DataFrame Creation Methods (7 marks)

**Methods to create a DataFrame:**

1. **From a Python Dictionary of Lists/Arrays** — Most common. Keys become column names; values become column data.
2. **From a List of Dictionaries** — Each dict becomes one row.
3. **From a 2-D NumPy Array** — Provides explicit control over both labels and values.
4. **From External Files** — `pd.read_csv()`, `pd.read_excel()` for ingestion.
5. **From a List of Series** — Combines multiple Series into a table.

**Program:**

```python
import pandas as pd

# Create DataFrame from a dictionary of lists
data = {
    'Name':   ['Anu', 'Raj', 'Meena', 'Kiran'],
    'Age':    [20, 21, 22, 20],
    'Marks':  [88, 76, 92, 85],
    'Grade':  ['B',  'C',  'A',  'B']
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Display basic attributes
print("DataFrame:\n", df)
print("\nShape:", df.shape)
print("Columns:", df.columns.tolist())
print("\nData Types:\n", df.dtypes)
```

**Output:**

```
DataFrame:
     Name  Age  Marks Grade
0    Anu   20     88     B
1    Raj   21     76     C
2  Meena   22     92     A
3  Kiran   20     85     B

Shape: (4, 4)
Columns: ['Name', 'Age', 'Marks', 'Grade']
Data Types:
 Name     object
Age       int64
Marks     int64
Grade    object
```

**Explanation of output:**
- `df.shape` → `(4, 4)` because there are 4 students and 4 attributes.
- `df.columns` → returns the column labels as a Pandas Index.
- `df.dtypes` → `object` dtype indicates string values, `int64` for integers.

**(Marks: 2 for listing methods + 4 for program + 1 for output explanation = 7 marks.)**

---

#### Part (b) — Indexing Demonstrations (7 marks)

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name':   ['Anu', 'Raj', 'Meena', 'Kiran'],
    'Age':    [20, 21, 22, 20],
    'Marks':  [88, 76, 92, 85],
    'Grade':  ['B',  'C',  'A',  'B']
}, index=['s1', 's2', 's3', 's4'])

print("Original DataFrame:\n", df)

# ----- .loc[] (Label-based) -----
print("\n.loc['s2']:\n", df.loc['s2'])
print("\n.loc[['s1','s3'], ['Name','Marks']]:\n",
       df.loc[['s1','s3'], ['Name','Marks']])

# ----- .iloc[] (Position-based) -----
print("\n.iloc[0]:\n", df.iloc[0])
print("\n.iloc[0:2, 1:3]:\n", df.iloc[0:2, 1:3])

# ----- Boolean Indexing -----
high_scorers = df[df['Marks'] > 80]
print("\nStudents with Marks > 80:\n", high_scorers)

young_toppers = df[(df['Age'] <= 21) & (df['Marks'] >= 85)]
print("\nYoung Toppers:\n", young_toppers)
```

**Outputs:**

```
.loc['s2']:
 Name      Raj
Age        21
Marks      76
Grade       C
Name: s2, dtype: object

.loc[['s1','s3'], ['Name','Marks']]:
     Name  Marks
s1    Anu     88
s3  Meena     92

.iloc[0:2, 1:3]:
    Age  Marks
s1   20     88
s2   21     76

Students with Marks > 80:
     Name  Age  Marks Grade
s1    Anu   20     88     B
s3  Meena   22     92     A
s4  Kiran   20     85     B

Young Toppers:
     Name  Age  Marks Grade
s1    Anu   20     88     B
s4  Kiran   20     85     B
```

**Logical Explanation:**
- `.loc['s2']` returns the row labeled `s2` as a Series.
- `.loc[['s1','s3'], ['Name','Marks']]` is a 2-D selection by labels — note `Marks > 80` filter excludes `s2` (Raj, 76).
- `.iloc[0:2, 1:3]` slices the first 2 rows and middle 2 columns by position.
- The boolean mask `df['Marks'] > 80` evaluates to `[True, False, True, True]`, retaining only those rows.
- Combined conditions use `&` (and), `|` (or) with parentheses around each clause.

**(Marks: 2 for .loc + 2 for .iloc + 2 for boolean + 1 for explanation = 7 marks.)**

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` (ALTERNATIVE)
**(a)** What is a DataFrame? Explain the internal structure of a DataFrame with a neat diagram. List any **five** essential attributes of a DataFrame. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to:
  (i) Create a DataFrame of products with columns `['Product', 'Price', 'Quantity']` containing at least 5 records.
  (ii) Add a new column `'Total'` computed as `Price × Quantity`.
  (iii) Display records where `Quantity > 10` and `Total > 500`.
  (iv) Sort the DataFrame by `'Total'` in descending order and display the top 3 rows. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-B

**Part (a) — Internal Structure (7 marks)**

A DataFrame internally consists of **three components**:

1. **Index (axis 0):** Row labels stored in a Pandas `Index` object.
2. **Columns (axis 1):** Column labels stored in a Pandas `Index` object.
3. **Values:** A 2-D NumPy block (or a list of 1-D blocks for heterogeneous data) holding the actual cell values.

Additionally, each column has its own **`dtype`** recorded in the DataFrame's metadata.

**Diagram:**

```
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                      DataFrame                        |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|  Index  |  Product   |  Price  |  Quantity |  Total   |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|   p1    |  Laptop    |  50000  |    5      |  250000  |
|   p2    |  Mouse     |    500  |   20      |   10000  |
|   p3    |  Keyboard  |   1500  |   12      |   18000  |
|   p4    |  Monitor   |  12000  |    8      |   96000  |
|   p5    |  Headphone |   2000  |   15      |   30000  |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
       |         |          |          |          |
       +---------+----------+----------+----------+
              Shared Row Index + 4 Column Series
```

**Five Essential Attributes:**

| Attribute | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `df.shape` | `(5, 4)` | Rows × Columns |
| `df.size` | `20` | Total cell count |
| `df.ndim` | `2` | Number of dimensions |
| `df.dtypes` | Series of dtypes | Column data types |
| `df.columns` | Index of labels | Column names |

**(Marks: 2 for definition + 2 for diagram + 3 for attribute table = 7 marks.)**

**Part (b) — Programmatic Implementation (7 marks)**

```python
import pandas as pd

# (i) Create DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Product':  ['Laptop', 'Mouse', 'Keyboard', 'Monitor', 'Headphone'],
    'Price':    [50000, 500, 1500, 12000, 2000],
    'Quantity': [5, 20, 12, 8, 15]
})

# (ii) Add 'Total' column
df['Total'] = df['Price'] * df['Quantity']
print("DataFrame with Total:\n", df)

# (iii) Filter records
filtered = df[(df['Quantity'] > 10) & (df['Total'] > 500)]
print("\nFiltered Records:\n", filtered)

# (iv) Sort by Total descending and show top 3
sorted_df = df.sort_values(by='Total', ascending=False)
print("\nTop 3 by Total:\n", sorted_df.head(3))
```

**Expected Output (abridged):**

```
DataFrame with Total:
     Product  Price  Quantity   Total
0    Laptop  50000         5  250000
1     Mouse    500        20   10000
2  Keyboard   1500        12   18000
3   Monitor  12000         8   96000
4 Headphone   2000        15   30000

Filtered Records:
     Product  Price  Quantity   Total
1     Mouse    500        20   10000
2  Keyboard   1500        12   18000
4 Headphone   2000        15   30000

Top 3 by Total:
     Product  Price  Quantity   Total
0    Laptop  50000         5  250000
3   Monitor  12000         8   96000
4 Headphone   2000        15   30000
```

**Explanation:**
- `df['Price'] * df['Quantity']` performs element-wise multiplication between two Series, yielding a new Series.
- `&` combines two boolean conditions; each condition must be in parentheses.
- `sort_values()` accepts `by=` (column) and `ascending=False` for descending order.
- `head(3)` returns the first 3 rows of the sorted DataFrame.

**(Marks: 1.5 for creation + 1.5 for new column + 2 for filter + 2 for sort+head = 7 marks.)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (DataFrame Basics):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `.loc[]` and `.iloc[]`** — `.loc[]` takes **labels**; `.iloc[]` takes **integer positions**. Using the wrong one gives either an error or wrong data. *Loss: 2-3 marks per question.*
> 
> 2. **Forgetting parentheses around boolean conditions** — `df[df['Age']>20 & df['Marks']>80]` raises an error. Must write `df[(df['Age']>20) & (df['Marks']>80)]`. *Loss: 1-2 marks.*
> 
> 3. **Using `df.drop()` without `axis=`** — Pandas cannot guess whether you mean row or column. Always specify `axis=0` (row) or `axis=1` (column). *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to `import pandas as pd`** — A silly mistake that loses a full mark.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning `inplace=True`** — When modifying in place, students forget this argument. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up `shape` with `size`** — `shape` is `(n, m)`; `size` is `n*m`. Many students write them interchangeably. *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — DataFrame Basics**

- ⭐ A **DataFrame** is a **2-D, labeled, size-mutable** tabular structure with **potentially heterogeneous** columns.
- ⭐ It is the **primary object** in the **Pandas** library — built on top of NumPy.
- ⭐ **Series** = 1-D labeled array (single column). **DataFrame** = collection of Series sharing the same row index.
- ⭐ **Five constructors** to remember: `dict`, `list of dicts`, `2-D ndarray`, `list of Series`, and `read_csv/read_excel`.
- ⭐ **Three internal components:** `index`, `columns`, `values`.
- ⭐ **Two indexers:** `.loc[]` (label) and `.iloc[]` (integer position). Slicing in `.loc` is **inclusive**; slicing in `.iloc` is **exclusive** at the end.
- ⭐ **Boolean indexing** uses a mask: `df[condition]`. Combine with `&` (and), `|` (or), `~` (not).
- ⭐ **Add column:** `df['new'] = ...`. **Drop column:** `df.drop('col', axis=1)`. **Drop row:** `df.drop('row', axis=0)`.
- ⭐ **Key attributes:** `df.shape`, `df.size`, `df.ndim`, `df.dtypes`, `df.columns`, `df.index`, `df.values`.
- ⭐ **Exploration trio:** `df.head(n)`, `df.tail(n)`, `df.info()`, `df.describe()`.
- ⭐ **Default index** is `RangeIndex(0, n-1)`; can be replaced with custom labels via `index=[...]`.
- ⭐ **Numerical operations** are element-wise and produce `NaN` for unaligned labels.
- ⭐ **Sorting:** `df.sort_values(by='col', ascending=False)`. **Top-k:** `df.head(k)`.
- ⭐ **Formula:** Mean $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$; Standard Deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i - \mu)^2}$.
- ⭐ **Engineering impact:** DataFrames drive **ETL pipelines, ML preprocessing, IoT analytics, and financial modeling** — they are the universal Python data structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Expect at least one **14-mark question** that combines DataFrame creation + indexing + filtering + column manipulation. Practice the four-question template (B-B above) thoroughly — it covers **80% of asked questions**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Deep Learning with Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Deep Learning with Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Deep Learning?

**Deep Learning (DL)** is a specialized sub-field of **Machine Learning (ML)** that uses multi-layered **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)** to automatically learn hierarchical representations of data. Unlike traditional ML algorithms that depend on hand-crafted features, deep learning algorithms **discover** intrinsic features directly from raw data by progressively composing simpler concepts into more abstract ones.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Aligned with Syllabus):**
> "Deep Learning is a class of machine learning algorithms that uses multiple layers of non-linear processing units for feature extraction and transformation, learning from data in a supervised or unsupervised manner."

The term **"deep"** refers to the presence of **multiple hidden layers** between the input and output layers, which is the architectural differentiator from shallow neural networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the practical implementation of deep learning models using **Python libraries** such as **TensorFlow**, **Keras**, and **PyTorch**. Students must be able to define, train, and evaluate basic DL models.

## 1.2 Core Building Blocks of Deep Learning

| Component | Description | Real-World Role |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Neuron (Node)** | The fundamental computational unit | Mimics a biological brain cell |
| **Weights (W)** | Learnable parameters that scale inputs | Represent the "importance" of each input |
| **Bias (b)** | A learnable constant added to the input sum | Allows shifting the activation function |
| **Activation Function** | A non-linear transformation | Introduces non-linearity for complex pattern learning |
| **Layer** | A collection of neurons operating at the same depth | Building block of the network architecture |
| **Loss Function** | Measures prediction error | The "compass" guiding weight optimization |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Courtroom Jury

Imagine a **courtroom jury** deciding a verdict (the output). Each juror (neuron) listens to evidence (inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$). However, not all evidence is equally important — some pieces are given more weight (weights $w_i$). After summing up all the weighted evidence and adding a personal predisposition (bias $b$), the juror casts a vote through a "decision rule" (activation function).

Now picture **multiple rounds of deliberation** (hidden layers), where jurors in each round refine the previous round's interpretation. The final verdict is compared against the **true judgment** (loss function), and the jury learns from its mistakes by adjusting how much each piece of evidence influenced the decision (gradient descent). This is precisely how a deep neural network learns!

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of a deep network as a **multi-stage filter** — early layers detect simple patterns (edges, sounds), middle layers detect motifs (shapes, phonemes), and deep layers detect high-level concepts (faces, words).

## 1.4 The Perceptron — The Smallest Neural Unit

The **Perceptron**, invented by **Frank Rosenblatt in 1958**, is the simplest neural network consisting of a single neuron. It forms the foundation upon which all deep learning architectures are built.

Mathematically, a perceptron performs the following computation:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

$$y = f(z)$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the **activation function** (e.g., step function historically, sigmoid/ReLU in modern deep learning).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **perceptron's step activation** with modern deep learning activations. Modern deep networks **never** use the step function because it is non-differentiable, making gradient-based learning impossible. Always use smooth activations like Sigmoid, Tanh, or ReLU.

## 1.5 Why "Deep" Learning Works

Deep learning excels because of three synergistic factors:

1. **Massive Data Availability** — Internet-scale datasets (ImageNet, Common Crawl, etc.) provide sufficient training samples.
2. **Computational Power** — GPUs and TPUs enable parallel matrix operations required for deep networks.
3. **Algorithmic Innovations** — ReLU, Batch Normalization, Dropout, Residual Connections, and Adam optimizer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Deep Learning Pipeline — Operational Walkthrough

A typical deep learning workflow follows these structured steps:

1. **Data Acquisition & Preprocessing** — Collect, clean, normalize, and split the data into training, validation, and test sets.
2. **Model Definition** — Specify the architecture (number of layers, neurons per layer, activation functions).
3. **Loss Function Selection** — Choose a metric quantifying prediction error (MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification).
4. **Optimizer Configuration** — Select the optimization algorithm (SGD, Adam, RMSprop) and learning rate $\alpha$.
5. **Forward Propagation** — Pass input data through the network to compute predictions.
6. **Loss Computation** — Compare predictions against ground truth labels.
7. **Backward Propagation (Backprop)** — Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every weight using the **chain rule of calculus**.
8. **Parameter Update** — Adjust weights using gradient descent: $W \leftarrow W - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$.
9. **Iteration** — Repeat steps 5–8 over many epochs until convergence.
10. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Test on unseen data and deploy the trained model.

## 2.2 Forward Propagation — The Prediction Engine

Forward propagation computes the network's output by passing inputs layer by layer. For a single neuron in layer $l$:

$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \cdot a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$

$$a^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

where $a^{(l-1)}$ is the activation output from the previous layer, $W^{(l)}$ is the weight matrix, $b^{(l)}$ is the bias vector, and $f^{(l)}$ is the activation function for layer $l$.

## 2.3 Backpropagation — The Learning Engine

Backpropagation uses the **chain rule of calculus** to efficiently compute gradients. Starting from the output layer and moving backward:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

This recursive gradient computation is what makes training deep networks computationally tractable.

## 2.4 The Three Pillars: Loss, Gradient, Update

- **Loss Function** $L(\hat{y}, y)$ measures how wrong the prediction is.
- **Gradient** $\nabla_W L$ indicates the direction of steepest ascent in the loss landscape.
- **Update Rule** moves weights in the opposite direction of the gradient to minimize loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Backpropagation is essentially an **efficient implementation of the chain rule** specifically tailored for computational graphs. Frameworks like **PyTorch** (autograd) and **TensorFlow** (GradientTape) automate this entirely — students should understand the *math* but appreciate the *automation*.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table contains every formula you are expected to know for the ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the symbols, derivatives, and use-cases.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Neuron Pre-activation | $z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Weighted sum plus bias (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Neuron Activation | $a = f(z)$ | Non-linear transformation output |
| 3 | Sigmoid Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output range: $(0, 1)$; used for binary classification |
| 4 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\left(1 - \sigma(z)\right)$ | Maximum value: $0.25$ at $z = 0$ |
| 5 | Tanh Function | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Output range: $(-1, 1)$; zero-centered |
| 6 | Tanh Derivative | $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | Maximum value: $1.0$ at $z = 0$ |
| 7 | ReLU Function | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Most popular for hidden layers |
| 8 | ReLU Derivative | $\text{ReLU}'(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \le 0 \end{cases}$ | Avoids vanishing gradient for $z > 0$ |
| 9 | Softmax Function | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability distribution |
| 10 | Mean Squared Error | $L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^{2}$ | Loss for regression (units: squared target) |
| 11 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $L_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Loss for binary classification |
| 12 | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $L_{\text{CCE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Loss for multi-class classification |
| 13 | Gradient Descent Update | $W \leftarrow W - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ | Standard weight update rule |
| 14 | SGD with Momentum | $v \leftarrow \beta v + \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}, \quad W \leftarrow W - v$ | Accelerates convergence ($\beta \approx 0.9$) |
| 15 | Adam Update | $m \leftarrow \beta_1 m + (1-\beta_1)g$, $v \leftarrow \beta_2 v + (1-\beta_2)g^{2}$, $W \leftarrow W - \alpha \frac{\hat{m}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \epsilon}$ | Adaptive learning rate optimizer |
| 16 | Softmax Derivative | $\frac{\partial \text{softmax}(z_i)}{\partial z_j} = \text{softmax}(z_i)(\delta_{ij} - \text{softmax}(z_j))$ | $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta |
| 17 | Universal Approximation | A feedforward network with one hidden layer and finite neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact set | Theoretical foundation of DL |
| 18 | Learning Rate Range | $10^{-4} \le \alpha \le 10^{-1}$ | Typical practical range for stable training |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deep learning is not an academic curiosity — it powers mission-critical systems across industries:

- **Healthcare:** Tumor detection in MRI scans, drug discovery, protein folding (AlphaFold).
- **Automotive:** Self-driving cars (Tesla, Waymo) using CNNs for perception.
- **Natural Language Processing:** ChatGPT, Google Translate, sentiment analysis.
- **Finance:** Fraud detection, algorithmic trading, credit scoring.
- **Agriculture:** Crop disease detection, yield prediction, precision farming.
- **Manufacturing:** Predictive maintenance, defect detection, quality control.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** When a 14-mark question asks for "applications," always list **at least 4 domains** with one specific use-case each. Examiners award marks for domain diversity, not just listing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Perceptron Geometric Decision Boundary

**Problem Setup:** A single perceptron with two inputs $x_1, x_2$ and weights $w_1, w_2$ and bias $b$ implements a binary classifier. We want to find the **decision boundary equation**.

**Step 1:** The perceptron computes a pre-activation:

$$z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b$$

**Step 2:** For the step activation, the output is $1$ if $z > 0$ and $0$ otherwise. The decision boundary is therefore the locus where $z = 0$:

$$w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $x_2$ as a function of $x_1$ to obtain the explicit line equation:

$$x_2 = -\frac{w_1}{w_2} x_1 - \frac{b}{w_2}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as the standard line equation $y = mx + c$ form, where the slope is $m = -\frac{w_1}{w_2}$ and the intercept is $c = -\frac{b}{w_2}$.

**Interpretation:** The perceptron learns a **linear decision boundary** in the input feature space. This is why a single perceptron cannot solve the XOR problem — XOR is not linearly separable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Descent Update Rule from First Principles

**Goal:** Minimize the loss function $L(W)$ by iteratively updating the weights $W$.

**Step 1 — Taylor Series Expansion:** For a small perturbation $\Delta W$ around the current weight $W$:

$$L(W + \Delta W) \approx L(W) + \nabla_W L \cdot \Delta W + \frac{1}{2} \nabla_W^{2} L \cdot (\Delta W)^{2} + \dots$$

**Step 2 — First-Order Approximation:** Ignoring higher-order terms (valid for small $\Delta W$):

$$L(W + \Delta W) \approx L(W) + \nabla_W L \cdot \Delta W$$

**Step 3 — Steepest Descent Direction:** The gradient $\nabla_W L$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** (i.e., where $L$ increases most rapidly). To minimize $L$, we must move in the **opposite direction**:

$$\Delta W = -\alpha \cdot \nabla_W L$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the **learning rate** controlling step size.

**Step 4 — Update Rule:** Substituting $\Delta W$ back:

$$W_{\text{new}} = W_{\text{old}} + \Delta W = W_{\text{old}} - \alpha \cdot \nabla_W L$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{new}} = W_{\text{old}} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}}$$

**Step 5 — Convergence Analysis:** For convex loss surfaces, gradient descent is guaranteed to converge to the global minimum if $\alpha$ is sufficiently small (satisfies the **Lipschitz condition**: $\alpha < \frac{2}{L}$ where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of $\nabla L$). For non-convex surfaces (typical in deep learning), it converges to a local minimum or saddle point.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Backpropagation for a Single Neuron with Sigmoid Activation

**Setup:** Consider a network with one hidden layer. Let:
- $x$ = input
- $W_1, b_1$ = weights and bias of hidden layer
- $W_2, b_2$ = weights and bias of output layer
- $\sigma$ = sigmoid activation
- $L$ = Mean Squared Error loss: $L = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^{2}$

**Step 1 — Forward Pass through Hidden Layer:**

$$z_1 = W_1 x + b_1$$

$$a_1 = \sigma(z_1)$$

**Step 2 — Forward Pass through Output Layer:**

$$z_2 = W_2 a_1 + b_2$$

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z_2)$$

**Step 3 — Loss Computation:**

$$L = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Output Pre-activation $z_2$** (Chain Rule):

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z_2} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z_2)$$

Since $\sigma'(z_2) = \sigma(z_2)(1 - \sigma(z_2)) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

**Step 5 — Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Output Weights $W_2$:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_2} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} \cdot \frac{\partial z_2}{\partial W_2} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} \cdot a_1$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_2} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot a_1$$

**Step 6 — Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Hidden Layer Pre-activation $z_1$** (Continuing the Chain Rule):

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} \cdot \frac{\partial z_2}{\partial a_1} \cdot \frac{\partial a_1}{\partial z_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} \cdot W_2 \cdot \sigma'(z_1)$$

Substituting $\sigma'(z_1) = a_1(1 - a_1)$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_1} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot W_2 \cdot a_1(1 - a_1)$$

**Step 7 — Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Hidden Layer Weights $W_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_1} \cdot \frac{\partial z_1}{\partial W_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_1} \cdot x$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_1} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot W_2 \cdot a_1(1 - a_1) \cdot x$$

**Step 8 — Parameter Updates:**

$$W_2 \leftarrow W_2 - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_2}$$

$$W_1 \leftarrow W_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_1}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Vanishing Gradient in Sigmoid Networks

**Step 1:** Recall the sigmoid derivative:

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$$

**Step 2:** The maximum value occurs at $z = 0$, where $\sigma(0) = 0.5$:

$$\sigma'(0) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$$

**Step 3:** In a deep network with $L$ layers, the gradient flowing back to the earliest layer is multiplied by $\sigma'$ at each layer. In the worst case (all derivatives at maximum $0.25$):

$$\left|\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(1)}}\right| \le 0.25^{L-1} \cdot \left|\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(L)}}\right|$$

**Step 4:** For $L = 10$ layers:

$$0.25^{9} = \frac{1}{4^{9}} = \frac{1}{262144} \approx 3.8 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5:** This near-zero gradient means the weights in early layers barely update — the network **cannot learn** effectively. This is the **vanishing gradient problem**, which motivated the adoption of **ReLU activation** (whose derivative is $1$ for positive inputs, eliminating the multiplicative decay).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Activation | Recommended Loss | Recommended Optimizer |
|----------|------------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Binary Classification (Output Layer) | Sigmoid | Binary Cross-Entropy | Adam |
| Multi-class Classification (Output Layer) | Softmax | Categorical Cross-Entropy | Adam |
| Hidden Layers (General) | ReLU | — | Adam or SGD + Momentum |
| Regression (Output Layer) | Linear (no activation) | MSE or Huber | Adam |
| Image Classification (Hidden) | ReLU / Leaky ReLU | CCE | Adam |
| RNN / LSTM Hidden State | Tanh | — | RMSprop or Adam |
| Output Range $(-1, 1)$ | Tanh | — | — |



## 4.3 Single Neuron Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    X2[x2] -->|w2| SUM
    X3[x3] -->|w3| SUM

    SUM[Summation Sigma] --> Z[Pre-activation z]
    Z --> ACT[Activation f]
    ACT --> OUT[Output a]
```

## 4.4 Training Loop Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Initialize Weights Randomly] --> P2[Shuffle Training Data]
    P2 --> P3[For Each Mini-batch]
    P3 --> P4[Forward Pass Predict]
```

```mermaid

    end

        M2[Hidden Layer Stack]

    end

    end

        E3[Visualization Logger]
    end
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    D4 --> M1
    M1 --> M2 --> M3
    M3 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    T4 -->|updated weights| M2
    M3 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
```

## 4.6 Perceptron Learning Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> INIT[Initialize weights to 0 or small random]
    INIT --> LOOP[For each training epoch]
    LOOP --> SAMPLE[For each input sample]
    SAMPLE --> COMPUTE[Compute output y_pred]
    COMPUTE --> ERR[Calculate error e equals y_true minus y_pred]
    ERR --> UPDATE[Update weights w_i plus learning rate times e times x_i]
    UPDATE --> CHECK{All samples processed}
    CHECK -->|No| SAMPLE
    CHECK -->|Yes| CONV{Convergence criteria met}
    CONV -->|No| LOOP
    CONV -->|Yes| FIN[Final Trained Model]
```
