# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> 
> **Define Regularization. List and briefly explain any two types of regularization techniques used in machine learning.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Regularization is a technique used in machine learning to prevent overfitting by adding a penalty term to the cost function that discourages complex models with excessively large parameter values. The general form is $J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = J_0(\theta) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$, where $\lambda$ controls the strength of regularization.

**Two Types:**

1. **L2 Regularization (Ridge Regression):** Adds the squared magnitude of parameters as the penalty term, $\Omega(\theta) = \sum \theta_i^2$. It shrinks parameters smoothly towards zero but does not force them to be exactly zero. It is computationally efficient and has a closed-form solution.

2. **L1 Regularization (Lasso Regression):** Adds the absolute magnitude of parameters as the penalty term, $\Omega(\theta) = \sum \mid \theta_i \mid$. It produces **sparse models** where many parameters become exactly zero, effectively performing **automatic feature selection**. It is useful when interpretability is important.

---

### Question 2

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> 
> **Explain the Bias-Variance Tradeoff in the context of regularization. How does the hyperparameter $\lambda$ control this tradeoff?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** decomposes the total expected error of a model into three components:

$$\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

- **Bias** represents the error from wrong model assumptions (underfitting). High bias models are too simple.
- **Variance** represents the error from sensitivity to fluctuations in training data (overfitting). High variance models are too complex.
- **Irreducible Noise** is the inherent randomness in the data that no model can eliminate.

**Role of $\lambda$:**
- As $\lambda \to 0$: No regularization, low bias but high variance (overfitting).
- As $\lambda \to \infty$: Excessive regularization, high bias but low variance (underfitting).
- **Optimal $\lambda$**: Found through cross-validation, balances bias and variance to minimize the total expected error on unseen data.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to exclude the bias term $\theta_0$** from the regularization penalty. This is incorrect; by convention, only the feature weights $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ are penalized.
> - Many students confuse the **$\lambda$ hyperparameter** with the **learning rate $\alpha$**. They are independent: $\lambda$ controls regularization strength, $\alpha$ controls step size in gradient descent.
> - Students often write L1 and L2 penalties without explaining **why** they differ in producing sparse versus dense solutions. Always mention the **kink at zero** for L1.
> - Forgetting to mention **cross-validation** for choosing $\lambda$ loses 1–2 marks.
> - In derivations, students skip the **factor of $\frac{1}{2}$** justification. Always explain its role in cancelling the derivative factor of 2.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 Internal Choice Set A]**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule for L2 regularized linear regression. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Compare L1 and L2 regularization in detail. Discuss their geometric interpretation and provide a real-world scenario where each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Define the L2 regularized cost function for linear regression with $m$ examples and $n$ features:

$$J_{\text{L2}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_{j}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $\theta_j$ (for $j \geq 1$):

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{L2}}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda \theta_j}{m}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the gradient descent update:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)} + \lambda \theta_j \right]$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange into the "weight decay" form:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j \left( 1 - \alpha \frac{\lambda}{m} \right) - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}$$

**Key Insight:** The factor $\left( 1 - \alpha \frac{\lambda}{m} \right)$ is slightly less than 1, causing $\theta_j$ to decay exponentially unless the gradient term provides sufficient counter-pressure. This is why L2 regularization is also called **"Weight Decay."**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table (Students should reproduce):**

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty | $\lambda \sum \mid \theta_i \mid$ | $\lambda \sum \theta_i^2$ |
| Sparsity | **Yes** (some $\theta_i = 0$) | **No** |
| Feature selection | **Yes** | **No** |
| Geometric shape | Diamond ($\ell_1$ ball) | Sphere ($\ell_2$ ball) |
| First contact with contours | At a corner (sparse) | On boundary (dense) |

**Geometric Interpretation:**
The optimization problem can be visualized as finding the point where the elliptical contours of the empirical loss first touch the constraint region. The L1 diamond has **sharp corners** that align with the axes, naturally producing solutions where some parameters are exactly zero. The L2 sphere is **smooth** and has no corners, so contact typically occurs at a non-axis point where all parameters are nonzero.

**Real-World Scenarios:**
- **L1 is best for:** Genomics, where we have thousands of gene features but suspect only a handful are disease-relevant. L1 automatically selects the most discriminative genes, producing an interpretable diagnostic model.
- **L2 is best for:** Multicollinear financial data (e.g., correlated macroeconomic indicators), where we want to retain all features but stabilize the coefficient estimates against noise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 Internal Choice Set B]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the concept of overfitting. How does regularization address it? Discuss with a suitable mathematical formulation. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** What is Elastic Net Regularization? Derive its gradient update rule and explain when it is preferred over pure L1 or pure L2 regularization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Overfitting:**
Overfitting occurs when a model learns the noise and random fluctuations in the training data to such an extent that it **negatively impacts the model's performance on new, unseen data**. The hallmark of overfitting is:
- **Low training error** (the model fits the training data very well)
- **High validation/test error** (the model fails to generalize)

**Mathematical Indicator:** Overfitting is detected when the validation loss $J_{\text{val}}(\theta)$ begins to **increase** while the training loss $J_{\text{train}}(\theta)$ continues to **decrease** during training iterations.

**How Regularization Addresses It:**
Regularization adds a penalty term to the cost function that **discourages the model from learning overly complex patterns**. The mathematical formulation is:

$$J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \underbrace{J_0(\theta)}_{\text{Training Loss}} + \underbrace{\lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)}_{\text{Complexity Penalty}}$$

**Mechanism:** The penalty term $\lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$ grows with the magnitude of the parameters $\theta$. This forces the optimizer to find a **trade-off** between fitting the training data well and keeping the parameters small. Small parameters correspond to **smoother, less oscillatory** decision functions, which generalize better to unseen data.

**Effect of $\lambda$:** By tuning $\lambda$, we can dial the model's effective complexity — small $\lambda$ allows overfitting, large $\lambda$ causes underfitting, optimal $\lambda$ achieves the best generalization.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition:** Elastic Net Regularization linearly combines L1 and L2 penalties using a mixing parameter $p \in [0, 1]$:

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\theta) = J_0(\theta) + \lambda \left[ p \Vert\theta\Vert_1 + (1-p) \Vert\theta\Vert_2^{2} \right]$$

**Expanded Form:**

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\theta) = J_0(\theta) + \lambda p \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mid \theta_j \mid + \lambda(1-p) \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^{2}$$

**Gradient Derivation:**

For $j \geq 1$, differentiating with respect to $\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{EN}}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial J_0}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda p \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j) + 2\lambda(1-p) \theta_j$$

**Subgradient Descent Update Rule:**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \frac{\partial J_0}{\partial \theta_j} + \lambda p \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j) + 2\lambda(1-p) \theta_j \right]$$

**When Elastic Net is Preferred:**

- **Over pure L1**: When features are **highly correlated** (e.g., multicollinear gene expressions). L1 would arbitrarily pick one from a correlated group, ignoring useful information. The L2 component of Elastic Net encourages **grouping behavior**, where correlated features are either all included or all excluded together.

- **Over pure L2**: When we still want **sparsity and feature selection** (which pure L2 cannot provide). The L1 component ensures that irrelevant features are driven to exactly zero.

- **Practical Use Case**: In **genomics and bioinformatics**, where the number of features (genes) vastly exceeds the number of samples, and groups of correlated genes are biologically meaningful, Elastic Net is the standard choice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Regularization**

- **Core Definition**: Regularization adds a penalty term $\lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta)$ to the cost function to prevent overfitting and improve generalization.

- **L1 (Lasso)**: Penalty is $\lambda \sum \mid \theta_j \mid$. Produces **sparse** solutions. Performs **automatic feature selection**. Non-differentiable at zero. Geometric shape is a **diamond**.

- **L2 (Ridge)**: Penalty is $\lambda \sum \theta_j^{2}$. Produces **dense** solutions. Shrinks parameters smoothly. Always differentiable. Geometric shape is a **sphere**. Also called **Weight Decay** in neural networks.

- **Elastic Net**: Hybrid of L1 and L2 with mixing parameter $p$. Combines sparsity (L1) with grouping effect (L2). Best for correlated high-dimensional data.

- **Hyperparameter $\lambda$**: Controls regularization strength. $\lambda = 0$ means no regularization. $\lambda \to \infty$ means excessive regularization (underfitting). Optimal $\lambda$ is found via **K-fold cross-validation**.

- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: $\lambda$ increases bias but decreases variance. The goal is to find the $\lambda$ that minimizes total expected error.

- **Bayesian Interpretation**: L2 $\iff$ Gaussian prior on parameters. L1 $\iff$ Laplace (double-exponential) prior on parameters. The sharp peak of the Laplace distribution at zero explains why L1 produces sparse solutions.

- **Weight Decay Interpretation**: L2 gradient update can be rewritten as $\theta_j := \theta_j(1 - \alpha\lambda/m) - \alpha \nabla J_0$. The factor $(1 - \alpha\lambda/m)$ causes exponential decay of weights.

- **Soft-Thresholding (L1 closed form for orthogonal $X$)**: $\theta_j^{*} = \text{soft}(\theta_j^{\text{OLS}}, \lambda/m)$ where the soft function zeros out small coefficients.

- **Dropout (Neural Network Regularization)**: Randomly zeroes out neurons during training with probability $p$. Equivalent to ensemble learning. Uses inverted dropout scaling of $\frac{1}{1-p}$ during training. No dropout at test time.

- **Exclude Bias**: By KTU and industry convention, the bias term $\theta_0$ is **NOT** regularized. Only feature weights $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n$ are penalized.

- **Convergence Condition**: For L2 gradient descent to be stable, we require $0 < \alpha \lambda / m < 1$.

- **Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid**:
  - Regularizing $\theta_0$ (the bias) — **WRONG**, exclude it.
  - Confusing $\lambda$ (regularization) with $\alpha$ (learning rate) — they are independent.
  - Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the L2 penalty — always explain its purpose.
  - Not mentioning cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning.
  - Calling L1 "Lasso Regression" without explaining it stands for "Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator."

- **Memory Aid**: *"L1 = Lasso = Lighter, sparser, selects; L2 = Ridge = Robust, dense, distributes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolutional Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Convolutional Neural Networks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a specialized class of deep neural networks designed primarily for processing data that has a grid-like topology, such as images (2D grids of pixels) or audio spectrograms (1D grids). CNNs use a mathematical operation called **convolution** in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one of their layers. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Convolutional Neural Network is a deep learning architecture that employs convolution operations with learned kernel filters to extract spatially-localized features from input tensors. It is composed of three primary building blocks: **convolutional layers**, **pooling layers**, and **fully-connected (dense) layers**, arranged hierarchically to learn a hierarchy of features — from low-level edges and textures to high-level semantic concepts. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective's Magnifying Glass Imagine you are a **detective** trying to identify a suspect (the object) in a **crowded photograph** (the input image). You don't look at the whole photo at once — that would be overwhelming. Instead, you: 1. **Slide a small magnifying glass** (the **kernel/filter**) across different parts of the image. 2. At each location, you ask: *"Does this small region look like an edge? a curve? a corner? an eye?"* 3. Each time the magnifying glass matches a pattern, it produces a **high signal**; where it doesn't match, a **low signal**. 4. You then **combine** all these local observations to reach a final conclusion: *"Yes, the suspect is here!"* This is exactly how a CNN works: - The **input image** = the photograph. - The **kernel/filter** = the magnifying glass. - The **feature map** = the detective's notebook recording where patterns were found. - The **final classification** = the detective's conclusion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Unlike a regular fully-connected network that treats every pixel independently (losing spatial relationships), a CNN **preserves the spatial structure** of the input by sharing the same filter across the entire image. This makes CNNs dramatically more parameter-efficient and translation-invariant. ## 1.3 Why CNNs Matter in Modern Engineering | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Spatial Hierarchy** | Learns simple patterns in early layers, complex objects in deeper layers | | **Parameter Sharing** | One filter is reused across the whole image — drastically fewer weights | | **Translation Equivariance** | A cat shifted 5 pixels right is still recognized as a cat | | **Local Connectivity** | Each neuron looks at only a small region (receptive field) | | **Scalability** | Works on small (32×32) to large (1024×1024) images | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Architecture of a CNN A typical CNN is a **pipeline** of alternating layers, each performing a specific function: 1. **Input Layer** — receives the raw image tensor (Height × Width × Channels). 2. **Convolutional Layer (Conv)** — applies learnable filters to produce feature maps. 3. **Activation Function (ReLU)** — introduces non-linearity: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$. 4. **Pooling Layer** — downsamples feature maps to reduce spatial size and computation. 5. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layer** — flattens features and performs final classification. 6. **Output Layer (Softmax/Sigmoid)** — produces class probabilities. ## 2.2 The Convolution Operation — Step by Step Given an input image $I$ of size $H \times W$ and a kernel $K$ of size $k \times k$, the convolution operation at spatial position $(i, j)$ is: **Step 1:** Place the kernel $K$ at position $(i, j)$ on the input $I$. **Step 2:** Perform element-wise multiplication between the kernel values and the corresponding input pixels they cover. **Step 3:** Sum all the products to obtain a single scalar — this is the output value at position $(i, j)$ of the feature map. **Step 4:** Slide the kernel to the next position (controlled by **stride**) and repeat. > [!NOTE] > **Why "convolution" is actually cross-correlation:** In strict mathematical terms, deep learning frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch) implement **cross-correlation**, not true convolution (which would require flipping the kernel). The naming convention has stuck historically, and in the context of CNNs, the two are equivalent because the kernel weights are *learned*. ## 2.3 Key Hyperparameters of a Convolution Layer ### (a) Stride ($s$) The number of pixels the kernel shifts at each step. - **Stride = 1:** Kernel moves 1 pixel at a time → large output feature map. - **Stride = 2:** Kernel moves 2 pixels → output size is halved. ### (b) Padding ($p$) Adding zero-valued pixels around the border of the input. - **Valid Padding (No Padding):** $p = 0$ → output shrinks. - **Same Padding:** $p = \frac{k-1}{2}$ → output size equals input size (for stride 1). ### (c) Dilation ($d$) Spacing between kernel elements — expands the receptive field without adding parameters. ### (d) Number of Filters ($F$) Determines the **depth** of the output feature map. Each filter learns to detect a different pattern. ## 2.4 Pooling Operations Pooling reduces the spatial dimensions of feature maps. Two common types: **Max Pooling:** Selects the maximum value within each pooling window. $$y_{i,j} = \max_{(m,n) \in \text{window}} x_{i+m, j+n}$$ **Average Pooling:** Computes the mean of values within each pooling window. $$y_{i,j} = \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{(m,n) \in \text{window}} x_{i+m, j+n}$$ > [!TIP] > **Why pool?** Pooling provides **translation invariance** (small shifts don't change the output), **reduces computation** in deeper layers, and helps **control overfitting** by acting as a form of downsampling. ## 2.5 Output Size and Parameter Count — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | **Output Height** | $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Spatial size of output feature map | | **Output Width** | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Same as height for square inputs | | **Conv Parameters** | $P_{conv} = k \times k \times C_{in} \times C_{out} + C_{out}$ | Weights + biases for one conv layer | | **FC Parameters** | $P_{fc} = N_{in} \times N_{out} + N_{out}$ | Weights + biases for dense layer | | **Receptive Field** | $r_{l} = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$ | Region of input visible to layer $l$ | | **Padding (Same)** | $p = \left\lfloor \frac{k \cdot (s-1) - s + 1}{2} \right\rfloor$ | Padding to preserve input size | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraint:** In the formulas above, the symbols mean: $H_{in}, W_{in}$ = input height/width, $k$ = kernel size, $s$ = stride, $p$ = padding, $C_{in}$ = input channels, $C_{out}$ = output channels (number of filters), $N_{in}, N_{out}$ = number of neurons in FC layer, $r_l$ = receptive field at layer $l$. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Use of CNNs | |---|---| | **Medical Imaging** | Tumor detection in MRI/CT scans (U-Net, ResNet) | | **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane detection, pedestrian recognition (YOLO, SSD) | | **Industrial Inspection** | Defect detection on manufacturing lines | | **Satellite Imaging** | Land cover classification, disaster monitoring | | **Agriculture** | Crop disease identification from leaf images | | **Biometrics** | Face recognition, fingerprint matching | | **Document Processing** | OCR, handwriting recognition | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Convolution Output Size Calculation **Problem:** An input image of size $32 \times 32 \times 3$ is passed through a convolutional layer with 16 filters, each of size $5 \times 5$, with stride $s = 1$ and padding $p = 2$. Compute the output feature map size and the number of parameters. **Step 1: Identify the given values.** - $H_{in} = 32$, $W_{in} = 32$, $C_{in} = 3$ - $k = 5$, $s = 1$, $p = 2$ - $C_{out} = 16$ (number of filters) **Step 2: Apply the output size formula.** $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$ **Step 3: Substitute the values.** $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 + 2(2) - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1$$ **Step 4: Simplify the numerator.** $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 + 4 - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 31 \right\rfloor + 1 = 32$$ **Step 5: State the final output size.** $$H_{out} \times W_{out} \times C_{out} = 32 \times 32 \times 16$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** With $p = 2$ and $k = 5$, the output spatial dimensions are **preserved** (same padding effect for stride 1). **Step 6: Calculate the number of parameters.** $$P_{conv} = k \times k \times C_{in} \times C_{out} + C_{out}$$ **Step 7: Substitute and compute.** $$P_{conv} = 5 \times 5 \times 3 \times 16 + 16 = 1200 + 16 = \mathbf{1216 \text{ parameters}}$$ **Step 8: Compare with a fully-connected alternative.** A fully-connected layer from $32 \times 32 \times 3 = 3072$ inputs to 16 outputs would need: $$P_{fc} = 3072 \times 16 + 16 = 49168 \text{ parameters}$$ This is **~40× more parameters**, illustrating CNN's efficiency. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Stacked Convolutional Layer Output Size **Problem:** An input of size $224 \times 224 \times 3$ passes through: - **Conv1:** $k=7, s=2, p=3, C_{out}=64$ - **MaxPool:** $k=3, s=2, p=0$ - **Conv2:** $k=3, s=1, p=1, C_{out}=128$ Compute the output size after each stage. **Step 1: Compute output after Conv1.** $$H_{out,1} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 2(3) - 7}{2} \right\rfloor + 1$$ $$H_{out,1} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 6 - 7}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{223}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112$$ So output = $112 \times 112 \times 64$. **Step 2: Compute output after MaxPool.** $$H_{out,2} = \left\lfloor \frac{112 + 0 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{109}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 54 + 1 = 55$$ So output = $55 \times 55 \times 64$. **Step 3: Compute output after Conv2.** $$H_{out,3} = \left\lfloor \frac{55 + 2(1) - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 54 \right\rfloor + 1 = 55$$ So output = $55 \times 55 \times 128$. ## 3.3 Backpropagation Through a Convolutional Layer — Derivation **Problem:** Derive the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the kernel weights $K$ and the input $I$. **Setup:** Let $S = I * K$ (convolution), where $S_{i,j} = \sum_{m,n} I_{i+m, j+n} \cdot K_{m,n}$. **Step 1: Loss depends on $S$, which depends on $K$.** By the chain rule: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{m,n}} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial S_{i,j}} \cdot \frac{\partial S_{i,j}}{\partial K_{m,n}}$$ **Step 2: Compute the inner partial derivative.** Since $S_{i,j} = \sum_{m,n} I_{i+m, j+n} \cdot K_{m,n}$: $$\frac{\partial S_{i,j}}{\partial K_{m,n}} = I_{i+m, j+n}$$ **Step 3: Substitute back.** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{m,n}} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial S_{i,j}} \cdot I_{i+m, j+n}$$ **Step 4: Recognize the form.** This is itself a **convolution** between the upstream gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial S}$ and the input $I$: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial S} * I$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** This is why convolutions are efficient in backprop — the gradient computation is also a convolution, meaning we can use the same highly-optimized routines (cuDNN, MKL-DNN) for both forward and backward passes. **Step 5: Gradient with respect to the input $I$** (needed to backprop to earlier layers): $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial I_{i,j}} = \sum_{m,n} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial S_{i-m, j-n}} \cdot K_{m,n}$$ This is a **full convolution** (or transposed convolution) of the upstream gradient with the flipped kernel. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Receptive Field Growth For a stack of layers each with kernel size $k$ and stride $s$, the effective receptive field after $L$ layers is: $$r_L = r_0 + \sum_{l=1}^{L} (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=0}^{l-1} s_i$$ **Example:** Three conv layers, each with $k=3, s=1$, applied sequentially. **Step 1: $r_0 = 1$ (initial single pixel).** **Step 2: After layer 1.** $$r_1 = 1 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 1 + 2 = 3$$ **Step 3: After layer 2.** $$r_2 = 3 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 3 + 2 = 5$$ **Step 4: After layer 3.** $$r_3 = 5 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 5 + 2 = 7$$ So three stacked $3\times 3$ convolutions yield a **receptive field of $7 \times 7$** — equivalent to one $7 \times 7$ convolution but with **fewer parameters** and **more non-linearity** (3 ReLU activations vs. 1). --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CNN ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INPUT IMAGE
HxWxC] --> B[CONV LAYER
Conv2D + ReLU] B --> C[POOLING LAYER
MaxPool2D] C --> D[CONV LAYER 2
Conv2D + ReLU] D --> E[POOLING LAYER 2
MaxPool2D] E --> F[CONV LAYER 3
Conv2D + ReLU] F --> G[FLATTEN LAYER
Vectorize] G --> H[FULLY CONNECTED
Dense Layer + ReLU] H --> I[OUTPUT LAYER
Softmax Classifier] I --> J[CLASS PROBABILITIES
P class x input] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style D fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style E fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style F fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style G fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style H fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style I fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style J fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` ## 4.2 Convolution Operation — Kernel Sliding Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IP[INPUT 5x5] I1[1 1 1 0 0] I2[0 1 1 1 0] I3[0 0 1 1 1] I4[0 0 1 1 0] I5[0 1 1 0 0] end subgraph KR[KERNEL 3x3] K1[1 0 1] K2[0 1 0] K3[1 0 1] end subgraph OP[OUTPUT 3x3 FEATURE MAP] O1[4 3 4] O2[2 4 3] O3[2 3 4] end IP --> KR KR --> OP style IP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style KR fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style OP fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Feature Learning Across Layers ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EARLY LAYERS
Detect Edges, Colors, Textures] --> B[MIDDLE LAYERS
Detect Parts, Shapes, Patterns] B --> C[DEEP LAYERS
Detect Objects, Faces, Scenes] C --> D[OUTPUT
Class Probabilities] A1[Vertical Edge] --> A A2[Horizontal Edge] --> A A3[Diagonal Edge] --> A A4[Color Blob] --> A B1[Eye] --> B B2[Nose] --> B B3[Wheel] --> B B4[Corner] --> B C1[Face] --> C C2[Car] --> C C3[Building] --> C style A fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style C fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` ## 4.4 Parameter Sharing — The Key CNN Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FULL[Full Connection: NO sharing] F1[Pixel 1] --> N1[Neuron 1] F2[Pixel 2] --> N1 F3[Pixel 3] --> N1 F4[Pixel 4] --> N1 F5[Pixel 5] --> N1 F6[Pixel 6] --> N1 end subgraph CNN[CNN: SAME filter shared] G1[Pixel 1] --> H1[Filter] G2[Pixel 2] --> H1 G3[Pixel 3] --> H1 G4[Pixel 4] --> H1 G5[Pixel 5] --> H1 G6[Pixel 6] --> H1 end style FULL fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style CNN fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** In the full connection (left), every pixel has its own weight to the neuron — yielding thousands of parameters. In the CNN approach (right), a **single filter** is applied across all positions — yielding just a handful of parameters that learn to detect the same pattern anywhere in the image. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define a Convolutional Neural Network. List its three main building blocks.** **Model Answer:** A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is a deep learning architecture specialized for processing grid-structured data (primarily images). It applies convolution operations with learnable kernel filters to automatically extract hierarchical spatial features. The three main building blocks are: 1. **Convolutional Layer** — Applies learnable filters to extract local features. 2. **Pooling Layer** — Downsamples feature maps to reduce spatial dimensions and provide translation invariance. 3. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layer** — Performs high-level reasoning and final classification based on extracted features. > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Three blocks listed (1.5 marks). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **What is the role of the stride and padding hyperparameters in a convolutional layer?** **Model Answer:** **Stride ($s$):** Controls the step size at which the convolution kernel moves across the input. A larger stride produces a smaller output feature map, effectively performing downsampling. Stride of 1 moves the kernel one pixel at a time; stride of 2 moves it two pixels. **Padding ($p$):** Adds extra rows/columns (typically zero-valued) around the input border. The main purpose of padding is to **preserve the spatial dimensions** of the input (called "same" padding) or to allow the kernel to be applied at border positions, preventing information loss at the edges. > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Stride role (1.5 marks) + Padding role (1.5 marks). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the convolution operation in detail with a suitable example. Discuss the role of the kernel and feature map. **(b) [7 Marks]** For an input image of size $64 \times 64 \times 3$, a convolutional layer uses 32 filters of size $3 \times 3$ with stride 1 and padding 1. Calculate: (i) the output feature map size and (ii) the number of trainable parameters in this layer. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — The Convolution Operation:** The convolution operation slides a small learnable matrix (the **kernel** or **filter**) across the input image and computes a dot product at every spatial location. Each kernel is a small matrix (e.g., $3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$) whose values are the **trainable weights** of the network. **Working Mechanism:** 1. **Step 1:** Position the kernel at the top-left corner of the input. 2. **Step 2:** Compute element-wise multiplication between kernel values and the covered input region. 3. **Step 3:** Sum all the products to produce **one scalar** — this is one entry of the **feature map** (also called the **activation map**). 4. **Step 4:** Slide the kernel (by `stride` pixels) and repeat until the entire input is covered. 5. **Step 5:** The resulting 2D grid of scalars is the **feature map**, which highlights *where* in the input the pattern encoded by the kernel was detected. **Example (Conceptual):** A vertical-edge-detection kernel applied to a photograph will produce a feature map where bright pixels correspond to locations of strong vertical edges, and dark pixels correspond to uniform regions. **Role of Kernel:** The kernel acts as a **feature detector**. Through training, the network learns kernel values that activate strongly for meaningful patterns (edges in early layers, textures in middle layers, object parts in deep layers). **Role of Feature Map:** The feature map is the **output of a convolution**, encoding the *spatial locations* and *strengths* of detected features. It serves as the input to the next layer, building a feature hierarchy. > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Mechanism (3 marks) + Example (1.5 marks) + Kernel role (1.5 marks) + Feature map role (1 mark). --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **Given:** $H_{in} = 64$, $W_{in} = 64$, $C_{in} = 3$, $k = 3$, $s = 1$, $p = 1$, $C_{out} = 32$. **(i) Output Feature Map Size:** $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$ **Substituting:** $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{64 + 2(1) - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1$$ **Simplifying:** $$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{64 + 2 - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 63 \right\rfloor + 1 = 64$$ **Therefore:** $$\text{Output feature map size} = 64 \times 64 \times 32$$ **(ii) Number of Trainable Parameters:** $$P_{conv} = (k \times k \times C_{in} \times C_{out}) + C_{out}$$ **Substituting:** $$P_{conv} = (3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 32) + 32$$ **Simplifying:** $$P_{conv} = 864 + 32 = \mathbf{896 \text{ parameters}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Formula application (3 marks) + Final answer (1 mark) for (i); Formula application (2 marks) + Final answer (1 mark) for (ii). --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture of a typical CNN with a neat block diagram. Explain the functions of the convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of stride, padding, and receptive field in CNNs. Compute the receptive field after three stacked $3 \times 3$ convolutions (each with stride 1) starting from an input pixel. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — CNN Architecture:** A typical CNN is organized as a **sequential pipeline** of layer types, transforming the input image progressively into a class prediction: 1. **Input Layer:** Holds the raw image tensor of shape $H \times W \times C$ (e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3$ for a color image). 2. **Convolutional Layer (Conv + ReLU):** Applies $F$ learnable kernels to the input. Each kernel produces one **feature map** of size $H' \times W'$. Stacking $F$ such maps gives an output of shape $H' \times W' \times F$. A **ReLU** activation $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ is applied element-wise to introduce non-linearity. 3. **Pooling Layer (typically Max Pooling):** Downsamples each feature map by a factor (e.g., $2 \times 2$ max-pool with stride 2 halves both dimensions). This reduces computation, controls overfitting, and provides translation invariance. 4. **Sequence of Conv-Pool Blocks:** Alternating convolutional and pooling layers are stacked, so deeper layers see larger receptive fields and detect more abstract features. 5. **Flatten Layer:** Reshapes the final 3D feature map into a 1D vector. 6. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layer:** Each neuron is connected to all neurons in the previous layer, performing high-level reasoning. 7. **Output Layer (Softmax):** For multi-class classification, softmax converts logits into a probability distribution over $K$ classes: $$P(y = k \mid x) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Block diagram description (2 marks) + Conv layer function (2 marks) + Pooling layer function (1.5 marks) + FC layer function (1.5 marks). --- **Part (b) — Stride, Padding, Receptive Field:** **Stride ($s$):** Number of pixels by which the kernel shifts at each step. A stride of 2 reduces the output size by half, effectively performing downsampling. A stride of 1 preserves the spatial size (modulo padding). **Padding ($p$):** Number of zero-valued pixels added around the input border. **Same padding** ($p = (k-1)/2$ for stride 1) preserves input dimensions. **Valid padding** ($p = 0$) allows natural shrinking. Padding is crucial for controlling output dimensions and preventing edge information loss. **Receptive Field:** The region of the original input image that influences a particular neuron in a deeper layer. It grows as we go deeper, allowing deep neurons to "see" larger portions of the input and detect more global patterns. **Numerical Computation — Receptive Field:** Given: three stacked $3 \times 3$ convolutions, each with $s = 1$, starting from a single input pixel ($r_0 = 1$). **Step 1: After layer 1.** $$r_1 = r_0 + (k_1 - 1) \cdot s_0 = 1 + (3 - 1) \cdot 1 = 1 + 2 = 3$$ **Step 2: After layer 2.** $$r_2 = r_1 + (k_2 - 1) \cdot (s_0 \cdot s_1) = 3 + (3 - 1) \cdot (1 \cdot 1) = 3 + 2 = 5$$ **Step 3: After layer 3.** $$r_3 = r_2 + (k_3 - 1) \cdot (s_0 \cdot s_1 \cdot s_2) = 5 + (3 - 1) \cdot (1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1) = 5 + 2 = 7$$ **Final Answer:** $$r_3 = \mathbf{7 \times 7}$$ > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Stride (1.5 marks) + Padding (1.5 marks) + Receptive field concept (1 mark) + Numerical computation (3 marks). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the bias term in parameter count.** Students often compute $k \cdot k \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out}$ but forget to add $C_{out}$ for biases. Always: **Parameters = (Weights) + (Biases)**. > 2. **Mixing up stride with padding.** Stride controls *step size*; padding controls *border extension*. These are independent hyperparameters. > 3. **Not applying the floor function.** When the division in the output size formula is not exact, you MUST take the floor: $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$. Skipping this costs full marks. > 4. **Confusing convolution with cross-correlation.** In deep learning, what is implemented is cross-correlation (no kernel flipping). In KTU exams, the terms are used interchangeably, but writing a one-line note earns extra credit. > 5. **Ignoring the number of input channels in parameter calculation.** A $3 \times 3$ filter on a 3-channel input has $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ weights, not just $9$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Convolutional Neural Networks:** - ⭐ **CNN = Conv Layers + Pooling Layers + Fully Connected Layers**, arranged hierarchically. - ⭐ **Convolution** slides a learnable kernel across the input, computing dot products to produce feature maps. - ⭐ **Stride ($s$)** = step size of kernel movement; **Padding ($p$)** = zeros added at borders; **Filters ($F$)** = number of kernels = depth of output. - ⭐ **Output Size Formula:** $H_{out} = \lfloor (H_{in} + 2p - k) / s \rfloor + 1$ - ⭐ **Parameter Formula (Conv):** $P = k \times k \times C_{in} \times C_{out} + C_{out}$ (weights + biases). - ⭐ **Parameter Formula (FC):** $P = N_{in} \times N_{out} + N_{out}$ (weights + biases). - ⭐ **Max Pooling** takes the maximum in each window; **Average Pooling** takes the mean. - ⭐ **Receptive Field** grows with depth; three $3 \times 3$ convs give a $7 \times 7$ receptive field with fewer parameters and more non-linearity than one $7 \times 7$ conv. - ⭐ **ReLU** is the standard activation: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$. - ⭐ **Softmax** in the output layer converts logits to a probability distribution. - ⭐ **Parameter sharing** is the key CNN innovation: one filter detects the same pattern anywhere in the image. - ⭐ **Translation equivariance:** CNNs inherently handle shifted versions of the same input. - ⭐ **Channel depth** increases through the network (e.g., 3 → 64 → 128 → 256 → 512) while **spatial size** decreases (e.g., 224 → 112 → 56 → 28 → 14). - ⭐ CNNs **backpropagate via convolution operations** — the gradient w.r.t. kernel is a convolution of upstream gradient with input. - ⭐ Famous architectures: **LeNet-5** (1998), **AlexNet** (2012), **VGGNet** (2014), **GoogLeNet/Inception** (2014), **ResNet** (2015). ---

topics.title: TensorFlow and Keras for Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TensorFlow and Keras for Deep Convolutional Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 TensorFlow — Formal Definition

**TensorFlow** is an open-source, end-to-end **machine learning (ML) platform** developed by Google Brain (first released in **2015**) that expresses computations as **static or dynamic dataflow graphs**, where nodes represent mathematical operations (ops) and edges represent the multi-dimensional data arrays called **tensors** that flow between them. The framework supports both **eager execution** (imperative, Python-like) and **graph execution** (XLA-optimized, deployable) modes, making it suitable for research prototyping as well as production deployment across CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEBT756, Module 3):** Students must understand TensorFlow's tensor data model, computational graph abstraction, and how **Keras** (`tf.keras`) acts as the official high-level API for rapid Deep Learning experimentation.

### 1.2 Keras — Formal Definition

**Keras** is an open-source, high-level neural network Application Programming Interface (API) written in Python, designed to enable **fast experimentation** with deep learning models. In the KTU 2024 scheme, the **official integration of Keras inside TensorFlow (`tf.keras`)** is the prescribed tool for implementing Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs). Keras prioritizes **user-friendliness, modularity, and extensibility** by exposing layers, optimizers, loss functions, and metrics as composable building blocks.

### 1.3 Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) — Formal Definition

A **Deep Convolutional Neural Network** is a specialized class of feedforward neural network designed to process data with a known grid-like topology (most commonly **2D image tensors** of shape $(H \times W \times C)$). It uses three architectural ideas — **local receptive fields, shared weights (convolutional kernels), and spatial pooling** — to extract hierarchical, translation-invariant feature representations. The term "**deep**" refers to the stacking of many such layers (often $>10$ or $>100$ in modern architectures such as **ResNet-50, VGG-16, Inception-v3**).

### 1.4 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "How a child learns to recognize a dog"**
> Imagine teaching a toddler what a "dog" is. The child does not memorize every pixel of every dog photo. Instead:
> - The first observation notices **low-level features** — edges, corners, and color blobs.
> - The next observation groups these into **mid-level features** — eyes, ears, a furry patch.
> - The final observation combines them into **high-level concepts** — a snout, a tail, the whole "dog."
>
> A **DCNN does exactly the same thing in layers**: early convolutional layers act as the "edge detectors," middle layers combine them into "shape detectors," and deeper layers assemble the complete object. **TensorFlow is the "factory"** that performs the millions of multiplications, and **Keras is the friendly "blueprint"** that lets the engineer design the factory floor with a few clean lines of code, without manually wiring every wire.

### 1.5 Why TensorFlow + Keras for DCNNs?

| Reason | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| **Automatic Differentiation** | `tf.GradientTape` computes gradients automatically, eliminating manual backpropagation. |
| **GPU/TPU Acceleration** | Tensors live on accelerators with minimal code change (`.to(device)` semantics). |
| **Pre-built CNN Layers** | `Conv2D`, `MaxPooling2D`, `BatchNormalization`, `Dropout` are ready-made. |
| **Production Pipeline** | `SavedModel` format deploys to Android, iOS, browsers (TF.js), and edge devices (TFLite). |
| **Ecosystem** | TensorBoard for visualization, TF Datasets for I/O, TFX for MLOps. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for KTU Examinations:** A Convolutional layer's output feature map size is computed as:
> $$O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$
> where $I$ = input size, $K$ = kernel size, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride. Always remember to **apply the floor** when the result is non-integer.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Tensor — The Atomic Unit of TensorFlow

A **tensor** is a generalization of scalars (0-D), vectors (1-D), and matrices (2-D) to $N$ dimensions. In TensorFlow, every datum — input, weight, gradient — is a `tf.Tensor` carrying three attributes:

1. **Rank (ndim):** Number of dimensions.
2. **Shape:** Tuple of dimension sizes, e.g., `[batch, height, width, channels]`.
3. **Dtype:** `float32`, `int32`, `bool`, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Tensor"?** The name is borrowed from physics (stress-energy tensor), but here it simply means an $N$-dimensional array living in a **uniform memory buffer**. KTU students should remember that a batch of 32 RGB images of size $224 \times 224$ is a 4-D tensor with shape `[32, 224, 224, 3]`.

### 2.2 TensorFlow Execution Modes

- **Eager Execution (Default since TF 2.x):** Operations are evaluated immediately, Python-style. Excellent for debugging.
- **Graph Execution (`@tf.function`):** Python function is traced and converted to a portable `tf.Graph` (an intermediate representation, not the deprecated `tf.compat.v1.Graph`). This enables XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) compilation and faster execution on GPUs.

### 2.3 The `tf.keras` Layer Stack for DCNNs

A Keras DCNN is built by **stacking layers in a `Sequential` model or via the Functional API**:

| Layer Type | Purpose | Key Hyper-parameters |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `Conv2D` | Learnable spatial filters (kernels) | `filters`, `kernel_size`, `strides`, `padding='same'/'valid'`, `activation` |
| `MaxPooling2D` | Spatial down-sampling (translation invariance) | `pool_size`, `strides`, `padding` |
| `AveragePooling2D` | Smooth down-sampling | Same as Max |
| `BatchNormalization` | Normalize activations to stabilize training | `momentum`, `epsilon` |
| `Dropout` | Regularization (random neuron deactivation) | `rate` (fraction, e.g., 0.5) |
| `Flatten` | Reshape 3-D tensor → 1-D vector | None |
| `Dense` | Fully-connected (FC) layer | `units`, `activation` |
| `GlobalAveragePooling2D` | Replace Flatten+FC with channel-wise mean | None |

### 2.4 Why Convolutional Layers (The "Why" Behind the "How")

1. **Local Receptive Fields:** A pixel's meaning depends on its neighbors, not the entire image. Each neuron in a Conv layer connects to a small region $K \times K$.
2. **Parameter Sharing:** The same kernel slides across the image, so $K^2 \cdot C_{out}$ parameters learn **all** spatial locations, drastically reducing parameters vs. a fully-connected net.
3. **Translation Equivariance:** A feature detector learned at position $(x,y)$ works at $(x+\Delta, y+\Delta)$ without retraining.
4. **Hierarchical Composition:** Stacking layers builds an exponentially larger receptive field; a neuron in layer 5 "sees" most of the image.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Must-Memorize Formulas for Convolutional Networks**
> Use `\vert` / `\mid` (NOT raw `\vert`) inside any markdown table cell. All equations are typeset in LaTeX.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conv output size (1-D) | $O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $I$=input, $K$=kernel, $P$=pad, $S$=stride | Pixels |
| 2 | Conv output size (2-D, square) | $O_{H}=O_{W}=\left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Same as above | Pixels |
| 3 | Parameters in Conv2D layer | $P_{conv} = K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$ | $C_{in}$=input channels, $C_{out}$=filters | Count; last term is bias |
| 4 | Parameters in Dense layer | $P_{dense} = n_{in} \cdot n_{out} + n_{out}$ | $n_{in}$, $n_{out}$ | Count; last term is bias |
| 5 | Receptive field growth | $RF_{l+1} = RF_l + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l} S_i$ | $K_l$=kernel at layer $l$, $S_i$=stride | Pixels |
| 6 | ReLU activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | — | Non-saturating |
| 7 | Softmax (multi-class) | $f(x_i) = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{x_j}}$ | $i,j \in \text{classes}$ | Sum to **1.0** |
| 8 | Categorical cross-entropy | $L = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | $y$=true, $\hat{y}$=predicted | Used with `softmax` |
| 9 | Padding for "same" | $P = \frac{K - 1}{2}$ | when $K$ odd, $S=1$ | Output $O = I$ |
| 10 | Floating-point ops (conv) | $\text{FLOPs} \approx 2 \cdot H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} \cdot K^2$ | per layer | Multiply-accumulate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The parameter-count formula is critical in **edge-AI deployment**. A model intended for a smartphone via **TensorFlow Lite** must typically stay under $\mathbf{10 \text{ MB}}$ and $\mathbf{30 \text{ MFLOPs}}$ to maintain $>30$ FPS inference on a mid-range mobile GPU.

### 2.6 Compilation, Training, and Evaluation Trifecta

A Keras model is brought to life through three steps:

1. **`model.compile(optimizer, loss, metrics)`** — choose the **optimizer** (e.g., `Adam` with learning rate $\eta=10^{-3}$), **loss** (e.g., `sparse_categorical_crossentropy`), and **metrics** (e.g., `accuracy`).
2. **`model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs, batch_size, validation_split)`** — performs mini-batch gradient descent. One **epoch** = one full pass over the training set.
3. **`model.evaluate(X_test, y_test)`** — reports loss and metrics on unseen data.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Convolution Output Size

**Problem Statement:** Given a square input feature map of side $I$ pixels, a square kernel of side $K$ pixels, symmetric zero-padding $P$, and stride $S$ (movement of the kernel per step), derive the side length $O$ of the output feature map.

**Assumption (KTU-Standard):** Padding is applied symmetrically to **both** sides of each spatial dimension.

**Step 1 — Effective Input Size After Padding:**
When we add $P$ rows/columns of zeros on each side, the input becomes effectively larger:
$$I_{\text{eff}} = I + 2P$$

**Step 2 — Number of Kernel Placements:**
The kernel's top-left corner can slide from position $0$ to position $I_{\text{eff}} - K$, in increments of $S$. The total number of distinct positions is:
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{\text{eff}} - K}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify:**
Substituting $I_{\text{eff}} = I + 2P$:
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{I + 2P - K}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form:**
$$\boxed{O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**
Let $I = 32$, $K = 5$, $P = 2$, $S = 1$ (this is the classic **first layer of VGG-16**):
$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 4}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 31 \rfloor + 1 = 32$$

The output is $32 \times 32$ — i.e., "**same**" padding preserved the spatial dimensions.

### 3.2 Derivation of Parameter Count in a Conv2D Layer

**Problem Statement:** Compute the number of trainable scalar parameters in a `Conv2D(filters=C_out, kernel_size=(K,K))` layer that takes an input with $C_{in}$ channels.

**Step 1 — One Kernel's Parameters:**
A single kernel is a 3-D filter of shape $(K, K, C_{in})$. Total scalars per kernel:
$$n_{\text{kernel}} = K \cdot K \cdot C_{in} = K^2 \cdot C_{in}$$

**Step 2 — One Bias per Output Channel:**
Each output feature map (one per filter) has its own bias term:
$$n_{\text{bias}} = C_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Total for $C_{out}$ Filters:**
$$P_{conv} = C_{out} \cdot K^2 \cdot C_{in} + C_{out}$$

$$\boxed{P_{conv} = K^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification with VGG-16 First Layer:**
Inputs: $C_{in}=3$ (RGB), $C_{out}=64$, $K=3$:
$$P = 3^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 64 + 64 = 9 \cdot 192 + 64 = 1728 + 64 = \mathbf{1{,}792 \text{ parameters}}$$

This matches the canonical VGG-16 specification in literature (a frequent KTU viva question).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Receptive Field

**Problem Statement:** Show how the theoretical receptive field (RF) grows as convolutions and poolings stack.

**Recursive Definition:**
The receptive field of a neuron in layer $l+1$ is the area of the input that influences it. Each layer $l$ with kernel $K_l$ and stride $S_l$ expands the RF as:
$$RF_{l+1} = RF_l + (K_l - 1) \cdot J_l$$
where $J_l$ is the cumulative "jump" (product of all prior strides):
$$J_l = \prod_{i=0}^{l-1} S_i, \quad J_0 = 1$$

**Step 1 — Expand the Product Form:**
$$J_l = S_0 \cdot S_1 \cdot \ldots \cdot S_{l-1}$$

**Step 2 — Final Closed Form:**
$$\boxed{RF_{l+1} = RF_l + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=0}^{l-1} S_i}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (Two $3 \times 3$ Convs, Stride 1):**
- After Conv 1: $RF_1 = 1 + (3-1)\cdot 1 = 3$
- After Conv 2: $RF_2 = 3 + (3-1)\cdot 1 = 5$

This proves a famous deep-learning rule: **two $3 \times 3$ convs have a receptive field of 5**, and three have an RF of 7 — a key reason VGG-16 prefers stacked small filters over a single large one (more non-linearity, fewer parameters).

### 3.4 Derivation of Cross-Entropy Loss (Why Log?)

**Problem Statement:** Show why KTU examinations prefer **categorical cross-entropy** over **mean squared error** for classification.

**Step 1 — Maximum Likelihood View:**
We want to maximize $P(y \mid x)$ for the true class. For a softmax output, the probability of the correct class is:
$$P = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{y}_i^{y_i}$$
(only the true class's term survives due to one-hot encoding).

**Step 2 — Take the Negative Log-Likelihood (monotonic transformation):**
$$L = -\log P = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Why It's Mathematically Superior:**
The gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}$ is steep when $\hat{y} \to 0$ for the true class, producing **large corrective updates**. MSE's gradient vanishes for confident wrong predictions (saturation problem).

$$\boxed{L_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Layer-wise Computation Log

The following table is the **canonical KTU-style analytical worksheet** a student must produce for a given DCNN architecture (e.g., the VGG-style network below):

| Layer | Input Shape | Output Shape | $K$ | $S$ | $P$ | Params | RF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input | — | $[32,224,224,3]$ | — | — | — | **0** | 1 |
| Conv2D-1 (64 filters) | $[32,224,224,3]$ | $[32,224,224,64]$ | 3 | 1 | 1 | $1{,}792$ | 3 |
| Conv2D-2 (64 filters) | $[32,224,224,64]$ | $[32,224,224,64]$ | 3 | 1 | 1 | $36{,}928$ | 5 |
| MaxPool-1 | $[32,224,224,64]$ | $[32,112,112,64]$ | 2 | 2 | 0 | **0** | 6 |
| Conv2D-3 (128 filters) | $[32,112,112,64]$ | $[32,112,112,128]$ | 3 | 1 | 1 | $73{,}856$ | 10 |
| MaxPool-2 | $[32,112,112,128]$ | $[32,56,56,128]$ | 2 | 2 | 0 | **0** | 12 |
| Flatten | $[32,56,56,128]$ | $[32,401{,}408]$ | — | — | — | **0** | 12 |
| Dense-1 (256 units) | $[32,401{,}408]$ | $[32,256]$ | — | — | — | $102{,}760{,}704$ | 12 |
| Dense-2 (10 softmax) | $[32,256]$ | $[32,10]$ | — | — | — | $2{,}570$ | 12 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The `Flatten` + `Dense-1` step contributes **$>\mathbf{99\%}$ of the total parameters**. This is the very reason modern architectures (ResNet, MobileNet, EfficientNet) replace Flatten+FC with **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** to slash parameter count.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TensorFlow Ecosystem Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the high-level interaction between the **TensorFlow runtime, the Keras API, the computational graph, and hardware accelerators**. Every node ID is alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Python Code] --> B[tf.keras High Level API]
    B --> C[Sequential or Functional Model]
    C --> D[Layer Stack: Conv2D, Pool, Dense]
    D --> E[tf.Graph Construction]
    E --> F[XLA JIT Compiler]
    F --> G{Accelerator Selection}
    G --> H[CPU Execution]
    G --> I[GPU CUDA cuDNN Kernels]
    G --> J[TPU XLA Tiles]
    H --> K[Predictions and Loss Output]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[TensorBoard Visualization]
    K --> M[SavedModel Export]
    M --> N[TF Lite Edge Deployment]
    M --> O[TF.js Browser Deployment]
```

### 4.2 DCNN Forward-Pass Data Flow (VGG-Style Block)

The next diagram illustrates how a single $224 \times 224 \times 3$ image propagates through the convolution → activation → pooling → dense pipeline. All labels are plain uppercase text — no inline markdown formatting inside the Mermaid nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I0[Input Image 224x224x3] --> L1[Conv 3x3 64 ReLU]
    L1 --> L2[Conv 3x3 64 ReLU]
    L2 --> P1[MaxPool 2x2 Stride 2]
    P1 --> L3[Conv 3x3 128 ReLU]
    L3 --> L4[Conv 3x3 128 ReLU]
    L4 --> P2[MaxPool 2x2 Stride 2]
    P2 --> L5[Conv 3x3 256 ReLU x3]
    L5 --> P3[MaxPool 2x2]
    P3 --> GAP[Global Average Pooling]
    GAP --> D1[Dense 256 ReLU]
    D1 --> DR[Dropout 0.5]
    DR --> D2[Dense 10 Softmax]
    D2 --> OUT[Class Probabilities]
```

### 4.3 Training Loop Block Diagram (Mini-Batch Gradient Descent)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start of Epoch] --> FB[Fetch Mini Batch X y]
    FB --> FP[Forward Pass: model.predict]
    FP --> LC[Compute Loss L]
    LC --> BP[Backward Pass: tf GradientTape]
    BP --> GA[Optimizer Adam Updates Weights]
    GA --> CK{Batch Complete?}
    CK -->|No| FB
    CK -->|Yes| EP{All Epochs Done?}
    EP -->|No| S
    EP -->|Yes| EV[model.evaluate on Test Set]
    EV --> END[Save SavedModel]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Why GAP Replaces Flatten)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Traditional[Traditional CNN Head]
        T1[Last Conv Feature Map HxWxC] --> T2[Flatten Vector HxWxC]
        T2 --> T3[Dense Layer Millions of Params]
    end
    subgraph Modern[Modern CNN Head GAP]
        M1[Last Conv Feature Map HxWxC] --> M2[Global Avg Pool Channel Wise Mean]
        M2 --> M3[Dense Layer Only CxClasses Params]
    end
    T3 --> Result1[Overfitting Risk High]
    M3 --> Result2[Compact Robust Features]
```
