## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term **tensor** in the context of TensorFlow. With a suitable example, explain the attributes **rank**, **shape**, and **dtype** of a `tf.Tensor` representing a batch of 16 grayscale images of size $28 \times 28$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **tensor** is an $N$-dimensional array of a uniform data type that is the fundamental data structure in TensorFlow. The three core attributes are:
- **Rank (ndim):** The number of axes/dimensions.
- **Shape:** A tuple giving the size along each axis.
- **Dtype:** The element type (e.g., `float32`, `int64`).

For a batch of 16 grayscale images of size $28 \times 28$, the tensor has:
- **Rank = 4** (batch, height, width, channels)
- **Shape = (16, 28, 28, 1)**
- **Dtype = float32** (typically normalized to $[0, 1]$)
*(3 marks for definition + correct attributes)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the **ReLU** activation function in a Convolutional Neural Network? Why is it preferred over the **sigmoid** activation in deep networks?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)** is defined as $f(x) = \max(0, x)$. It introduces non-linearity into the network, enabling it to learn complex mappings. It is preferred over **sigmoid** in deep networks because:
1. **No vanishing gradient:** Its derivative is exactly 1 for $x>0$, allowing gradients to flow through many layers.
2. **Sparsity:** Outputs exactly zero for negative inputs, producing sparse activations.
3. **Computational efficiency:** Only a comparison and a max operation — no exponentials.

Sigmoid, in contrast, saturates at both extremes, leading to vanishing gradients and stalled training. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the comparative justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)**. With a neat diagram, describe the function of the following layers: `Conv2D`, `MaxPooling2D`, `Flatten`, and `Dense`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a CNN layer with input feature map of size $64 \times 64 \times 16$, kernel size $3 \times 3$, padding $1$, stride $1$, and 32 filters, compute:
   1. The output feature map size.
   2. The total number of trainable parameters.
   3. The number of FLOPs (multiply-accumulate operations). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

A **CNN** is a deep feedforward network specialized for grid-structured data such as images. Its architecture typically consists of three stages repeated $N$ times, followed by a fully-connected classifier:

1. **Convolutional Layer (`Conv2D`):** Applies $C_{out}$ learnable $K \times K$ kernels across the input to produce $C_{out}$ feature maps. It captures **local spatial patterns** using parameter sharing. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Activation (default `ReLU`):** Introduces non-linearity element-wise, enabling the network to model non-linear decision boundaries. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Pooling Layer (`MaxPooling2D`):** Down-samples each feature map by taking the maximum over a $P \times P$ window (typically $2 \times 2$, stride 2). This provides **translation invariance** and reduces computation. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Flatten Layer:** Reshapes the 3-D feature tensor (e.g., $H \times W \times C$) into a 1-D vector of size $H \cdot W \cdot C$ so that it can be fed into a Dense layer. *(1 mark)*

5. **Dense (Fully-Connected) Layer:** Standard MLP layer where every neuron is connected to all inputs, used in the final classifier head. *(1 mark)*

6. **Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above):** A clean VGG-style block diagram earns the remaining *(1.5 marks)*.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $I = 64$, $K = 3$, $P = 1$, $S = 1$, $C_{in} = 16$, $C_{out} = 32$.

**1. Output Feature Map Size (2 marks):**
$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{64 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1$$
$$O = \lfloor 63 \rfloor + 1 = 64$$

The output is $\mathbf{64 \times 64 \times 32}$ (height, width, channels).

**2. Trainable Parameters (2.5 marks):**
$$P_{conv} = K^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$$
$$P = 3^2 \cdot 16 \cdot 32 + 32 = 9 \cdot 512 + 32 = 4{,}608 + 32 = \mathbf{4{,}640 \text{ parameters}}$$

**3. FLOPs (2.5 marks):**
$$\text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} \cdot K^2$$
$$\text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot 64 \cdot 64 \cdot 16 \cdot 32 \cdot 9 = \mathbf{37{,}748{,}736 \text{ MACs} \;(\approx 37.75 \text{ MFLOPs})}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Alternative]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **TensorFlow execution architecture** and the role of **`tf.keras`** as a high-level API. Differentiate between **eager execution** and **graph execution (`@tf.function`)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Keras `Sequential` model is defined for an image-classification task as follows:
```
Conv2D(32, kernel=3, padding='same', activation='relu', input_shape=(64,64,3))
MaxPooling2D(pool=2)
Conv2D(64, kernel=3, activation='relu')
MaxPooling2D(pool=2)
Flatten()
Dense(128, activation='relu')
Dropout(0.5)
Dense(10, activation='softmax')
```
Compute the output shape and parameter count of **each layer** and the **total trainable parameters** of the model. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

**1. TensorFlow Execution Architecture (3 marks):**
TensorFlow follows a layered architecture: at the top sits the **Python front-end (eager execution)** used for research and debugging. Operations are recorded into a **`tf.Graph`** dataflow representation, which is then optimized by the **XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) compiler** and dispatched to one of three back-ends: **CPU**, **GPU (CUDA + cuDNN)**, or **TPU**. This decoupling allows the same Python code to scale from a laptop to a data center without modification.

**2. Role of `tf.keras` (2 marks):**
`tf.keras` is the official high-level API that sits atop TensorFlow. It provides modular building blocks — `Layer`, `Model`, `Optimizer`, `Loss`, `Metric` — so that practitioners can compose DCNN architectures in a few lines. It handles weight initialization, training loops, serialization (`model.save`), and integration with TensorBoard.

**3. Eager vs Graph Execution (2 marks):**
- **Eager Execution:** Operations are evaluated **immediately** as they are called from Python. Pros: easy debugging, native Python control flow. Cons: slower due to Python overhead.
- **Graph Execution (`@tf.function`):** A Python function is **traced** once, converted into a `tf.Graph`, and executed as a single optimized op. Pros: 10×–100× speed-ups via XLA fusion, deployable on TF Lite / TF.js. Cons: tracing-related constraints (e.g., variable creation must respect `tf.init_scope`).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:**

We apply the output-size formula and parameter-count formula layer by layer.

| # | Layer | Output Shape | Parameters | Calculation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input | $(64, 64, 3)$ | 0 | — |
| 2 | Conv2D-1 (32 filters, $K=3$, $P=1$) | $(64, 64, 32)$ | **896** | $3^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 32 + 32 = 864 + 32$ |
| 3 | MaxPool-1 ($2 \times 2$, $S=2$) | $(32, 32, 32)$ | 0 | Pooling has no params |
| 4 | Conv2D-2 (64 filters, $K=3$, $P=0$) | $(30, 30, 64)$ | **18,496** | $3^2 \cdot 32 \cdot 64 + 64 = 18{,}432 + 64$ |
| 5 | MaxPool-2 ($2 \times 2$, $S=2$) | $(15, 15, 64)$ | 0 | — |
| 6 | Flatten | $(14{,}400)$ | 0 | $15 \cdot 15 \cdot 64 = 14{,}400$ |
| 7 | Dense-1 (128) | $(128)$ | **1,843,328** | $14{,}400 \cdot 128 + 128$ |
| 8 | Dropout (0.5) | $(128)$ | 0 | — |
| 9 | Dense-2 (10 softmax) | $(10)$ | **1,290** | $128 \cdot 10 + 10$ |

**Total Trainable Parameters:**
$$P_{total} = 896 + 18{,}496 + 1{,}843{,}328 + 1{,}290 = \mathbf{1{,}864{,}010 \approx 1.86 \text{ M parameters}}$$

**Observations (extra credit):**
- The `Flatten → Dense-1` step accounts for $1{,}843{,}328 / 1{,}864{,}010 \approx \mathbf{98.9\%}$ of all parameters — confirming the analytical matrix finding in Section 3.5.
- Replacing `Flatten + Dense-1` with `GlobalAveragePooling2D` would yield only $64 \cdot 128 + 128 + 128 \cdot 10 + 10 = 9{,}370$ parameters — a $\mathbf{200\times}$ reduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term:** When asked for parameter count, students often compute only $K^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out}$ and forget to add $C_{out}$. This costs **2 marks** in 7-mark problems.
> 2. **Wrong padding interpretation:** "Same" padding for stride 1 with an **odd** kernel means $P = (K-1)/2$ — not $P = K-1$. Using $P = K-1$ yields $O = I + K - 1$ and is a common blunder.
> 3. **Mixing up channels-first vs. channels-last:** TensorFlow uses `channels_last` (NHWC) by default. PyTorch uses `channels_first` (NCHW). Writing shape as `(batch, channels, H, W)` for a Keras model loses marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the floor operation:** When the input is not divisible by the stride, the floor is essential. Skipping the floor on a 14-mark problem can lose **1–2 marks** at the KTU valuation table.
> 5. **Conflating `Dense` and `Conv2D` parameter formulas:** Dense uses $n_{in} \cdot n_{out} + n_{out}$; Conv2D uses $K^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$. Mixing them up is the most common conceptual error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — TensorFlow and Keras for Deep CNNs**

- **TensorFlow's core data unit** is the `tf.Tensor`, characterized by **rank, shape, dtype**.
- **Keras (`tf.keras`)** is the official high-level API; it exposes `Sequential` and **Functional API** for model construction.
- **Eager execution** = immediate, Python-like; **`@tf.function`** = traced, graph-compiled, faster, deployable.
- **A DCNN** stacks `Conv2D → ReLU → MaxPool` blocks, followed by `Flatten → Dense → Softmax`.
- **Output size formula:** $O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Parameter count for Conv2D:** $K^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$ (don't forget the bias!).
- **Receptive field** grows by $(K_l - 1) \cdot J_l$ at every layer where $J_l$ is the cumulative stride product.
- **ReLU** is preferred over sigmoid in deep nets due to non-saturation and gradient flow.
- **Cross-entropy loss** $L = -\sum y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ is the standard for classification with softmax outputs.
- **Global Average Pooling** dramatically reduces parameters compared to Flatten+FC heads.
- **Three-step workflow:** `model.compile()` → `model.fit()` → `model.evaluate()`.
- **Common optimizers:** SGD (with momentum), Adam, RMSprop — Adam is the KTU-default for DCNN training.
- **Regularization tools in Keras:** `Dropout`, `BatchNormalization`, `L1/L2` kernel regularizers, **early stopping callback**.
- **Hardware acceleration:** Use `tf.config.list_physical_devices('GPU')` to verify GPU availability before training.
- **Model persistence:** `model.save('name.h5')` (HDF5) or `model.save('name/', save_format='tf')` (SavedModel) for deployment to **TF Lite / TF.js / TF Serving**.
- **Batch dimension is always first** in TensorFlow/Keras tensors: `(batch_size, H, W, C)`.
- **VGG-16 first layer** has exactly **1,792 parameters** — a favorite KTU viva question.
- **Modern best practice:** Replace large FC heads with GAP; use **BatchNorm** before activation; apply **data augmentation** (`tf.keras.layers.RandomFlip`, `RandomCrop`) to combat overfitting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Neural Network and Transfer Learning Using Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deep Neural Network and Transfer Learning Using Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deep Neural Network (DNN) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deep Neural Network (DNN):** A Deep Neural Network is a class of **machine learning models** composed of an **input layer**, **two or more hidden layers**, and an **output layer**, where each layer performs a parameterized linear transformation followed by a **non-linear activation function**. The depth (number of hidden layers) enables the network to learn **hierarchical, distributed representations** of input data, where shallow layers capture simple patterns (edges, textures) and deeper layers capture abstract semantic concepts (objects, intents).

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This concept maps to **Course Outcome CO3** — *Apply deep learning paradigms to solve complex engineering problems using Python frameworks.* It also aligns with **Bloom's Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**.

## 1.2 Transfer Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Learning (TL):** Transfer Learning is a **machine learning paradigm** in which a model trained on a **large source dataset** (e.g., ImageNet with **1.4 million** images across **1000 classes**) is **reused as the starting point** for a model on a **smaller, related target dataset**. Knowledge in the form of learned weights, feature maps, or representations is transferred from the source task $T_S$ to a target task $T_T$, formally defined by **Pan & Yang (2010)** as:

$$\mathcal{T}_S = \{D_S, T_S\} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathcal{T}_T = \{D_T, T_T\}$$

where $D_S \neq D_T$ (different domains) but the learned features remain useful.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Apprentice Mechanic**
>
> - **Training a DNN from scratch** is like teaching a fresh apprentice to **build an entire car engine from raw metal**. They must first learn metallurgy, geometry, thermodynamics — months of foundational study before producing useful work.
> - **Transfer Learning** is like hiring a **master mechanic who already knows engines** and asking them to learn your specific **hybrid engine model**. They reuse their existing knowledge (intake, combustion, exhaust) and only retrain the parts that differ (hybrid battery integration).
>
> In software terms: the convolutional base of a pre-trained CNN already understands *what edges, shapes, and textures look like* — you only teach it *your specific medical X-ray abnormalities*.

## 1.4 Why Python for DNN and Transfer Learning?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Rich Ecosystem | TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras are Python-native |
| Hardware Abstraction | One line: `model.fit(X, y, epochs=10)` — GPU acceleration automatic |
| Pretrained Models | `tensorflow.keras.applications` ships VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNet, EfficientNet |
| Community | Massive academic and industrial code repositories |

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used

- **Accuracy:** $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ — overall correctness
- **Precision:** $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — quality of positive predictions
- **Recall (Sensitivity):** $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — coverage of actual positives
- **F1-Score:** $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ — harmonic balance
- **Top-5 Accuracy:** Fraction of samples whose correct label appears in top 5 predictions (used in **ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Deep Neural Network

A DNN is built from **layers**. Each layer $l$ performs two operations:

1. **Linear Transformation (Affine Map):** Computes a weighted sum plus bias.
2. **Non-linear Activation:** Applies an element-wise non-linear function.

### Forward Propagation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Weighted Sum (Pre-activation):**
For layer $l$ with input vector $\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}$:

$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$

where $W^{(l)}$ is the weight matrix of shape $(n_l \times n_{l-1})$ and $b^{(l)}$ is the bias vector.

**Step 2 — Activation:**
$$a^{(l)} = f^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

**Step 3 — Stack Layers:**
$$\mathbf{a}^{(L)} = f^{(L)}(W^{(L)} f^{(L-1)}(W^{(L-1)} \cdots f^{(1)}(W^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + b^{(1)}) + b^{(L-1)}) + b^{(L)})$$

### Why Non-linearity?
> [!NOTE]
> Without non-linear activations, **stacking layers is mathematically equivalent to a single linear transformation** — the network cannot learn XOR, spiral patterns, or any non-linearly separable decision boundary.

## 2.2 Activation Functions (Comprehensive)

| Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Binary classification output |
| Tanh | $\tanh(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centered) |
| ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | **Default hidden layer** |
| Leaky ReLU | $\max(0.01z, z)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $0.01$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | Avoids dying ReLU |
| Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, $\sum=1$ | $\sigma_i(\delta_{ij}-\sigma_j)$ | **Multiclass output** |

## 2.3 Loss Functions

**Mean Squared Error (Regression):**
$$L_{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Binary Cross-Entropy:**
$$L_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$$

**Categorical Cross-Entropy (Multiclass):**
$$L_{CCE} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{c=1}^{C}y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

## 2.4 Backpropagation — The Core Learning Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation** computes the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to every weight in the network by applying the **chain rule of calculus** from output layer back to input layer. It is mathematically the **reverse-mode automatic differentiation**.

### Algorithm Steps
1. **Forward Pass:** Compute activations $\mathbf{a}^{(1)}, \mathbf{a}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$ and predictions $\hat{y}$.
2. **Loss Computation:** Evaluate $L(\hat{y}, y)$.
3. **Backward Pass:** Compute $\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(L)}}$ and propagate:
$$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot f'^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$
where $\odot$ is element-wise (Hadamard) product.
4. **Gradient Calculation:**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^T \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$
5. **Parameter Update (Gradient Descent):**
$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

## 2.5 Transfer Learning — Three Strategic Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Strategies** (in increasing order of complexity and parameter retraining):

### Strategy 1 — Feature Extraction (Frozen Base)
- **Freeze** all convolutional base layers: `layer.trainable = False`
- Replace and retrain only the **classifier head** (top dense layers)
- **Use when:** Target dataset is **small** (< 1000 images) and similar to source domain

### Strategy 2 — Fine-Tuning (Partial Unfreeze)
- **Unfreeze** top few convolutional blocks
- Train with a **small learning rate** (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$)
- **Use when:** Target dataset is **medium-sized** (1000–100,000 images)

### Strategy 3 — Full Fine-Tuning
- Unfreeze the entire network
- Use a **discriminative learning rate schedule** (lower for early layers, higher for later)
- **Use when:** Target dataset is **large** (> 100,000 images) and differs substantially from source

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM QUICK-REFERENCE — All Formulas Required for This Topic**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Pass (layer $l$) | $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ | $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{l-1}}$ |
| 2 | Activation Output | $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$ | Non-linearity applied element-wise |
| 3 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output range $(0,1)$ |
| 4 | ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | Default for hidden layers |
| 5 | Softmax | $\sigma_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multiclass probability |
| 6 | MSE Loss | $L = \frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression |
| 7 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $L = -\frac{1}{n}\sum[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Binary classification |
| 8 | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $L = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_i\sum_c y_{i,c}\log\hat{y}_{i,c}$ | Multiclass |
| 9 | Gradient Descent Update | $W := W - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate |
| 10 | Backprop Error Term | $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot f'(z^{(l)})$ | Chain rule |
| 11 | Weight Gradient | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ | Matrix outer product |
| 12 | Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range $[0,1]$ |
| 13 | Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| 14 | Recall | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate |
| 15 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 16 | Transfer Learning Update | $W_{\text{target}}^{(l)} = W_{\text{source}}^{(l)} - \eta_{\text{small}} \nabla L_T$ | Small $\eta$ for fine-tuning |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Transfer learning from ImageNet → chest X-ray classification (COVID-19 detection) with only **500 training images**.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Pre-trained object detection models (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) fine-tuned for specific traffic sign datasets.
- **Industrial Defect Detection:** Manufacturing plants reuse VGG/ResNet backbones to detect micro-cracks in metal castings.
- **NLP:** BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) pre-trained on the entire Wikipedia corpus is fine-tuned for sentiment analysis, question answering, and named-entity recognition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Pass for a 2-Layer Network

Consider a network with **1 input**, **1 hidden layer with 3 neurons**, and **1 output neuron**. Let:
- Input: $x$
- Hidden layer weights: $W^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} \\ w_{21} \\ w_{31} \end{bmatrix}$, bias $b^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \end{bmatrix}$
- Output weights: $W^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(2)} & w_{12}^{(2)} & w_{13}^{(2)} \end{bmatrix}$, bias $b^{(2)}$
- Activation: ReLU for hidden, Sigmoid for output.

**Step 1: Compute hidden pre-activation $z^{(1)}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z^{(1)} &= W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} \\ w_{21} \\ w_{31} \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} x + b_1 \\ w_{21} x + b_2 \\ w_{31} x + b_3 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Each hidden neuron computes a weighted sum of inputs plus its own bias — this is the dot product of its weight vector with the input.

**Step 2: Apply ReLU activation to obtain $a^{(1)}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a^{(1)} &= \text{ReLU}(z^{(1)}) \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} \max(0,\ w_{11}x + b_1) \\ \max(0,\ w_{21}x + b_2) \\ \max(0,\ w_{31}x + b_3) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* ReLU keeps positive signals and zeroes out negative ones, introducing non-linearity. Any negative pre-activation becomes exactly zero.

**Step 3: Compute output pre-activation $z^{(2)}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z^{(2)} &= W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} w_{11}^{(2)} & w_{12}^{(2)} & w_{13}^{(2)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1^{(1)} \\ a_2^{(1)} \\ a_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} + b^{(2)} \\
&= w_{11}^{(2)} a_1^{(1)} + w_{12}^{(2)} a_2^{(1)} + w_{13}^{(2)} a_3^{(1)} + b^{(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Output neuron takes a linear combination of all hidden activations and adds a final bias.

**Step 4: Apply Sigmoid activation for prediction $\hat{y}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(2)}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Sigmoid squashes the real-valued $z^{(2)}$ into the probability range $[0, 1]$, suitable for binary classification.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Backpropagation for One Sample

Given true label $y$ and squared-error loss $L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$, derive the gradient with respect to output weights $W^{(2)}$.

**Step 1: Initialize — Loss**

$$L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$$

**Step 2: Derivative of Loss w.r.t. $\hat{y}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}}\left[\frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2(y - \hat{y}) \cdot (-1) \\
&= -(y - \hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Power rule applied to $(\cdot)^2$, then chain rule gives the inner derivative $-1$.

**Step 3: Derivative of Sigmoid — a key identity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'(z) &= \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}\right] \\
&= \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2} \\
&= \frac{1+e^{-z}-1}{(1+e^{-z})^2} \\
&= \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}} \\
&= \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Used algebraic manipulation to express the derivative in terms of the function itself — a crucial identity for efficient backprop.

**Step 4: Derivative of Loss w.r.t. $z^{(2)}$ (Chain Rule)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \\
&= (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Chain rule applied: loss depends on $\hat{y}$, which depends on $z^{(2)}$. We multiply the two derivatives.

**Step 5: Derivative of Loss w.r.t. $W^{(2)}$ (Final Step)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial W^{(2)}} \\
&= (\hat{y} - y) \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \cdot a^{(1)T}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Since $z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}$, its derivative w.r.t. $W^{(2)}$ is the transpose of the input $a^{(1)}$.

**Step 6: Update Weights via Gradient Descent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} &= W^{(2)}_{\text{old}} - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} \\
&= W^{(2)} - \alpha (\hat{y} - y)\hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) (a^{(1)})^T
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Standard gradient descent — move weights in the **opposite direction** of the gradient, scaled by learning rate $\alpha$ (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Transfer Learning Update Rule

In transfer learning, the source model has weights $W_S^*$ (already optimal on source data). We fine-tune on target data with loss $L_T$.

**Step 1: Initialise from Source**

$$
W_T^{(0)} = W_S^*
$$

*Reasoning:* Start optimization at the solution of the source problem — this is the entire conceptual core of transfer learning.

**Step 2: Modified Loss Function (Regularisation Toward Source)**

To prevent **catastrophic forgetting** (the model forgetting source knowledge), we add a penalty:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{combined}}(W_T) &= L_T(W_T) + \lambda \cdot L_{\text{reg}}(W_T, W_S^*) \\
&= L_T(W_T) + \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert W_T - W_S^* \vert\vert_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda$ is a small regularisation coefficient (often called **L2-SP** or **knowledge distillation-style anchoring**).

**Step 3: Compute Combined Gradient**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L_{\text{combined}}}{\partial W_T} &= \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial W_T} + \lambda (W_T - W_S^*)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Gradient of $\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert W_T - W_S^* \vert\vert^2$ w.r.t. $W_T$ is simply $(W_T - W_S^*)$, pulling weights back toward the source.

**Step 4: Fine-Tuning Update (Discriminative Learning Rate)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_T^{(t+1)} &= W_T^{(t)} - \eta \left[\nabla L_T(W_T^{(t)}) + \lambda(W_T^{(t)} - W_S^*)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta \ll \eta_{\text{from-scratch}}$ (typically $\eta = 10^{-4}$ vs. $10^{-3}$).

*Reasoning:* Use a **small learning rate** to make conservative updates — large steps would obliterate the source knowledge.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example

Suppose source model has weight $W_S^* = 0.7$, target initialised at the same value. After one gradient descent step on target data, compute the update.

**Given:**
- $\nabla L_T(W_T) = 0.15$ (gradient on target loss)
- $\lambda = 0.01$
- $\eta = 0.001$
- $W_T^{(0)} = 0.7$, $W_S^* = 0.7$

**Compute Regularisation Gradient:**
$$
\lambda (W_T^{(0)} - W_S^*) = 0.01 \cdot (0.7 - 0.7) = 0
$$

**Combined Gradient:**
$$
g_{\text{combined}} = 0.15 + 0 = 0.15
$$

**New Weight:**
$$
W_T^{(1)} = 0.7 - 0.001 \cdot 0.15 = 0.7 - 0.00015 = 0.69985
$$

*Interpretation:* The weight moved a **tiny amount** ($0.015\%$) — exactly the conservative behavior expected in fine-tuning. The regularisation term contributed zero here because $W_T = W_S^*$ initially.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deep Neural Network — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Layer x] --> B[Hidden Layer 1 - 128 neurons ReLU]
    B --> C[Hidden Layer 2 - 64 neurons ReLU]
    C --> D[Hidden Layer 3 - 32 neurons ReLU]
    D --> E[Output Layer - Softmax or Sigmoid]
    E --> F[Prediction yhat]
    F --> G[Loss Computation L cross entropy or MSE]
    G --> H[Backpropagation via chain rule]
    H --> I[Gradient Descent Update W minus alpha times dL by dW]
    I --> B

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style D fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style G fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24
    style H fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404
    style I fill:#bee5eb,stroke:#0c5460
```

## 4.2 Transfer Learning — Feature Extraction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pre-trained Source Model e.g. ResNet50 ImageNet] --> P2[Convolutional Base Feature Extractor]
    P2 --> P3[Freeze All Convolutional Layers trainable equals False]
    P3 --> P4[Remove Original Classifier Top 1000 classes]
    P4 --> P5[Add New Classifier Head Dense 256 ReLU Dropout 0.5 Dense num_classes Softmax]
    P5 --> P6[Compile Model Adam optimizer low learning rate]
    P6 --> P7[Train on Target Dataset e.g. medical X-rays]
    P7 --> P8[Evaluate on Test Set Accuracy F1 Score]
    P8 --> P9[Optional Fine-tuning Unfreeze top blocks learning rate 10 to power minus 5]

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style P2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style P3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style P4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style P5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style P6 fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404
    style P7 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style P8 fill:#bee5eb,stroke:#0c5460
    style P9 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5a2d82
```

## 4.3 Backpropagation — Information Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Loss L y minus yhat squared] --> A1[Compute dL by dyhat]
    A1 --> A2[Compute dL by dz output = dL by dyhat times sigma prime]
    A2 --> A3[Compute dL by dW2 = dL by dz2 times a1 transpose]
    A2 --> A4[Compute dL by da1 = W2 transpose times dL by dz2]
    A4 --> A5[Compute dL by dz1 = dL by da1 times ReLU prime]
    A5 --> A6[Compute dL by dW1 = dL by dz1 times x transpose]
    A3 --> U1[W2 new = W2 minus alpha times dL by dW2]
    A6 --> U2[W1 new = W1 minus alpha times dL by dW1]

    style L fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style A1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style A2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style A3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style A4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style A5 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style A6 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style U1 fill:#bee5eb,stroke:#0c5460
    style U2 fill:#bee5eb,stroke:#0c5460
```

## 4.4 Python Implementation — DNN with Transfer Learning (TensorFlow/Keras)

```python
# ============================================================
# KTU PEEBT756 - Module 3
# Deep Neural Network + Transfer Learning using Python
# ============================================================

import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import layers, models, optimizers
from tensorflow.keras.applications import ResNet50
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator

# ---------- STEP 1: Load Pretrained Source Model ----------
base_model = ResNet50(
    weights='imagenet',      # pretrained on ImageNet
    include_top=False,       # remove 1000-class classifier
    input_shape=(224, 224, 3)
)

# ---------- STEP 2: Freeze Convolutional Base ----------
for layer in base_model.layers:
    layer.trainable = False

# ---------- STEP 3: Add New Classifier Head ----------
model = models.Sequential([
    base_model,
    layers.GlobalAveragePooling2D(),
    layers.Dense(256, activation='relu'),
    layers.Dropout(0.5),
    layers.Dense(2, activation='softmax')   # binary: cat vs dog
])

# ---------- STEP 4: Compile with Low Learning Rate ----------
model.compile(
    optimizer=optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=1e-4),
    loss='categorical_crossentropy',
    metrics=['accuracy']
)

# ---------- STEP 5: Prepare Data Generators ----------
train_gen = ImageDataGenerator(
    rescale=1./255,
    rotation_range=20,
    horizontal_flip=True
).flow_from_directory('data/train', target_size=(224, 224),
                      batch_size=32, class_mode='categorical')

val_gen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1./255).flow_from_directory(
    'data/val', target_size=(224, 224),
    batch_size=32, class_mode='categorical'
)

# ---------- STEP 6: Train (Feature Extraction Phase) ----------
history = model.fit(train_gen, epochs=10, validation_data=val_gen)

# ---------- STEP 7: Fine-Tuning Phase ----------
for layer in base_model.layers[-10:]:    # unfreeze last 10 layers
    layer.trainable = True

model.compile(
    optimizer=optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=1e-5),  # even smaller LR
    loss='categorical_crossentropy',
    metrics=['accuracy']
)

model.fit(train_gen, epochs=5, validation_data=val_gen)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Code-to-Concept Mapping:**
> - **Step 2** implements **Transfer Learning Strategy 1 (Feature Extraction)** — freezing convolutional base.
> - **Step 7** implements **Strategy 2 (Fine-Tuning)** — unfreezing the last 10 layers.
> - The learning rate drop from `1e-4` to `1e-5` is the **discriminative learning rate** principle in action.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Deep Neural Network (DNN). Differentiate between shallow and deep neural networks with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A Deep Neural Network (DNN) is an artificial neural network with **two or more hidden layers** between the input and output layers. Each hidden layer learns progressively abstract representations of the input data through non-linear transformations.

**Difference (1.5 Marks):**

| Feature | Shallow Network | Deep Network |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden Layers | 0 or 1 | $\geq 2$ |
| Feature Learning | Manual / Hand-crafted | Automatic, hierarchical |
| Example | Logistic Regression, Single Perceptron | ResNet-50, VGG-16, BERT |
| Data Requirement | Low | High |
| Computational Cost | Low | High (GPUs required) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is Transfer Learning? List and briefly explain its two main strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Transfer Learning is a machine learning technique where a model developed for a **source task** is reused as the starting point for a model on a **related target task**, leveraging previously learned features to reduce training time and data requirements.

**Two Strategies (2 Marks):**

1. **Feature Extraction (1 Mark):** The convolutional base of a pre-trained model is **frozen** (weights not updated) and used as a fixed feature extractor. Only a new classifier head is trained on top.

2. **Fine-Tuning (1 Mark):** Some top layers of the pre-trained base are **unfrozen** and trained with a **small learning rate** along with the new classifier head, allowing the model to adapt high-level features to the new task.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Deep Neural Network with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of activation functions in enabling non-linear learning. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the backpropagation algorithm for a 2-layer neural network with sigmoid activation using the chain rule. Show all gradient computations explicitly. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Architecture (4 Marks):**

A DNN consists of:
1. **Input Layer** — receives raw feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$
2. **Hidden Layers** — multiple layers, each performing $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ followed by activation
3. **Output Layer** — produces final prediction $\hat{y}$

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Input] -> [Hidden L1: 128 ReLU] -> [Hidden L2: 64 ReLU] ->
[Hidden L3: 32 ReLU] -> [Output: Softmax/Sigmoid] -> [Prediction]
```

**Role of Activation Functions (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without activation functions, stacking multiple linear layers is **mathematically equivalent** to a single linear transformation:
> $$f(W_2(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2) = W'x + b'$$
> where $W' = W_2 W_1$ and $b' = W_2 b_1 + b_2$. The network cannot solve **non-linearly separable** problems like XOR.

Activation functions introduce **non-linearity**, allowing the network to approximate **any continuous function** (Universal Approximation Theorem). Common choices:
- **ReLU** — default for hidden layers; computationally efficient, mitigates vanishing gradient.
- **Sigmoid/Softmax** — used in output for probability outputs.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Setup (1 Mark):**
Network has input $x$, one hidden layer with sigmoid activation, output with sigmoid. Loss: $L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$.

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (2 Marks):**
$$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}, \quad a^{(1)} = \sigma(z^{(1)})$$
$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}, \quad \hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$$

**Step 2 — Output Layer Error (1 Mark):**
$$\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{(2)}) = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Step 3 — Hidden Layer Error via Chain Rule (1 Mark):**
$$\delta^{(1)} = (W^{(2)})^T \delta^{(2)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(1)})$$
$$= (W^{(2)})^T \delta^{(2)} \odot a^{(1)}(1 - a^{(1)})$$

**Step 4 — Gradients (1 Mark):**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} (a^{(1)})^T$$
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} x^T$$

**Step 5 — Update Rule (1 Mark):**
$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}, \quad \forall l \in \{1, 2\}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — July 2024] (Alternative Choice)**
**(a)** What is Transfer Learning? Explain with a Python code example how feature extraction is implemented using a pre-trained ResNet50 model in TensorFlow/Keras. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the three transfer learning strategies. Under what data conditions is each most appropriate? Provide the mathematical formulation of fine-tuning. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Transfer Learning is the practice of reusing a model trained on one task as the starting point for a model on a second, related task. It reduces training time, data requirements, and improves generalization.

**Python Code — Feature Extraction with ResNet50 (5 Marks):**

```python
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.applications import ResNet50
from tensorflow.keras import layers, models

# Load pretrained model WITHOUT classifier
base = ResNet50(weights='imagenet', include_top=False,
                input_shape=(224, 224, 3))

# Freeze all layers in base
for layer in base.layers:
    layer.trainable = False

# Add custom classifier head
model = models.Sequential([
    base,
    layers.GlobalAveragePooling2D(),
    layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    layers.Dropout(0.3),
    layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')   # 10 target classes
])

model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='categorical_crossentropy',
              metrics=['accuracy'])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concepts Tested:**
> - `include_top=False` removes ImageNet classifier **(0.5 Mark)**
> - `layer.trainable = False` freezes weights **(0.5 Mark)**
> - New `Dense` layers added **(1 Mark)**
> - Compilation with appropriate optimizer and loss **(1 Mark)**
> - Correctness of overall structure **(2 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Strategy | Layers Trainable | Data Needed | Compute | Use When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feature Extraction | Only classifier head | Small (< 1K samples) | Low | Small dataset, similar to source |
| Fine-Tuning | Top few conv blocks + head | Medium (1K–100K) | Medium | Moderate dataset, somewhat different |
| Full Fine-Tuning | All layers | Large (> 100K) | High | Large dataset, very different from source |

**Mathematical Formulation of Fine-Tuning (4 Marks):**

Initialise target weights from source optimum:

$$W_T^{(0)} = W_S^*$$

Define combined loss with regularisation toward source:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{combined}}(W_T) &= L_T(W_T) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\vert\vert W_T - W_S^* \vert\vert_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Take gradient and update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla L_{\text{combined}} &= \nabla L_T(W_T) + \lambda(W_T - W_S^*) \\
W_T^{(t+1)} &= W_T^{(t)} - \eta \nabla L_{\text{combined}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta \approx 10^{-5}$ (much smaller than from-scratch learning rate) and $\lambda$ controls preservation of source knowledge.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DNN & Transfer Learning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the non-linearity argument (–2 marks):** Students often state that activation functions "speed up training" but miss the **mathematical impossibility** of learning XOR without non-linearity.
> 2. **Confusing backprop with gradient descent (–2 marks):** Backpropagation is the **algorithm to compute gradients**; gradient descent is the **algorithm to update weights using those gradients**. They are not the same.
> 3. **Using high learning rate during fine-tuning (–1 mark):** Fine-tuning with $\eta = 10^{-3}$ will destroy pre-trained features. Always use $\eta \leq 10^{-4}$.
> 4. **Forgetting `include_top=False` in code (–1 mark):** Loading ResNet50 without this argument keeps the 1000-class ImageNet head, breaking transfer learning.
> 5. **Skipping the loss function justification (–1 mark):** When using `softmax` output, the correct loss is **`categorical_crossentropy`**, not `binary_crossentropy` or `mse`.
> 6. **Sign error in backprop derivation (–2 marks):** The derivative of $L = \frac{1}{2}(y-\hat{y})^2$ w.r.t. $\hat{y}$ is **$(\hat{y} - y)$**, not $(y - \hat{y})$. Get the chain rule direction wrong and you lose the entire derivation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Deep Neural Networks & Transfer Learning**

- ✅ A **Deep Neural Network** has $\geq 2$ hidden layers and learns **hierarchical features** automatically.
- ✅ Forward propagation: $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$, then $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$.
- ✅ **ReLU** is the default activation for hidden layers; **Softmax/Sigmoid** for output.
- ✅ **Backpropagation** = reverse-mode automatic differentiation using the **chain rule**.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent update:** $W := W - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$, where $\alpha$ = learning rate.
- ✅ Loss functions: **MSE** for regression, **Cross-Entropy** for classification.
- ✅ **Transfer Learning** reuses pre-trained weights ($W_S^* \rightarrow W_T^{(0)}$).
- ✅ Three strategies: **Feature Extraction** (frozen base), **Fine-Tuning** (partial unfreeze), **Full Fine-Tuning** (all layers).
- ✅ Fine-tuning requires **small learning rate** ($\sim 10^{-5}$) to prevent **catastrophic forgetting**.
- ✅ Regularisation term $\lambda \cdot \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert W_T - W_S^* \vert\vert^2$ anchors fine-tuned weights to source.
- ✅ In Keras: `layer.trainable = False` freezes; `include_top=False` removes classifier head.
- ✅ Discriminative learning rates: lower for early layers, higher for later layers during fine-tuning.
- ✅ Metrics: **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score** — choose based on class imbalance.
- ✅ KTU 2024 maps this topic to **CO3** (Apply deep learning paradigms) and **CO4** (Analyze results).
- ✅ Common architectures: **VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNet, EfficientNet, BERT, GPT** — all support transfer learning.
- ✅ Code pattern: `base = ResNet50(weights='imagenet', include_top=False)` → freeze → add head → `compile` → `fit`.
- ✅ Vanishing gradient occurs with sigmoid/tanh in deep nets — **ReLU mitigates this**.
- ✅ Batch normalization and dropout are **regularisation techniques** that improve generalization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT756 - ADVANCED COMPUTER PROGRAMMING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Navigation Stack — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation Stack Definition (KTU Standard)**
> The **Navigation Stack** is a modular software framework, primarily implemented within the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, that enables a mobile robot to autonomously move from a start pose to a goal pose in a known or partially known environment while avoiding obstacles. It integrates **perception, localization, path planning, and motion control** into a unified, real-time, message-passing architecture governed by the ROS master node.

The Navigation Stack is **not** a single algorithm — it is a **collection of nodes, topics, services, and configuration files** that collectively solve the autonomous navigation problem. The official KTU 2024 syllabus refers to this as a *hierarchical planner-controller* system operating on **transformed coordinate frames** linked through the `tf` (transform) library.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Syllabus Highlights**
> - Framework: ROS (Robot Operating System)
> - Central Node: `move_base`
> - Standard Algorithm Pair: **Global Planner (Dijkstra / A\*)** + **Local Planner (DWA / TEB)**
> - Localization: **AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)**
> - Mapping Support: `map_server` with occupancy grid maps

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car in a Mall"

Imagine you are dropped inside a **huge shopping mall** for the first time. You need to walk from the main entrance (start) to a specific shop (goal). What do you do?

| Real-World Action | Navigation Stack Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You check the mall directory map on your phone | **Static Map** served by `map_server` |
| You figure out "Where am I right now?" by looking around | **AMCL Localization** using laser scans |
| You plan a rough path: turn left, go straight, take escalator | **Global Planner (A\*)** — global path |
| You walk step-by-step, dodging people and stalls | **Local Planner (DWA / TEB)** — reactive control |
| You stop, look at a kiosk, recompute when lost | **Recovery Behaviors** |
| Your phone knows how your body is oriented | **Odometry + IMU** giving `tf` transforms |

This three-tier structure — *plan the big picture → react to the immediate world → recover when stuck* — is the philosophical backbone of the Navigation Stack.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Constants & Limits Used in the Stack**
> - **Maximum planning frequency:** Typically **20 Hz** for `move_base` control loop
> - **Standard costmap resolution:** **0.05 m** (5 cm) per cell
> - **Inflation radius:** Default **0.55 m** for safe obstacle padding
> - **Robot footprint radius:** Commonly **0.30 m** to **0.45 m** for differential drive
> - **Linear velocity bounds:** `min_vel_x = 0.1 m/s`, `max_vel_x = 0.5 m/s`
> - **Angular velocity bounds:** `min_vel_theta = 0.4 rad/s`, `max_vel_theta = 1.0 rad/s`
> - **Controller frequency:** **10 Hz – 20 Hz** (real-time constraint)

These values appear in `costmap_common_params.yaml`, `local_costmap_params.yaml`, `global_costmap_params.yaml`, and `base_local_planner_params.yaml`.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

The Navigation Stack is a **mandatory Module 4 topic** because it tests a student's ability to:

1. Integrate multiple algorithms (planning + control) into one system.
2. Understand **ROS communication primitives** (topics, services, actions).
3. Debug a real-time robotic system using `rviz`, `tf`, and log files.
4. Translate mathematical cost functions into working configuration files.

Board examiners frequently test whether students can **draw the block diagram**, **list the components**, and **explain the role of costmaps** — not just memorize the name "move_base".

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Navigation Stack — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Mandatory Components of the ROS Navigation Stack

The KTU syllabus explicitly mandates knowledge of the **five interacting packages** that compose the stack. These are:

1. **`tf` (Transform Library)**
2. **Sensor Sources** (Laser, RGB-D, Sonar)
3. **Odometry Source** (wheel encoders + IMU)
4. **`map_server`** (Static map provider)
5. **`amcl`** (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)
6. **`move_base`** (Central high-level planner-controller)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Favourite Question:** *"List the components of the ROS Navigation Stack."* — Memorize these six in order.

---

## 2.2 Component-Wise Breakdown

### 2.2.1 `tf` — The Transform Library

`tf` maintains the **spatial relationship** between every coordinate frame in the robot. A typical mobile robot has at least these frames:

$$
\text{map} \rightarrow \text{odom} \rightarrow \text{base\_footprint} \rightarrow \text{base\_link} \rightarrow \text{laser\_frame}
$$

Each arrow represents a **6-DOF transformation** $(x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw})$ that is **broadcast continuously** as `tf` messages.

**Why is `tf` essential?**
Without `tf`, the robot cannot answer: *"Is that laser scan point measured in laser-frame actually 1.2 m ahead of my wheels?"* Every planner and controller subscribes to `tf` to translate sensor data into the planning frame.

### 2.2.2 Sensor Sources

The stack requires **range-finder data** on the topic:

```
/scan  (sensor_msgs/LaserScan)        — for 2D
/pointcloud  (sensor_msgs/PointCloud2) — for 3D converted to 2D
```

The sensor data is used to build a **local costmap** in real time.

### 2.2.3 Odometry Source

Odometry is published as:

```
/odom  (nav_msgs/Odometry)
```

It contains the robot's **estimated pose** derived from wheel encoders fused with an IMU through an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or `robot_pose_ekf` / `robot_localization` package.

### 2.2.4 `map_server`

`map_server` serves a **pre-built occupancy grid map** (e.g., from `gmapping` or `cartographer`). The map is a 2D array where:

- Cell value `0` → Free space
- Cell value `100` → Occupied
- Cell value `-1` → Unknown

### 2.2.5 `amcl` — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization

AMCL localizes the robot in the known map using a **particle filter**. It maintains $N$ particles (typically **500 – 5000**), each representing a hypothesis of the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$.

The particle weights are updated using the **observation likelihood**:

$$
w_i^{(t)} = w_i^{(t-1)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)
$$

where:
- $w_i^{(t)}$ = weight of particle $i$ at time $t$
- $z_t$ = current laser scan measurement
- $x_i^{(t)}$ = hypothesized pose of particle $i$
- $m$ = the known map
- $p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)$ = likelihood of seeing $z_t$ from $x_i^{(t)}$ in map $m$

Particles with low weights are **resampled** — keeping only plausible hypotheses.

### 2.2.6 `move_base` — The Brain

`move_base` is the **single coordinating node** that orchestrates everything. It has three internal sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Role | Typical Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Global Planner** | Computes path from start to goal | Dijkstra, A*, NavFn |
| **Local Planner** | Tracks the path while avoiding obstacles | DWA, TEB, base_local_planner |
| **Recovery Behaviors** | Reacts when stuck | Rotate Recovery, Clear Costmap |

---

## 2.3 The Two-Layer Costmap Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** The Navigation Stack uses TWO simultaneous costmaps.

### Global Costmap
- Built from the **static map + sensor data**
- Used by the global planner
- **Slow update** (e.g., 1.0 Hz)
- Large inflation radius to keep global paths away from walls

### Local Costmap
- Built only from **rolling sensor data** (rolling window)
- Used by the local planner
- **Fast update** (e.g., 5.0 Hz)
- Smaller inflation radius for tight reactive maneuvers

### Costmap Cell Values

The costmap stores a value $c$ for each cell:

$$
c = \begin{cases}
\text{LETHAL} = 254 & \text{if occupied} \\
\text{INSCRIBED} = 253 & \text{inside robot footprint} \\
\text{possible values } 0 \text{ to } 252 & \text{inflation layer decay} \\
\text{FREE} = 0 & \text{unoccupied}
\end{cases}
$$

The **inflation layer** adds a smooth cost gradient around obstacles so the planner prefers to stay away from them.

---

## 2.4 Global Planner — A* Path Search

The global planner runs **A\*** (or Dijkstra) on a discretized grid derived from the global costmap. A* uses the cost function:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal
- For an 8-connected grid with diagonal moves:

$$
h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{\text{goal}})^2 + (y_n - y_{\text{goal}})^2}
$$

The heuristic must be **admissible** (never overestimate) to guarantee optimality.

### Typical A* Implementation in `navfn` / `global_planner`

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| `use_dijkstra` | `true` (for strict optimality) |
| `use_quadratic` | `true` |
| `use_grid_path` | `false` (prefer smooth path) |
| `old_planner` | `false` |
| `lethal_cost` | `253` |
| `neutral_cost` | `50` |
| `cost_factor` | `0.55` |
| `publish_potential` | `true` |

---

## 2.5 Local Planner — Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

DWA samples a **reachable velocity space** within the robot's dynamic limits and chooses the velocity that maximizes a weighted score:

$$
V(v, \omega) = \sigma \left( \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{clearance}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v,\omega) \right)
$$

where:
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting gains
- $\sigma$ = smoothing function
- $\text{heading}$ = alignment with global path
- $\text{clearance}$ = distance to nearest obstacle
- $\text{velocity}$ = forward speed

The **Dynamic Window** restricts sampling to velocities reachable in one control cycle:

$$
v \in [v_{\text{current}} - \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t, \; v_{\text{current}} + \dot{v}_{\max} \cdot \Delta t]
$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| AMCL particle weight | $w_i^{(t)} = w_i^{(t-1)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)$ | Probabilistic localization |
| A* total cost | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Global path optimality |
| Heuristic (Euclidean) | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n-x_g)^2+(y_n-y_g)^2}$ | Admissible grid search |
| DWA score | $V = \sigma(\alpha \cdot H + \beta \cdot C + \gamma \cdot V_v)$ | Local control selection |
| Dynamic window range | $v_{\min} \leq v \leq v_{\max}$ | Acceleration limit |
| Inflation radius | $r_{\text{infl}} = r_{\text{robot}} + r_{\text{safety}}$ | Obstacle padding |
| Costmap decay | $c(d) = \text{LETHAL} \cdot e^{-\gamma d}$ | Smooth obstacle cost |
| Control frequency | $f_c = 1 / \Delta t$ | Real-time guarantee |
| Odometry transform | $(x, y, \theta) = \int (v\cos\theta, v\sin\theta, \omega) \, dt$ | Dead reckoning |
| TF chain | $\text{map} \rightarrow \text{odom} \rightarrow \text{base\_footprint}$ | Frame consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Navigation Stack is used in **warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva)**, **hospital delivery bots**, **autonomous forklifts**, **agricultural rovers**, and **last-mile delivery drones**. Every commercial mobile robot above ₹5 lakh budget uses some variant of this architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Navigation Stack — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — A* Optimality Proof (Sketch)

The KTU 2024 paper sometimes asks to show **why A\* is optimal under an admissible heuristic**. We present the key steps.

**Step 1 — Definition of Optimality**

A search algorithm is *optimal* if, for any goal node $n^*$, the first time $n^*$ is expanded (removed from the open list), we have $g(n^*) = g^*(n^*)$ where $g^*$ is the true minimum cost.

**Step 2 — Assume for Contradiction**

Suppose A* expands a suboptimal goal node $n'$ with $g(n') > g^*(n')$. Let $n^*$ be an optimal goal on the open list.

**Step 3 — Use Admissibility of h**

Since $h$ is admissible, $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Therefore:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + h^*(n)
$$

**Step 4 — Bound $f(n^*)$**

For the optimal goal $n^*$ on the open list:

$$
f(n^*) = g(n^*) + h(n^*) = g^*(n^*) + h(n^*) \leq g^*(n^*) + h^*(n^*) = C^*
$$

where $C^*$ is the optimal path cost.

**Step 5 — Bound $f(n')$**

Since $n'$ is suboptimal:

$$
f(n') = g(n') + h(n') \geq g(n') > C^*
$$

**Step 6 — Contradiction**

A* always selects the open node with **lowest $f$**. Since $f(n^*) \leq C^* < f(n')$, A* must expand $n^*$ before $n'$. This contradicts the assumption. Hence A* is optimal.

$$
\boxed{\text{A* is optimal when } h(n) \leq h^*(n) \text{ for all } n}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — AMCL Particle Update

The **sequential importance sampling** update in AMCL proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Posterior at Time $t-1$**

We have a set of particles $\{x_i^{(t-1)}, w_i^{(t-1)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ representing:

$$
p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t-1)} \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_i^{(t-1)})
$$

where $u_{1:t-1}$ are control inputs (odometry).

**Step 2 — Prediction Step (Motion Model)**

Draw a new pose from the motion model:

$$
x_i^{(t)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_i^{(t-1)}, u_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Update Step (Measurement Model)**

Compute new weights:

$$
w_i^{(t)} = w_i^{(t-1)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize**

$$
\tilde{w}_i^{(t)} = \frac{w_i^{(t)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j^{(t)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Resample**

Draw $N$ new particles from the weighted set to combat **particle degeneracy**.

**Step 6 — Adaptive Particle Injection**

AMCL adds random particles when localization confidence drops. The number of injected particles:

$$
N_{\text{random}} = \max\left(0, \; \left\lceil \alpha \left( 1 - \frac{1}{w_{\text{avg}}} \right) \right\rceil \right)
$$

where $w_{\text{avg}}$ is the average particle weight and $\alpha$ is a tuning constant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — DWA Velocity Sampling

**Step 1 — Search Space**

The robot has linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. The full search space is:

$$
V_s = \{(v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_{\min}, v_{\max}], \; \omega \in [\omega_{\min}, \omega_{\max}]\}
$$

**Step 2 — Admissible Velocities (Collision-Free)**

Only velocities that allow stopping before hitting the nearest obstacle:

$$
V_a = \{(v, \omega) \mid v \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) \cdot \dot{v}_{\max}}, \; \omega \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_{\max}}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic Window (Reachable in $\Delta t$)**

$$
V_d = \{(v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_c - \dot{v}_{\max}\Delta t, \; v_c + \dot{v}_{\max}\Delta t], \; \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_{\max}\Delta t, \; \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_{\max}\Delta t]\}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Sample Space**

$$
V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Each $(v, \omega) \in V_r$**

For each candidate, simulate a short trajectory (typically **1 s – 2 s**) and score:

$$
G(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot v
$$

**Step 6 — Select Best Velocity**

$$
(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_r} G(v,\omega)
$$

The selected velocity is published as `cmd_vel` for one control cycle $\Delta t$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Parameter Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners love asking:** *"What happens if you change parameter X?"* Use this matrix.

| Parameter | Increased Value Effect | Decreased Value Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `inflation_radius` | Robot keeps greater distance from obstacles | Robot hugs walls |
| `cost_scaling_factor` | Sharper cost gradient near obstacles | Softer, riskier paths |
| `max_vel_x` | Faster traversal, less precise | Slower, more stable |
| `min_vel_x` | Robot stops too often | Robot creeps at low speed |
| `vx_samples` | Better DWA quality, slower compute | Faster but coarser choice |
| `sim_time` | Longer lookahead, smoother paths | More reactive, jittery |
| `pdist_scale` | Robot prefers global path | Robot more freedom to deviate |
| `gdist_scale` | Robot rushes toward goal | Robot more cautious |
| `occdist_scale` | Robot more obstacle-averse | Riskier maneuvers |
| `controller_frequency` | Smoother motion, more CPU | Jerky motion, lower CPU |
| `AMCL particles` | Better localization, more CPU | Faster but loses robot |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Costmap Inflation

**Problem:** Robot radius $= 0.30$ m. Inflation radius $= 0.55$ m. Cost-scaling factor $= 10$. Find the inscribed and inflated cost at a point $0.40$ m from an obstacle.

**Step 1 — Identify Zones**

- Distance to obstacle: $d = 0.40$ m
- Robot inscribed radius: $r_{\text{ins}} = 0.30$ m
- Inflation radius: $r_{\text{infl}} = 0.55$ m
- Since $r_{\text{ins}} < d < r_{\text{infl}}$, the point is in the **inflation zone**.

**Step 2 — Apply Decay Formula**

$$
\text{cost} = \text{LETHAL} \cdot \exp\left( -1 \cdot \text{cost\_scaling\_factor} \cdot (d - r_{\text{ins}}) \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$
\text{cost} = 254 \cdot \exp\left( -10 \cdot (0.40 - 0.30) \right)
$$

$$
\text{cost} = 254 \cdot \exp(-1) = 254 \cdot 0.3679 = 93.4
$$

**Step 4 — Round and Interpret**

$$
\boxed{\text{cost} \approx 93}
$$

This cell will be avoided by the global planner because the cost is high, but it is **not lethal** (i.e., the planner can still traverse it if necessary).

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example — AMCL Particle Weight Update

**Problem:** Particle $i$ has weight $w_i^{(t-1)} = 0.02$. The likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m) = 0.65$. Find the unnormalized weight.

**Step 1 — Apply Update Rule**

$$
w_i^{(t)} = 0.02 \cdot 0.65 = 0.013
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation**

The weight **decreased**, meaning this particle's hypothesized pose is now less consistent with the new scan. After normalization across all particles, particle $i$ is **less likely to survive resampling**.

---

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Full Navigation Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeRB[Recovery Behaviors]

    nodeMB --> nodeLP
    nodeMB --> nodeRB
    nodeGP --> nodeMB
    nodeLP --> nodeCV

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMap[frame: map]
    nodeOdom[frame: odom]
    nodeBaseF[frame: base_footprint]
    nodeBaseL[frame: base_link]
    nodeLaser[frame: laser_frame]

    nodeBaseL -- static transform --> nodeLaser
    nodeBaseL -- static transform --> nodeWheelL
    nodeBaseL -- static transform --> nodeWheelR
    nodeBaseL -- static transform --> nodeIMU
```

---

## 4.3 `move_base` Internal State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Planning
    Planning --> Controlling : global path found
    Controlling --> Planning : goal updated or path blocked
    Controlling --> Recovering : stuck detected
    Recovering --> Planning : recovery succeeded
    Recovering --> Recovering : try next recovery
    Recovering --> [*] : all recoveries failed
    Controlling --> [*] : goal reached
    Planning --> [*] : no valid path
```

---

## 4.4 Costmap Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Costmap[Costmap 2D Manager]
        nodeSL[Static Layer]
        nodeOL[Obstacle Layer]
        nodeIL[Inflation Layer]
        nodeVL[Voxel Layer optional 3D]
        nodeCM[Combined Costmap Array 2D Grid]
        nodeSL --> nodeCM
        nodeOL --> nodeCM
        nodeIL --> nodeCM
        nodeVL --> nodeCM
    end

    nodeSM[map_server /map] --> nodeSL
    nodeSC[Sensor /scan] --> nodeOL
    node3D[3D Point Cloud] --> nodeVL
    nodeCM --> nodeGP2[Global Planner]
    nodeCM --> nodeLP2[Local Planner]
```

---

## 4.5 Data Flow Sequence Diagram — Single Goal Navigation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant MB as move_base
    participant AMCL as amcl
    participant CM as Costmaps
    participant GP as Global Planner
    participant LP as Local Planner
    participant R as Robot

    U->>MB: action goal 2D Nav Goal
    MB->>AMCL: get current pose via tf
    AMCL-->>MB: pose x, y, theta
    MB->>CM: build global costmap
    MB->>GP: plan path start to goal
    GP-->>MB: global path
    loop every 50 ms
        MB->>CM: update local costmap
        MB->>LP: compute velocity cmd
        LP-->>MB: v, omega
        MB->>R: publish cmd_vel
    end
    R-->>AMCL: odometry feedback
    R-->>MB: odometry via tf
    MB->>U: action feedback status
    MB->>U: action result success
```

---

## 4.6 Recovery Behavior Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart[Stuck Detected]
    nodeR1[Recovery 1: Rotate in place]
    nodeCheck1{Unstuck?}
    nodeR2[Recovery 2: Clear costmap]
    nodeCheck2{Unstuck?}
    nodeR3[Recovery 3: Slow backup]
    nodeCheck3{Unstuck?}
    nodeFail[Goal Aborted]

    nodeStart --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeCheck1
    nodeCheck1 -- Yes --> nodePlan[Resume Planning]
    nodeCheck1 -- No --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeCheck2
    nodeCheck2 -- Yes --> nodePlan
    nodeCheck2 -- No --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeCheck3
    nodeCheck3 -- Yes --> nodePlan
    nodeCheck3 -- No --> nodeFail
```

# Navigation Stack — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three components of the ROS Navigation Stack and state their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`map_server`** — Serves the pre-built static occupancy grid map to all other nodes via the `/map` topic.
2. **`amcl`** — Performs probabilistic localization using a particle filter; estimates the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within the known map.
3. **`move_base`** — Acts as the central coordinating node, integrating global planning, local control, and recovery behaviors through ROS actions.

> [!NOTE]
> Acceptable alternative components: `tf` library, sensor sources, odometry source, global planner, local planner, recovery behaviors.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each correct component with function)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a costmap? Differentiate between global costmap and local costmap.**

**Model Answer:**

A **costmap** is a 2D grid map where each cell stores a cost value (0–254) representing the difficulty or danger of traversing that location.

| Feature | Global Costmap | Local Costmap |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Static map + sensor data | Sensor data only |
| Update rate | Slow (≈ 1 Hz) | Fast (≈ 5 Hz) |
| Window | Whole map | Rolling window around robot |
| Used by | Global planner | Local planner |
| Inflation radius | Larger | Smaller |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for table)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the ROS Navigation Stack**. List and describe any **six components** involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of the **AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)** algorithm. Derive the **particle weight update equation** and explain how resampling is performed. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### (a) Block Diagram and Six Components (7 marks)

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSens[Sensors LaserScan]
    nodeOdom[Odometry Wheel Encoder + IMU]
    nodeTF[tf Library]
    nodeMap[map_server Static Map]
    nodeAMCL[amcl Localization]
    nodeMB[move_base]
    nodeGP[Global Planner A star]
    nodeLP[Local Planner DWA]
    nodeCM1[Global Costmap]
    nodeCM2[Local Costmap]
    nodeRB[Recovery Behaviors]
    nodeOut[cmd_vel to Motors]

    nodeSens --> nodeCM2
    nodeSens --> nodeAMCL
    nodeOdom --> nodeTF
    nodeOdom --> nodeAMCL
    nodeTF --> nodeAMCL
    nodeMap --> nodeAMCL
    nodeMap --> nodeCM1
    nodeAMCL --> nodeMB
    nodeCM1 --> nodeMB
    nodeMB --> nodeGP
    nodeMB --> nodeLP
    nodeMB --> nodeRB
    nodeLP --> nodeOut
```

**Six Components Description:**

1. **`tf` (Transform Library):** Maintains the spatial relationship between coordinate frames (`map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → laser_frame`). Every other node subscribes to `tf` to convert data between frames.

2. **Sensor Sources:** Publish `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` on `/scan` topic. Used to detect obstacles and update local costmap.

3. **Odometry Source:** Publishes `nav_msgs/Odometry` containing the robot's pose and twist, derived from wheel encoders fused with IMU via EKF.

4. **`map_server`:** Loads a pre-built map (PGM + YAML) and serves it on `/map` and `/static_map` topics.

5. **`amcl`:** Uses a particle filter to localize the robot in the known map. Publishes the `map → odom` transform.

6. **`move_base`:** The central node; subscribes to goals, runs global planner, executes local planner, manages recovery behaviors.

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 5 for description)*

---

#### (b) AMCL Working and Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initialization**

AMCL initializes $N$ particles uniformly across the free space of the map, each with equal weight $w_i^{(0)} = 1/N$.

**Step 2 — Motion Update (Prediction)**

When odometry $u_t$ arrives, each particle's pose is updated using the motion model:

$$
x_i^{(t)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_i^{(t-1)}, u_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Measurement Update (Correction)**

When a laser scan $z_t$ arrives, weights are updated using the observation likelihood:

$$
w_i^{(t)} = w_i^{(t-1)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)
$$

The likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)$ is computed by **ray-casting** the scan in the map from the particle's pose and comparing the expected vs. actual obstacle distances.

**Step 4 — Normalization**

$$
\tilde{w}_i^{(t)} = \frac{w_i^{(t)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j^{(t)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Resampling**

Particles are resampled with replacement proportional to their weights. Particles with low weights die; particles with high weights multiply. This combats **particle degeneracy**.

**Step 6 — Adaptive Particle Injection (KLD-Sampling)**

AMCL dynamically adjusts the number of particles using Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD) sampling. When localization is confident, fewer particles are needed; when uncertain (e.g., kidnapped robot), more particles are injected randomly to recover.

**Step 7 — Pose Estimation**

The final pose estimate is the **weighted mean** of all particles:

$$
\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_i^{(t)} \cdot x_i^{(t)}
$$

This pose is broadcast as the `map → odom` `tf` transform.

*(7 marks — 2 for working, 3 for derivation, 2 for resampling + KLD explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** for local path planning. Derive the **velocity sampling space** $V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **costmap**? Explain the **inflation layer** with its mathematical decay function. A robot of radius 0.25 m uses inflation radius 0.60 m and cost-scaling factor 8. Calculate the cost at a point 0.45 m from an obstacle. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### (a) DWA Local Planner (7 marks)

**Concept:** The Dynamic Window Approach samples velocities within a robot's reachable and safe space, simulates short trajectories, and selects the velocity that maximizes a weighted objective.

**Three Velocity Spaces:**

1. **Search Space $V_s$:** All velocities the robot hardware permits:

$$
V_s = \{ (v, \omega) \mid v \in [v_{\min}, v_{\max}], \; \omega \in [\omega_{\min}, \omega_{\max}] \}
$$

2. **Admissible Velocities $V_a$:** Velocities that allow the robot to **stop before hitting the nearest obstacle**:

$$
V_a = \left\{ (v, \omega) \,\Big|\, v \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v,\omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b}, \; \omega \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d(v,\omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b} \right\}
$$

where $d(v, \omega)$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle along the trajectory and $\dot{v}_b, \dot{\omega}_b$ are braking accelerations.

3. **Dynamic Window $V_d$:** Velocities **reachable in one control cycle $\Delta t$** given current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$:

$$
V_d = \left\{ (v, \omega) \,\Big|\, v \in [v_c - \dot{v}_{\max}\Delta t, \; v_c + \dot{v}_{\max}\Delta t], \; \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_{\max}\Delta t, \; \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_{\max}\Delta t] \right\}
$$

**Final Sample Space:**

$$
\boxed{V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d}
$$

**Trajectory Simulation and Scoring:**

For each $(v, \omega) \in V_r$, simulate a forward rollout of duration `sim_time` (typically 1.5 s – 2.0 s) and compute:

$$
G(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot v
$$

where:
- `heading` = alignment of final pose with global path
- `dist` = minimum obstacle distance along trajectory
- $v$ = forward speed

The best velocity $(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max G(v, \omega)$ is published to `cmd_vel`.

*(7 marks — 2 for concept, 3 for derivation of three spaces, 2 for scoring)*

---

#### (b) Costmap and Inflation Layer (7 marks)

**Definition:** A **costmap** is a 2D occupancy grid where each cell stores a value $c \in [0, 254]$ representing traversal cost. Value 0 = free, 254 = lethal (occupied), intermediate values = inflation.

**Inflation Layer Working:**

The inflation layer takes the obstacle map and adds a smooth cost gradient around obstacles so that the planner naturally prefers paths farther from walls.

**Decay Function:**

For a cell at distance $d$ from the nearest obstacle:

$$
\text{cost}(d) = \begin{cases}
\text{LETHAL} = 254 & \text{if } d < r_{\text{ins}} \\
\text{LETHAL} \cdot \exp(-1 \cdot \gamma \cdot (d - r_{\text{ins}})) & \text{if } r_{\text{ins}} \leq d < r_{\text{infl}} \\
0 & \text{if } d \geq r_{\text{infl}}
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $r_{\text{ins}} = $ inscribed (robot footprint) radius
- $r_{\text{infl}} = $ inflation radius
- $\gamma = $ cost-scaling factor

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- Robot radius $r_{\text{ins}} = 0.25$ m
- Inflation radius $r_{\text{infl}} = 0.60$ m
- Cost-scaling factor $\gamma = 8$
- Distance to obstacle $d = 0.45$ m

Since $r_{\text{ins}} = 0.25 \leq 0.45 < r_{\text{infl}} = 0.60$, the cell is in the inflation zone.

$$
\text{cost} = 254 \cdot \exp(-8 \cdot (0.45 - 0.25))
$$

$$
\text{cost} = 254 \cdot \exp(-8 \cdot 0.20)
$$

$$
\text{cost} = 254 \cdot \exp(-1.6)
$$

$$
\text{cost} = 254 \cdot 0.2019
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{cost} \approx 51.3}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a moderate-cost cell. The global planner will avoid it if a cheaper alternative path exists.

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for formula explanation, 2 for numerical)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `tf`:** Many students list only 5 components and omit `tf`. **Always mention `tf` as the sixth.**
> 2. **Confusing costmap layers:** Students often write "obstacle layer" and "static layer" as separate costmaps — they are **layers within one costmap**.
> 3. **Wrong decay formula:** The exponential decay uses $(d - r_{\text{ins}})$, **not** $d$ directly. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Confusing AMCL with SLAM:** AMCL **localizes** in a known map; SLAM **builds** the map. Examiners frequently use this as a discriminator.
> 5. **DWA error:** Students write $V_d$ with $v_{\max}$ instead of $v_c \pm \dot{v}\Delta t$. This breaks the "dynamic" property.
> 6. **No units in numerical:** Costmap cost is dimensionless, but **distances must be in metres** and **velocities in m/s**. Missing units = -0.5 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation Stack — Final Rapid-Revision Summary**
> - **Framework:** ROS-based modular system; **not** a single algorithm.
> - **Six Components:** `tf`, Sensors, Odometry, `map_server`, `amcl`, `move_base`.
> - **Central Node:** `move_base` orchestrates everything via **ROS Actions**.
> - **Two Costmaps:** Global (slow, large inflation) + Local (fast, small inflation).
> - **Costmap Value Range:** 0 (free) to 254 (lethal); cell cost = $254 \cdot \exp(-\gamma(d - r_{\text{ins}}))$.
> - **Global Planner:** A* / Dijkstra; uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$; heuristic must be admissible.
> - **Local Planner:** DWA samples $V_r = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$; scores by heading + clearance + velocity.
> - **AMCL:** Particle filter for localization; weights updated by $w_i^{(t)} = w_i^{(t-1)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_i^{(t)}, m)$.
> - **Resampling:** Performed to combat particle degeneracy; weighted resampling with replacement.
> - **KLD-Sampling:** AMCL dynamically adjusts particle count based on localization confidence.
> - **Recovery Behaviors:** Rotate → Clear Costmap → Backup → Abort.
> - **TF Chain:** `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → laser_frame`.
> - **Control Topic:** `cmd_vel` (`geometry_msgs/Twist`).
> - **Goal Topic:** `move_base_simple/goal` (`geometry_msgs/PoseStamped`).
> - **Configuration Files:** `costmap_common_params.yaml`, `local_costmap_params.yaml`, `global_costmap_params.yaml`, `base_local_planner_params.yaml`, `global_planner_params.yaml`.
> - **Standard Frequencies:** Costmap update 5 Hz, controller loop 10–20 Hz, AMCL scan 5 Hz.
> - **Real-World Deployments:** Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics, Clearpath Husky, TurtleBot 3, hospital delivery bots.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
