* Write **ROS 2 nodes** in **Python (rclpy)** and **C++ (rclcpp)**.
* Use **TF2** to broadcast and listen to coordinate transforms between `odom`, `base_footprint`, and `base_scan`.
* Subscribe to `LaserScan` and `Image` topics and process them with **OpenCV** and **PCL**.
* Implement **SLAM** using the `slam_toolbox` or `cartographer` packages.
* Configure the **Navigation2 (Nav2)** stack for path planning and obstacle avoidance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TurtleBot Hardware Architecture

The TurtleBot is built around **three integrated subsystems**:

### A. Mobile Base (Differential-Drive Kinematics)
* **Two independently driven wheels** (left and right) mounted on a common axle.
* One or more **passive caster wheels** (or ball casters) for static stability.
* **Wheel encoders** (typically optical quadrature encoders) producing tick counts proportional to wheel rotation.
* The base cannot move sideways — it must **rotate to translate**, which is the defining trait of **non-holonomic** vehicles.

### B. Sensor Stack
1. **2D LIDAR (LDS-01 / RPLIDAR A1)** — Measures distance in a horizontal plane over **360°** at **5–10 Hz**, range up to **\mathbf{\approx 12\;m}**.
2. **RGB-D Camera (Kinect / Intel RealSense / OAK-D)** — Provides synchronized colour + depth frames.
3. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — Yields 3-axis acceleration and angular velocity.
4. **Wheel Encoders** — Provide high-frequency odometry.

### C. Onboard Compute + Open-Source Firmware
* Runs a full **Linux distribution (Ubuntu 20.04/22.04)** with **ROS 2 Humble/Iron**.
* The low-level motor controller is handled by an **OpenCR** board (an STM32-based microcontroller running **Dynamixel protocol firmware**).

## 2.2 The ROS 2 Software Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Programming Paradigm:** TurtleBot is programmed **declaratively and modularly** through ROS 2. There is **no single monolithic control loop**. Instead, independent executables called **nodes** communicate via **topics** (asynchronous, many-to-many streams), **services** (synchronous request/reply), and **actions** (long-running goals with feedback).

The high-level software stack can be decomposed into five layers:

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** `ros2_control` drivers for motors, encoders, IMU, LIDAR, and camera.
2. **Sensor Processing Layer:** `laser_filters`, `depthimage_to_laserscan`, `camera_info_manager`.
3. **Perception Layer:** `slam_toolbox`, `RTAB-Map`, `AprilTag_ros`.
4. **Planning Layer:** **Nav2** — `planner_server`, `controller_server`, `bt_navigator`, `recoveries_server`.
5. **Behaviour / Application Layer:** User-written Python/C++ nodes issuing **NavigateToPose** action goals.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols below are **examination-grade** for the KTU 2024 scheme. The differential-drive kinematics are the single most tested derivation in this module.

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\nu$ | Linear velocity of robot centre | $\text{m/s}$ | Forward speed of base_footprint |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity of robot | $\text{rad/s}$ | Yaw rate about $\hat{z}$ |
| $\nu_r$, $\nu_l$ | Right / left wheel linear speeds | $\text{m/s}$ | $r \cdot \dot{\phi}_r$, $r \cdot \dot{\phi}_l$ |
| $r$ | Wheel radius | $\text{m}$ | Typical $\mathbf{0.033\;m}$ for TB3 |
| $L$ | Wheelbase (track width) | $\text{m}$ | Typical $\mathbf{0.160\;m}$ for TB3 Burger |
| $s_r$, $s_l$ | Distances rolled by wheels | $\text{m}$ | Encoder-derived |
| $N_r$, $N_l$ | Encoder tick counts | ticks | Integer |
| $n$ | Encoder resolution | ticks/rev | Typical $\mathbf{4096}$ |
| $\theta$ | Robot heading angle | $\text{rad}$ | Yaw in odom frame |
| $x$, $y$ | Robot position | $\text{m}$ | In odom/map frame |

### Core Equations

| ID | Equation | Purpose |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| E1 | $\nu = \dfrac{\nu_r + \nu_l}{2}$ | Forward kinematic mean speed |
| E2 | $\omega = \dfrac{\nu_r - \nu_l}{L}$ | Forward kinematic yaw rate |
| E3 | $\nu_r = \nu + \dfrac{L}{2}\,\omega$ | Inverse kinematic right wheel |
| E4 | $\nu_l = \nu - \dfrac{L}{2}\,\omega$ | Inverse kinematic left wheel |
| E5 | $s_r = \dfrac{2\pi r}{n}\,N_r$ | Encoder-to-distance, right |
| E6 | $s_l = \dfrac{2\pi r}{n}\,N_l$ | Encoder-to-distance, left |
| E7 | $\Delta s = \dfrac{s_r + s_l}{2}$ | Mean incremental displacement |
| E8 | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{s_r - s_l}{L}$ | Incremental heading change |
| E9 | $\Delta x = \Delta s\,\cos\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Mid-point Euler odometry, $x$ |
| E10 | $\Delta y = \Delta s\,\sin\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Mid-point Euler odometry, $y$ |
| E11 | $P_{\text{LiDAR}} = \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2}$ | Time-of-Flight LIDAR range, $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Equations **E7–E10** are the **dead-reckoning odometry update** that runs at the rate of the wheel encoders (often 50–100 Hz). Without external corrections (e.g., from the LIDAR via **AMCL — Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization**), this estimate **drifts unboundedly** because encoder errors are integrated over time. This is precisely why the perception layer (SLAM) must run in parallel.

## 2.4 Why the Mid-Point Variant (E9, E10) is Used

A naive Euler update would set

$$\theta_k = \theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta$$

and then use $\theta_{k-1}$ inside the sine/cosine — a **first-order** approximation. The mid-point formulation uses the **average heading** $\theta_{k-1} + \Delta\theta/2$, which is a **second-order** accurate Runge–Kutta-like step. This drastically reduces drift during high-$\omega$ rotations (e.g., in-place spins).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Differential-Drive Kinematics

**Statement.** Given the two wheel angular speeds $\dot{\phi}_r$ and $\dot{\phi}_l$, derive the robot's body-frame linear velocity $\nu$ and angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 1 — Express wheel surface speeds.**

Each wheel rolls without slipping, so the linear speed of the contact point equals $r \cdot \dot{\phi}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_r &\;=\; r\,\dot{\phi}_r \\
\nu_l &\;=\; r\,\dot{\phi}_l
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-body rolling constraint.**

The base is a rigid body. The centre-of-mass (which is the midpoint of the axle) has linear velocity $\nu$. By the rigid-body velocity field:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu \;&=\; \nu_l + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} \\
\nu \;&=\; \nu_r - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the two equations to eliminate $\omega$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\nu \;&=\; \nu_r + \nu_l \\
\Rightarrow \quad \nu \;&=\; \frac{\nu_r + \nu_l}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two equations to eliminate $\nu$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \;&=\; \nu_r - \nu_l - L\,\omega \\
\Rightarrow \quad \omega \;&=\; \frac{\nu_r - \nu_l}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion.** Equations **E1** and **E2** are the **forward kinematics** of the TurtleBot. $\square$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Differential-Drive Kinematics

**Statement.** Given a desired body-frame twist $(\nu, \omega)$, find the corresponding wheel commands $(\nu_r, \nu_l)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the forward map written in matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \nu_r \\ \nu_l \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & +\dfrac{L}{2} \\[4pt] 1 & -\dfrac{L}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \nu \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify by direct substitution of E1 and E2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_r &\;=\; \nu + \frac{L}{2}\,\omega \;\;=\;\; \frac{\nu_r + \nu_l}{2} + \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\nu_r - \nu_l}{L} \\
&\;=\; \frac{\nu_r + \nu_l + \nu_r - \nu_l}{2} \;=\; \nu_r \quad \checkmark \\
\nu_l &\;=\; \nu - \frac{L}{2}\,\omega \;\;=\;\; \frac{\nu_r + \nu_l}{2} - \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\nu_r - \nu_l}{L} \\
&\;=\; \frac{\nu_r + \nu_l - \nu_r + \nu_l}{2} \;=\; \nu_l \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion.** The matrix is its own inverse only when the determinant is non-zero, i.e., the matrix is square and invertible — which it is, since $L > 0$ always. $\square$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check — Straight-line motion:** Set $\omega = 0$. Then $\nu_r = \nu_l = \nu$. Both wheels spin at the same rate — the robot moves in a straight line. **Sanity check — Pure rotation:** Set $\nu = 0$. Then $\nu_r = -\nu_l = \tfrac{L}{2}\,\omega$. The two wheels spin in **opposite directions** at equal magnitudes — the robot rotates about its own centre.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Encoder-to-Odometry Update (Mid-Point Method)

**Step 1 — Convert raw ticks to wheel distances (E5, E6).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_r &\;=\; \frac{2\pi r}{n}\,N_r \\
s_l &\;=\; \frac{2\pi r}{n}\,N_l
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean displacement and heading increment (E7, E8).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s &\;=\; \frac{s_r + s_l}{2} \\
\Delta\theta &\;=\; \frac{s_r - s_l}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Update pose using the mid-point heading.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &\;=\; x_k + \Delta s\,\cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{k+1} &\;=\; y_k + \Delta s\,\sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{k+1} &\;=\; \theta_k + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Evaluation.** Let $r = 0.033\;\text{m}$, $n = 4096$ ticks/rev, $L = 0.160\;\text{m}$. In one sampling interval, $N_r = 220$ and $N_l = 180$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_r &\;=\; \frac{2\pi (0.033)}{4096} \cdot 220 \;=\; 0.011135\;\text{m} \\
s_l &\;=\; \frac{2\pi (0.033)}{4096} \cdot 180 \;=\; 0.009110\;\text{m} \\
\Delta s &\;=\; \frac{0.011135 + 0.009110}{2} \;=\; 0.010123\;\text{m} \\
\Delta\theta &\;=\; \frac{0.011135 - 0.009110}{0.160} \;=\; 0.012656\;\text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $\theta_k = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &\;=\; 0 + 0.010123 \cdot \cos(0.006328) \;\approx\; 0.010123\;\text{m} \\
y_{k+1} &\;=\; 0 + 0.010123 \cdot \sin(0.006328) \;\approx\; 0.0000640\;\text{m} \\
\theta_{k+1} &\;=\; 0.012656\;\text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert the **per-sample** $\Delta s$ and $\Delta\theta$ into the **full trajectory** by accumulating the increments. The odometry frame grows in accuracy as the control loop runs; a single-step computation is never the final answer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — LIDAR Time-of-Flight Range

**Step 1 — The LIDAR emits a laser pulse and measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$.**

**Step 2 — The pulse travels to the obstacle and back, covering twice the range $P$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c \cdot \Delta t \;&=\; 2\,P_{\text{LiDAR}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{LiDAR}} \;&=\; \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$ and $\Delta t = 67\;\text{ns}$:**

$$
P_{\text{LiDAR}} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \cdot 67 \times 10^{-9}}{2} \;=\; 10.05\;\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **RPLIDAR A1** on the TurtleBot 3 has a maximum range of **\mathbf{\approx 12\;m}** and an angular resolution of **1°**, producing 360 samples per full scan at **5 Hz**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Update

| Scenario | Method | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slow straight-line cruise | First-order Euler is acceptable | $\omega$ is near zero, $\Delta\theta$ small |
| Fast in-place rotation | **Mid-point** (E9, E10) is mandatory | $\Delta\theta$ per step can be > 0.1 rad |
| Open-loop dead reckoning only | All three, integrated over time | Drift accumulates linearly with distance |
| Fused with AMCL | Use E1–E10 *as prediction*, then correct | Particle filter reweights by sensor likelihood |
| Visual SLAM (RTAB-Map) | Replace E1–E10 with visual odometry | Eliminates wheel-slip drift on carpets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full TurtleBot Software Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LIDAR LDS01 Sensor] --> B[ROS2 Driver Node]
    C[IMU BNO055] --> D[IMU Driver Node]
    E[Wheel Encoders] --> F[OpenCR Firmware]
    G[Depth Camera OAKD] --> H[Camera Driver Node]
    F --> I[Differential Drive Controller]
    B --> J[Sensor Fusion Node]
    D --> J
    H --> J
    I --> K[Odometry Publisher]
    J --> L[SLAM Toolbox Node]
    K --> L
    L --> M[TF2 Transform Tree]
    J --> M
    M --> N[Nav2 Planner Server]
    L --> N
    N --> O[Nav2 Controller Server]
    O --> P[Behaviour Tree Navigator]
    P --> I
    Q[User Application Node] --> P
    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style Q fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — TF2 Coordinate Transform Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[World Frame] --> M[Map Frame]
    M --> O[Odom Frame]
    O --> BF[Base Footprint]
    BF --> BS[Base Stabilized]
    BS --> BC[Base Link]
    BC --> LW[Left Wheel Link]
    BC --> RW[Right Wheel Link]
    BC --> L[Laser Frame]
    BC --> C[Camera Optical Frame]
    BC --> I[IMU Frame]
    style W fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style BC fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Examiner's Note):** The arrows in a TF2 tree point from **parent to child** frames. Transformations compose left-to-right: $T_{W \to L} = T_{W \to M}\,T_{M \to O}\,T_{O \to BF}\,T_{BF \to BC}\,T_{BC \to L}$. The chain is computed by `tf2_echo` or `ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames`.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — NavigateToPose Action Call

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Node
    participant BT as Nav2 BT Navigator
    participant PL as Planner Server
    participant CT as Controller Server
    participant RB as Recovery Server
    participant BB as OpenCR Base
    U->>BT: Send NavigateToPose Goal
    BT->>PL: Request Global Path
    PL-->>BT: Path Array
    BT->>CT: Request Velocity Commands
    CT->>BB: Publish cmd_vel Twist
    BB-->>CT: Encoder Feedback
    CT-->>BT: Status Feedback
    BT->>RB: Trigger Recovery if Stuck
    RB-->>BT: Recovery Outcome
    BT-->>U: Final Result Succeeded
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Differential-Drive Kinematic Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Angular Velocity phi_r] --> B[Multiply by r]
    C[Wheel Angular Velocity phi_l] --> D[Multiply by r]
    B --> E[Add nu_r and nu_l]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Divide by 2]
    F --> G[nu Linear Velocity]
    B --> H[Subtract nu_l from nu_r]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Divide by L]
    I --> J[omega Angular Velocity]
    style G fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive complexity rationale:** Because the TurtleBot is a *physical* robotic system rather than a pure mathematics topic, the Mermaid diagrams above map the **data flow, transform chain, message-passing sequences, and kinematic signal flow** at block-level fidelity. This is preferred over attempting an unsupportable photorealistic mechanical drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** *What is TurtleBot? List any two generations with one distinguishing feature each.*

**Model Answer:**
TurtleBot is an open-source, low-cost, ROS-based mobile robot platform used for education and research.
*(i)* **TurtleBot 2 (2013):** Uses a **Kobuki base** with **Kinect sensor** for RGB-D perception.
*(ii)* **TurtleBot 3 Burger (2017):** Uses a **Raspberry Pi 3** as onboard compute and an **LDS-01 360° LIDAR**, with a modular 3D-printed chassis.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** *Differentiate between ROS 2 topics, services, and actions with a TurtleBot example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Primitive | Communication Pattern | TurtleBot Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Topic** | Asynchronous, many-to-many, continuous streams | `/scan` — LIDAR continuously publishes `LaserScan` messages |
| **Service** | Synchronous request/reply, one-shot | `/reset_odometry` — manually reset the base odometry |
| **Action** | Asynchronous with feedback, can be cancelled | `/navigate_to_pose` — goal to move to a target coordinate |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[Differential-Drive Kinematics Heavy]*

> **(a)** Derive the forward and inverse kinematics of a **differential-drive mobile robot** such as the TurtleBot 3 Burger. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A TurtleBot 3 Burger has $r = 0.033\;\text{m}$, $L = 0.160\;\text{m}$, and encoders of resolution $n = 4096$ ticks/rev. In one control cycle, the right and left wheels produce $N_r = 800$ and $N_l = 600$ ticks respectively. The current pose is $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k) = (1.50, 2.20, 0.524\;\text{rad})$. Using the **mid-point odometry update**, compute the new pose $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \theta_{k+1})$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*Assumptions:* (i) Both wheels roll without slipping. (ii) The robot is a single rigid body. (iii) The motion occurs on a flat 2D plane.

From the rigid-body velocity field for the two contact points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_l + \frac{L}{2}\,\omega &\;=\; \nu \\
\nu_r - \frac{L}{2}\,\omega &\;=\; \nu
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding and subtracting yields the forward kinematics **E1** and **E2**. Inverting the $2 \times 2$ linear map gives the inverse kinematics **E3** and **E4**. *(Full derivation already shown in Section 3.1.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert ticks to wheel distances.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_r &\;=\; \frac{2\pi(0.033)}{4096} \cdot 800 \;=\; 0.04049\;\text{m} \\
s_l &\;=\; \frac{2\pi(0.033)}{4096} \cdot 600 \;=\; 0.03037\;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean displacement and heading increment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s &\;=\; \frac{0.04049 + 0.03037}{2} \;=\; 0.03543\;\text{m} \\
\Delta\theta &\;=\; \frac{0.04049 - 0.03037}{0.160} \;=\; 0.06325\;\text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mid-point heading.**

$$
\theta_{\text{mid}} \;=\; \theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} \;=\; 0.524 + 0.03163 \;=\; 0.5556\;\text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Update pose.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &\;=\; 1.50 + 0.03543 \cdot \cos(0.5556) \;=\; 1.50 + 0.03543 \cdot 0.8490 \\
&\;=\; 1.50 + 0.03008 \;=\; 1.5301\;\text{m} \\
y_{k+1} &\;=\; 2.20 + 0.03543 \cdot \sin(0.5556) \;=\; 2.20 + 0.03543 \cdot 0.5284 \\
&\;=\; 2.20 + 0.01872 \;=\; 2.2187\;\text{m} \\
\theta_{k+1} &\;=\; 0.524 + 0.06325 \;=\; 0.5873\;\text{rad} \quad (\approx 33.65^{\circ})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (7-mark sub-part):** Most students lose **2–3 marks** because they (i) use the **first-order** Euler update `cos(theta_k)` instead of the **mid-point** `cos(theta_k + delta_theta/2)`, (ii) forget to convert encoder ticks to metres using the factor $\frac{2\pi r}{n}$, and (iii) do not state the **unit rad** in the final $\theta_{k+1}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Software Stack / Programming Heavy]*

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ROS 2 software architecture** running on a modern TurtleBot 4. Clearly label the role of (i) `ros2_control`, (ii) `slam_toolbox`, and (iii) `Nav2` Behaviour-Tree Navigator. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A TurtleBot must navigate from $(0, 0)$ to $(4, 0)$ in a corridor. The onboard 2D LIDAR returns a single scan showing an obstacle at angle $\alpha = 0^{\circ}$ (front) and range $P = 1.5\;\text{m}$. The robot's linear velocity is $\nu = 0.25\;\text{m/s}$ and angular velocity is $\omega = 0$. Using the **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** or **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** concept, describe the algorithm that would be invoked by the `controller_server` to avoid the obstacle. Show the calculation of the **collision time** $t_c = P / \nu$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The diagram from **Section 4.1** should be redrawn. Roles of the three highlighted components:

* **`ros2_control`** — Provides a **standardized hardware abstraction layer** for motors, encoders, and IMUs. It exposes a `CommandInterface` (e.g., `velocity`) and a `StateInterface` (e.g., `position`) so that higher-level controllers (e.g., `diff_drive_controller`) can be swapped without rewriting hardware drivers.
* **`slam_toolbox`** — Runs **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping** by fusing LIDAR scans with wheel-odometry / IMU data. It publishes a continuous `map → odom` correction to TF2 and the topic `/map`.
* **`Nav2 Behaviour-Tree Navigator**` — Receives a `NavigateToPose` action goal, queries the `planner_server` for a global path, then uses the `controller_server` (typically DWA or TEB) to compute local velocity commands at 20 Hz. A **behaviour tree** sequences **recovery behaviours** (rotate, back-up, wait) when progress is blocked.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute collision time.**

$$
t_c \;=\; \frac{P}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{1.5}{0.25} \;=\; 6.0\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — DWA sampling window.**

The DWA samples admissible velocities $(\nu, \omega)$ within a dynamic window bounded by:

* Maximum achievable speed: $\nu_{\max} = 0.30\;\text{m/s}$.
* Maximum acceleration: $a_{\max} = 0.5\;\text{m/s}^{2}$ over the control period $\Delta t = 0.1\;\text{s}$.

Thus the velocity window is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_{\text{low}} &\;=\; \nu - a_{\max}\Delta t \;=\; 0.25 - 0.05 \;=\; 0.20\;\text{m/s} \\
\nu_{\text{high}} &\;=\; \nu + a_{\max}\Delta t \;=\; 0.25 + 0.05 \;=\; 0.30\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Score each candidate trajectory.**

The DWA objective function combines three terms:

$$
G(\nu, \omega) \;=\; \alpha\,\text{heading}(\nu,\omega) + \beta\,\text{clearance}(\nu,\omega) + \gamma\,\text{velocity}(\nu,\omega)
$$

Because $t_c = 6.0\;\text{s} < t_{\text{horizon}}$ (typically 3–5 s), the **clearance** term dominates and the **optimal trajectory** is one that **curves to the left** (positive $\omega$) so the predicted arc misses the obstacle. The controller then publishes the chosen $(\nu, \omega)$ to `/cmd_vel`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b, 7-mark sub-part):** Students often forget the **units** in $t_c$ (must be in seconds) and forget to include the **obstacle clearance term** in the DWA cost function. Marks are awarded for explicitly stating the **three-term cost function** and that **$\beta \cdot \text{clearance}$** dominates when $t_c$ is short.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

* ✅ **TurtleBot** = open-source, ROS-based, **differential-drive**, low-cost mobile robot.
* ✅ Three core subsystems: **Mobile Base**, **Sensor Stack**, **Onboard Compute + Firmware**.
* ✅ Sensor Stack = **LIDAR + RGB-D camera + IMU + wheel encoders**.
* ✅ **Differential drive** is a **non-holonomic** system — it **cannot translate sideways**; sideways motion requires a sequence of in-place rotations.
* ✅ **Forward kinematics**: $\nu = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nu_r + \nu_l)$, $\omega = \tfrac{1}{L}(\nu_r - \nu_l)$.
* ✅ **Inverse kinematics**: $\nu_r = \nu + \tfrac{L}{2}\omega$, $\nu_l = \nu - \tfrac{L}{2}\omega$.
* ✅ **Encoder distance**: $s = \tfrac{2\pi r}{n}\,N$ where $n$ is the **ticks-per-revolution** resolution.
* ✅ **Mid-point odometry update** is the **second-order** accurate Runge–Kutta-like step — **always preferred** during high-$\omega$ rotations.
* ✅ **ROS 2 primitives**: **Topics** (asynchronous streams), **Services** (one-shot request/reply), **Actions** (long-running with feedback).
* ✅ **TF2 tree** flows: `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → {wheels, sensors}`.
* ✅ **SLAM** fuses LIDAR + odometry + IMU to publish the `map → odom` correction.
* ✅ **Nav2 stack** = `planner_server` + `controller_server` (DWA/TEB) + `bt_navigator` + `recoveries_server`.
* ✅ **LIDAR range formula**: $P = \tfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$, with $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$.
* ✅ **Dead-reckoning drift** is unbounded — it **must** be corrected by a localization filter (e.g., AMCL) or loop closure in SLAM.
* ✅ **DWA cost function** = weighted sum of **heading + clearance + velocity**.
* ✅ **Straight-line motion check** → $\omega = 0 \Rightarrow \nu_r = \nu_l$.
* ✅ **Pure-rotation check** → $\nu = 0 \Rightarrow \nu_r = -\nu_l = \tfrac{L}{2}\,\omega$.
